instruction: Is breastfeeding right for me?
output: Breastfeeding is described as right for you and definitely right for your baby. Some people choose it because science shows many benefits for babies and mothers, and others choose it because it feels naturally right and brings mothers close to their children.

instruction: How does breastfeeding help my baby besides giving milk?
output: Breastfeeding provides warmth, security, comfort, and love as well as food. Your milk also contains every vitamin, mineral, and other nutritional elements the baby needs, changes subtly to match his needs, has living cells that inhibit harmful bacteria and viruses, and includes interferon and interleukins, which are powerful anti-infectives.

instruction: My mother couldn’t breastfeed—does that mean I will have trouble too?
output: Many mothers have had tough experiences, but we've learned a lot since then about understanding and respecting mother and baby instincts. Fewer interventions during birth make those instincts easier to tap into, and La Leche League is available to help you work through any issues. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Why doesn’t breastfeeding always come naturally to everyone?
output: Some women report tough experiences. The book says we've learned more about understanding and respecting instincts and that the fewer interventions during birth, the easier those instincts are to tap into.

instruction: My newborn seems to need constant closeness—what does a baby really need?
output: A newborn has three demands: warmth in the arms of his mother, food from her breasts, and security in the knowledge of her presence. Breastfeeding satisfies all three.

instruction: Can formula give my baby everything breast milk does?
output: No formula comes close to a mother's milk. Your milk contains every vitamin, mineral, and other nutritional elements your baby needs, changes to match his requirements, and includes living cells and anti-infective substances that inhibit harmful bacteria and viruses.

instruction: What if I run into breastfeeding problems—where can I get help?
output: La Leche League is always available to help you work through breastfeeding issues. Their meetings address questions and include pregnant women, mothers with new babies, and mothers with older babies or children. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: Does breastfeeding affect the mother's health too?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding is described as important to mothers' bodies and is said to affect a surprising number of mothers' health issues and to help protect mothers' health.

instruction: Does my milk actually change for my baby over time?
output: Yes. Your milk changes subtly through the meal, day, and year to match subtle changes in your baby's requirements.

instruction: Are there infection-fighting components in breast milk?
output: Yes. Your milk contains living cells unique to milk that inhibit the growth of harmful bacteria and viruses, and it contains interferon and interleukins, which are described as powerful anti-infectives.

instruction: Can breast milk be used for eye infections or skin problems?
output: A squirt of breast milk can be used to treat eye infections and can speed the healing of skin problems, according to the text.

instruction: Will having fewer interventions during birth help breastfeeding get started?
output: The text says that the fewer interventions you have during birth, the easier the instincts that support breastfeeding will be to tap into.

instruction: What happens at a La Leche League meeting?
output: A meeting would include a mix of pregnant women, mothers with new babies, and mothers with older babies or children. The cornerstone of meetings is addressing questions from women at different stages of motherhood.

instruction: Why is breastfeeding described as so important emotionally and physically?
output: Breastfeeding is described as extremely important; almost nothing you do for your child will affect him both emotionally and physically as profoundly. It also affects many aspects of the mother's health and helps protect the mother's health.

instruction: Is breastfeeding really that important for my baby and me?
output: Extremely important. Breastfeeding affects a child emotionally and physically in profound ways. Your milk is the baby’s normal food and contains every vitamin, mineral, and other nutritional elements your baby needs. It also affects many aspects of your own health.

instruction: What is colostrum and why does it matter for newborns?
output: Colostrum is the small amount of milk produced in the first couple of days after birth (it starts during pregnancy). It has concentrated immunological properties, including high levels of sIgA and much more pancreatic secretory trypsin inhibitor (PSTI) than later milk. It helps coat and protect the newborn’s intestines, encourages beneficial bacteria, and acts as a laxative to help clear meconium.

instruction: Can a squirt of breast milk really help an eye infection or skin problem?
output: Yes. A squirt of breast milk can treat eye infections and can speed the healing of skin problems.

instruction: Does breast milk change during the day or even during a single feeding?
output: Yes. Breast milk changes through a meal, over the course of a day, and across the year to match subtle changes in your baby’s needs. During a feeding, shorter nursing provides lower-fat milk, switching sides can give a thirst-quencher, and longer nursing pulls more higher-calorie fat.

instruction: If I formula-feed, will my baby respond to vaccines the same way?
output: No. A formula-fed baby has a less effective immune response to vaccinations compared with a breastfed baby.

instruction: Are there long-term health risks if my baby isn’t breastfed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. As an older child and adult, a baby who wasn’t breastfed is at greater risk of Crohn’s disease, ulcerative colitis, type 1 diabetes, heart disease, and certain cancers. There is also a higher risk of obesity, type 2 diabetes, heart disease, osteoporosis, and higher blood pressure and a more negative response to stress later in life.

instruction: Does breastfeeding affect jaw and dental development?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding exercises and massages the jaw muscles so the face and jaw bones develop more fully. Bottle-feeding and pacifier use tend to produce a narrower jaw and a higher palate that can restrict nose breathing. Babies who use pacifiers instead of soothing at the breast are more likely to need speech therapy later, and children who breastfeed less than a year are much more likely to need orthodontia.

instruction: Is colostrum a laxative for newborns? Why is that helpful?
output: Yes. Colostrum acts as a laxative that helps get the newborn’s intestines working and helps clean out the meconium that accumulated before birth.

instruction: Does human milk contain antibodies and other immune factors for my baby?
output: Yes. Human milk contains sIgA, interferon, interleukins, white blood cells, PSTI, and human growth factor, among other immune and protective components. These help protect and develop the infant’s immune system toward being nearly as sturdy as the mother’s.

instruction: Does formula make a baby’s kidneys and liver work harder?
output: Yes. A formula-fed baby’s kidneys and liver work harder to process the waste products from formula.

instruction: Is the risk of SIDS higher if a baby isn’t breastfed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The risk of SIDS (sudden infant death syndrome) and infant death from many other causes is higher if a baby isn’t breastfed.

instruction: Does breast milk contain nutrients that help brain and heart development?
output: Yes. Breast milk contains long-chain fatty acids that support a healthy heart and lactose that supports brain development. It also includes insulin for digestion and other nutrients in forms available to a baby.

instruction: Can babies control the milk they get to meet thirst or hunger?
output: Yes. Babies can design parts of their own meal: nursing briefly delivers lower-fat milk that quenches thirst; switching sides gives another thirst-quencher; nursing longer or more vigorously brings higher-calorie, higher-fat milk for greater hunger needs.

instruction: Is the iron added to infant formulas different from breast milk iron and can it be harmful?
output: Yes. Iron added to formulas is in forms the baby can’t readily use and can be harmful because it increases the risk of intestinal infection, intestinal bleeding, and anemia.

instruction: What does colostrum do for my newborn's intestines?
output: Colostrum has an acid level that encourages a baby's intestines to welcome beneficial bacteria. Colostrum is also a laxative that helps get his intestines working and helps clean out the tar-like stool called meconium that built up before birth.

instruction: When does mature breast milk replace colostrum?
output: Mature milk phases in during the first two weeks after birth.

instruction: What immune and growth substances are in breast milk?
output: Breast milk contains interferon, interleukins, white blood cells, and SIgA, and gives the breastfed baby an immune system nearly as sturdy as his mother's. It also contains human growth factor, insulin for digestion, long-chain fatty acids, and lactose.

instruction: I'm worried about iron in formula — is it safe?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Iron is added to formulas in forms that the baby can't readily use and which can actually be harmful, because it increases the risk of intestinal infection, intestinal bleeding, and anemia.

instruction: Can breastfeeding affect how my baby's face and jaw develop?
output: When a baby breastfeeds, the muscles in his jaws are exercised and massaged so the bones in his face and jaw develop more fully. A jaw that results from bottle-feeding and pacifiers is described as narrower with a higher palate that is more likely to restrict nose breathing.

instruction: How does my baby control what kind of milk he gets when nursing?
output: If he's thirsty he nurses for a shorter time and gets lower-fat milk; if still thirsty he may switch sides sooner for another thirst-quencher. If he's extra hungry he stays longer or nurses more vigorously on the first side to pull down higher-calorie fat. If he takes more milk during a growth spurt, more milk will be available the next time he nurses; if he drinks less, your milk production scales back.

instruction: I'm worried about bleeding after delivery — does breastfeeding help with that?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. When your newborn takes your breast soon after delivery, your uterus contracts and bleeding slows. Hemorrhage is a greater risk with formula-feeding, and the belly stays larger longer with formula-feeding.

instruction: Will exclusive breastfeeding affect when my periods come back and my chances of getting pregnant?
output: If you breastfeed exclusively and your baby nurses often, including at least once during the night, your periods most likely won't come back for at least six months. Your chances of getting pregnant again will be extremely low during that time.

instruction: Does breastfeeding help with losing pregnancy weight?
output: Breastfeeding helps many (not all) women lose weight readily. Nature gave some pregnancy weight for the purpose of making milk in the first few months, and the natural design is for it to melt away by the time the baby is well started on solids.

instruction: Does breastfeeding change a mother's long-term risk of diseases like cancer or diabetes?
output: Breastfeeding is described as an insurance policy against breast, uterine, and cervical cancer, and the longer you breastfeed the stronger that protection. Women who haven't breastfed are said to be at greater risk for metabolic syndrome, which makes heart disease and diabetes more likely. Osteoporosis and fractures are also more common in women who didn't breastfeed.

instruction: Can my breast make antibodies for germs my baby picks up?
output: Yes. If the baby picks up germs, he communicates those germs to your breast at his next nursing, and the breast starts producing specialized antibodies.

instruction: Are claims that breastfeeding raises IQ or prevents many illnesses reliable?
output: Many people have heard those claims, but the excerpt points out that much breastfeeding research has design problems. Accurate science focuses on controlled experiments, and the text notes that a lot of research on breastfeeding is simply research on breastfeeding rather than experimental comparisons, which calls those claims into question.

instruction: Does breastfeeding change how much insulin I need if I have insulin-dependent diabetes?
output: If you already have insulin-dependent diabetes, you're likely to need less insulin while you're a nursing mother.

instruction: How does nighttime nursing affect milk supply and other breastfeeding effects?
output: If your baby nurses often, including at least once during the night, and you breastfeed exclusively, your periods most likely won't return for at least six months. Also, milk production responds to demand: if your baby takes more milk now, more will be available next time; if he drinks less, production scales back.

instruction: Will giving a pacifier instead of breastfeeding affect my child's speech or orthodontic needs?
output: Babies who use pacifiers instead of soothing themselves at the breast are more likely to need speech therapy later. A child who breastfeeds for less than a year is much more likely to need orthodontia later on, and pacifier- or bottle-related jaw shapes are more likely to restrict nose breathing.

instruction: Does breastfeeding lower my risk of osteoporosis or fractures later in life?
output: Yes. Osteoporosis and fractures are more common in women who did not breastfeed, so women who breastfeed are less likely to have those problems later on.

instruction: Will formula-feeding affect my blood pressure?
output: Yes. A formula-feeding mother's blood pressure is likely to be higher, probably because her neurological and endocrine responses are more pronounced than those of a nursing mother.

instruction: Does formula feeding increase my risk of autoimmune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis later on?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A formula-feeding mother's overall physical and mental health takes a hit, and in later years she remains at increased risk of developing autoimmune diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis.

instruction: Does breastfeeding raise my baby's IQ?
output: No. Breastfeeding does not add IQ points; breastfeeding results in normal IQ, and claims that it raises IQ are not true according to the discussion on research design.

instruction: Is breast milk from a mother in a poor country as good as from a well-nourished mother?
output: Yes, unless the mother is in extremely poor nutritional health. Breast milk from a mother in an African village is described as being as good as breast milk from a mother in a Manhattan apartment, except in cases of extreme maternal undernutrition.

instruction: Does breastfeeding help me feel more attached to my baby?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding triggers surges of prolactin and oxytocin that make you feel loving and nurturing and that deepen the attachment between you and your baby.

instruction: Why do some studies say breastfeeding protects babies when others disagree?
output: Many studies treated breastfeeding as the normal baseline rather than studying formula as the experimental exposure, which made formula-related problems less visible; more recent research starts with the breastfed baby as the normal reference and looks at what happens when that system is altered.

instruction: Can exclusive breastfeeding reduce the health gap between poor and affluent babies?
output: Yes. Exclusive breastfeeding goes a long way toward canceling out the health differences between being born into poverty and being born into affluence; breastfeeding is described as a safety net against the worst effects of poverty.

instruction: Do the hormones released during breastfeeding help me recover from childbirth?
output: Yes. The hormones prolactin and oxytocin released during breastfeeding help you recover from the emotional and physical stress of birth.

instruction: Will my response to my baby's fussing be different if I bottle-feed instead of breastfeed?
output: Yes. When a formula-feeding mother hears her baby fuss or cry she tends to respond using her mind, whereas a breastfeeding mother's whole body chemistry responds; the intimacy of feeding and how the baby is held can also differ.

instruction: Is breastfeeding simply the normal way to raise a baby rather than an added bonus?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding is described as the normal way to raise a baby; it does not give "brownie points" but provides the baseline of normal good health and normal IQ, while formula feeding represents a change from that normal.

instruction: Some mothers say breastfeeding made them feel euphoric. Is that common?
output: Some mothers do report that. Hormones released during breastfeeding have been described as producing a blissed-out, euphoric state for some mothers, and as giving feelings of confidence and connection.

instruction: How does breastfeeding affect my bond with my baby?
output: Breastfeeding deepens attachment: each time you nurse there’s a surge of prolactin and oxytocin that makes you feel loving and nurturing and helps you recover from the stress of birth. The nursing relationship becomes a foundation for how your baby will think of himself and others and the repeated full‑body closeness builds a strong emotional connection.

instruction: What hormones are released when I breastfeed and what do they do?
output: Every time you breastfeed there’s a surge of prolactin and oxytocin. These hormones foster a feeling of love and nurturing and also help you recover from the emotional and physical stress of birth.

instruction: If I have to be apart from my baby in the early months, will that hurt our bond or my milk supply?
output: Being away from each other, especially in the early months, can be emotionally difficult and can also be difficult for your milk supply. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: I had a difficult, traumatic birth. Can breastfeeding help me heal emotionally?
output: Breastfeeding can help heal many emotional wounds, including ones from a difficult or traumatic birth. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Do mothers who bottle-feed love their babies less than breastfeeding mothers?
output: No. The text says that mothers who are bottle‑feeding do love and value their babies. However, there is a difference: breastfeeding tends to trigger body chemistry responses and full‑body closeness that many mothers experience as a different kind of intimacy.

instruction: Why does breastfeeding feel more intimate than bottle feeding?
output: When a mother breastfeeds her body is the feeding: the baby’s cheeks are on the mother’s skin and the whole nursing is like a full‑body hug. By contrast, bottle‑feeding is often done with the baby held more loosely or off to the side so the baby feels the mother’s clothes against his face, and as he gets older he may face away or hold the bottle himself.

instruction: Will breastfeeding help me learn my baby’s cues faster than my partner?
output: Yes. With the intimate time of breastfeeding you get to know your baby’s body and personality and often learn to interpret his cries sooner than your partner. Day by day breastfeeding builds your confidence and mothering skills.

instruction: I’ve never breastfed before. Could I become very passionate about it once I start?
output: Many women are surprised by the passion they come to feel about breastfeeding, and many remember it as one of the best things they do in their lives.

instruction: Does breastfeeding influence how my child will see himself and others later on?
output: The nursing relationship becomes the foundation of the way a baby will think of himself and others, according to the text.

instruction: Do the hormones from breastfeeding change how I interact with my baby during a feed?
output: Yes. The surges of prolactin and oxytocin make you feel loving and nurturing and help you recover from birth; without these hormonal effects, mothers tend to talk to their babies less, interact less, and touch less.

instruction: Will other breastfeeding mothers feel an instant connection to me if we meet while nursing?
output: Yes. Many breastfeeding women feel a connection when they meet another breastfeeding mother, regardless of cultural differences or how long ago they breastfed. The anecdote about mothers sitting together while nursing illustrates that feeling of connection.

instruction: Why are baby blues more common where mother and baby can’t stay together after birth?
output: The text says that baby blues are more common in societies where mother and baby are not able to stay together nursing from birth, implying that separation is linked to higher rates of baby blues.

instruction: If I bottle-feed, am I likely to regret it later?
output: Many bottle‑feeding mothers do wish they had breastfed, while very few breastfeeding mothers wish they had bottle‑fed. It’s common for parents to have questions like this.

instruction: How does breastfeeding help me become a mother?
output: Breastfeeding eases you into your identity as a mother: you’re your baby’s food source and main comforter, your body responds instinctively once you get the hang of it, and the intimate time together builds your confidence and mothering skills.

instruction: Can breastfeeding help create a lasting close relationship with my child?
output: Yes. The text describes breastfeeding as building a strong emotional connection and gives an example of a long, close parent–child relationship that began with breastfeeding until the children weaned themselves.

instruction: Will my breasts work for breastfeeding if they're small or shaped differently?
output: Many parents wonder about this. There is a wide range of normal — big to small, low to high, soft to firm, and many nipple shapes. Breast size doesn’t matter and how much milk you make isn’t related to breast size. However, a few breast shapes and breasts that have had surgery can have trouble making enough milk, and some women do need to breastfeed more often.

instruction: How will breastfeeding help me understand my baby?
output: Breastfeeding gives you intimate time to learn your baby’s body and personality. You often learn to interpret his cries sooner than others, and day by day breastfeeding builds your confidence and mothering skills.

instruction: Can nursing calm my baby when he's overstimulated or upset?
output: Yes. After the early weeks, once breastfeeding becomes second nature, you can gentle your baby out of almost anything — hunger, tiredness, overstimulation, fear, or pain — with a little nursing.

instruction: Should I schedule nursing sessions the way I would schedule feedings?
output: Scheduling nursings, like scheduling kisses, would just make life harder for both of you. Breastfeeding works through frequent at-breast exchanges that help keep your milk supply in good shape and your baby growing well.

instruction: Will I have to wash and sterilize bottles if I choose to breastfeed?
output: No. Breastfeeding means no cans of formula or bottles, no washing, sterilizing, or storing, and no measuring, spilling, or heating. Your milk is always available, always the right temperature, and never spoiled.

instruction: Is breastfeeding helpful in emergencies or during disasters?
output: Yes. In an emergency such as shortages of formula and clean water, breastfeeding is one way to ensure you’ll have a way to feed your baby. Your milk is always available and not dependent on supplies or clean water to prepare it.

instruction: Do breastfeeding mothers often feel a special connection with other nursing mothers?
output: Many women are surprised by the passion they come to feel about breastfeeding. If you meet another breastfeeding woman anywhere in the world, you often feel a connection regardless of cultural differences or how long ago either of you breastfed. Shared nursing can make other differences seem unimportant.

instruction: Will breastfeeding mean fewer trips to the pediatrician?
output: Most likely you’ll have a lot fewer trips to the doctor when you breastfeed.

instruction: Can I nurse my baby during unexpected travel delays or long flights?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding can keep you and your baby comfortable during long delays. One mother nursed and played with her toddler through a long, unexpected plane delay and they were perfectly happy while a formula-fed baby and mother struggled after running out of formula.

instruction: Does breastfeeding affect my milk supply?
output: The little social at-breast exchanges help keep your milk supply in good shape and your baby growing well.

instruction: Will I have to stop and be still to nurse my baby during a busy day?
output: Yes. Breastfeeding lets you take little breaks throughout your busy day. You’ll often gather him in your arms and be still for a few minutes, and that can make life feel simpler afterwards.

instruction: Will my baby like my breast shape even if it doesn't look like the women in breastfeeding videos?
output: Yes. There are many normal breast shapes and sizes, and your baby will love your shape, whatever it is.

instruction: Do most women become very passionate about breastfeeding after they start?
output: Many women are surprised by the passion they come to feel about breastfeeding, and many remember it as one of the best things they did in their lives.

instruction: Can certain nipple or breast shapes make it harder to produce enough milk?
output: It’s understandable to worry about this. There is a wide range of normal nipple and breast shapes, but a few breast shapes and breasts that have had surgery can have trouble making enough milk, and some women need to breastfeed more often.

instruction: Does breastfeeding influence a baby’s social development or early experiences?
output: Yes. The breastfeeding relationship is described as the foundation for the development of human social relationships, and the warmth of the mother’s skin provides the first socializing experiences of life.

instruction: Will breastfeeding always be an option if we get stuck somewhere without formula or food?
output: It’s one way to ensure you’ll always have a way to feed your baby. The text gives an example of a long plane delay where a nursing mother could feed her toddler while a formula-fed baby ran out of formula and became very upset.

instruction: Does having small or large breasts affect how much milk I make?
output: Breast size doesn’t matter. How much milk you make isn’t related to how big your breasts are.

instruction: I had breast surgery before pregnancy — will I be able to breastfeed?
output: Some breasts that have had surgery can have trouble making enough milk, and some women need to breastfeed more often to keep a good supply. There are many ways to help breast-related problems.

instruction: My nipples look flat right after birth — can I still breastfeed?
output: It’s common for some nipples to become temporarily flat right after birth if many IV fluids during birth caused the breasts to swell. Some nipples are inverted, but there are ways to help them untuck, and breastfeeding can almost always work.

instruction: Does the size of my nipples matter for my baby to latch?
output: Nipple size doesn’t matter. Most babies can latch, and if a nipple is too big or wide for a very small mouth, the baby usually just needs to grow until his mouth is a bit bigger.

instruction: Are newborns born ready to breastfeed even after a difficult birth?
output: Yes. Babies are born expecting to breastfeed and have many innate reflexes designed to help them do it well even after a tough birth. They are built to deal with difficult births, cold, hunger, separation, and germs and still breastfeed well.

instruction: Breastfeeding hurts — what should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Nipple sensitivity is common in the early days, but if breastfeeding actually hurts that’s your body’s signal to change something. Pain guides you to find a more comfortable position, and most nipple pain is solved with a few simple changes. Nipple pain and damage are not normal.

instruction: Why do I automatically hold my crying baby upright against my chest?
output: Most mothers, without thinking, hold a crying baby vertically so the baby’s chest and tummy are against their shoulder and chest. That posture calms the baby and also puts him in a good spot to head toward the breast if he’s hungry.

instruction: My baby seems groggy and won’t latch well after birth — could birth medications be the cause?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Today we see more babies who can’t get their act together for a while, and this is usually because their systems are overwhelmed with birth medications and interventions. There is information to help understand and minimize this possibility.

instruction: If my baby’s mouth is too small for a large nipple, do I need special equipment?
output: If the nipple is too big or wide for the baby to latch, the baby usually just needs to grow until he has a bit bigger mouth and then he’ll do fine.

instruction: Is it normal for nipples to be sensitive in the first few days?
output: Yes. Nipple sensitivity is common in the early days.

instruction: Are nipple injuries a normal part of breastfeeding?
output: It’s common to worry about this. Nipple pain and damage are not normal.

instruction: Can most breast and nipple problems be helped so breastfeeding works?
output: Yes. Though some breast or nipple types require extra effort especially at first, there are many ways to help any breast-related problems and breastfeeding can almost always work.

instruction: Could IV fluids during labor make my nipples temporarily flat?
output: Yes. Some nipples become temporarily flat right after birth if too many IV fluids during birth caused the breasts to swell.

instruction: Why is it good to hold my baby with chest and tummy against my chest and shoulder?
output: Holding your baby chest-to-chest calms the baby, puts him in a good spot to head toward the breast if he’s hungry, and gives calming, access, and support.

instruction: Could my baby be having trouble latching because of the medications or interventions during birth?
output: Yes. The text says we see more babies who can’t get their act together for a while because their systems are overwhelmed with birth medications and interventions.

instruction: Will breastfeeding hurt my nipples?
output: Nipple sensitivity is common in the early days. If breastfeeding actually hurts, that’s your body’s signal to change something. Pain guides you to find a more comfortable position, and most nipple pain is solved with a few simple changes.

instruction: What should I do if nursing actually hurts a lot?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Pain is the body’s way of guiding you to find a more comfortable position, so change something—adjust position or the baby’s angle. Most nipple pain is solved with a few simple changes rather than continuing to nurse in a painful way.

instruction: I’m worried I won’t make enough milk — how likely is that?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Most mothers are able to make plenty of milk and the chances are really good that you will be able to, too. Some women do have difficulties, but most of the reasons are fixable.

instruction: If I can’t make a full milk supply, can I still breastfeed successfully?
output: Yes. Many mothers nurse very satisfactorily with a partial milk supply. They supplement to make up what they can’t make and focus on the milk they can provide; every drop of your milk contains immunities and elements absent from formula.

instruction: Does hand-expressing colostrum ahead of time help?
output: Consider hand-expressing some colostrum to freeze ahead of time. It can give you a small just-in-case supply of colostrum, and there’s some evidence that it may also boost your future supply.

instruction: How long should I breastfeed my baby?
output: Breastfeed as long as you and your baby want. Experts such as the World Health Organization and many national pediatric associations advise exclusive breastfeeding for about six months, adding solid foods gradually, and continuing breastfeeding for at least two years.

instruction: Why do many women stop breastfeeding sooner than they planned?
output: Many women end up weaning sooner than they planned because they ran into challenges and didn’t know how to get help.

instruction: Is nipple damage and pain a normal part of breastfeeding?
output: Nipple sensitivity is common in the early days, but nipple pain and damage are not normal.

instruction: Can previous breast surgery make breastfeeding harder?
output: Yes. Occasionally women have anatomical problems that make breastfeeding difficult, such as previous breast surgery or a breast or nipple problem.

instruction: Can a baby’s difficulty removing milk cause a low milk supply?
output: Yes. Sometimes the baby has a problem removing milk effectively, and that can be one reason a mother has difficulties with milk production.

instruction: What do the experts say about exclusive breastfeeding?
output: The World Health Organization and many national pediatric associations advise exclusive breastfeeding (no other drinks or solid foods) for about six months, with solid foods gradually added and breastfeeding continuing for at least two years.

instruction: If I get ongoing nipple pain, does that mean I should stop breastfeeding?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Pain is your body’s signal to change something—most nipple pain is solved with a few simple changes and nipple pain and damage are not considered normal.

instruction: Are there ways to increase milk supply if I have trouble producing enough?
output: Yes. The text says there are lots of ways to increase your milk supply and that help is available, including support from LLL leaders.

instruction: Is every little bit of breast milk important, even if my baby only gets a small amount?
output: Yes. Every single drop of your milk is beneficial — it contains the immunities of a full milk supply and many elements that are completely absent from formula.

instruction: How long should I plan to breastfeed my baby?
output: You can breastfeed as long as you and your baby want. Based on research, the World Health Organization and many national pediatric associations advise exclusive breastfeeding (no other drinks or solid foods) for about six months, with solids gradually added and breastfeeding continuing for at least two years.

instruction: I'm worried I might have to stop breastfeeding earlier than I want — why does that happen and what can help?
output: Many women end up weaning sooner than they had planned because they ran into challenges and didn’t know how to get help. It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Having access to support groups like La Leche League and other resources can help you get assistance so you can continue if you choose.

instruction: Do I need special nursing clothes to breastfeed comfortably?
output: Not really. Clothes made especially for nursing are nice but not necessary. Most mothers wear a top that’s loose enough to pull up from the hem, or a button-front shirt unbuttoned from the bottom. A loose jacket or over-shirt with a T-shirt or tank top underneath can also provide extra coverage.

instruction: Do I have to wear a nursing bra after my baby is born?
output: Not necessarily. Bras aren’t a health necessity — if you’re happy braless, you can stay braless. Many women find that whatever bra they wore in late pregnancy will probably fit after the baby is born, though cup size may be somewhat bigger for the first couple of months. Two or three bras is often enough, and a regular loose, flexible bra that you can pull the cup down or up can work well.

instruction: Are underwire bras okay while I’m breastfeeding?
output: It’s a good idea to avoid underwires, at least at first. Underwires can cut across milk-making tissue and increase the risk of milk plugs or infections. Also avoid any bra that’s tight enough to leave marks on your skin.

instruction: Should I use breast pads for leaking milk?
output: It depends. Some women find breast pads helpful to soak up leaking milk, but some breasts never leak at all. Leaking has nothing to do with milk supply. Both disposable and washable brands are available, or you can make washable pads from layers of soft cotton circles sewn together. Many women also leave a cloth diaper around the house to catch drips while nursing and don’t need pads in between.

instruction: Do I need a breast pump if I’m going to be home with my baby most of the time?
output: Not always. If you won’t be separated regularly, you probably don’t need a pump — many breastfeeding mothers never need to pump or express their milk. If you want one for unexpected separations or an occasional night out, hand expression or a good-quality manual pump may be all you need.

instruction: How can I express milk without buying an electric pump?
output: Hand expression works fine and doesn’t cost anything. A good-quality manual pump is another inexpensive option and may remove milk better than some mid-priced electric pumps sold at discount stores.

instruction: I heard some bras are more comfortable than others for nursing — what fabric should I look for?
output: Cotton is usually the most comfortable fabric for nursing bras. Better-quality nursing bras also tend to stand up better to a lot of wear and tear.

instruction: How many nursing bras should I buy before the baby arrives?
output: You’ll probably want two or three nursing bras to start with.

instruction: My breasts leak a lot — does that mean I don’t have enough milk for my baby?
output: Leaking has nothing to do with milk supply. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Would a small footstool help me be more comfortable while nursing?
output: Not necessarily, but some mothers who nurse sitting up and have short legs find that a low stool raises their lap to support the baby and helps them relax.

instruction: Where can I find good nursing bras or get advice about them?
output: You can find good nursing bras at specialty breastfeeding and maternity stores and online. La Leche League carries a line of good-quality nursing bras and La Leche League meetings are a really good place to ask other women about what they’ve found helpful.

instruction: Where can I buy nursing bras and who can I ask for advice about fit?
output: You can buy nursing bras online at llliclothes.com and in select stores listed on llliclothes.com locations.html. A good place to ask about fit and real-world experience is a La Leche League meeting, where women are happy to share what they’ve learned.

instruction: Do I need breast pads if I leak a little milk?
output: Some women find breast pads helpful to soak up leaking milk, but some breasts never leak at all. Leaking has nothing to do with milk supply. Both disposable and washable brands are available, and many women use a cloth diaper around the house to catch drips instead of pads.

instruction: I’m worried about being away from my baby occasionally. Do I need a pump?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. If you won’t be separated regularly, you probably don’t need a pump; many breastfeeding mothers never need to pump or express their milk. For an occasional separation or a night out, hand expression or a good-quality manual pump may be all you need. Hand expression works fine and doesn’t cost anything.

instruction: Are manual pumps better than electric ones from drugstores?
output: Manual pumps are relatively inexpensive and may remove milk better than the mid-priced electric pumps available at discount stores and drugstores.

instruction: Would a nursing footstool help me be more comfortable while breastfeeding?
output: Mothers who nurse sitting up and have short legs may find that a low stool raises their lap to support their baby and relaxes their lower back. Mothers who lean back slightly when they nurse, or who cross one knee over the other, may have no need for a nursing stool. If you use one, it doesn’t have to be a commercial product; a thick book, diaper bag, or coffee-table crossbar can work.

instruction: How long can I use a soft carrier or sling for my baby?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A cloth carrier, sling, or wrap can be used from birth until your baby is about 35–40 pounds (16–18 kg).

instruction: Does wearing my baby in a carrier really help with fussing?
output: Research shows that babies who aren’t worn by adults for much of the day fuss more than those who are. Wearing a carrier also makes it easier on your back and arms and lets you have your hands free.

instruction: How should I position my baby in a sling so they’re safe and comfortable?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Be sure the sling is cinched firmly so your baby is secure and fairly upright against your chest, not flopping loosely in a hammock. Avoid positions that have the baby with his head tucked toward his chest.

instruction: Will people notice if I nurse my baby while wearing a sling?
output: Most people won’t be able to tell when you nurse your baby in a sling.

instruction: What kinds of carriers are available and can I make one myself?
output: Carriers come in many fabrics—from mesh to cotton to silk—and in many colors and designs, including ones that tie in a criss-cross pattern and basic styles secured with two large rings. Patterns for sewing your own custom sling or carrier are available online; sewing can be as simple as two straight seams, and many slings are just a length of fabric with no sewing needed. Simple styles often work best.

instruction: Can dads or other caregivers use slings too?
output: Yes. People other than mothers use slings; for example, fathers and grown sons in the examples carried babies in slings when running errands. Slings come in sizes to fit all body types.

instruction: Do I need to do a lot to prepare my body for breastfeeding before the baby is born?
output: Not much — most of the heavy-duty preparation is automatic. Some of your earliest milk-carrying ducts were formed before your very first period.

instruction: If I don’t want to buy pads, can I make washable breast pads at home?
output: Yes. You can make washable pads from layers of soft cotton circles sewn together. Both disposable and washable brands are also available commercially.

instruction: Can a carrier double as a changing surface or blanket?
output: Carriers can serve as a clean surface for diaper changing in a pinch and as a blanket to cover a sleeping baby. Some carriers even have pockets for small items like a phone or keys.

instruction: How should I position my baby in a sling so they are safe and comfortable?
output: Be sure the carrier is cinched firmly so your baby is secure and fairly upright against your chest, not flopping loosely in a hammock. Avoid positions that have the baby with his head tucked toward his chest.

instruction: My baby seems to slump down and flop in the sling — is that okay?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The carrier should be cinched firmly so the baby is secure and fairly upright against your chest rather than flopping loosely in a hammock; avoid positions where the baby’s head is tucked toward the chest.

instruction: Can I sew my own baby sling, or do I need special skills?
output: Yes. Patterns are available online, sewing can be as simple as two straight seams, and many slings are just a length of fabric with no sewing needed. Simple styles often work best.

instruction: Are there slings that fit different body types and have pockets for things like a phone?
output: Yes. Some carriers come in sizes to fit all body types, and some even have pockets for your cell phone and keys.

instruction: What breast changes begin before your very first period?
output: Some of your earliest milk-carrying ducts form then, looking like a cluster of trunks and small branches; separate trunks (ducts) begin forming behind your nipple, from which smaller branches grow.

instruction: What happens to breasts just before pregnancy or before each period?
output: The ducts add more branches, the branches begin to sprout buds, and milk-making tissue grows, which can cause the breast tenderness you may have felt just before your period.

instruction: What changes happen to my breasts during the first three months of pregnancy?
output: Growth accelerates and more buds are added. Breasts are probably more tender and will likely become visibly larger. Each bud grows into a tiny cluster of milk-making cells called an alveolus; milk collects in the hollow center of each alveolus and is squeezed out by tiny muscles and carried along ducts toward the nipple. Some ducts join so you will probably have between four and eighteen nipple pores where milk flows out. You may also notice visible blue veins and darker, possibly larger areolae, and small bumps (Montgomery glands) begin to form on the areolae.

instruction: What are Montgomery glands and what do they do?
output: Montgomery glands are small bumps on the areolae that secrete a small amount of oil and maybe milk after the baby comes, and they may help keep your nipples clean and moisturized.

instruction: Can I leak or express colostrum before the baby is born?
output: Yes. In the last trimester many mothers can express or even leak drops of colostrum, the thick, yellowish (or other-colored) early milk. Colostrum is a mixture of carbohydrates, proteins, lipids (fats), and immune factors. If you express colostrum you will make more to replace it—there isn’t a finite amount.

instruction: If my pregnancy ends in the last trimester, when will full milk production start?
output: If a pregnancy ends at any time from the last trimester on, almost all mothers will automatically begin full milk production within a few days.

instruction: My breasts haven’t felt tender or changed during pregnancy — should I be worried?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you haven’t experienced tenderness or visible changes and you’re concerned, consider talking with a breastfeeding-savvy health care provider.

instruction: How can I get more comfortable handling my changing breasts during pregnancy?
output: Lifting and shifting your breasts will give you a better sense of their changing weight and contour and increase your comfort with them. Gently moving them at least twice a day is suggested.

instruction: How do I do the breast-moving exercise that’s often recommended in pregnancy?
output: Bend at the waist, cup your hands under each breast, and gently move your hands in an up-and-down motion as if fluffing a pillow. Doing this at least twice a day is suggested.

instruction: Will expressing colostrum before birth reduce how much I can make later?
output: No. If you express colostrum, you’ll make more to replace it; the text says there isn’t a finite amount.

instruction: How many openings are there on the nipple where milk can come out?
output: Some ducts join along the way, so you will probably have between four and eighteen nipple pores where the milk flows out.

instruction: Why are my breasts changing during the second trimester?
output: Breast growth continues inside and out during the second trimester. Lifting and shifting your breasts will help you sense their changing weight and contour and can increase your comfort with this part of your body.

instruction: How should I move my breasts to help with comfort and awareness?
output: Bend at the waist, cup your hands under each breast, and gently move your hands in an up-and-down motion as if using a pillow. A nurse and massage therapist suggests doing this at least twice a day, especially during pregnancy and nursing, to improve lymph drainage, reduce breast tenderness, and increase awareness of changes.

instruction: What is colostrum and when might I start leaking it?
output: In the last trimester many mothers can express or leak drops of colostrum. Colostrum is a thick, yellowish (or other-colored) early milk that contains carbohydrates (sugars), proteins, lipids (fats), and immune factors.

instruction: If I express colostrum before birth, will I run out of it?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you express colostrum, you'll make more to replace it; there isn't a finite amount.

instruction: What changes happen to my milk in the first few days after birth?
output: When the placenta is delivered the hormonal wall holding back milk production comes down. The alveoli continue to secrete colostrum. Around the third or fourth day the sugar and fat content rise, pulling additional water into the alveoli so the milk becomes whiter and increases in volume.

instruction: When will I be near full milk production after birth?
output: By about ten days you are near full production, and your supply generally won't increase much from about one month on.

instruction: How is my milk supply established in the early weeks?
output: Milk production works on a supply-and-demand system: the amount of new milk made depends on how much has been taken out. Milk removal is especially important during the first two to three weeks because that's when your milk production capability is established. Each breast is calibrated independently.

instruction: I'm worried I won't be able to make more milk later if I don't produce much now — is that true?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you don't remove much milk in the first few weeks, it will be harder (but not impossible) to make more milk later; the system is built to work even with a rough start, but getting started well helps a lot.

instruction: Why do my breasts never feel truly empty between feedings?
output: Milk is made more rapidly when the breast is less full and more slowly when the breast is fuller. For that reason, breasts are never truly empty.

instruction: What causes the milk ejection or let-down reflex and how might I feel it?
output: Nerves in the nipple and areola send a signal to the pituitary to release oxytocin. Oxytocin causes muscles around the alveoli to squeeze, building milk pressure and creating a milk release. Tiny ducts open and milk spurts down them. You might feel a tingling in your breasts or shoulder blades, feel thirsty or sleepy, or feel nothing at all. In the early weeks a release may be triggered by thinking about nursing or by hearing a baby cry.

instruction: How can I prepare myself mentally for breastfeeding before the baby arrives?
output: Talk to friends and relatives who have enjoyed breastfeeding, spend time with someone who is currently breastfeeding, do some reading, and consider checking out a La Leche League meeting. Picture yourself happily feeding your baby and decide this is something you are GOING to do rather than something you'll only try.

instruction: Should I learn hand expression before my baby is born?
output: The last three months of pregnancy are a great time to learn hand expression. Even though it's usually just drops or less, learning how to hand-express is a skill that may come in handy after the birth.

instruction: If I do nothing after birth and don't want to breastfeed, will my body still make milk?
output: All of this will happen whether or not milk is removed. Even if you do nothing at all, your body is going to do everything it can to feed your baby. The milk-making process can continue for years once it starts, but it continues only as long as milk keeps being removed from the breasts.

instruction: Can one breast make more milk than the other?
output: Yes. Each breast is calibrated independently; the more milk you remove during the early weeks, the more milk each breast will be able to make for this baby.

instruction: What can unexpectedly trigger my milk to let down in the early weeks?
output: In the early weeks a milk release may be triggered just by thinking about nursing or by hearing a baby cry (any baby).

instruction: Why do my breasts feel less full after nursing?
output: Milk is always being made, and it is made more rapidly when the breast is less full. A fuller breast makes milk more slowly, so after your baby nurses the breast becomes less full because milk production adjusts; breasts are never truly empty.

instruction: What exactly triggers the milk let-down when my baby nurses?
output: When your baby nurses, nerves in the nipple and areola send a signal to the pituitary to release oxytocin. Oxytocin makes the little muscles around the alveoli squeeze, which builds milk pressure and creates milk release (let-down); the tiny ducts open as milk spurts down them.

instruction: I often feel nothing when my milk lets down. Is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Some people feel a tingling in the breasts or shoulder blades, or feel thirsty or sleepy, but you can also feel nothing at all during a milk release and that is described as a normal response.

instruction: Can just thinking about breastfeeding make my milk come?
output: In the early weeks, a milk release may be triggered just by thinking about nursing or by hearing a baby cry; thinking about nursing can trigger let-down.

instruction: How can I prepare my mindset for breastfeeding during pregnancy?
output: Talk to friends and relatives who have enjoyed breastfeeding, spend time with someone who is currently breastfeeding, do some reading, and consider attending a La Leche League meeting (pregnant women are welcome, with no pressure or commitment). Decide you are GOING to breastfeed rather than only TRY to do it. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What books, videos, and websites does the excerpt recommend for breastfeeding help?
output: Recommended books include The Breastfeeding Café; Breastfeeding Made Simple; So That’s What They’re For; The Ultimate Breastfeeding Book of Answers. Videos include Baby-Led Breastfeeding; The Mother-Baby Dance; Biological Nurturing: Laid-Back Breastfeeding; Follow Me Mum; Breastfeeding: A Guide for Success. Websites listed include llli.org, ilca.org, usbreastfeeding.org, lowmilksupply.org, normalfed.com, kellymom.com.

instruction: Who might offer to help me breastfeed and should I be cautious about anyone?
output: Many people and companies will offer help—formula companies, furniture companies, pump companies, baby food companies, ointment and cream companies, relatives, friends, websites, and authors. They have different styles and levels of expertise. Almost all have good intentions, but those funded by formula companies are noted as having a different agenda; commercial companies may put their bottom line first, and friends’ experiences are not always how it will be for you. It makes sense to be attentive to these differences.

instruction: What does a La Leche League leader do to help with breastfeeding?
output: A La Leche League leader offers in-person and phone help, leads meetings, and helps find information and resources. She is an experienced nursing mother, usually not medically trained, and can direct you to medical and other resources in the community.

instruction: What role can my obstetrician play with breastfeeding after birth?
output: An obstetrician can help by minimizing interventions and keeping you and your baby together. An obstetrician may have little or no breastfeeding training.

instruction: What about midwives—how do they help with breastfeeding and what should I expect?
output: A midwife can facilitate a normal birth and keep you and your baby together. A midwife may have little or no training in breastfeeding.

instruction: How can a doula support breastfeeding?
output: A doula can help with a smoother birth and is usually trained in basic breastfeeding help.

instruction: If I wait until my breasts are very full before feeding, will that affect my milk production?
output: Milk production slows down if you wait to feed your baby until your breasts fill up, because milk is made more rapidly when the breast is less full. It makes sense to be concerned about this.

instruction: Are breasts ever completely empty of milk?
output: Breasts are never truly empty; milk is always being made and breasts are never completely emptied.

instruction: Why does milk sometimes seem to spurt quickly when my baby nurses?
output: Oxytocin causes the muscles around the alveoli to squeeze, which builds milk pressure and creates milk release; the tiny ducts open wide and milk can spurt down them, which is why you may notice a quick flow.

instruction: Will attending a La Leche League meeting require commitment or pressure to breastfeed?
output: La Leche League meetings welcome pregnant women and are described as no pressure and no commitment, so attending is presented as casual and informative.

instruction: Who are the people and groups who might try to help me breastfeed, and in what order am I likely to meet them?
output: In the order you’re likely to meet them: La Leche League leaders (in-person and phone help, lead meetings, find resources), obstetrician (works to minimize interventions and keep you and baby together), midwife (facilitates normal birth and keeps you and baby together), doula (helps with a smoother birth and some postpartum doulas have extra breastfeeding training; usually trained in basic breastfeeding help), hospital nurse (helps keep mother and baby together), pediatrician (keeps mother and baby together), various lactation consultants/counselors/educators/peer counselors/breastfeeding specialists (may help with basic problems; titles aren’t standardized), and International Board Certified Lactation Consultants (IBCLC) (experienced and legally certified to help in special situations).

instruction: Can I trust companies that say they want to help me breastfeed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Many companies and organizations offer help, but commercial companies generally put their bottom line first, and those funded by formula companies have a different agenda than breastfeeding support.

instruction: What does a La Leche League leader do and what is their background?
output: A La Leche League leader offers in-person and phone help, leads meetings, and finds information and resources. She is an experienced nursing mother, not usually medically trained, and can direct you to medical and other community resources.

instruction: Will my obstetrician be trained to help with breastfeeding?
output: Obstetricians focus on minimizing interventions and keeping you and your baby together, but they may have little or no breastfeeding training.

instruction: What can a midwife do for breastfeeding support?
output: Midwives work to facilitate normal birth and keep you and your baby together. They may have little or no training in breastfeeding.

instruction: Do doulas help with breastfeeding after birth?
output: Doulas help with a smoother birth and some postpartum doulas have had extra training. They are usually trained in basic breastfeeding help and can assist with early breastfeeding.

instruction: Are hospital nurses a reliable source of breastfeeding help?
output: Hospital nurses help keep mother and baby together, but they may have little or no training in breastfeeding.

instruction: What's the difference between general lactation helpers and an IBCLC?
output: General lactation consultants, counselors, educators, peer counselors, and breastfeeding specialists may be able to help with basic breastfeeding problems, but their titles have no standardized or legal meaning and individuals may have very little experience. An IBCLC is experienced and legally certified to give breastfeeding help in special situations, though an IBCLC may not have breastfed her own children.

instruction: Can I rely on friends and family for breastfeeding advice?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Friends and relatives often mean well, but they tend to assume their own experiences are how it always works, so their advice may not fit your situation.

instruction: Do the titles 'lactation consultant' or 'breastfeeding specialist' guarantee expertise?
output: Those titles have no standardized or legal meaning; individuals using them may have very little or no experience helping mothers.

instruction: Will an IBCLC always have breastfed her own children?
output: No. An IBCLC may not have breastfed her own children, but she is experienced and legally certified to give breastfeeding help in special situations.

instruction: How should I decide whose advice to follow about breastfeeding?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Many people and organizations want to help and have different styles and levels of expertise; you need to do what’s right for you and your family. Ultimately the decisions about breastfeeding are up to you, and you know or will know your baby better than anyone else because you are your baby’s mother.

instruction: Are websites and books always reliable sources for breastfeeding information?
output: Not all websites or books will suit your style, and not all experts are truly experts. Different resources have different styles and levels of expertise, so they won’t all be equally helpful for everyone.

instruction: Should I be cautious about advice from companies that make baby products?
output: Yes. Commercial companies put their own bottom line first, so while many have good intentions, their recommendations may be influenced by business interests.

instruction: My 10-month-old can sit and play and is picking up tiny bits of food with thumb and forefinger — is that normal development for this age?
output: Yes. At this stage many babies can sit alone, play with toys, and pick up tiny bits of food with their thumb and forefinger and put them into their mouths.

instruction: My baby’s first clear word was "duh," not "mama" — should I be worried that he didn’t say mama first?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Many babies’ first words are sounds like "duh," "buh," or "hah," often referring to something that fascinates them. Don’t be disappointed — that first word isn’t always "mama."

instruction: When nursing my toddler he sometimes says "Side!" and wants the other breast. Is that normal?
output: Yes. By this age many nurslings have opinions about when and which side to nurse; some will announce "Side!" to indicate they’re finished with one side and want to switch.

instruction: Should I start giving my 10-month-old cow or goat milk now?
output: There’s no need to introduce milk from other species as long as your child is breastfeeding well. If you do decide to give cow or goat milk, it’s a good idea to wait until your baby is a year old to reduce the risk of allergies.

instruction: Is it okay to give soy or rice milk before my baby turns one?
output: The same caution applies to soy milk: it’s a good idea to wait until a year to reduce the risk of allergies. Rice milk may be safer for some, but some babies can still be allergic to it. Introduce any such milks slowly to watch for allergic reactions.

instruction: How much juice can I give my baby and should I dilute it?
output: Fruit juices can be a treat, but whole fruits are generally a better choice. If you give juice, consider limiting it to between a quarter and a half cup each day and possibly diluting it with water. Sipping juice on and off through the day can increase the risk of cavities.

instruction: Do I need to buy special baby juices or toddler drinks for my child?
output: There’s no need to buy special baby juices or toddler drinks.

instruction: Lately my milk seems to come out more slowly when my toddler nurses. Is slowed milk release normal?
output: Yes. Many mothers find that milk release happens less quickly as time passes, probably because the overall volume has slowed down. Parents sometimes hear toddlers jokingly ask to "turn it on."

instruction: If my child stopped nursing now, would he suddenly be much thirstier and hungrier?
output: If your child were to stop nursing right now, an increase in thirst and appetite might surprise you; you may be providing more food and drink through nursing than you realize.

instruction: Does breast milk still provide immune factors and nutrients after nine months?
output: Yes. Even as other foods become more important, many or most essential nutrients and immune factors still come from your milk. As your baby takes more solids and supply begins to decline, the concentration of antibodies in your milk actually increases and some nutrient proportions change to meet your child’s needs.

instruction: When do babies start sharing family foods and how much should they eat?
output: By nine months most babies are eating some solid foods, and over time they tend to share most of the foods the family eats. The amounts may still be tiny at first, and other foods gradually become a larger part of their nutrition.

instruction: My one-year-old started crawling and won’t cuddle like before; now nursings are very short. Is that normal or am I doing something wrong?
output: Many parents wonder about this. It’s common for babies who can crawl to be too busy to cuddle; nursings may become shorter. Some babies still sleep in a parent’s lap, and other activities like looking at books can become the new cuddle time.

instruction: Can giving my toddler juice or water all day affect how much nutritious food they eat?
output: Yes. Toddlers tend to have small appetites, and filling them up with juice or water can mean they have less interest in eating more nutritious foods.

instruction: Should I introduce new milks slowly to check for allergies?
output: Yes. As with solids, it’s best to introduce cow, goat, soy, or other milks slowly so you can watch for allergic reactions.

instruction: Is slowed weight gain normal between nine and twelve months for breastfed babies?
output: Between nine and twelve months, breastfed babies begin slowing down their weight gain even more, to an average of about 0.3 ounce.

instruction: When should I start giving my baby cow's milk to reduce allergy risk?
output: Wait until your baby is a year old to reduce the risk of allergies. Some evidence also suggests families with a predisposition for diabetes do best by waiting until a year to introduce cow milk.

instruction: Is it okay to give soy milk to my baby before one year?
output: The same advice applies to soy milk: waiting until your baby is a year old reduces the risk of allergies, because some babies seem to be sensitive to soy.

instruction: Is rice milk a safe alternative for my baby under one year?
output: Rice milk should be safer, but some babies are even allergic to that. Introduce any of these milks slowly so you can watch for allergic reactions.

instruction: How should I introduce non-dairy milks to my infant?
output: Introduce these milks slowly, just like you introduce solids, so you can watch for allergic reactions.

instruction: Can I give my toddler fruit juice every day and how much is okay?
output: Fruit juices can be a treat, but whole fruits are generally a better choice because they give more fiber and nutrients. If you do give juice, consider limiting it to between a quarter and a half cup each day and possibly diluting it with water. Sipping juice through the day can increase the risk of cavities.

instruction: My toddler drinks a lot of juice and then won't eat dinner — is that normal and what happens?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Toddlers tend to have small appetites, so filling them up with juice or water can mean they have less interest in eating more nutritious foods.

instruction: Why does my breastmilk seem to let down more slowly now?
output: Milk release often happens less quickly as time passes, probably because the overall milk volume has slowed down. This slowed release is a common change.

instruction: My breastfed baby seems to be gaining weight more slowly — is that expected?
output: Between nine and twelve months, breastfed babies begin slowing their weight gain to an average of about 0.3 ounce (9 g) a day. Only the World Health Organization weight gain charts reflect these normal changes in the weight gain rate of breastfed babies, so if a doctor raises a red flag, check whether she is using the right chart for a breastfed baby.

instruction: My period returned and my milk seems lower during it — will that keep happening?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Fertility commonly returns sometime between nine and eighteen months, and ovulation can happen weeks before your first period. Some mothers feel their milk supply drops slightly during their periods, or that milk lets down more slowly; these changes are short-lived each month, and solids help make up the difference.

instruction: Are there supplements that might help if my supply dips during my period?
output: A daily dose of 500 to 1,000 mg of a calcium and magnesium supplement from the middle of your cycle through the first three days of your period may help minimize any drop in supply.

instruction: Is it normal that my toddler's nursing sessions are only a few minutes now?
output: Yes. By this age some nursings are probably just a few minutes long and solid foods may be greeted with enthusiasm. Breastfeeding continues to be important nutritionally and emotionally; the American Academy of Pediatrics encourages nursing for at least a year, and the Canadian Paediatric Society, the UK Department of Health, and the World Health Organization encourage nursing for two or more years.

instruction: My active baby is waking more at night — should I night-wean now?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure. As your baby learns to crawl and walk he may wake more at night, especially when acquiring a new skill, and he may need to tank up at night. This is not the time to night-wean, because he is using these nursings for fuel; if he doesn't nurse at night and doesn't make time to nurse during the day, he may not get the milk his body needs.

instruction: How can I encourage more breastfeeding with a busy toddler who doesn't always ask?
output: Remember that 'don't offer, don't refuse' is actually weaning advice. Your baby may not think to ask for nursings, so if he is fussy, cranky, or hasn't had a snuggle or snack in a while, make the offer. You will probably get several more quick nursings a day this way, which can make a significant difference in your relationship as well as your milk supply.

instruction: Do I need to buy special juices marketed for babies or toddlers?
output: There is no need to buy special baby juices or toddler drinks.

instruction: My ten-month-old barely nurses for a few minutes now and loves solids. Is it okay to wean at one year?
output: The American Academy of Pediatrics encourages nursing for at least a year, not weaning at a year. The Canadian Paediatric Society, the U.K. Department of Health, and the World Health Organization encourage nursing for two or more years. A child who’s nearly one year old still doesn’t have a very effective digestive or immune system, so although he can manage without human milk, he isn’t really designed to.

instruction: He’s not asking to nurse much anymore — should I worry that he’s weaning himself?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Babies sometimes stop asking on their own; “don’t offer, don’t refuse” is actually weaning advice. Your baby may not think to ask for nursings, so offering a nursing when he’s fussy, cranky, or hasn’t had a snuggle or snack in a while will often get you several more quick nursings a day and help maintain your relationship and milk supply.

instruction: My crawler wakes more at night since learning to walk. Is that normal and should I night-wean now?
output: It’s common for babies to start waking more at night when they acquire a new skill like crawling or walking. They may need to tank up at night because their days are busy and exciting. This is not the time to night-wean — those night nursings are being used for fuel, just as when he was tiny. If he doesn’t nurse at night and also doesn’t make time to nurse during the day, he may not get the milk his body needs.

instruction: What can I do when my busy baby forgets to nurse during the day?
output: If your baby doesn’t seem to think to ask for nursings, offer them when he’s fussy, cranky, or you feel he hasn’t had a snuggle or snack in a while. Making the offer will probably get you several more quick nursings a day. Also make room for a few more leisurely nursings at times and places with fewer distractions, such as early morning, naptime, and bedtime, when a sleepy baby is more likely to relax and nurse longer.

instruction: My toddler keeps fiddling with my other nipple while nursing. Any simple fixes?
output: As nipple fiddling becomes more sophisticated, try offering substitutes to keep your baby’s hands busy. Examples that have worked include a doll with long hair, a blanket with a silky binding, or a small squeezable toy to hold. You’ll probably have to experiment to find an acceptable substitute; over time a child may bring the fiddly object to you when he wants to nurse.

instruction: She crawls away to explore and then comes back to nurse for just a few sucks before going off again. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Babies often have mixed feelings about being mobile and will touch base by nursing briefly between adventures. Those touching-base nursings are normal, flattering, integral to maintaining milk, and part of their way of playing it safe.

instruction: My baby seems afraid of strangers once she started crawling. Is that expected?
output: Yes. A baby’s fear of strangers often develops around the time she becomes mobile. A baby in arms is automatically safe but once she can crawl she begins making decisions for herself and may err on the side of caution — thinking she doesn’t know the person or place and asking where her mother is.

instruction: Is it okay for me to leave the room while my baby is crawling around exploring?
output: It’s okay for your baby to crawl away from you, but leaving the room while she’s off on one of her little adventures may make her really upset. Child development experts describe the mother as both the secure base from which a child dares to venture and the welcoming shore to which she returns, and nursing serves both roles automatically.

instruction: Do I need to stop nursing at bedtime so my toddler will sleep on their own?
output: Nursing to sleep is very normal at this age and not a bad habit. As children move from baby to toddler, many consolidate naps and then sleep for a reliable chunk of time provided they can nurse to sleep. A small baby often wants only nursing to sleep.

instruction: Are short, quick nursings enough for an active one-year-old who’s also eating solids?
output: Some nursings may be just a few minutes long as your baby approaches one year, and solid foods may be greeted with enthusiasm. But breastfeeding continues to be nutritionally and emotionally important, and if your baby doesn’t nurse at night and doesn’t make time to nurse during the day, he may not get the milk his body needs.

instruction: How can I get my busy baby to nurse more during the day without interrupting play?
output: Offer nursing when he’s fussy, cranky, or hasn’t had a snuggle or snack in a while; you’ll probably get several more quick nursings a day. Also set aside a few times for more leisurely nursings in low-distraction settings such as early morning, naptime, or bedtime, when a sleepy baby is more likely to relax and nurse longer.

instruction: My baby pulls my hair or twists it while nursing. What can I try?
output: You can try offering a substitute to keep his hands busy, such as a doll with long hair or a blanket with a silky binding. Experiment to find an acceptable substitute; some babies will eventually bring their fiddly object to you when they want to nurse.

instruction: He used to nap in tiny bursts but now plays hard and then sleeps for a long stretch — is that normal?
output: That’s a common change. As children move from baby to toddler, many consolidate naps more and more. A baby who slept for ten minutes here and there may become a toddler who plays hard for several hours and then sleeps for a reliable, useable chunk of time, provided he can nurse to sleep.

instruction: I feel guilty about offering nursing so often because he’s eating solids. Should I cut back?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Even as babies enjoy solids, breastfeeding continues to be nutritionally and emotionally important. Offering nursings when he seems fussy or hasn’t had a snuggle or snack can give you several more quick nursings a day and help maintain your milk supply and relationship.

instruction: Sometimes when I offer to nurse in a group, my baby arches away but will take a toy and go play. Is that okay?
output: Yes. Babies often have mixed feelings about nursing while engaged in other activities. They may accept a toy instead and go off to play. This is a normal way for them to balance exploration and the secure base nursing provides.

instruction: Is it a problem that my baby still needs to nurse to fall asleep at naps and bedtime?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Not at all — nursing to sleep is very normal at this age. Small babies often want only nursing to sleep; older babies may want nursing plus a book; older still may be satisfied with a book. Falling asleep on their own usually happens in its own time, so there’s no need to rush naptime and bedtime nursings.

instruction: My toddler only naps if I nurse him. Does nursing to sleep create a bad habit I should break?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Nursing to sleep does not create a bad habit according to how this stage develops. Many children need nursing to fall asleep at this age, and falling asleep independently typically develops automatically in its proper time.

instruction: Sometimes my baby will let someone else put him to sleep if I’m not home but insists on nursing when I’m there. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Before independent sleep develops, most nursing mothers find their child can fall asleep for someone else without nursing if Mom is out, but really needs to nurse if the mother is in the house.

instruction: My ten-month-old came to my lap during a class and didn’t want to nurse but took a toy instead. Is it normal for babies to accept a substitute and go off playing?
output: It’s normal to see that. Babies can touch base and then accept a different object or toy and crawl off to play — you can mother while continuing an adult conversation. That behavior was described for a ten-month-old who chose a toy over nursing and then left to play.

instruction: Should I stop letting my child nap with nursing because I think it will make sleep problems later?
output: No need to rush a child out of naptime and bedtime nursings. Those nursings are a normal part of milk maintenance and one of the last luxuries of babyhood; falling asleep on their own happens in its own time.

instruction: My baby has stopped napping for long periods — could that be normal until preschool?
