instruction: What is Mothers More and what do they stand for?
output: Mothers More is an organization (mothersandmore.org) that believes women deserve recognition and support for the right to choose if and how to combine parenting and paid employment.

instruction: Are there ways to stay involved in my field while spending most of my time with my children?
output: Yes. The text says there are often ways to stay involved in your field while still spending most of your time with your children, even though technology changes rapidly.

instruction: How did the author manage to keep breastfeeding while earning an income?
output: She changed careers and became an independent consultant for a beauty products company, giving facials and makeovers and selling skin-care products from home. She worked about ten to fifteen hours a week and made more than before, which allowed her to continue breastfeeding while bringing in income.

instruction: Is it okay to change careers to protect breastfeeding and care for my baby?
output: Many parents wonder about this. The author put breastfeeding first and chose a creative work option that fit her family; changing careers allowed her to continue breastfeeding while earning income. That example shows changing careers is one possible way to prioritize breastfeeding.

instruction: How long does it usually take before a mother feels on top of her role after birth?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. The text says there are not very many mothers who really feel on top of their role before about six weeks after the birth.

instruction: Should I worry about keeping up with housework and meals when my newborn is here?
output: Housework, meals, and other responsibilities will always be there, and they don’t matter as much as getting to know your newborn and developing a strong breastfeeding relationship, which takes time and happens only now.

instruction: Will returning to work be difficult for me and my baby?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Returning to work or school is a transition that can feel overwhelming. The early days or first couple of weeks at work will be rocky no matter how old your child is, but the text says it will pass and you’ll find your own rhythm and style together.

instruction: Is it okay to hold back emotionally from my baby during maternity leave because I plan to return to work early?
output: Many parents wonder about this. The text notes some mothers harden themselves to the reality of an early return to work to protect their hearts, but most mothers are far happier in the long run if they give their heart freely to their baby even though the transition is tough at the start.

instruction: Where can I find other working mothers to ask about pumping and managing work?
output: Find other working mothers to talk with; they can give reassurance and practical information. The text specifically mentions Mothers on the llli.org forums as eager to hear ideas and suggests talking with other working mothers as a first "pumping experience" for information.

instruction: What happened to the author’s job and finances around her first baby?
output: During her first pregnancy she developed a very painful condition, went on disability, and was told her job as a social worker would not be waiting for her when she recovered. She stayed home for the first twelve months, and later, when bills increased and her husband lost his job, she had to go back to work.

instruction: Will breastfeeding help keep me and my baby connected when I go back to work?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about the transition. The text says breastfeeding will help you through returning to work and will help keep you and your baby together.

instruction: I'm going back to work in a few weeks and I'm scared I'll lose my closeness with my baby. Will breastfeeding help us stay connected when I return to work?
output: It will help and keep you together. It can be a real support during the transition back to work, and you and your baby will find your way and a rhythm together.

instruction: I feel guilty about not getting housework done because I want to spend time breastfeeding. How should I prioritize in these first weeks?
output: Housework, meals, and other responsibilities don’t matter as much as getting to know your newborn and developing a strong breastfeeding relationship, which happens only now. It takes time, and very few mothers feel on top of their role before about six weeks after the birth. Stressing won’t help and just takes time away from what matters now.

instruction: How long does it usually take before a mother feels "on top" of being a parent?
output: There are not very many mothers who really feel on top of their role before about six weeks after the birth.

instruction: I'm worried returning to work early will determine how close I'll be with my child. Will a job ruin our relationship?
output: Try not to let the demands of your job determine the depth of your relationship. You’ll both find ways to survive an early return to work, and a strong relationship with your child will outlast any job you’ll ever have.

instruction: Where can I find other working moms to ask about pumping and returning to work?
output: Find some other working mothers to talk with — they can be your first pumping experience and can give you reassurance and practical ideas. Mothers on the llli.org forums are eager to hear your ideas.

instruction: I need to attend a wedding and might be away from my baby for a couple days. Will that hurt breastfeeding?
output: Being away for more than a workday is stressful, and too long a separation can jeopardize breastfeeding itself. It’s worth considering whether you really need to go, whether you can take the baby with you, or whether you can arrange care so you’re only separated for a short time. With enough lead time you can provide your own milk or get donor milk to make up the difference. Pumping regularly while you’re away will help keep up your supply.

instruction: I want to bring my baby to an evening event. What can I wear so I can breastfeed there easily?
output: What you wear will affect how easily and where you can breastfeed when you bring your baby along. Some companies make nursing evening outfits, and many two-piece outfits already work. Making or buying a sling in a complementary fabric can also make breastfeeding at an event easier.

instruction: If I have to leave my baby and I can't store milk while I'm away, should I still pump?
output: Yes. While you’re away, it will help to pump regularly and thoroughly to keep up your supply, even if you don’t have a way to store it and bring it home.

instruction: What should I do if I need to be hospitalized and can’t have my baby with me full time?
output: If it’s not possible to have your baby with you full-time, you may want to begin pumping a couple of weeks in advance to increase your supply and build up some stored milk in the freezer. Then plan to continue expressing while you’re separated and arrange for your milk to be given to your baby.

instruction: Can my baby stay with me in the hospital if I need a procedure?
output: There may be a policy allowing your baby to stay with you so long as the baby has a second caregiver with him. Depending on your situation and hospital policy, it may be possible to arrange to miss only a couple of feedings at most.

instruction: Should I ask the hospital for a breast pump if I'm admitted?
output: You can ask to have a breast pump available and make sure that it includes the user’s kit, or arrange to bring your own pump or kit from home.

instruction: I’m worried about having anesthesia. Do I need to stop breastfeeding after surgery?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Unless your baby is premature or seriously ill, there’s no need to stop breastfeeding (or to pump and throw your milk away) just because you’ve had anesthesia. As soon as you are awake, the anesthesia is essentially out of your blood, so it’s essentially out of your milk.

instruction: A doctor wants me to take a medication while I’m breastfeeding. Does that mean I must stop breastfeeding?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. It’s rarely necessary to stop breastfeeding just because you have to take a medication. Almost all medications, with the exception of a few such as certain radioactive drugs and chemotherapy, are safe for breastfeeding. The single best resource for information about a particular drug is Medications and Mothers’ Milk by Dr. Thomas Hale, updated every other year; your LLL Leader should have access to a copy. Information in the Physician’s Desk Reference is not considered reliable for breastfeeding compatibility.

instruction: Who can I talk to at the hospital about breastfeeding help?
output: No matter what hospital unit you find yourself on, you can ask to talk to a lactation consultant from the maternity unit.

instruction: If I must leave my baby for a short trip, is using formula acceptable?
output: Consider formula your last option. With enough lead time you can provide your own milk or get donor milk to make up the difference. Even a little formula introduced into the diet of a fully breastfed baby increases his risk of health problems now and in the future, so it’s worth working hard to avoid it.

instruction: How should I approach the first couple of weeks after returning to work?
output: Understand that the early days or first couple of weeks at work will be rocky no matter how old your child is. There’s no way around that completely, but it will pass, and you’ll find your own rhythm and style together.

instruction: If I have to be hospitalized, can my baby stay with me?
output: Depending on the hospital policy, your baby may be allowed to stay with you so long as the baby has a second caregiver with him. You can ask about that with the hospital.

instruction: What if I can’t have my baby with me in the hospital full-time?
output: If you can’t have your baby with you full-time, you may want to begin pumping a couple of weeks in advance to increase your supply and build up frozen milk. Plan to continue expressing while you’re separated and arrange for your milk to be given to your baby.

instruction: Can I ask the hospital to provide a breast pump while I’m there?
output: Yes. You can ask to have a breast pump available and make sure that it includes the user’s kit, or you can arrange to bring your own pump or kit from home.

instruction: Should I keep pumping even if I don’t have a way to store milk at the hospital?
output: Yes. Pump your milk regularly and thoroughly to keep up your supply, even if you don’t have a way to store it and bring it home.

instruction: Will anesthesia mean I have to stop breastfeeding?
output: It’s rarely necessary to stop breastfeeding just because you’ve had anesthesia. As soon as you are awake, the anesthesia is essentially out of your blood and essentially out of your milk. Unless your baby is premature or seriously ill, there’s no need to stop breastfeeding or to pump and throw your milk away because of anesthesia. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: I need to take a medication after surgery—can I still breastfeed?
output: Almost all medications, with the exception of a few such as certain radioactive drugs and chemotherapy, are safe for breastfeeding. The single best resource for information about a particular drug is Medications and Mothers' Milk by Dr. Thomas Hale, updated every other year; your La Leche League (LLL) Leader should have access to a copy. Many parents worry about this.

instruction: Is the Physician’s Desk Reference (PDR) a reliable source about drugs and breastfeeding?
output: Information in the Physician’s Desk Reference is not reliable for breastfeeding. It is provided by drug companies and often states that a drug is not compatible with breastfeeding, whether or not it is.

instruction: Who can I ask for help with breastfeeding while I’m in the hospital?
output: You can ask to talk to a lactation consultant from the maternity unit; she can be a wonderful ally. A local La Leche League Leader may also know about hospital policy, helpful nurses, and layout.

instruction: If I have to miss a few feedings while hospitalized, how many might I be able to miss?
output: Depending on your situation and hospital policy, it may be possible to arrange to miss only a couple of feedings at most.

instruction: My baby refused to nurse after I came back from the hospital—is that normal?
output: Sometimes after a separation of more than a day a baby will refuse to nurse and seem to reject his mother; most of these breast refusals are temporary. Some reacquainting time, maybe a warm bath together, and some shared sleep can help soothe his hurt feelings. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What should I do if breast refusal continues after we’ve tried reacquainting?
output: If breast refusal continues, see the information on nursing strikes in Chapter 18 or talk to a La Leche League Leader for ideas.

instruction: Should I start pumping earlier if I know I will be separated from my baby?
output: If it’s not possible to have your baby with you full-time, you may want to begin pumping a couple of weeks in advance to increase your supply and build up some stored containers of milk in the freezer.

instruction: Can I arrange for the milk I pump to be given to my baby while I’m in the hospital?
output: Yes. Plan to continue expressing while you’re separated and arrange for your milk to be given to your baby.

instruction: If I’m worried about a specific drug and breastfeeding, where should I look first?
output: The single best resource is Medications and Mothers' Milk by Dr. Thomas Hale, updated every other year; your La Leche League Leader should have access to a copy. The PDR is not a reliable source for breastfeeding information.

instruction: Can a La Leche League Leader help with hospital-related breastfeeding issues?
output: Yes. A local La Leche League Leader may know things about hospital policy, helpful nurses, and even layout that can be helpful to you.

instruction: My baby is in the hospital and can't nurse. Can I express milk for him and will it help my supply?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Expressing milk when you can't be with your baby provides food for him and also helps keep your milk production going.

instruction: What kinds of breast pumps can I find?
output: Most women today start by looking at pumps. You can find manual or electric pumps, single or double, hands-free models, and rental-grade (hospital-grade) pumps, among others.

instruction: Is hand expression worth learning?
output: Hand expression is free and highly portable and doesn't require an electrical outlet. It does take some practice, will probably take longer than a pump at first, and not all women feel comfortable with it. Once you get the hang of it, it can become second nature.

instruction: How do I hand-express milk step by step?
output: Begin by waking up the breast with breast massage, breast shaking, and nipple tugs or rolls. Hold the breast in a C shape with fingers and thumb cupped near but not touching the areola. Then press your fingers and thumb back toward your chest, compress the breast toward the nipple without lifting your hand, release without removing your hand, and repeat. Move your hand to a different place around the breast after every few compressions or whenever milk flow stops to compress all the milk ducts.

instruction: Do I need to buy a pump right away or can I rely on hand expression?
output: Many women start by looking at pumps, and there are many types available. Hand expression is an alternative that is free and portable and can be useful in many situations. The text describes both options but does not prescribe one over the other.

instruction: Can hand expression be a quick method for a busy schedule?
output: Yes. Although hand expression can take longer than a pump at first, some mothers can do it quickly — for example, one busy mother could hold a cup under each breast and be done in about five minutes once she was practiced.

instruction: Why should I still learn hand expression if I have a pump at home?
output: It can be worth learning because there are times when you just want to express a little milk, such as nights when the baby sleeps extra long. Hand expression is portable and doesn't need an outlet, so it can be handy for quick needs.

instruction: What should I have ready when I practice hand expression?
output: You may want a big bowl in your lap and a towel handy so you don't need to aim carefully and can mop up any drips. A cup can also be used to catch milk.

instruction: How do I stimulate milk release before I start expressing?
output: Begin by waking up the breast with a combination of breast massage, breast shaking, and nipple tugs or rolls to stimulate a milk release before moving the milk through the ducts.

instruction: Should I move my hand around the breast while hand-expressing?
output: Yes. Move your hand to a different place around the breast after every few compressions or whenever milk flow stops so that you compress all the milk ducts.

instruction: Can every mother hand-express milk?
output: Not all women feel comfortable hand-expressing, and techniques vary between women. However, once you get the hang of it, it can quickly become second nature for many.

instruction: Is hand expression free and does it need electricity?
output: Hand expression is free and highly portable and does not require an electrical outlet.

instruction: Compared with a pump, how long does hand expression usually take?
output: At least at first, hand expression will probably take longer than using a pump.

instruction: What is the three-part process when you express milk for your baby?
output: The three parts are Milk Out (out of your breasts), Milk Stored (until your baby receives it), and Milk In (to your baby).

instruction: My baby couldn't nurse effectively at first. What are reasons parents end up expressing milk?
output: Many parents express milk for different reasons: returning to work when the baby is young, having a baby born early, the baby not nursing effectively at first leading to sore nipples and reduced milk transfer, or choosing not to breastfeed but wanting the baby to have the parent's milk. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: How do I hand-express breastmilk step by step?
output: Start by waking the breast with breast massage, breast shaking, and nipple tugs or rolls. Hold the breast with your fingers and thumb cupped in a C shape near but not touching the areola. PRESS your fingers and thumb back toward your chest. COMPRESS the breast between your fingers and thumb, moving them slightly toward the nipple without lifting them from the breast. RELEASE without removing your hand. REPEAT, moving your hand to a different place around the breast after every few compressions or whenever milk flow stops so you compress all the milk ducts.

instruction: My nipples feel sore when I pump. Can a pump actually bruise or wound nipples?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Pumps must balance suction strength and cycling precisely. If the suction is too strong or the cycles are too long, a pump can bruise or wound your nipples; if the suction is too weak or the cycles too short, it may not collect milk well.

instruction: What does ‘Press, Compress, Release’ mean when hand-expressing?
output: It means: hold the breast in a C shape with fingers and thumb near but not touching the areola; PRESS your fingers and thumb back toward your chest; COMPRESS the breast between fingers and thumb, moving slightly toward the nipple without lifting; RELEASE without removing your hand; then repeat and move to different places around the breast after a few compressions or when milk flow stops.

instruction: How will I know I found the right spot to express milk?
output: It usually takes time to locate your personal sweet spots. When you get a spray of milk from at least one nipple pore (rather than drops or a dribble), you’ve found what works. What’s most effective varies by woman, so it’s a process of trial and error.

instruction: What’s a good way to catch milk when I practice hand expression?
output: Put a big bowl in your lap and have a towel handy so you don’t need to aim carefully and can mop up drips. You can express into any container: a flexible bowl you can form into a spout, a pump flange or regular funnel attached to a bottle or bag, or aim into a cup like the example with a coffee cup.

instruction: Should I choose a rental-grade pump or a consumer-grade pump?
output: Rental-grade pumps are built to last many years and users, are usually rented, have cycle speeds that match the average baby’s suck, often can cycle quickly at first to stimulate release, offer a range of suction levels, are closed systems, allow expressing from both breasts at once with no loss of suction or speed, and almost always remove milk more thoroughly. Consumer-grade pumps vary: better-quality consumer pumps are usually good enough when you have an established milk supply and are not pumping exclusively; some mothers maintain a full supply with them but should monitor supply and be prepared to switch to a rental-grade pump if supply drops. Consumer pumps are designed to be used by only one person.

instruction: Can I pump both breasts at the same time with all pumps?
output: Rental-grade pumps let you express milk from both breasts at the same time with no loss of suction or speed. Some higher-end consumer-grade pumps also allow double pumping without sacrificing suction or speed, but consumer pumps vary in ability.

instruction: What does it mean that a rental pump is a 'closed system'?
output: A closed system means the working parts of the pump do not come in contact with the pumped milk. Each user must purchase a personal kit of parts—tubing, flanges, valves, etc.—to attach to the motor.

instruction: How do breast pumps mimic a baby’s sucking?
output: An effective pump mimics a baby’s suck cycle, which is not continuous but a suck-release-suck-release rhythm. Pumps balance the cycling and suction: they need the right cycle timing and suction strength to draw out milk without causing tissue damage and to collect milk effectively.

instruction: I want to exclusively pump. Will a consumer-grade pump keep up my milk supply long term?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Better-quality consumer-grade pumps can be good enough for some mothers who have an established supply, and some mothers are able to maintain a full supply with them even without breastfeeding. However, consumer pumps vary; if you find your supply slowly dropping you should be prepared to switch to a rental-grade pump.

instruction: How should I start to stimulate milk release before I try expressing?
output: Begin by waking up your breast with a combination of breast massage, breast shaking, and nipple tugs or rolls. This helps stimulate a milk release before you start moving milk through the ducts to the nipple pores.

instruction: How often should I move my hand around the breast during hand expression?
output: Repeat the press-compress-release sequence, moving your hand to a different place around the breast after every few compressions or whenever milk flow stops so that you compress all the milk ducts.

instruction: I’m only getting drops when I hand-express. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Even with good technique you usually won’t get milk right away. It takes time to find your sweet spots and the technique that’s best for you. Drops or a dribble can occur before you eventually get a spray from a nipple pore when you’ve found what works.

instruction: Can store-bought consumer pumps be used by more than one person?
output: Consumer-grade pumps are designed to be used by only one person. Rental-grade (hospital-grade) pumps are built to last many users, and each user must purchase a personal kit of parts to attach to the motor.

instruction: What are the main advantages of using a pump instead of hand expressing?
output: A pump has advantages in some circumstances: there’s very little to learn, you can multitask, and you don’t need to focus on what you’re doing. Rental-grade pumps also usually remove milk more thoroughly, and pumping both breasts at once saves time and usually stimulates better milk production.

instruction: What is a rental-grade pump and how is it different from other pumps?
output: A rental-grade pump has one or more cycle speeds that match the average baby’s suck cycle. Some are programmed to cycle quickly at first to stimulate milk release, and many have a range of suction levels so you can tailor speed and suction. They are closed systems, meaning the working parts don’t come in contact with the pumped milk. Each user must purchase a personal kit of parts (tubing, flanges, valves, etc.). Rental-grade pumps can express from both breasts at the same time with no loss of suction or speed, and they almost always remove milk more thoroughly and effectively than any other kind of pump.

instruction: Are consumer-grade electric pumps as good as rental-grade pumps?
output: No — you shouldn’t assume they’re as good. The better-quality consumer-grade pumps are usually good enough when you have an established milk supply and are not pumping exclusively. Some higher-end consumer models can maintain adequate suction and cycling patterns and allow double pumping, but less expensive consumer pumps may only withdraw enough milk for an occasional feeding and can’t maintain a milk supply.

instruction: Is it safe to buy a used consumer-grade pump from eBay or Craigslist?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Consumer-grade pumps aren’t built to last much more than a year; when they wear out, suction and cycling mechanisms slowly break down so you may pump less milk or notice suction problems. Also, consumer-grade pumps aren’t closed systems, so milk or moisture may have entered mechanical parts where bacteria, mold, and viruses can grow. Consider the source, use your best judgment, and at least buy a new pump kit if you use a used pump.

instruction: How can I tell if a used rental-grade pump might be stolen?
output: A rental-grade pump might be stolen. A legitimate seller will give you the serial number so you can contact the manufacturer, who keeps a list of serial numbers of stolen pumps.

instruction: Will a manual pump work if I only pump occasionally?
output: Yes. Manual pumps will probably work fine if you’re pumping only occasionally, for an emergency, or for infrequent short separations. Some mothers even buy two so they can double-pump without electricity.

instruction: Can consumer-grade pumps be used by more than one person?
output: Consumer-grade pumps are designed to be used by only one person.

instruction: What does it mean that a pump is a closed system?
output: A closed system means the working parts of the pump don’t come in contact with the pumped milk.

instruction: Do consumer-grade pumps use batteries, and are batteries reliable for pumping?
output: Many consumer-grade brands include a battery option, which can be handy when electricity isn’t available. However, pumps drain batteries quickly, which can be expensive and problematic if they go dead in the middle of pumping.

instruction: How do some pumps mimic a baby’s initial quick sucks to start milk flow?
output: Some pumps are programmed to cycle quickly at first to stimulate a milk release, similar to a baby’s small, rapid sucks when he first latches.

instruction: My consumer pump’s suction seems weaker than before—what does that mean?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. For consumer-grade pumps, when they start to wear out their suction and cycling mechanisms can slowly break down, and you may pump less milk or notice weak (or sometimes too strong) suction. If you’re relying on a consumer pump, monitor your milk production and be prepared to switch to a rental-grade pump if you find your supply slowly dropping.

instruction: Can I double-pump with a consumer-grade pump without losing suction or speed?
output: Some higher-end consumer-grade pumps allow double pumping without sacrificing suction or speed. Less expensive consumer pumps may not have the power to combine suction and cycling well enough for regular double pumping.

instruction: How can I make a homemade hands-free pumping bra?
output: Buy an inexpensive zip-front sports bra that fits snugly over your nursing bra. Put the sports bra on with your nursing bra’s flaps down and adjust your breasts normally. Using small scissors, cut increasingly larger holes in the sports bra fabric at your nipples until the holes are big enough for the flanges to pass through but small enough to hold the flanges firmly and evenly against your breasts.

instruction: If I buy a used pump, should I replace the pump kit parts?
output: Yes. The text’s advice is to consider the source, use your best judgment, and at least buy a new pump kit (tubing, flanges, valves, etc.) if you’re using a used pump.

instruction: Do companies say used pumps are risky and is there solid research about that risk?
output: Companies say used pumps can be risky, but there isn’t good research either way. Some potential bacteria and viruses die within hours or days, some don’t cause illness in humans, and some microbes (for example, tuberculosis bacteria) can persist for a very long time.

instruction: Is a manual pump okay if I only pump occasionally or just want milk for emergencies?
output: Yes. Manual pumps are suitable if you pump only occasionally, for an emergency, or for infrequent short separations. Some mothers even buy two so they can double-pump without electricity.

instruction: How can I make a homemade hands-free pumping bra?
output: You can buy a hands-free pumping bra or kit, or make your own. Three ways mentioned are: use a sports bra that zips up over your nursing bra and cut holes for the flanges; skip the nursing bra and use only a sports bra with holes cut for the flanges; or try the rubber-band method described online. LLLI also sells a hands-free option.

instruction: Step by step, how do I use a sports bra over my nursing bra for hands-free pumping?
output: Put an inexpensive zip-front sports bra on over your nursing bra with the nursing bra flaps down and adjust your breasts normally. Using small scissors, cut progressively larger holes in the sports bra at your nipples until the holes are big enough to work the flanges through but still hold the flanges firmly and evenly against your breasts.

instruction: Can I use oil to reduce friction when pumping?
output: You can use a little edible oil, such as olive oil, to cut down on friction, but avoid possible allergens like peanut or corn oil. If a flange fits correctly, you likely won't need lubrication.

instruction: My nipples hurt every time I pump. Could my flange size be causing the pain?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A mismatched flange size is a common cause of pain: a too-small flange can cause friction, pain, swelling, and even abrasion of the nipple and areola; a too-large flange can pull in too much tissue and also cause swelling.

instruction: How can I tell if my flange is the right size? My nipples look swollen after pumping.
output: Many parents wonder about this. With the right flange you should see the nipple move easily in the tunnel, pulling the areola slightly with it. Immediately after pumping the nipple will probably be bigger, but it should not look darker or truly swollen, and it should feel comfortable both while you pump and afterward.

instruction: What flange sizes come with pumps and are there other options?
output: Most pumps include a standard flange size of about 24–26 mm, which may be too small for some women. Some manufacturers include the next size up (about 27–29 mm) or offer larger sizes as accessories. A few women find a 21 mm flange or an insert more comfortable.

instruction: Are there specialty flange types and what do they do?
output: Yes. Some specialty flanges have a soft funnel section for better comfort and stimulation. Others have an adjustable or angled neck so you can lean back while pumping without leaking milk, which can help if you find yourself leaning forward to tip milk into bottles.

instruction: My newborn is a few days old. How often should I pump to establish my milk supply? I'm worried I can't manage a lot of sessions.
output: It's common to have questions like this. If you are expressing instead of nursing and your baby is only a few days old, it's best to have eight to twelve pumping sessions a day, with at least one during the night, for the first few weeks to establish a full milk supply. Practical advice from others: aim for ten, work for eight, never go under six. Don't try to force a strict three-hour spacing; think and do eight sessions. Cluster-pump as needed and don't worry about evenly spacing sessions—babies don't.

instruction: What does condensation in the pump tubing mean?
output: Some lactation consultants find that condensation in the pump tubing can indicate that the flanges are too small.

instruction: Can I reuse leftover flanges for other purposes?
output: Yes. Leftover flanges can be recycled for other uses such as kitchen funnels or bathtub toys; one person still uses hers decades later.

instruction: Is it possible to double-pump without electricity?
output: Yes. Some mothers have bought two manual pumps so they can double-pump without electricity.

instruction: How can I tell if my pump flange is the right size for me?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Use comfort and milk flow as your guide: the flange should feel comfortable and should allow milk to flow well. You may need to try several sizes or visit a breastfeeding helper or store to find the right one. Nipples and breasts can change over time, so a size you rejected earlier might work later.

instruction: I bought extra flanges—can I reuse them for anything else?
output: Yes. Leftover flanges can be recycled and used as kitchen funnels or as bathtub toys.

instruction: What are specialty flanges and when might I use one?
output: Some companies make specialty flanges. One type has a soft funnel section for more comfort and stimulation. Another type has an adjustable or angled neck so you can lean back while pumping without leaking milk; that can help if you find yourself tipping milk into bottles.

instruction: My baby is a few days old—how often should I pump to build my milk supply?
output: If you are expressing instead of nursing and your baby is only a few days old, it’s best to have eight to twelve pumping sessions each day, with at least one of them during the night, for the first few weeks to establish a full milk supply.

instruction: I’m worried I can’t get in 12 pumpings a day—what is realistic?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Aim for ten, work for eight, and try not to go under six pumpings a day. Don’t rigidly plan every three hours, since spacing can stretch; instead focus on getting about eight sessions spread through the day.

instruction: What does "cluster-pump" mean and do you have any simple tips to do it?
output: Cluster-pumping means not worrying about evenly spacing sessions and grouping pumpings closer together. Simple tips from others include putting eight to ten pieces of candy next to the pump and eating one at each pumping, or finding eight half-hour TV programs spaced through the day and pumping while you watch.

instruction: How can I keep track of when I pumped during the day?
output: Keep it simple: have a sheet with all the hours of the day and circle the hours when you pump. If one day doesn’t go well, note what went wrong and try harder the next day.

instruction: After my supply is established, how often will I need to pump?
What steps do most women find helpful when pumping?
output: Find one or two comfortable places that let you attend to other needs. Coat the inside of the flanges with oil if desired. Open your clothing and bra. Wake up your breasts with massage, shaking, lifting, or stroking. Tug or roll your nipples to encourage milk release. Center the flanges on your nipples top-to-bottom and side-to-side. Use pillows or a stool for comfort. Turn the pump on at your starting setting. Try breast compressions to increase milk flow, take a break midway and massage to boost yield, and consider pumping one side while the baby nurses at the other.

instruction: Should I press the flange hard or lean forward when I pump?
output: Pumping should be as comfortable as nursing. Flanges don’t need to be pressed uncomfortably hard to the breast, and you don’t have to lean forward to pump. Use pillows, a stool, or whatever you need for comfort.

instruction: What pump setting should I start with?
output: Turn the pump on at your starting setting; this starting setting may be faster and gentler than your keep-it-flowing setting.

instruction: I’m not getting much the first few minutes—what should I expect during a pumping session?
output: Your first milk release will start to flow or spray, then subside after a few minutes. If you keep pumping you will probably see a second milk release after a few minutes and possibly a third. Keep pumping until your breasts feel soft (unless you are engorged), and sometimes it’s worth pumping a bit longer to see if you get another release.

instruction: Any tips to increase milk during a pumping session?
output: Try breast compressions while you pump to increase milk flow. Take a break midway and massage your breasts to help increase yield. Tugging or rolling the nipples and waking the breasts with massage, shaking, lifting, or stroking can also encourage milk release.

instruction: Can I pump one breast while nursing the baby on the other?
output: Yes. Pumping one side while your baby nurses at the other can encourage more milk releases.

instruction: Is single-pumping better or worse, and how long should I do each side?
output: Single-pumping (doing one side first and then the other) lets you use breast compression and massage while you pump, but it may not yield as much milk and often takes longer. Some women with very low supplies get more this way. If you single-pump, do each side for five minutes, then repeat.

instruction: How should I position the pump flanges on my nipples so pumping works well and feels comfortable?
output: Center the flanges on your nipples, top to bottom and side to side; the angle may be different on each breast. Pumping should be as comfortable as nursing — you don’t need to press the flanges uncomfortably hard to your breast or lean forward. Use pillows, a stool, or whatever you need for comfort.

instruction: What setting should I start my pump on and why?
output: Turn the pump on at your starting setting, which may be faster and gentler than your keep-it-flowing setting.

instruction: I’m anxious because I’m not sure how long to pump — when should I stop a pumping session?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Your first milk release will start to flow or spray, then subside after a few minutes; keep pumping and you’ll probably see a second, maybe a third release. Keep pumping until your breasts feel soft (unless you’re engorged). If you can’t tell yet, start by pumping for fifteen to twenty minutes and pay attention to how your breasts feel. A session replacing a nursing is usually no more than fifteen minutes if you double-pump or about twenty minutes if you single-pump.

instruction: Should I use breast compressions or massage while pumping?
output: Yes. As you pump, try breast compressions to increase milk flow, and take a break midway to massage your breasts to help increase milk yield. After you finish, tip the flange down and spend a minute hand-expressing a bit more into it to strip out the last remaining milk.

instruction: Is it better to pump both breasts at once or one at a time?
output: Single-pumping (first one side then the other) lets you use breast compression and massage while you pump but may not yield as much milk and often takes longer. Some women with very low supplies get more this way. If you single-pump, do each side for five minutes, then repeat. Double-pumping is usually faster and a pumping session replacing a nursing will probably last no more than fifteen minutes when double-pumping.

instruction: Can I pump one breast while my baby nurses on the other?
output: Yes. Try pumping one side while your baby nurses at the other — it can encourage more milk releases.

instruction: How should I clean the flanges between pumpings and at the end of the day?
output: Unless you’re pumping for a premature or ill baby, just rinse the flanges and set them aside for the next pumping, washing them at the end of the day. Hot, soapy water is fine for healthy, full-term babies. If your baby is hospitalized, follow the hospital’s guidelines for expressing and storing your milk.

instruction: Do people use any tricks to make pumping hands-free or easier while caring for the baby?
output: Yes. Some mothers put a battery-operated pump in a sling or backpack and made a hands-free pumping bra. One mother double-pumped by bracing a forearm against a flange and holding the other flange with two fingers while using both hands to work a piston and cylinder. You can use furniture, body parts, clothing, or anything that works to make pumping easier, and pass along ideas that work for you.

instruction: When is the best time to pump if I want to increase my supply or build a freezer stash while still breastfeeding?
output: Most moms pump after breastfeeding to get milk the baby leaves. If the baby isn’t removing milk efficiently or nursing long enough, pumping after breastfeeding can be important to drain your breasts thoroughly and keep up your supply. You can also pump between breastfeeding sessions or during the baby’s nap — you’ll probably get more milk then because your breasts have had a chance to replenish some of what the baby took at the last feeding.

instruction: My supply is low and the baby seems unsettled after nursing — what can I do so I can pump?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your supply is low and the baby is too unsettled after nursing for you to pump, try offering him the rest of his meal by supplementing, with a little dessert at the breast if you can, and then pump. Another option is to pump between breastfeeding sessions or during a nap so the breasts have time to replenish some milk.

instruction: If my baby wakes and wants to nurse soon after I pump, is that a problem?
output: If your baby wakes and wants to nurse soon after you pump, there might be less milk available in your breasts then, but he’ll be getting the pumped milk soon, and the pumping will have helped to boost your supply.

instruction: Should I watch the clock to know when my pumping session is over?
output: Don’t watch the clock to know when your session is over — feel your breasts to see if they’re lighter and softer. If you can’t tell yet, begin by pumping for fifteen to twenty minutes and pay attention to how your breasts feel until you learn your body’s cues.

instruction: Is it worth pumping a little longer sometimes to get more milk?
output: Yes. Sometimes it’s worth pumping a bit longer to see if you can get another milk release; you’ll learn what works for you. Many women get their double-pumping routine down to a total of ten minutes including setup and cleanup, but it takes practice and learning your body’s quirks.

instruction: Are there resources if I’m starting with a really low supply and need more tips?
output: If you’re starting with a really low supply, there are additional thoughts in Chapter 18. There’s also a helpful video about maximizing milk collection developed by Dr. Jane Morton at newborns.stanford.edu Breastfeeding MaxProduction.html.

instruction: What should I do immediately after I finish pumping to get the most milk out?
output: After you’ve finished pumping, tip the flange down and spend a minute hand-expressing a bit more into it. This strips out the last remaining milk the pump couldn’t get, further increasing your output and thus your supply.

instruction: My pump seems to be giving me less milk each time. Could the pump itself be the problem?
output: It could be. Check that all parts are plugged in and properly connected and inspect tubes, valves, discs, and membranes for fraying, tearing, pinching, clogging, wetness, or dried milk that prevents a valve from sealing. Start at the flange and check every part back to the motor. Also have suction and cycling speed checked at a rental station; you can find local rental stations through the manufacturer’s website.

instruction: I often miss pumping sessions because I'm busy. Will that affect my milk supply?
output: Yes. Missing sessions or cutting them short can lead to less milk over time. A full breast slows milk production, and pumping less often or for shorter sessions can let your supply diminish. Try scheduling pumping time or using a hands‑free arrangement so you can pump while you do other things; but any amount you can do is still helpful.

instruction: When is the best time to pump if I'm with my baby and want to build up a freezer stash?
output: Many moms pump after breastfeeding to collect the milk the baby leaves. If your baby nurses well you may not get much, but pumping after a feeding still signals your breasts to make more. You can also pump between breastfeeding sessions or during a nap to get more milk because the breasts have had time to replenish.

instruction: My baby often doesn't nurse long enough. Should I pump right after a feeding?
output: If your baby is not removing milk efficiently or nursing long enough, pumping after feeding can be important to drain your breasts thoroughly and help keep up your supply.

instruction: I want to pump but my baby gets upset after nursing, so I can't pump then. Any options?
output: If your baby is too unsettled after nursing for you to pump, you can offer supplement for the rest of the meal and then pump, or try pumping between nursing sessions or during a nap when breasts have had time to replenish.

instruction: Is it okay to pump at night or should I just nurse?
output: Whether to pump at night depends on your situation and need for sleep. Milk supply is somewhat higher at night in response to higher prolactin levels, which can be a reason to add pumping, but sleep deprivation can decrease milk production. If you must follow an aggressive pumping schedule, pump in addition to nursing if you wake during the night, but it’s also okay if you don’t.

instruction: I feel like I can't release milk to the pump even when I'm connected. What can I try to increase flow?
output: Try smelling something your baby has worn or looking at photos or videos of your baby while you pump. Use massage, compression, and hand‑expression techniques. A hot shower or warm wet compresses on the breast just before pumping or hand‑expressing may also help the milk flow more easily.

instruction: Are there simple ways to make pumping easier when I need both hands free?
output: Yes. You can use a hands‑free arrangement so you can multitask while pumping. You can also use furniture, body parts, clothing, or anything that works for you to make pumping easier, and share ideas that work well for you with others.

instruction: Could lower milk output be because my baby is older and nursing less?
output: Yes. If your baby is nursing less overall, your milk supply will naturally be lower and your baby won’t need as much milk.

instruction: What should I check if the suction or cycling speed doesn't feel right?
output: Check that all parts are properly connected and inspect tubing, valves, discs, and membranes for damage or clogging. Then take the pump to a rental station and ask to have the suction and cycling levels checked; you can find local rental stations on the manufacturer’s website.

instruction: I'm panicking because I get almost no milk when I pump after nursing. Does that mean my supply is gone?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby is nursing well you may not get much when you pump after a feeding, but that still tells your breasts to make more. If your baby isn’t removing milk efficiently, pumping after feeding is important to help drain the breast and support supply.

instruction: Can dried milk on parts cause problems with pumping?
output: Yes. Dried milk can prevent a valve from sealing and affect performance. Inspect parts for dried milk, clogs, fraying, or wet membranes and clean or replace parts as needed.

instruction: If I sometimes have to cut a pumping session short, is any pumping still worthwhile?
output: Yes. If you really can’t pump more than you are doing now, any amount of pumping you can do is beneficial. Try scheduling or a hands‑free setup to increase consistency over time.

instruction: How can I know if a membrane or disc is causing low output?
output: Look for tears, wetness, or other damage on discs and membranes, and inspect tubing for pinches or breaks. Problems with these parts can prevent proper sealing or suction and reduce milk removal; check all parts from the flange back to the motor.

instruction: Why might my milk supply be dropping even though I'm still pumping sometimes?
output: If you pump less often or for shorter sessions than you used to, your milk supply can gradually diminish. Also, if you wait until your breasts feel full before pumping, a full breast slows milk production and can reduce supply over time. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: Could waiting until my breasts feel full before pumping cause my supply to fall?
output: Yes. A full breast slows down milk production, which means your milk supply will gradually diminish if you regularly wait until your breasts feel full before pumping.

instruction: I've missed a few pumping sessions because I'm busy. Will that make my supply go down?
output: Missing sessions or cutting them short here and there can reduce your milk supply. Try scheduling pumping time on your daily calendar or using a hands-free arrangement so you can multitask while you pump. Any amount of pumping you can do is still helpful.

instruction: How can I get milk to let down when the pump doesn't seem to work for me?
output: If you find it hard to release milk to the pump, try smelling something your baby has worn or looking at photos or videos of your baby while you pump. You can also concentrate on massage, compression, and hand expression techniques, or take a hot shower or use warm wet compresses on the breast just before pumping or handexpressing to help milk flow more easily.

instruction: My baby is nursing less now that they're older. Should I expect to pump less milk?
output: Yes. If your baby is nursing less overall, your milk supply will naturally be lower and your baby won’t need as much milk.

instruction: Pumping really hurts me. What should I try first to make it more comfortable?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Start with rapid, light suction to ease your nipples into the process, and use lower suction overall. Set the pump near the upper end of fully comfortable rather than uncomfortable, and make sure the sucking speed allows intermittent breaks in suction.

instruction: My nipples rub in the flange and look swollen after pumping. Could the flange size be the problem?
output: Yes. If your nipple or the skin around it rubs in the tunnel, or if your nipple is swollen or darker after pumping, you may need a different size flange.

instruction: Can I put oil in the flange to make pumping less painful? Are any oils not recommended?
output: Coating the interior of the flange or your breast with a bit of olive or vegetable oil may provide enough lubrication to make pumping comfortable. The text advises not to use corn or nut oil.

instruction: Would getting a better pump help with soreness and milk output?
output: Rental-grade pumps work best for almost everyone, so upgrading to a higher-grade pump may help both comfort and milk expression.

instruction: Could my pump settings be causing nipple soreness even if the flange and suction seem okay?
output: Yes. Make sure the pump is set up correctly for the way you’re using it. For example, using the double-pumping setting while single-pumping can cause soreness. Also check the sucking speed so nipples get intermittent breaks.

instruction: Could an infection make pumping painful for me?
output: Yes. Yeast and bacterial infections can make pumping painful.

instruction: Is it possible I'm allergic to parts of the pump and that's why pumping hurts?
output: Yes. You may be having a rare skin reaction to the type of plastic used to make the flange.

instruction: I've tried changing settings and flange and it still hurts. Who can I contact for help?
output: If none of these ideas helps, call an LLL Leader, another pumping mother, the place where you bought the pump, or the pump company’s hotline. For more tips and empathy, you can visit the llli.org forums.

instruction: Are there techniques I can use by hand if the pump isn't getting milk out?
output: Yes. Massage, compression, and hand expression techniques can increase your ability to release milk, and are useful if the pump alone isn’t working.

instruction: Any small tricks to help milk let-down when I'm distracted or have other children around?
output: Some mothers find smelling something their baby has worn or keeping photos or videos of the baby to look at while pumping helps. One mother also found that singing nursery rhymes she used with her older child helped milk release when she needed to express for the baby.

instruction: My nipples hurt when I pump. What might be causing the pain and what can I try to make it more comfortable?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Possible causes and things to try include: using a little lubrication; upgrading your pump (rental-grade pumps work best for almost everyone); adjusting the sucking speed so nipples get intermittent breaks; making sure the pump is set up correctly (for example, using a double-pumping setting while single-pumping can cause soreness); checking for yeast or bacterial infection; and checking for a rare allergic skin reaction to the flange plastic. If none of these helps, contact an LLL Leader, another pumping mother, the place where you bought the pump, or the pump company’s hotline, and you can also look for tips and empathy on llli.org forums.

instruction: Can I use any clean container to store milk for a healthy full-term baby?
output: Yes. Milk to be given later to healthy full-term babies can be expressed into any clean container, though most mothers use bottles or disposable bags made specifically for human milk collection and freezing.

instruction: Which plastics should I avoid when storing expressed milk?
output: Avoid containers made with bisphenol-A (BPA), identified with a number 3 or 7 recycling symbol. A safer plastic alternative is polypropylene, which is soft and semi-cloudy and marked with the number 5 recycling symbol or the letters PP. You can also avoid plastic entirely by using glass bottles.

instruction: Can I use disposable bottle liners or random plastic bags to freeze milk?
output: Don’t use disposable bottle liners or plastic bags that aren’t made specifically for human milk collection and storage. Other kinds are less durable, tend to leak, and some types of plastic can destroy nutrients in your milk.

instruction: How should I label milk I pump for daycare?
output: Put the date on the bag or bottle you pump into, and if your baby goes to day care, also write your baby’s name on the label. It’s much easier to write the date on an empty container rather than a full one, and be sure to use waterproof ink.

instruction: How long can expressed milk sit at room temperature before I need to refrigerate it?
output: Your milk will stay fresh for at least four to eight hours at room temperature (about 77°F / 22°C). The hotter the day, the shorter the time. For a healthy, full-term baby, there’s usually no need to rush to refrigerate immediately within that timeframe.

instruction: If the baby doesn’t finish a bottle, can I refrigerate the remainder and use it at the next feeding?
output: Yes. If the baby doesn’t finish the bottle, you can refrigerate it promptly and give it at the next feeding. There is usually some backwash of bacteria from the baby’s saliva that reduces how long a partly used container can sit unrefrigerated; exact times haven’t been researched, but refrigerating until the next feeding should be fine.

instruction: Is frozen breast milk still good for my baby compared with formula?
output: Even frozen human milk retains the nutritional properties babies need and, depending on how long it’s been frozen, still offers antiinfective qualities that formula cannot match. Refrigerated milk, however, has more antiinfective properties than frozen milk.

instruction: Does how long milk is frozen affect its antiinfective properties?
output: Yes. Milk that has been frozen for two weeks has more antiinfective properties than milk that has been frozen for two months.

instruction: Should I refrigerate or chill my milk right after expressing it?
output: It’s best to refrigerate or chill your milk right after expressing it if it can’t be given to your baby in the next few hours.

instruction: Could a rash or redness from the flange be an allergic reaction to the pump plastic?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A rare skin reaction to the type of plastic used to make the flange can occur, so you should check for an allergic reaction to the flange material.

instruction: Can using the wrong pumping setting cause nipple soreness?
output: Yes. For example, using the double-pumping setting while single-pumping can cause soreness, so make sure the pump is set up correctly for how you’re using it.

instruction: What infections can make pumping painful and what should I check for?
output: Yeast and bacterial infections can make pumping painful, so check for those if pumping is uncomfortable.

instruction: If none of the tips help with pumping problems, who can I contact for help and support?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If none of the ideas helps, call an LLL Leader, another pumping mother, the place where you bought the pump, or the pump company’s hotline. For additional pump tips and empathy, you can visit the llli.org forums.

instruction: Is it true that fresher milk has more nutritional and immune benefits than older milk?
output: Yes. The fresher the milk is when your baby gets it, the more of the important nutritional and immunological qualities he’ll receive. Refrigerated milk has more anti-infective properties than frozen milk, and fresher is better overall.

instruction: How long can I leave freshly expressed breast milk out at room temperature before I need to refrigerate it?
output: Freshly expressed milk will stay fresh for at least four to eight hours at room temperature (about 77°F / 22°C). Hotter days shorten that time, and for a healthy, full-term baby you don’t need to rush to refrigerate within that window.

instruction: If my baby doesn't finish a bottle, can I refrigerate the rest and give it at the next feeding?
output: Yes. If the baby doesn't finish the bottle, you can refrigerate the partly used container promptly and give it at the next feeding. There is usually some backwash of bacteria in a partly used bottle, which reduces the amount of time it can sit out unrefrigerated; exact hours haven’t been researched, but refrigerating until the next feeding should be fine.

instruction: Can I combine milk from different pumping sessions into one container?
output: Yes. You can combine milk from several pumping sessions in one container as long as you chill the newly expressed milk separately before adding it so you are adding cold to cold.

instruction: How long can I keep expressed breast milk in the refrigerator?
output: Freshly pumped milk can stay refrigerated for at least three to eight days.

instruction: What's the safest way to warm refrigerated breast milk for a feeding?
output: Warm the chilled milk by holding the container under warm running water for several minutes or by immersing the container in a pan of water that has been heated but is not truly hot. Do not heat the milk directly on the stove because it can get too hot, and do not use a microwave because it can create hot spots that could burn your baby badly.

instruction: My refrigerated milk separates into two layers. Do I need to shake it before feeding?
output: Separation into a top cream layer and a lower whey layer is normal. Swirl the milk gently to redistribute the cream before giving it to your baby. Avoid shaking because that breaks up some long-chain fatty acids; it’s not harmful but reduces their value.

instruction: How much milk should I freeze in each container to avoid waste?
output: Milk expands as it freezes, so don’t fill containers all the way. It may make sense to fill containers with only about 2 ounces (60 ml). Two to four ounces (60 to 120 ml) is what a baby is likely to want in a single feeding, so freezing in those amounts reduces waste.

instruction: Can I add refrigerated milk to milk that is already frozen?
output: Yes. You can add refrigerated milk to previously frozen milk as long as you add cold to cold.

instruction: Where in the freezer should I store breast milk and how should I store milk bags?
output: Store milk in the middle of the freezer, as far away from the door and sides as possible because temperatures can fluctuate there. If you use milk bags, consider double-bagging them and storing them horizontal to save space, or store them upright inside a sealable container to reduce freezer burn or freezer taste. Use the oldest frozen milk first.

instruction: What should I do with frozen milk if the power goes out? Will it still be OK?
output: If the freezer is full, milk is probably okay for up to two days; if it is half full, milk is probably okay for up to one day. Grouping foods together helps them stay frozen longer. A freezer compartment inside a refrigerator may defrost faster. Adding available ice to the freezer will help milk stay frozen longer. If it’s below freezing outside you can store milk in snow or deep shade, but don’t leave it exposed to the sun because it could thaw. If the milk still has a frozen core, it’s okay to refreeze it. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Is it safe to thaw frozen breast milk in the microwave or on the stove?
output: No. Do not thaw or heat milk in the microwave because it can create hot spots that could burn your baby badly, and do not heat milk directly on the stove because it can get too hot. The safest ways to thaw are to thaw in the refrigerator overnight or to hold the container under cool running water, gradually increasing the water temperature.

instruction: Can I freeze milk in containers that are completely full?
output: No. Milk expands as it freezes, so do not fill containers all the way if you plan to freeze them.

instruction: My milk changed color after I ate something—does that mean it's spoiled?
output: No. Milk can change color after you eat certain foods, and that does not harm the milk.

instruction: Does freezing breast milk make it less protective against infection compared with refrigerated milk?
output: Yes. Refrigerated milk has more anti-infective properties than frozen milk. Milk that has been frozen for two weeks has more anti-infective properties than milk that has been frozen for two months. Even long-frozen milk is far superior to formula.

instruction: How full should I fill containers before I freeze breast milk?
output: Milk expands as it freezes, so don’t fill containers all the way to the top. Many people fill containers with about 2 ounces (60 ml) each so there’s room for expansion and less waste, since babies usually want 2 to 4 ounces (60–120 ml) per feeding.

instruction: Can I add freshly refrigerated milk to milk that’s already frozen?
output: Yes. Add cold to cold — you can add refrigerated milk to previously frozen milk.

instruction: Where in the freezer should I put stored breast milk?
output: Store milk in the middle of the freezer, as far away from the freezer door and sides as you can, because temperatures can fluctuate near those areas. Also use the oldest frozen milk first so it doesn’t get too old.

instruction: How should I store breast milk if I use freezer bags?
output: If you freeze milk in bags, consider double-bagging them (the outer bag doesn’t need to be a milk bag) and store them horizontal to save space. You can also store them upright inside a sealable container to reduce long-term freezer burn or freezer taste.

instruction: The power just went out — how long will my frozen milk be okay?
output: It’s understandable to be worried. If the freezer is full, milk is probably okay for up to two days; if it is half full, about one day. Grouping foods together helps them stay frozen longer. A freezer compartment inside a fridge may defrost faster, so adding any available ice can help. If it’s below freezing outside you can store milk in snow or deep shade, but don’t leave it in the sun because it could thaw. If the milk still has a frozen core, it’s okay to refreeze it.

instruction: What’s the safest way to thaw frozen breast milk?
output: The safest way is to thaw milk in the refrigerator overnight. You can also hold it under cool running water and gradually increase the water temperature until it reaches a comfortable feeding temperature. Some babies will drink cold milk and don’t need it heated.

instruction: How do I know the milk is the right temperature for feeding?
output: Heat or warm the milk until it feels warm but not hot on your wrist — that is usually a comfortable feeding temperature.

instruction: My thawed milk smells soapy — is it safe to give to my baby?
output: Many parents notice this. A soapy smell or taste after thawing or standing can happen when milk has a high level of lipase enzymes. The milk is absolutely safe and most babies will still drink it. You can deactivate the lipase before freezing by heating freshly expressed milk to scalding (bubbling around the edges, but not boiling), then quickly cooling and freezing it.

instruction: If I scald milk to deactivate lipase, does that change anything else in the milk?
output: Scalding freshly expressed milk to deactivate lipase reduces some of the anti-infective elements somewhat, but formula is still far higher in risk and far lower in value.

instruction: How long can I keep milk in the refrigerator after I’ve thawed it?
output: Previously frozen milk that has been thawed can be kept in the refrigerator for up to twenty-four hours. If it hasn’t been used by that time, it should be discarded or refrozen.

instruction: Do I have to sterilize my pump parts and bottles after each use?
output: Fresh human milk kills almost all bacteria, viruses, and fungi it comes in contact with, so you don’t have to sterilize pump and bottle parts. Washing them thoroughly with hot, soapy water is usually all they need. You can run the pump flange, connector, and bottles through the dishwasher when convenient.

instruction: Should I sterilize pump parts that don’t touch the milk, like tubing or diaphragms?
output: Don’t sterilize pump parts that don’t come in contact with milk; tubes, diaphragms, and pistons can melt under high heat. If they have to be washed (which is unlikely), wash them by hand.

instruction: What’s the quickest way to dry pump tubing if it’s wet?
output: Stand in an empty space away from people, pets, and furniture and whip the tubing in circles so centrifugal force sends the water out the ends. If a small amount of moisture remains, hook the tubing to your pump, turn the pump on, and whoosh the tubing dry.

instruction: Can I refreeze milk that has partially thawed in my freezer after a power outage?
output: If the milk still has a frozen core, it’s okay to refreeze it.

instruction: Can I reheat leftover breast milk that was refrigerated after a previous feeding?
output: Yes. It is okay to reheat leftover milk that was refrigerated after a previous feeding.

instruction: Can I put my whole pumping kit with flanges attached into the refrigerator between sessions?
output: Yes. You can pump and put the whole pumping kit (flanges attached to containers) in the fridge until the next pumping session. Clean the whole setup at the end of the day.

instruction: Is it okay to use a sniff test to check if breast milk has gone bad?
output: Many mothers have used the sniff test to see if milk is still fresh. The sniff test doesn't detect germs, but it can tell you if milk has soured.

instruction: If I taste my milk to check it, is that something others do?
output: Yes. More than one mother has tasted her milk as well.

instruction: What does it mean if breast milk smells soapy?
output: A soapy smell is the result of lipase.

instruction: Is sour breast milk dangerous for a healthy full-term baby?
output: Sour milk isn't a health hazard, though it might upset intestines briefly.

instruction: I mixed a lot of storage steps and I'm worried: room temp for hours, partially used, refrigerated, frozen, then refrozen after a freezer failure — should I keep it?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Don't combine too many risky steps; in the example you describe (milk stood at room temperature for six hours, was partially consumed, then refrigerated for a day, then frozen, then went through a freezer failure and was refrozen) the guidance is to throw it out.

instruction: How should I use glass bottles when freezing milk so they don't break?
output: Screw the lid on lightly to allow for expansion when freezing. Some people also use canning jars.

instruction: What should I bring when I fly with a breast pump to avoid problems at security?
output: With increased security, it may be necessary to prove to airline personnel that your pump isn't a detonation device. Bringing the instruction book should help.

instruction: Could an airport ask me to drink some of my milk to prove it's safe?
output: Some airports might still ask you to drink a bit of the milk to prove it isn't poison, although U.S. regulations no longer require this.

instruction: How can I transport milk on a trip so it stays cold?
output: Putting your milk along with some frozen ice packs in a small cooler or insulated bag should get you where you're going.

instruction: Can I ship frozen milk home and how should I pack it?
output: Milk can be shipped home if it's packed in dry ice. Wrap the frozen containers in newspaper, scatter chipped dry ice around the outside of the grouped containers, and seal the package tightly; it should last this way for several days.

instruction: If the milk I ship arrives slightly frozen, can I refreeze it?
output: If your milk is even slightly frozen when it arrives, it can be refrozen.

instruction: If I'm away from my baby and can't save or ship my pumped milk, what should I do with it?
output: If you're traveling away from your baby and you can neither save nor ship your milk, then you'll have to discard it. Many parents wonder about this. Remember that every bit you pump makes it possible for your baby to have that same amount when you're back together.

instruction: How long will milk shipped in dry ice last?
output: Packed with chipped dry ice around grouped frozen containers and sealed tightly, the package should last this way for several days.

instruction: How should I freeze breastmilk in a glass bottle so it won't break?
output: Screw the lid on lightly so the milk can expand as it freezes. It also mentions people using canning jars the same way.

instruction: Can I use canning jars to freeze my milk?
output: Yes. The text notes that people have used canning jars for freezing milk.

instruction: I'm worried airport security will think my breast pump is dangerous — what should I bring?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Bringing the pump's instruction book should help if you need to show that it isn't a detonation device. U.S. regulations no longer require drinking milk to prove it's safe, but some airports might still ask you to do that.

instruction: What's an easy way to carry expressed milk when I'm flying?
output: Put your milk with some frozen ice packs in a small cooler or insulated bag; that should get you where you're going.

instruction: Can I ship my pumped milk back home? How do I pack it?

instruction: How long will milk last if I ship it with dry ice?
output: Packed with dry ice as described, it should last for several days.

instruction: If the breastmilk I shipped arrives even slightly frozen, can I refreeze it?
output: Yes. If your milk is even slightly frozen when it arrives, it can be refrozen.

instruction: What should I do if I can't save or ship milk when I'm away from my baby?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you can neither save nor ship your milk, you'll have to discard it; the text notes that every bit you pump makes it possible for your baby to have that same amount when you're back together.

instruction: Does expressing milk empty the breast as well as a baby nursing?
output: No. Expressing milk does not empty the breast as thoroughly as a well-nursing baby.

instruction: Is expressing breastmilk worth the effort if it's not fun?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Expressing can be extremely important in getting you and your baby through breastfeeding difficulties, making working and breastfeeding simpler, or giving your baby his normal food when breastfeeding isn't possible.

instruction: If I freeze milk in glass bottles, should I screw the lid on tightly or loosely?
output: Screw the lid on lightly to allow for expansion as the milk freezes; screwing it on tightly would not allow that expansion.

instruction: Will packing milk with frozen ice packs in a small cooler keep it safe for travel?
output: Putting your milk along with some frozen ice packs in a small cooler or insulated bag should get you where you're going.

instruction: What exact steps does the text give for shipping milk with dry ice?
