instruction: If an airport asks me to drink a bit of my milk to prove it's not poison, do regulations require that?
output: U.S. regulations no longer require this, but the text says some airports might still ask you to drink a bit of the milk to prove it isn't poison.

instruction: Can expressing milk replace the breastfeeding relationship with my baby?
output: With a little care, expressing milk can be used to support, not replace, the age-old relationship that you share with your baby.

instruction: Is it normal for a 3-year-old to still be breastfeeding?
output: Most babies, if given the opportunity, will breastfeed until they naturally outgrow the need, which appears to be sometime between two and a half and seven years. UNICEF, WHO, and the Canadian Paediatric Society recommend breastfeeding for at least the first two years and beyond as long as mutually desired.

instruction: What exactly counts as the start of weaning?
output: Weaning begins the first time your baby eats or drinks something other than your milk or even sucks on something other than your breast. Some cultures count the first non-mother-milk swallow as weaning, while others define weaning as the end of breastfeeding. The process starts at that first change and ends the last time your child nurses.

instruction: How long does the weaning process usually take?
output: Weaning can take several days, weeks, months, or years. It begins at the first time your baby has something other than your milk and ends the last time your child nurses, so the length varies a lot between families.

instruction: I'm worried extended breastfeeding will make my child clingy and dependent. Is that true?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Research indicates the opposite: children who are not nursed past infancy tend to be somewhat less secure and less independent than their peers who breastfed longer.

instruction: Can breastfeeding help when my toddler gets sick?
output: Yes. When a breastfeeding child is sick, illnesses are often milder and easier to deal with. Your milk may be all the child can keep down and provides nutrition, anti-infectives, and growth factors that help recovery. A sick child needs plenty of breastmilk, and breastfeeding also comforts an upset child.

instruction: When should I stop breastfeeding — is there a set age?
output: It is a very personal decision and only you and your child know what is best. The baby has a biological and emotional need for breastfeeding for at least the first couple of years and frequently longer. There is no single set age that everyone should stop.

instruction: If my baby starts solids or formula, does that mean we've weaned?
output: In some cultures the first swallow of something other than mother’s milk is considered weaning. Generally, the weaning process has begun once your baby eats or drinks something other than your milk, but weaning only ends the last time your child nurses.

instruction: Does nursing help with tantrums and getting my child to sleep?
output: Yes. Nursing often soothes tears and tantrums, and the hormones and enzymes released by nursing help gentle a child into sleep. It’s an effective comfort and calming method for many situations.

instruction: Will breastfeeding make traveling harder once my child is a toddler?
output: It can be more complicated if you try to replace breastfeeding with bottles, formula, or a special object. With breastfeeding, your child feels instantly secure no matter where he is because the familiar and comforting breasts are still there.

instruction: Are there health advantages for toddlers who keep breastfeeding while they put things in their mouths?
output: Breastfed children tend to be very healthy through the phase when they put everything in their mouths. Breastmilk is nutritious and easily digestible, and it provides immune benefits that help during this stage.

instruction: I'm feeling unsure about when to wean and feel guilty. Is that normal?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Ending breastfeeding is a very personal decision, and only you and your child know what’s best; experiences often differ from plans.

instruction: What are the advantages to me if I continue nursing a toddler?
output: Nursing is an all-purpose mothering tool that makes parenting—from tantrums to illness—a lot easier. It provides instant comfort and security for your child, is a highly effective pain reliever with no negative side effects, and simplifies travel and soothing needs.

instruction: Do official organizations recommend how long to breastfeed?
output: Yes. UNICEF, the World Health Organization, and the Canadian Paediatric Society recommend breastfeeding for at least the first two years of life and beyond for as long as mutually desired.

instruction: My toddler suddenly stopped nursing in the morning — could that be the start of weaning?
output: Yes. If a child begins to lose interest and starts choosing other foods or drinks instead of nursing, that can mark the beginning of the weaning process. Weaning begins when a baby eats or drinks something other than your milk and ends the last time the child nurses.

instruction: Is breastfeeding the same across all cultures when it comes to weaning?
output: No. Weaning means different things in different cultures. In some, the first swallow of something other than mother’s milk is considered weaning; in others, weaning means ending breastfeeding completely. The authors consider weaning to involve both the first non-milk feeding and the last nursing session.

instruction: What are the advantages of continuing to nurse my toddler?
output: Continued nursing helps calm tantrums and pain, often making tears and tantrums melt away. Illnesses tend to be milder and easier to deal with. Nursing provides comfort and nutrition when a child is sick and may be all the child can keep down. Breastmilk contains nutrition, anti-infectives, and growth hormone. Hormones and enzymes released by nursing help ease a child into sleep. Travel is simpler because breastfeeding provides an instant familiar source of comfort.

instruction: Can nursing help when my child is sick?
output: Yes. Nursing provides comfort, and breastmilk may be all a sick child can keep down. Breastmilk is a nutritious, easily digestible food and contains anti-infectives and growth hormone that help children get better. Many mothers use nursing as a tool when their children are ill.

instruction: Is it normal for a toddler to still want to nurse?
output: Yes. Many mothers didn’t imagine nursing a toddler at first but reached that point one nursing at a time. There’s no reason to stop before you and your child are ready, and the longer you breastfeed the better it is for both of you.

instruction: How does natural weaning usually happen on its own?
output: Weaning at nature’s pace is often two steps forward, one step back. Babies start solids, nurse less, and their stools become more like adult stools. Illness can make them nurse more for a time and may loosen stools again. Gradual spacing of nursings and shorter nursing times, negotiation with the older child, and occasional setbacks are typical. Eventually nursing may wind down to one or two short nursings a day and then taper off or stop when the child prefers other foods.

instruction: My toddler wants both a story and nursing at bedtime; will that phase pass?
output: It can take longer before it gets shorter. Children may want both a story and nursing for a time, but one day they may be so busy playing they forget to nurse for an entire day, or they may start sleeping through the night. This gradual change is part of natural weaning. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: I want my body back and am thinking about weaning now. What might happen if I wean early?
output: Wanting your body back is a common feeling. Because of cultural expectations to nurse about a year, impatience can come early. Early weaning will remove a powerful mothering tool you use to calm and settle your child. After early weaning, your baby may still want holding and cuddling, tantrums and illness may increase, and sleep times for you and your child may be more problematic. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: Will my child’s stools change when starting solids or nursing more during a cold?
output: When babies start solids their stools often become more like adult stools. If the child gets a cold and nurses more, the increased milk can make stools looser and maybe yellow again. This change does not last permanently in most cases.

instruction: How does breastfeeding make travel easier?
output: Breastfeeding is simpler for travel because it avoids bottles and formula and the need for a special teddy or blanket. With breastfeeding, a child feels instantly secure wherever they are because their familiar and comforting breasts are still there.

instruction: If I never say anything or intervene, will my child eventually wean on their own?
output: Yes. Children naturally move on to the next stage as the wider world opens up. Even without active intervention, a child will wean on their own over time — doing nothing often works fine.

instruction: Does breastmilk do anything besides provide nutrition when my child is ill?
output: Yes. In addition to nutrition, breastmilk contains anti-infectives and growth hormone. Nursing also releases hormones and enzymes that help gentle a child into sleep and provides a highly effective pain reliever with no negative side effects described.

instruction: I feel guilty about still nursing my toddler — is that common and what should I expect?
output: A lot of mothers felt the same; many who couldn’t imagine nursing a toddler early on adjusted one nursing at a time. There’s no reason to stop before you and your child are ready, and gradually breastfeeding longer is described as better for both of you. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Will nursing help calm my child after a scraped knee?
output: Yes. Tears and tantrums often melt away with a little nursing, and nursing is described as a highly effective pain reliever that helps calm a child in those situations.

instruction: After my child weaned, I felt helpless when he got sick. Is that a common experience?
output: Some mothers report feeling helpless after their child weaned because nursing was what they used when their children were sick. Nursing is presented as a reliable way many mothers comfort and care for an ill child, so it’s not unusual to feel that way.

instruction: Can nursing help my child fall asleep?
output: Yes. The hormones and enzymes released by nursing help gentle a child into sleep, making nursing a useful way to soothe and settle a child for sleep.

instruction: How do parents usually start to space out nursing sessions during weaning?
output: Parents typically use gradual spacing of nursings and decrease nursing time as part of normal weaning. Responses slow down with older children and caregivers are more likely to negotiate, which gradually puts on the brakes until nursing decreases to one or two short sessions a day and then tapers further.

instruction: My toddler seems less interested in nursing lately. Will she stop on her own if I do nothing?
output: Children naturally have a strong desire to move on to the next stage of development. As the wider world opens up to them, they gradually close the door on babyhood. Even if you never lift a finger or ask, your child will wean. Doing nothing often works fine.

instruction: I want my body back and am thinking of weaning now. Is that a common feeling and what should I expect if I stop early?
output: It's common to have questions like this. Wanting your body back is a common feeling. Cultural expectations can make impatience happen earlier than usual. Early weaning will remove an easy, very effective tool for calming and settling your child. You will get your breasts back, but life may not be easier: your baby may still want to be held, carried, and cuddled (maybe even more), tantrums and illness may increase, and sleep times can become more problematic. Weaning doesn't make your child need you less; it simply removes one tool to meet their needs.

instruction: If I have to be away for a week and my child doesn't nurse, will he forget or stop nursing altogether?
output: Some children may go without nursing during a week away and then stop; others may have only brief nursing during separation and go on nursing for years afterward. Differences in outcomes can relate to mothering style and the child's personality. Each child often moves on when he is ready.

instruction: If I wean early, will my child still want to be held and cuddled?
output: Yes. Early weaning does not remove your child's need to be held, carried, and cuddled; they may want those things as much or more than before. Tantrums and illness may increase and sleep times may become more problematic after weaning.

instruction: Would breastfeeding-free life be easier when I go out with the baby?
output: Many mothers think weaning would make life easier, but the reality for a baby under a year is more work: mixing, heating, feeding, and cleaning several bottles a day. When you go out you must take enough bottles, formula, and water, and carry used, often smelly bottles until you get home. If an outing lasts longer than expected, you may run out of feeding supplies.

instruction: I'm exhausted from constant nursing. Are there ways to get more time to myself while still breastfeeding?
output: It makes sense to feel worn out. You can try connecting with other nursing moms at a playgroup or an LLL meeting. Get together with a friend who has a baby or toddler and share housework and baby care. Read in five-minute spurts or listen to a book on a podcast or CD while nursing or doing dishes. Record TV shows to watch in short segments. Have your partner take the baby out for a while, or have your partner stay home while you go out. You can also arrange a 'Mommy's night in' while your partner cares for the baby in another room.

instruction: If I stop nursing, will my child need me less overall?
output: Weaning doesn't make your child need you less. It only removes one tool—the easy way to calm and settle your child—so your child will still need feeding and nurturing and you will have one less method to meet those needs.

instruction: Is it possible to nurse only occasionally, like once a week, during a separation and still continue nursing later?
output: Yes. One parent nursed once a week during six weeks of separation and the child went on to nurse for several more years. Outcomes can vary, but occasional nursing during separation has occurred.

instruction: Could differences in my child's personality affect how long they nurse?
output: Yes. Personality was mentioned as one factor in why two children from the same parent behaved differently about nursing. Mothering style also plays a part, and each child often moves on when ready.

instruction: Would stopping breastfeeding now reduce tantrums at bedtime?
output: Weaning does not necessarily reduce tantrums; early weaning may actually increase tantrums and can make sleep times more problematic. It removes an easy calming tool you previously used.

instruction: Are mother groups or La Leche League meetings helpful when I'm overwhelmed by nursing?
output: Yes. Going to a playgroup or an LLL meeting to connect with other nursing moms is suggested; these get-togethers are often the glue that holds a mother together.

instruction: If I stop breastfeeding, will I just have fewer worries about bottles and feeding logistics?
output: Not necessarily. If your baby is under a year old, you will face the expense and bother of mixing, heating, feeding, and cleaning several bottles a day. You'll need to take enough bottles, formula, and water when you go out and carry used bottles until you return home. Unexpectedly long outings can lead to running out of supplies.

instruction: I'm thinking about weaning because I'm tired of always breastfeeding. Will breastfeeding-free life definitely be easier?
output: Many parents wonder about this. While you may think life would be easier, the reality is your baby still needs to be fed and nurtured. Early weaning gives you your breasts back but may not make daily life easier—your baby will still want holding and cuddling, tantrums and illness may increase, and sleep can become more difficult. Sometimes the issue is exhaustion and needing to refill your own resources rather than the method of feeding.

instruction: What are simple ways to fit small pleasures or hobbies into a busy day with a nursing baby?
output: Try reading books in five-minute spurts or listening to a book on a podcast or CD while you nurse the baby to sleep or wash dishes. Record your favorite TV show and watch it in short segments. These small strategies let you enjoy things in brief blocks of time.

instruction: I'm considering weaning because it seems easier. What extra work will I have to do if my baby is under a year old and I switch to formula?
output: If your baby is under a year, switching to formula means you will need to mix, heat, feed, and clean several bottles each day. When you go out you'll have to take enough bottles, formula, and water for the time you're away and carry used, often smelly, bottles until you get home. If an outing lasts longer than you expect, you may run out of feeding supplies.

instruction: I'm exhausted from nursing — are there quick ways to get more time for myself during the day?
output: It makes sense to feel worn out. Try these ideas from other nursing moms: go to a playgroup or an LLL meeting to connect with nursing moms; meet a friend with a baby to share housework and baby care; read in five-minute spurts or listen to a podcast or CD while you nurse or wash dishes; record a TV show and watch it a few minutes at a time; have your partner take the baby out or stay home while you go out; have a "Mommy's night in" while your partner cares for the baby in another room; get out of the house with your child; take a hot bath with bath oils and candles; put a laptop on the counter so you can sneak online time while your child plays; lie down with your nursling at naptime and bring a book; or start a morning or weekend routine where your partner and baby have breakfast together.

instruction: Are there groups I can join to connect with other breastfeeding moms?
output: Yes. You can go to playgroups or La Leche League (LLL) meetings to connect with other nursing moms. LLL Leaders also offer phone help, and many provide help at monthly meetings; some may even come to your home, with no fee for LLL Leader help.

instruction: Breastfeeding has been a nightmare for me — what kinds of help exist for serious breastfeeding problems like low supply or nipple pain?
output: Many parents wonder about this. There are breastfeeding helpers such as LLL Leaders who offer phone help, meetings, and sometimes home visits with no fee. If a problem is beyond their expertise, they may refer you to an International Board Certified Lactation Consultant (IBCLC), who may charge a fee, or to a specialist. Helpers have tools, tips, and education developed from experience that often go beyond books and websites.

instruction: If LLL can't solve my problem, who might they refer me to?
output: If a problem is beyond the expertise of LLL Leaders, they may refer you to an International Board Certified Lactation Consultant (IBCLC), who may charge a fee, or to a specialist.

instruction: How can my partner help so I can get a break from the baby?
output: Your partner can take the baby out for a while, stay home while you go out, or care for the baby in another room while you have a "Mommy's night in." You can also start a morning or weekend routine in which your partner and baby have breakfast together.

instruction: I've tried lots of things and still feel like I can't keep breastfeeding — is it okay to stop?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you've really reached the end of your rope and decide you can't do it anymore, that can be the right choice for you. Give yourself a warm hug for your efforts. Any amount of milk or nursing you provided is a lifetime gift to both of you, and a tough breastfeeding experience doesn't mean future ones will be the same. If you had a milk supply problem before, you will likely have more milk next time because pregnancy and nursing both help with future supply.

instruction: Where can I find lists of board-certified lactation consultants in my area?
output: You can find lists of board-certified lactation consultants by area at ilca.org Find a Lactation Consultant. You can also ask local friends or look on the llli.org forums.

instruction: Is there a cost for help from LLL Leaders?
output: There is never a fee for help from LLL Leaders. If they refer you to an IBCLC, that consultant may charge a fee.

instruction: Are there practical ways to get short pockets of time to read or relax while the baby is around?
output: Yes. Read in five-minute spurts or listen to an audiobook or podcast while you nurse or wash dishes; record a TV show and watch it a little at a time; put a laptop on the counter and sneak in online time while your child plays at your feet; lie down with your nursling at naptime and bring a book; or take a hot bath when someone else cares for the baby.

instruction: What should I do if the breastfeeding helper I'm seeing doesn't seem very helpful?
output: Breastfeeding helpers vary in experience and skills. If the help you're getting isn't helping, try someone else—different helpers have different tools, skills, and up-to-date knowledge. You can find other LLL Leaders through llli.org Find Local Support and ask local friends or the llli.org forums for recommendations.

instruction: If I switch to formula, what problems might I face when I'm out of the house?
output: When you're out you must be sure to take enough bottles, formula, and water for the length of time you'll be away. You will also have to carry used, often smelly, bottles until you get home, and if the outing lasts longer than expected you may run out of feeding supplies.

instruction: Does any breastfeeding count as beneficial if I stop early?
output: Yes. Any amount of milk or nursing that you provided is described as a lifetime gift to both you and your baby. Even a difficult breastfeeding experience doesn't mean future ones will be the same.

instruction: Where can I find local LLL Leaders for more help and meetings?
output: You can find LLL Leaders in your area at llli.org Find Local Support. LLL Leaders provide phone help, many offer help at monthly meetings, and some may even come to your home, with no fee for their help.

instruction: How can I find a good lactation consultant or breastfeeding helper in my area?
output: You can ask for a referral to an International Board Certified Lactation Consultant (IBCLC). There are lists of board‑certified lactation consultants by area at ilca.org Find a Lactation Consultant. You can also find La Leche League (LLL) Leaders at llli.org Find Local Support, ask local friends, or check the llli.org forums.

instruction: I've tried everything and feel like giving up. Is there really more help out there for breastfeeding problems?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Yes — experienced breastfeeding helpers often have tools, tips, and education developed over many years that aren’t in books or on websites. If the help you’re getting isn’t helping, try someone else; helpers vary in training, skills, and fit.

instruction: Is paying an IBCLC worth it if I'm struggling with breastfeeding?
output: There may be a fee for an IBCLC, but anything she charges will likely be much less expensive than formula and its related illnesses.

instruction: I feel guilty about possibly stopping breastfeeding. Is it okay if I decide I can't continue?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you’ve really reached the end of your rope and can’t do it anymore, that can be the right decision for you — that’s how good parenting works. Give yourself credit for your efforts: any amount of milk or nursing you provided is a lifetime gift. Future breastfeeding experiences may be different and possibly easier.

instruction: Does nursing while pregnant harm the fetus?
output: Nursing while pregnant does not hurt the fetus.

instruction: What changes can I expect if I keep nursing during early pregnancy?
output: About three‑quarters of nursing mothers have some nipple tenderness or pain in early pregnancy. More than half begin to feel dgety and impatient when they nurse during pregnancy. Most find that their milk supply drops significantly.

instruction: Should I wean during pregnancy if I have had premature labor before?
output: It makes sense to feel worried about this. There are extremely rare cases in which it’s important to wean to maintain a pregnancy because oxytocin released with nursing could stimulate uterine contractions. Women with a history of premature labor may be advised to avoid sex, orgasm, and nursing during pregnancy; the sex prohibition is usually to avoid mechanical disturbance of the cervix. The uterus doesn’t develop many oxytocin receptors until hours before birth, so it generally can’t detect oxytocin well until then.

instruction: How does frequent nursing affect getting pregnant again?
output: Frequent nursing is a powerful fertility suppressor. The World Health Organization’s review finds it takes a good two years for a woman’s body to recover fully from pregnancy and childbirth, and survival rates for both are greater with more than two years between children. A high‑need child who nurses more frequently keeps the mother infertile a bit longer.

instruction: My periods haven't returned and I want to conceive. Can changing my nursing help?
output: If your periods haven’t returned and you want to get pregnant soon, you could consider night weaning, not pumping during the day, or generally cutting back to see if that starts them up. Often a break of six hours on one or several nights is enough to kick‑start fertility.

instruction: What is a short luteal phase and could breastfeeding cause it?
output: A short luteal phase is the time from the day after ovulation until your period starts. Short luteal phases are associated with high levels of prolactin, which can be caused by breastfeeding. Charting your cycles can tell you when you ovulate and the length of your luteal phase. Some doctors recommend weaning; you might ask your doctor about progesterone supplements to correct a short luteal phase.

instruction: Where can I find local LLL support or talk to other mothers online?
output: You can find an LLL Leader in your area at llli.org Find Local Support and join discussions on the llli.org forums. You can also ask local friends for recommendations.

instruction: My current breastfeeding helper doesn't seem to be helping. What should I do next?
output: Breastfeeding helpers vary in how up to date they are, their diagnostic skills, and whether they’re a good fit. If the help you’re getting isn’t helping, try someone else — you may find a better‑fit helper or an IBCLC who can resolve more difficult situations.

instruction: If this breastfeeding experience was hard, does that mean future ones will be the same?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Just because one breastfeeding experience was tough doesn’t mean future ones will be. If you had a milk supply problem, you’ll likely have more milk the next time because pregnancy and nursing both help with future supply. If your baby had difficulties, your next baby most likely won’t have those same issues. The next experience may even help heal some of the emotional pain from this one.

instruction: Is there a book about tandem nursing if I want to nurse while pregnant and after the new baby arrives?
output: Yes — one resource mentioned for tandem nursing is Hilary Flower’s Adventures in Tandem Nursing: Breastfeeding During Pregnancy and Beyond.

instruction: How does frequent breastfeeding affect my fertility?
output: Frequent nursing is a powerful fertility suppressor. When a baby nurses less often, that signals the mother’s body that she may be ready for another pregnancy. Most women find fertility returns around the time their periods return regularly, though ovulation can occur before the first period comes back.

instruction: How long does it take a woman’s body to recover from pregnancy before getting pregnant again?
output: A review by the World Health Organization finds it takes a good two years for a woman’s body to recover fully from pregnancy and childbirth. Survival rates for both mother and baby are greater with more than two years between children.

instruction: My periods haven’t returned and I want to try for another baby. What can I do that might help restart my fertility?
output: If your periods haven’t returned, you could try night weaning, not pumping during the day, or generally cutting back on nursing to see if that starts them up. Often a break of about six hours on one or several nights is enough to kick-start fertility. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What is a short luteal phase and how could breastfeeding be involved?
output: A short luteal phase is a brief time from the day after ovulation until your period starts (the excerpt notes a short 4-day luteal phase as an example). A short luteal phase can affect fertility even if your overall cycle length looks normal. Short luteal phases are associated with high levels of prolactin, and breastfeeding can raise prolactin. Some doctors recommend weaning for this reason, and you might ask your doctor about progesterone supplements to correct a short luteal phase.

instruction: I’m going back to work and won’t have time to pump during the day. Do I have to stop breastfeeding?
output: Breastfeeding is flexible and doesn’t have to be all or nothing. If you can’t pump during the day, you can still breastfeed when you’re with your baby; that helps fill in nutritional and immunological gaps from formula and helps you reconnect. Your milk supply will decrease after a few days, so at work you can express enough to stay comfortable and nurse more at home to rebuild supply.

instruction: If I start working full time, will my milk supply just disappear?
output: Your milk supply will decrease after a few days without regular nursing, but expressing enough milk at work can keep you comfortable. If you later nurse more, your supply will increase again.

instruction: My surgeon says I’ll need general anesthesia for a procedure. Do I have to wean my baby?
output: You don’t need to stop breastfeeding or to pump and throw your milk away for general anesthesia. As soon as you’re awake, the anesthesia is essentially out of your blood and therefore essentially out of your milk. These guidelines apply to normal-term and older infants; premature or ill babies may need some time before breastfeeding again.

instruction: Are there medicines that definitely mean I must stop breastfeeding?
output: Very few medications are incompatible with breastfeeding. The main exceptions named are certain radioactive drugs, chemotherapy, and a very few long-lasting drugs. A resource called Medications and Mothers’ Milk (updated every two years) lists specific information; the excerpt suggests finding someone who has a copy before accepting that a drug isn’t compatible with breastfeeding.

instruction: If I have diabetes, will breastfeeding change my insulin needs?
output: Many illnesses are improved or unaffected by breastfeeding. Diabetic mothers often find their insulin needs are lower while they are breastfeeding.

instruction: My family keeps criticizing me for still nursing my older child. How can I respond?
output: This is your breastfeeding relationship, and weaning will change your life and your child’s, not theirs. If you’re not ready to stop, you don’t have to. Telling them you appreciate their concern often defuses tension; humor can disarm, and you can gently say you’ve studied the issues and made an informed decision. If none of that works, you may have to agree to disagree. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Can a high-need or fragile nursling delay my return to fertility?
output: Yes. A high-need or fragile child who nurses more frequently than average keeps his mother infertile a bit longer, to make sure his own needs are fully met.

instruction: Would tracking my cycle help me understand my fertility while breastfeeding?

instruction: Is it worth breastfeeding for only a short time after birth?

instruction: How long should I try to breastfeed to reduce my baby's early risks of illness?

instruction: If I start weaning at three or four months, will my baby tolerate formula better?

instruction: How long should I wait before starting solid foods according to major health organizations?

instruction: Can exclusive breastfeeding during the first six months prevent pregnancy?

instruction: What are the benefits of breastfeeding for nine months?

instruction: If I wean my baby at one year, will my child be okay on family foods and will I avoid formula costs?

instruction: What are the benefits of continuing breastfeeding to eighteen months?

instruction: I need to wean faster than I'd like — how should I do it to avoid problems?

instruction: How do I wean a baby under six months old?

Start by offering one bottle for two or three days, then slowly add bottles while decreasing breastfeedings. instruction: What can happen if I stop breastfeeding cold turkey?

instruction: My baby seemed to react badly to the first formula attempt—what can I do?

instruction: Is breastfeeding still valuable at two years old?

instruction: Which nursing times are usually the last to give up when weaning?

instruction: Will long-term nursing affect my child's emotional security?

instruction: How should I start switching my baby from breastfeeding to formula?

instruction: Can I give my 8-month-old cow's milk instead of formula?

instruction: My baby is over one year old — do I still need to give formula?

instruction: How can I stop nursing at naps and bedtime without upsetting my toddler?

instruction: Should I wean my baby abruptly because of my situation?

instruction: How can I replace bedtime nursing so my toddler still feels sleepy?

instruction: My toddler protests when I try to shorten nursing. What can I do instead of forcing it?

instruction: Is abrupt weaning ever necessary and what should I do if I have to stop suddenly?

instruction: Are there herbs or medicines that can help reduce milk supply?

instruction: Will breast binding make my milk dry up faster?

instruction: If I have to wean suddenly, how can I comfort my baby during the change?

instruction: Can we celebrate the end of nursing with a party, and is there a best day to do it?

instruction: If I stop nursing at night, will I need to pump and could that defeat the purpose?

instruction: My baby wakes at night a lot. What are some reasons besides hunger?

instruction: Is letting my baby "cry it out" a good option for night weaning?

instruction: How long might it take for my breasts to stop producing milk after I begin reducing supply?

instruction: What bedtime strategies can help an older child learn to get back to sleep without nursing?

Making sure the child eats and drinks enough during the day and has plenty of exercise may also help him sleep as much as he is physically and developmentally ready for.

instruction: If I have to wean in an emergency, should I change other parts of our routine right away?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If possible, change as little else right now as possible. Emergencies often call for less-than-ideal arrangements, but both you and your baby are losing something important and have a right to mourn, so minimizing other changes can help.

instruction: Can an older baby understand that nursing is ending, and how can I show that?
output: An older baby may understand that your nummies are "broken," especially if you put adhesive bandages on them. He may do better if you wear a dress or a tucked-in shirt that can’t be opened or lifted by searching hands.

instruction: Can my partner take over bedtime duties to help with weaning?
output: Yes. It may help to have your partner take over bedtime duties while you work on weaning, and talking with your child about what’s happening can support the change.

instruction: I'm thinking of stopping nursing at night so I can get uninterrupted sleep. Is that a good idea?
output: It’s important to think this through. Early night weaning is a risk factor for early weaning. Babies wake at night for different reasons (reconnecting after separation, teething, illness, or allergies). Night feedings may still contribute significantly to a baby’s nutrition into toddlerhood. Replacing nursing with cuddles and lullabies can help, but it simply substitutes one form of soothing for another and you may need to put more energy into settling the child than before. Many mothers try again a few weeks later and find the child fusses briefly and then sleeps more soundly.

instruction: If I stop night feeds, could my milk supply drop and mean I have to pump at night?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you have a younger baby, you may need to pump at least once in the night to keep your supply strong, which can partly defeat the purpose of night weaning.

instruction: Will stopping night nursing affect my baby's weight or nutrition?
output: It may. Nighttime feedings can contribute significantly to nutrition right into toddlerhood, and if your baby needs to breastfeed at night for nutrition, stopping could make weight gain falter.

instruction: Are night nursing and tooth decay linked? Should I wean at night to prevent cavities?
output: Human milk itself rarely contributes to decay and actually has tooth-strengthening properties. Historically children who nursed all night had little or no decay until decay-inducing foods became common, so night weaning is not likely to help with tooth decay.

instruction: My toddler wakes repeatedly at night. Should I try letting him cry it out to stop the wakings?
output: It makes sense to feel worried about this. Some parents resort to letting the baby cry it out to encourage self-soothing, but research has shown that this approach is very stressful for babies.

instruction: What can I try instead of nursing at night to help my child go back to sleep?
output: You can replace bedtime nursing with cuddles and lullabies. If your child is old enough to understand, you can explain that you won’t nurse at night (for example, say the nummies have to go to bed or they must wait for the sun). Some mothers assign one breast to daytime and one to nighttime to reduce feedings. Making sure the child eats and drinks enough during the day and has plenty of exercise may also help the child sleep as much as he is physically and developmentally ready for.

instruction: If I try night weaning, will night waking stop immediately as my child gets older?
output: Eventually children do start sleeping for longer periods at night, but some night waking is still normal through the preschool years. Many mothers who tried to night-wean and were met with strong protest tried again a few weeks later and found their child fussed a night or two and then slept more soundly.

instruction: Can I reduce night nursing by using only one breast at night and the other during the day?
output: Yes. Some mothers of toddlers assign one breast to daytime nursing and one to nighttime nursing to cut things down a little.

instruction: Is it possible my baby is waking for reasons other than hunger?
output: Yes. Babies wake at night for different reasons, including using nighttime to reconnect after many hours of separation, teething, illness, or allergies.

instruction: How might I feel emotionally when my child finally weans?
output: Many mothers feel a mix of nostalgia, wistfulness, and relief; these feelings tend to be muted when the process is slow and the child is ready for more mature interaction. If this is your last child, the sadness may be deeper. If you weaned before about two years, you might feel surprised by strong emotions—reveling in non-nursing status but also feeling weepy or super-sensitive, or feeling a sense of rejection if the child stopped easily. Be gentle with yourself and consider a small celebration to mark the transition.

instruction: I want to stop nursing at night so someone else can settle the baby while I sleep. Is that a reasonable reason?
output: That’s a reasonable reason. The text notes it’s fine to night-wean if your reason is to allow someone else to settle the baby while you sleep or to increase your chances of getting pregnant.

instruction: If I cut out night nursing, could I end up spending more energy getting my child back to sleep than before?
output: Yes. In many cases you may end up with a baby or toddler who still wakes at night and you actually have to put more energy into getting him back to sleep than before.

instruction: Would explaining 'the nummies have to go to bed' to a toddler help stop night nursing?
output: If your child is old enough to understand basic concepts, you might be able to explain that you aren’t going to be nursing at night and use simple explanations such as the nummies have to go to bed or waiting for the sun to come up.

instruction: Does breastfeeding have lasting effects on the parent–child relationship after weaning?
output: Weaning is an important transition and the end of a process that began at conception and a unique intimacy. Women who have both nursed and bottle-fed often recognize a difference in the relationship, and the effects of the close relationship that began with breastfeeding can continue in children after weaning.

instruction: I'm sad thinking about weaning my last child. Is that a normal reaction?
output: It makes sense to feel sad about this. Mothers whose children wean after several years often feel nostalgia and wistfulness, and if this is your last child the sadness may be deeper; consider a small celebration to mark the change and be gentle with yourself as you both find your new footing.

instruction: I'm feeling very sad about weaning because I know I won't be a nursing mother again. What can I do to mark this change?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Consider some little celebration for yourself to put a different spin on it. Be gentle with yourself as you both find your new footing; this is a turning point.

instruction: I weaned my baby before two years or so and now I feel weepy and extra sensitive. Is that normal?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. If you weaned before two years or so, the strength of your feelings about it might surprise you. You may revel in your non-nursing status but also feel weepy or super-sensitive for a time.

instruction: My child stopped nursing very easily and I feel rejected. Do other parents feel that way?
output: Many parents wonder about this. You may feel a sense of rejection if your child stopped too easily. Be gentle with yourself as you both find your new footing.

instruction: I nursed and bottle-fed my older child. Will that change our relationship later on?
output: Women who have both nursed and bottle-fed often recognize a difference in the relationship with their children. Having older weaned children, some people still see the effects of the close relationships that began with breastfeeding continuing in their children to this day.

instruction: Are there any books you can recommend about weaning?
output: Some good weaning books include: Adventures in Tandem Nursing: Breastfeeding During Pregnancy and Beyond, by Hilary Flower; How Weaning Happens, by Diane Bengson; Maggie’s Weaning, by Mary Joan Deutschbein; Mothering Your Nursing Toddler, by Norma Jane Bumgarner; The Nursing Mother’s Guide to Weaning, by Kathleen Huggins.

instruction: How should I treat myself emotionally after weaning?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Be gentle with yourself as you both find your new footing. Consider some little celebration for yourself to put a different spin on it.

instruction: Will I always remember breastfeeding even after weaning?
output: Many women can tell you vividly about their breastfeeding experiences, even when they are elderly. Weaning is an important transition and many people remember those experiences.

instruction: Is it normal to feel both relieved and sad when I stop nursing?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. You may revel in your non-nursing status but also feel weepy or super-sensitive for a time.

instruction: What does it mean that weaning is the end of a process that began at conception?
output: Weaning is described as the end of a process that began at conception and the end of a unique intimacy.

instruction: My child nursed one last time and said, 'Mama, your milk will last me forever.' Do some children have lasting moments like that?
output: As one little nursling said at a final nursing of his own choosing, "Mama, your milk will last me forever." Maybe weaning never really happens completely.

instruction: I feel guilty about weaning my toddler. Any advice for dealing with this guilt?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Be gentle with yourself as you both find your new footing, and consider some little celebration for yourself to put a different spin on it.

instruction: Does weaning completely end the intimacy of breastfeeding?
output: Weaning is described as the end of a unique intimacy, but the text also says maybe weaning never really happens completely.

instruction: I want to acknowledge the milestone of weaning. What wording could I use to describe it?
output: You could describe it as an important transition, the end of a process that began at conception and the end of a unique intimacy. It is also called a turning point.

instruction: Is it expected to have strong feelings if I weaned earlier than planned?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. If you weaned before two years or so, the strength of your feelings about it might surprise you.

instruction: How do other women describe the long-term effects of breastfeeding after weaning?
output: Women who have both nursed and bottle-fed often recognize a difference in the relationship with their children. Those who have older weaned children say they see effects of the close relationships that began with breastfeeding continuing in their children to this day.

instruction: I'm exclusively pumping — how often should I pump when my baby doesn't breastfeed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Newborns generally nurse 8–12 times in 24 hours, so one approach is to aim for about 10 expressions per day and not to fall below eight, doing more in the first few weeks. Pump whenever you think of it, even for short times. Pump at least once at night during the first six weeks to help establish supply. Around six weeks, when your supply is more stable, you can experiment to see how many sessions you need. Also remember milk is made most quickly when the breast is least full; if breasts stay too full for too long, production will slow down dramatically.

instruction: Do I need a special pump to remove most milk?
output: Most women are able to remove the most milk with rental-grade pumps. Renting can be costly, but the text notes it is still far less than the expense of formula plus increased health care expenses.

instruction: How much milk should I try to pump by the time my baby is two weeks old?
output: Ideally you’ll need to pump an average of 25 to 33 ounces (750 to 1,000 ml) daily by the time your baby is two weeks old. This helps set your milk supply high and may give you a freezer stockpile.

instruction: My milk supply dropped around five months. Could bottle overfeeding be the reason?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Supply problems often seem to happen around four to six months. Sometimes the issue is that the baby is getting too much by bottle. One way to know if overfeeding is the problem is weight gain: so long as he is gaining appropriately, that suggests overfeeding is not the issue. If you’ve ruled out overfeeding, you may need to take steps to increase your production.

instruction: Should I be pumping at night?
output: Pump at least once at night during the first six weeks as part of establishing a solid supply. After those first six weeks, whether you pump at night depends on how long your breasts will let you go without becoming uncomfortably full and how much you can pump during the day to meet your baby’s needs.

instruction: Is exclusive pumping the same as breastfeeding?
output: No. Exclusive pumping is very different even though both involve lactation. Breastfeeding is child-driven and varies in timing and length; pumping is mother-driven and tends to follow a more consistent schedule. Because pumping lacks the many little top-offs that help keep milk production strong, most exclusively pumping mothers have to make extra efforts to keep their supplies up, and the regimen takes enormous dedication.

instruction: How can I tell my baby wants to feed when I'm bottlefeeding with expressed milk?
output: Rooting signs can indicate that he wants to feed: fists to the mouth, turning his face strongly to one side, sticking out his tongue, or making sucking motions with his mouth. Also, human milk is digested more quickly than formula, so an exclusively human-milk-fed baby is likely to need feedings more frequently than a formula-fed baby.

instruction: Can I feed my baby on demand even though I'm using bottles of pumped milk?
output: Yes. Just because he’s being bottlefed doesn’t mean he can’t be fed on demand.

instruction: Do I need to pump as often as a nursing baby if I'm exclusively pumping?
output: Newborns generally nurse 8–12 times in 24 hours, so many exclusively pumping mothers aim for about ten expressions per day and not to fall below eight in the early weeks. Once your supply is stable at about six weeks, some women can get by on fewer pumping sessions; you can experiment to find what works for you.

instruction: My baby refuses to nurse and prefers the bottle. Is it okay to pump and feed my milk instead?
output: It makes sense to feel upset about this. Some mothers do pump and feed their milk when babies prefer the bottle; one reported feeding her milk for fourteen months after her baby refused to nurse, and felt good knowing he was getting normal nutrition and anti-infectives from her milk.

instruction: Should I give my bottle-fed baby a pacifier since he isn't getting sucking at the breast?
output: Because the baby isn’t getting sucking opportunities at the breast and sucking is important for soothing, a pacifier may be appreciated after bottlefeedings.

instruction: Will my milk dry up if I miss a lot of pumping sessions?
output: It’s common to worry about this. There isn’t a set minimum below which supply will dry up; most women will keep making some milk so long as some milk is being removed. That said, early on it helps to pump more frequently, and you should avoid letting your breasts stay too full for too long because fuller breasts make milk more slowly and production can slow dramatically.

instruction: When can I try to reduce how often I pump each day?
output: Around six weeks, when your supply is more stable, you can experiment to see how many times you need to pump each day to get the amount your baby needs. Some women can get by on fewer pumping sessions than others.

instruction: Who can I contact for personal guidance about exclusive pumping?
output: It may be very useful to contact a local La Leche League Leader or an International Board Certified Lactation Consultant (IBCLC) for personal guidance and support.

instruction: Do I need a rental-grade pump to remove most of my milk?
output: Most women are able to remove the most milk with rental-grade pumps. Renting one can be costly, but the cost is still far less than the expense of formula plus the increased health care expenses.

instruction: My supply dropped around five months of exclusive pumping—what might cause that?
output: Making enough milk is a common challenge for moms who are exclusively pumping, and this often seems to happen around four to six months. Sometimes the issue is that the baby is getting too much by bottle; weight gain can show whether overfeeding is the problem. If you have ruled out overfeeding, you may need to take some steps to increase your production.

instruction: How often should I feed my baby pumped breast milk compared with formula?
output: Human milk is digested more quickly than formula, so an exclusively human-milk-fed baby is likely to need to be fed more frequently than a formula-fed baby. Being bottlefed doesn’t mean the baby can’t be fed on demand.

instruction: What signs should I watch for that mean my baby wants to eat?
output: Rooting signs can indicate that your baby wants to feed: fists to the mouth, turning the face strongly to one side, sticking out the tongue, or making sucking motions with the mouth.

instruction: Since I'm bottlefeeding pumped milk, can my baby use a pacifier?
output: Because the baby isn’t getting sucking opportunities at the breast and sucking is important for soothing, a pacifier may be appreciated after bottlefeedings.

instruction: It feels emotionally hard to keep pumping—are there places where I can get support from other mothers?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Other mothers who are in the same boat can make your experience much easier to cope with. There is a very active forum just about exclusive pumping on the La Leche League website at llli.org, and there is an e-mail group just for exclusively pumping moms at pumpingmoms.org.

instruction: My premature baby is in the NICU and I feel helpless—does my voice or touch help?
output: It makes sense to feel helpless. Your baby knows your voice and your smell and will respond differently to your touch than to anyone else’s. Even the most fragile baby will benefit from hearing you talk to her and from your gentle touch.

instruction: What is kangaroo care and what does it do for a premature baby?
output: When you hold your baby skin-to-skin for long stretches of time (kangaroo care), research has shown several benefits: your baby’s oxygenation levels and breathing rates are more regular and stable and in harmony with his heart rate; his heart rate is slightly higher, showing a positive response to being close to you; you can warm and stabilize your baby’s temperature better than an incubator, automatically adjusting your own temperature to raise or lower your baby’s to his optimal level; he is more likely to breastfeed more easily and your milk production increases; he will have a better immune system with less vulnerability to allergies and infections in his first year; and you will feel closer to your baby, which helps heal the trauma of the premature birth. The text notes there are no downsides.

instruction: How long should skin-to-skin sessions be to help my preemie handle transitions from the Isolette?
output: The transition from Isolette to you or you to Isolette can be difficult for premies; it’s easier on your baby if your skin-to-skin stays are at least a couple of hours long. Hospital policies about skin-to-skin contact vary, and your baby’s age and developmental stage are factors, but spending as much time as you possibly can skin to skin is encouraged.

instruction: If my premature baby is not in kangaroo care, what problems might occur?
output: A premature baby who is not in kangaroo care is not as well oxygenated and has less stable heart and breathing rates, has more trouble keeping a stable temperature, has more trouble with breastfeeding, and his mother also has a poorer milk supply.

instruction: Is skin-to-skin contact better than an incubator for keeping my preemie warm?
output: Research has shown that you have the ability to warm and stabilize your baby’s temperature better than an incubator, and you will automatically adjust your own temperature to raise or lower your baby’s to his optimal level. Hospital policies vary.

instruction: My exclusively pumped baby is gaining weight—does that mean I'm overfeeding him?
output: So long as he is gaining appropriately, then all is well. Weight gain is one way to know whether overfeeding is the problem; if he is gaining appropriately, overfeeding is unlikely to be the issue.

instruction: Are there any downsides to kangaroo care for premature babies?
output: According to the research described, there are no downsides to kangaroo care.

instruction: Will kangaroo care help my milk production and breastfeeding attempts?
output: Research shows that with kangaroo care your baby is more likely to breastfeed more easily, and your milk production increases.

instruction: Seeing my newborn with tubes and monitors is frightening—should I still try to hold her?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Seeing a newborn with tubes and monitors can be frightening, but you are hugely important: your baby knows your voice and smell and will respond differently to your touch. Even the most fragile baby will benefit from hearing you talk to her and from your gentle touch.

instruction: Where can I find online communities specifically for exclusive pumpers?
output: There is a very active forum just about exclusive pumping on the La Leche League website at llli.org, and there is an e-mail group just for exclusively pumping moms at pumpingmoms.org.

instruction: What is Kangaroo Care and how does it help my premature baby?
output: Kangaroo Care is holding your baby skin-to-skin for long stretches. It makes your baby’s oxygenation levels and breathing rates more regular and stable and his heart rate slightly higher. You can warm and stabilize his temperature better than an incubator by automatically adjusting your own temperature. He is more likely to breastfeed more easily, your milk production increases, and he will have a better immune system with less vulnerability to allergies and infections in his first year. You will also feel closer to your baby, which helps heal the trauma of premature birth. There are no downsides.

instruction: Are there any downsides to Kangaroo Care?
output: There are no downsides.

instruction: What is Kangaroo Mother Care and how is it different from regular Kangaroo Care?
output: Kangaroo Mother Care makes the mother the primary caregiver, wearing the baby in a special wrap nearly twenty-four hours a day while the hospital provides backup care and expertise as needed. It was refined in African hospitals that lacked equipment, and babies receiving this approach did better when compared with babies receiving standard Western care.

instruction: Can holding my baby skin-to-skin really help with temperature more than an incubator?
output: Yes. Your body can warm and stabilize your baby’s temperature better than an incubator, and you will automatically adjust your own temperature to raise or lower your baby’s to his optimal level.

instruction: Is my breast milk different when my baby is premature?
output: Yes. Your milk is different from the milk of a mother whose baby was born full-term and contains more of the nutrients your preterm baby needs.

instruction: I'm worried about using formula — is it dangerous for a premature baby?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Formula, even special premature formulas, increases the risk of damage to a premature baby’s sensitive and immature digestive system and makes it more likely she’ll get infections and illnesses, especially necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC), a very serious condition that premature babies on formula are at high risk of developing.

instruction: What are milk fortifiers and why might they be added to my milk?
output: Milk fortifiers are added to the milk pumped by mothers of the most premature babies (under 1,500 grams at birth) to increase levels of protein and some other nutrients so the baby can gain weight more quickly during the early weeks. Attempts are under way to provide fortifiers that are human-milk-based rather than cow-milk-based to reduce the risk of allergies and illness. You can ask if they are available if you’re told your baby needs a fortifier.

instruction: How should I start building my milk supply in the first days after a preterm birth?
output: In the first few days when you are producing colostrum, hand expression will probably work better. You won’t produce large amounts at first, and that’s okay. When you notice your milk supply increasing, you can start using a breast pump. Frequent pumping is important because your future milk production is based on the amount of milk removed in these early days. If your baby is fed by nasogastric tube or bottle, it’s ideal to pump or hand-express before each feeding so the baby can have fresh, just-pumped milk. Any extra can be refrigerated or frozen for future feedings.

instruction: My baby is tiny and I can’t use all the milk I’m pumping now — should I pump less?
output: Many parents wonder about this. It’s better to overproduce in the beginning so you’ll have enough later; your little baby is going to grow fast and will need a full milk supply as she catches up.

instruction: If my baby is being fed by a nasogastric tube, when should I pump?
output: If your baby is being fed by a nasogastric tube or bottle, it’s ideal to pump or hand-express before each feeding so that your baby can have the fresh, just-pumped milk.

instruction: Will pumping less now affect how much milk I have later on?
output: Yes. Your future milk production is based on the amount of milk removed in the early days, so if you pump less now there will be less milk six weeks down the road. Frequent pumping is important to build supply.

instruction: Are many hospitals using Kangaroo Mother Care already?
output: Very few hospitals are even aware of Kangaroo Mother Care yet, but the idea is spreading.

instruction: Should I do hand expression after using a pump during the early weeks?
output: Yes. Remember to do a little hand expression after you pump during these early weeks; it could make a real difference.

instruction: Will Kangaroo Care help my baby breastfeed more easily?
output: Yes. Babies held skin-to-skin are more likely to breastfeed more easily, and your milk production increases.

instruction: My baby was born very small and the neonatal team said he might need a fortifier. What are fortifiers and are there different kinds?
output: Fortifiers are added to the milk you pump for very small babies to increase protein and some other nutrients so the baby can gain weight more quickly in the early weeks. Many fortifiers are cow-milk-based, but attempts are under way to offer human-milk-based fortifiers, which would reduce the risk of allergies and illness. You can ask if human-milk-based fortifiers are available if you’re told your baby needs one.

instruction: I just had a preterm baby. In the first few days, should I pump or hand-express my colostrum?
output: In the first few days when you are producing colostrum, hand expression will probably work better. You won’t produce large amounts at first, and that’s okay for a very small baby.

instruction: My baby is on a nasogastric tube. How often should I pump so she can have fresh milk at feedings?
output: If your baby is being fed by nasogastric tube or bottle, it’s ideal to pump or hand-express before each feeding so the baby can have the fresh, just-pumped milk. Any extra milk can be refrigerated or frozen for future feedings.

instruction: I keep seeing the milk I pump but my baby isn't using much yet. Is it really important to pump so much now?
output: Yes. Frequent pumping now helps build your future milk supply because future production is based on the amount of milk removed in these early days. It’s better to overproduce in the beginning so you’ll have enough later when your baby grows and needs a full supply. Remember to do a little hand expression after you pump and try breast compressions while pumping; both can help increase amounts.

instruction: I’m worried because my milk dropped for a day or two while my baby was in the NICU. Is that normal and what helps?
output: It’s common for mothers of preemies to see an occasional temporary dip in milk production, often related to stress. Breast compressions while pumping can be especially helpful during these times.

instruction: I’ve been pumping a lot but still don’t have enough milk. Are there other options to increase supply?
output: If you are consistently having trouble making as much milk as your baby needs even though you’re pumping at least eight times a day, an herb or medication (a galactagogue) might increase your production. Check your planned approach with a breastfeeding-knowledgeable doctor.

instruction: The hospital says my baby must take a bottle before trying the breast. Is that required?
output: Some hospitals have a firm policy that the baby must take bottles before trying breastfeeding or before discharge, but research doesn’t support this. Bottle-feeding is more stressful for a premature baby because a tiny baby has more trouble coordinating sucking and breathing with a bottle.

instruction: How can my preemie practice breastfeeding if she’s not ready for the full flow at my breast?
output: If the baby doesn’t seem ready to handle the flow from the breast, she can practice by nursing on your breast right after you’ve pumped; she’ll get a little milk in the process. If you are doing Kangaroo Care, the baby may also move to your breast and sometimes latch on without much help, which is an excellent sign of readiness.

instruction: Would a nipple shield help my premature baby latch? I’m not sure it’s safe.
output: A nipple shield can make it easier for some premature babies to latch and nurse effectively. Premies often lack the fat pads in their cheeks that help fill the mouth, so they must work harder to create suction and may pause often, which can make the nipple slip. With a nipple shield, the baby doesn’t need to work as hard when drinking or pausing. It’s worth trying to breastfeed without a shield first, but keep it in mind if your baby seems to need extra help.

instruction: When should I start using a breast pump after delivery of my preemie?
output: Start with hand expression for the first few days while you’re producing colostrum. When you notice your milk supply increasing, you can begin using a breast pump. Continue frequent pumping to build your supply and do a little hand expression after pumping during these early weeks.

instruction: I feel scared to ask for help with breastfeeding my premature baby—what if they tell me I can’t produce enough?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Many mothers find it hard to ask for help; one mother described feeling anxious and waiting two weeks to call a La Leche League Leader. That leader encouraged her and said she could breastfeed, which helped her keep trying and succeed.

instruction: Are breast compressions useful while I pump? How do they help?
output: Breast compressions while you pump can help increase the amounts of milk you remove. Doing a little hand expression after you pump can also make a real difference for your overall supply.

instruction: My premature baby was on a nasogastric tube and the nurses say he must take a bottle before he can try breastfeeding. Is that necessary?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Some hospitals have firm policies requiring a baby to take a bottle before breastfeeding or discharge, but the research doesn’t support that policy. Bottle-feeding is more stressful for a premature baby than breastfeeding, and a tiny baby often has more trouble coordinating sucking and breathing with a bottle. If the tube is removed (or wasn’t used), the baby can often practice nursing on the breast right after you’ve pumped and get a little milk while learning to latch.

instruction: Why do some hospitals keep premature babies on a strict three- or four-hour feeding schedule?
output: Hospitals often keep premature babies on a fairly rigid schedule of feedings every three or four hours with the goal of not tiring the baby too much. However, a baby who is in skin-to-skin contact with his mother will probably have the energy and interest to nurse more frequently.

instruction: Is bottle-feeding harder for a tiny premature baby than breastfeeding?
output: Yes. The text says bottle-feeding is more stressful for a premature baby than breastfeeding. A tiny baby has more trouble coordinating sucking and breathing with a bottle.

instruction: My baby seems not ready for the flow of milk at the breast. Can he still practice nursing?
output: Yes. If the baby doesn’t seem ready to handle the flow from the breast, he can practice by nursing on your breast right after you’ve pumped — he’ll get a little milk in the process.

instruction: I’ve heard about Kangaroo Care. How does it affect a premature baby’s readiness to breastfeed?
output: If you are doing Kangaroo Care (skin-to-skin contact), you may find that at some point your baby starts moving down your body toward your breast and may even latch on without much help. That is described as an excellent sign that your baby is developmentally ready to breastfeed.

instruction: Should we try a nipple shield if my premie has trouble latching?
output: Some babies will need more help, and sometimes a nipple shield can make it easier for the baby to latch on and nurse effectively. It’s worth trying to breastfeed without a shield first, but keep a shield in mind if your baby seems to need extra help.

instruction: My baby’s nipple keeps slipping away when he pauses. Why might that be happening?
output: Premature babies lack the fat pads in their cheeks that help fill the space in a full-term baby’s mouth, so they have to work harder to create enough suction and must pause often. With every pause your nipple can slip away. With a nipple shield in place, the baby doesn’t need to work as hard either drinking or pausing.

instruction: The nurse warned me not to breastfeed too much because it would tire my baby. Should I worry about that?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Nurses may warn that feeding schedules are intended to avoid tiring a premature baby, and hospitals often use a rigid schedule for that reason. At the same time, a baby in skin-to-skin contact will probably have the energy and interest to nurse more frequently, and some mothers find that keeping their milk production going allows breastfeeding to work out.

instruction: I’ve been pumping around the clock every three hours because my preemie was tiny. Is that what other mothers do?
output: In one described case, a mother whose baby was born nine weeks early and needed a respirator pumped every three hours around the clock and sometimes more so she’d have milk for him when he was ready to eat. Pumping also helped her feel like she was doing something for him.

instruction: If I keep my milk production up while the hospital wants a strict routine, will I still be able to breastfeed later?
output: Don’t be discouraged. The text says that if you keep your milk production going well, you can feel confident that you’ll be able to breastfeed even if you go along with hospital instructions to get home sooner.

instruction: Can a premature baby latch on the breast without much help during skin-to-skin contact?
output: Yes. During Kangaroo Care (skin-to-skin contact), a baby may move toward the breast and even latch on without much help — a sign the baby is developmentally ready to breastfeed.

instruction: The hospital staff is resisting my doing Kangaroo Care overnight. Should I go along with them so we can go home sooner?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. The text notes that some mothers decide to go along with hospital staff instructions in order to get home more quickly, and if that happens you shouldn’t be discouraged. Keeping your milk production going can help you breastfeed later.

instruction: My baby needed a respirator at first and his weight dropped. What helped the mother in the story prepare to breastfeed later?
output: In that story, the mother pumped right away and pumped every three hours around the clock so she would have milk when he was ready to eat. Pumping helped her feel like she was doing something for him. When he was breathing on his own she began Kangaroo Care and later moved toward breastfeeding.

instruction: We were using bottles in the hospital and came home partly bottle-feeding and partly breastfeeding. How did she switch to full breastfeeding?
output: She and her LLL Leader reviewed the situation and found she was pumping more milk than the baby could eat in a single day and that he was nursing well. She then tossed out the bottles and went to full-time breastfeeding.

instruction: Will using a nipple shield make my baby stop having to pause while nursing?
output: The text says that with a nipple shield in place, your baby doesn’t need to work as hard either drinking or pausing. It does not claim it will eliminate pauses entirely, but a shield can make it easier for the baby to nurse effectively.

instruction: I'm anxious about breastfeeding my premature baby at home — will he be able to nurse?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Expect to feel a bit anxious the first time; try a laid-back nursing position with you semireclining and baby tummy down on your chest. Premature babies may latch, take a couple of sucks, then fall asleep; that can be normal. If he doesn’t take much from the breast, he can be fed some milk through a tube while he rests and you can try again when he wakes. If he doesn’t take enough, you’ll need to pump after breastfeeding to remove milk and keep your supply up.

instruction: What positions can help my premie breastfeed more easily?
output: Try a laid-back nursing position with you semireclining and baby tummy down on your chest. The clutch hold can help: the baby is on his back next to your side, with your hand supporting his neck and the base of his head while he feeds. If you sit up, cup your hand under his chin to support chin and cheeks. Skin-to-skin holding also helps keep him warm and secure.

instruction: My baby latches, sucks a couple times, then falls asleep — should I worry?
output: Many parents wonder about this. It’s common for premature babies to take a few sucks and then fall asleep; they may be feeling very content. While he rests you can feed him some milk through a tube if needed, and try nursing again when he wakes. If he doesn’t take much or enough from the breast, you’ll need to pump after breastfeeding to remove milk and maintain your supply.

instruction: I’m pumping more milk than my baby drinks — can I stop using bottles and just breastfeed?
output: If you find you are pumping more milk than the baby can eat in a day and he is nursing well, some parents have switched to full-time breastfeeding and stopped bottles. One parent found she had plenty of milk, the baby nursed well, and she went to full-time breastfeeding.

instruction: What is breast compression and when should I use it?
output: Breast compression is gently squeezing your breast as the baby sucks to increase the flow of milk when the baby’s suck isn’t very strong. Be cautious using it if you have a generous milk supply, because too much milk can overwhelm a small baby; if you worry you might flood him, you can pump before offering the breast.

instruction: How will I know if my premature baby is getting enough milk?
output: Your doctor or midwife will monitor his weight gain closely. Wet and poopy diapers are not a reliable way of estimating whether a premature baby is getting enough.

instruction: My near-term baby is sleepy and tires during feeds — what can I do to help him breastfeed better?
output: Many parents wonder about this. For near-term babies, minimize stimulation (lights down, TV off, voices quiet, less handling) and keep your baby skin to skin to help him stay warm and secure. Consider the laid-back breastfeeding position for stability. If he sleeps longer than three hours before his due date, wake him for feedings. If you sit up, cup your hand under his chin to support his chin and cheeks. Use breast compression and massage during feeding to make nursing easier.

instruction: When should my premature baby start solids?
output: That’s up to your baby. The timing for starting solids is determined by the baby rather than a fixed schedule.

instruction: Is it okay to carry my premie in a sling or wrap?
output: Carrying him in a wrap designed for Kangaroo Mother Care or in a sling can help him grow faster by saving calories and keeping him in his natural habit. Because premature babies are so tiny, check with someone knowledgeable about carriers and premature babies before using one.

instruction: If my baby doesn’t take much from the breast, do I need to pump?
output: Yes. When he doesn’t take much or enough from the breast, you’ll need to pump after breastfeeding to remove the milk and keep your supply up.

instruction: What feeding pattern changes should I expect once we get home from the hospital?
output: Expect some transition time at home. Your baby will shift from infrequent feeds to more frequent ones, and you may need to initiate feedings at first. He may or may not need a little support from supplemental feeds initially.

instruction: Could having a very generous milk supply be a problem for a small baby?
output: Too much milk can be a bit overwhelming to a small baby. Be cautious with breast compressions if your supply is generous; if you feel you might flood him, you can pump before offering the breast.

instruction: What is the clutch hold and how do I use it?
output: The clutch hold positions the baby on his back next to your side, with your hand supporting his neck and the base of his head as he feeds. This can provide extra support for premature babies.

instruction: Will my premature baby have different sucking patterns than a full-term baby?
output: Yes. When he nurses, a premature baby will probably have more pauses and shorter sucking bursts than a full-term baby.

instruction: My baby was born at 37 weeks and seems sleepy and doesn't feed well. What problems do near-term babies often have and what can help breastfeeding go more smoothly?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Near-term babies (born between 36 and 39 weeks) often seem a bit immature: they can go from deep sleep to very fussy, have trouble tuning out noise, have jerky or inconsistent rooting and sucking reflexes, have difficulty coordinating sucking, swallowing, and breathing during breastfeeding, tire quickly during feeds, have low muscle tone and not enough cheek fat for stable feeding, and have trouble regulating their body temperature. To help: minimize stimulation (lights down, TV off, quiet voices, less handling); keep the baby skin to skin to keep him warm and secure; until he reaches his due date, wake him for feeds if he sleeps longer than three hours; consider the laid-back breastfeeding position for stability; if you sit up, cup your hand under his chin to support chin and cheeks; and use breast compression and massage during feeding to make nursing easier.

instruction: How long can my near-term baby sleep between feedings before I should wake him?
