instruction: While using the syringe suction device, the device fills with milk — what should I do?
output: If the syringe fills with milk while you are maintaining suction, pull back farther on the plunger to keep the suction up so the nipple stays drawn out.

instruction: If my nipple won’t evert at all, can I still breastfeed?
output: If your nipple won’t evert at all, breastfeeding may be more difficult, but it still only takes one nipple to breastfeed, so breastfeeding can still be possible.

instruction: Should I use a nipple shield for latching problems?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A nipple shield may help if nothing else works, but make sure you work closely with someone experienced with nipple shields so you use it effectively.

instruction: My nipples seem short only when I’m engorged — what can help?
output: Some nipples only seem short when you’re very engorged. In that case, try the Reverse Pressure Softening technique described in the engorgement section to help the nipple become more accessible.

instruction: How long might it take before my baby can nurse well?
output: Babies can begin nursing and nurse just fine months after they’re born. Time is on your side as nipples and the baby’s mouth can grow to match each other.

instruction: How do I remove the homemade suction device after using it?
output: To remove the suction device, depress the plunger enough to stop the suction, then quickly offer your breast to your baby.

instruction: Will pumping briefly before nursing help with latch problems?
output: Pumping briefly before nursing may help with making the nipple more available for your baby to latch.

instruction: My baby won't latch at the breast. Could a nipple nudge or a quick pump before nursing help?
output: It may help to try a nipple nudge to encourage a latch. Pumping briefly before nursing can also help. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: My nipples are flat and won't evert. Will that stop me from breastfeeding?
output: If your nipple won’t evert at all, breastfeeding may be more difficult, but other nipple shapes are common and it takes only one nipple to breastfeed.

instruction: One of my nipples is oval not round. How can my baby take it in his mouth?
output: If your nipple tip is oval instead of round, try having your baby take the longer dimension from corner to corner in his mouth.

instruction: My newborn's mouth seems too small to reach the milk. What should I do so they get fed and my supply stays up?
output: If your baby’s mouth is too small at first and can’t reach the milk ducts, he may not get much milk. Pump or hand-express to keep your milk supply up and keep him well fed until his mouth and your nipples grow to match.

instruction: What does a nipple shield look like and when might it help?
output: A nipple shield looks something like a hat made of silicone. It doesn’t move or recede when a baby tries to latch and can sometimes help a persistently non-latching baby, especially one used to bottle nipples, or a baby trying to latch onto an inverted nipple.

instruction: Can nipple shields help premature babies who are tired or weak?
output: Yes. A nipple shield can make it easier for premies who don’t have much energy or fat and muscle in their cheeks because it stays put when they pause, so less energy goes into holding everything in place.

instruction: My nipples hurt. Will using a nipple shield fix nipple pain?
output: A nipple shield is often suggested as a solution for nipple pain but isn’t usually helpful.

instruction: How do I get the right size and fit for a nipple shield?
output: Experiment to see what size works best; premies may do best with the smallest size that’s comfortable. You can try turning the shield almost inside out so the stiff nipple section is folded back, place it so your nipple is closer to the shield’s tip, spread the rim into place (moistening underneath may help it stick), and wiggle the nipple section to pop it out for a better fit.

instruction: Is there a trick to getting some milk into the shield tip before the baby latches?
output: If you compress your breast a few times you may be able to add a few drops to the space in the tip so the baby finds instant reward when he starts to suck.

instruction: My baby seems to remove milk poorly when using the shield. What should I do?
output: Babies sometimes remove milk poorly with a nipple shield for the same reasons they can’t use a breast well (for example, a tongue-tie) or simply because it’s an unnatural intervention. You may need to pump afterward to drain the breast thoroughly and to supplement. Keep an eye on your baby’s weight.

instruction: How can I tell I don’t need to pump after nursing with a shield?
output: If you hear good swallowing and your breast softens well in response to nursing, you may not need to pump after nursing with the shield.

instruction: I’m worried my baby will refuse the breast without a shield. Is that a real risk?
output: One significant risk of using nipple shields is that babies can become so used to them that they won’t nurse without them. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: How can I wean my baby off a nipple shield?
output: Try removing the shield after the baby is fed, dozing, and still contentedly sucking so he may open his mouth and search for the nipple. You can also try removing the shield in the middle of a nursing session at a calm time; if he gets upset, give him the shield and try again later. With an older baby you may need to wait a day or two between tries.

instruction: My baby likes the bland taste of the shield. Could the taste of my skin bother him when I take it away?
output: A baby who is used to the bland taste of the silicone shield may be bothered by the taste of your skin at first. Try rinsing your nipple and areola.

instruction: Can I try switching to the breast in the middle of a feeding if my baby is latched to the shield?
output: Yes; try removing the shield in the middle of a nursing session during a calm time. If he starts to get upset, give him what he wants and try again later.

instruction: Can I put a few drops of milk into the tip of a nipple shield to encourage my baby to suck?
output: You may be able to add a few drops to the space in the tip so your baby gets an instant reward when he starts to suck.

instruction: How will I know if my baby is actually getting milk when nursing with a shield?
output: Listen for good swallowing and notice whether your breast softens in response to nursing; also keep an eye on your baby’s weight.

instruction: Can a baby get used to a nipple shield and refuse the breast without it?
output: Yes. Babies can become so used to nipple shields that they won’t nurse without them.

instruction: What are gentle ways to help my baby stop using a nipple shield?
output: It can help to remove the shield after the baby is fed, dozing, and contentedly sucking so he may search for the nipple. Try removing the shield in the middle of a nursing session at a calm time; if he gets upset, give him the shield and try again later. With an older baby you may need to wait a day or two between tries. Rinse your nipple and areola and coat them with your milk if the baby seems bothered by the change in taste. Wait for a time when the baby looks different and try then. Do not cut away the tip of the shield.

instruction: Is it safe to trim or cut away the tip of a silicone nipple shield a little at a time?
output: No. Do not cut away the tip of a nipple shield, because that can leave a sharp edge on a silicone shield.

instruction: My baby suddenly refuses the breast — what might cause a nursing strike?
output: It could be physical, such as a stuffy nose or an earache, or emotional, such as a move, a new job, being startled while nursing, or a change in routine. Changes in lotion, perfume, deodorant, or detergent, a low supply or change in milk taste, contact or negotiated nursings, too many bottles or pacifiers, or oversupply problems may also contribute. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: During a nursing strike, should I start giving lots of other drinks and foods so the baby doesn't go hungry?
output: Don’t be too quick to give lots of other drinks and foods; the baby may just need a little time. Pump or hand-express your milk to maintain supply and to help prevent plugged ducts or mastitis. If the strike continues for more than a day, you could offer expressed milk in a cup but avoid giving bottles if you can so his sucking urge will encourage him toward breastfeeding. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: Will some babies still nurse at night during a nursing strike?
output: Yes. Many babies on strike will continue to nurse, reluctantly or as always, at night; some babies won’t nurse at all.

instruction: What practical things can I try to get my baby to latch during a nursing strike?
output: Try nursing the baby in his sleep. Sing or rock with your shirt open and see if he’ll relax enough to latch. Nurse somewhere different — walking, in the car, at a friend’s house, outside, or in a warm bath. Offer a whole new position, for example sitting facing you or holding him vertical against your shoulder. Try a gentle dance or baby bounce, starting small and getting bouncier while holding him in a nursing position (stop if he doesn’t like it). You can also try not offering for a day to let tensions cool, then keep your shirt up and breast available for a day without pushing the issue. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: If my baby doesn't remove milk well with a shield, do I need to pump afterward?
output: If your baby removes milk poorly with a shield, you may need to pump afterward to drain the breast thoroughly and to supplement. If you hear good swallowing and your breast softens well in response to nursing with the shield, you may not need to pump. Keep an eye on your baby’s weight.

instruction: Can a change in my lotion or detergent make my baby refuse to nurse?
output: Yes. A change in lotion, perfume, deodorant, or detergent can contribute to a nursing strike.

instruction: If the nursing strike lasts more than a day, is it okay to give expressed milk in a bottle?
output: If the strike continues for more than a day, you could offer expressed milk in a cup but avoid giving bottles if you can so that his sucking urge will encourage him toward breastfeeding.

instruction: Is it okay to try removing the nipple shield in the middle of a nursing session?
output: Yes. Try removing the shield in the middle of a session at a calm time; if he starts to get upset, give him what he wants and try again later.

instruction: My baby used to like the shield but now seems bothered by the taste of my skin — what can I do?
output: Try rinsing your nipple and areola; a baby used to the bland taste of silicone may be bothered at first by your skin. Coat your nipple and areola with your milk to make them more enticing.

instruction: Could oversupply problems make my baby more likely to go on a nursing strike?
output: Yes. Babies who deal with oversupply problems may be especially prone to a nursing strike.

instruction: My baby stopped nursing after being stuffy but her nose is clear now. Should I give her formula or let her wait it out?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Don’t be too quick to give the baby lots of other drinks and foods to make up for the milk he’s missing. He’s probably sturdy and may just need a little time. Meanwhile, pump or hand‑express your milk to maintain your supply and to prevent plugged ducts or mastitis.

instruction: How long should I wait before giving expressed milk when my baby is refusing the breast?
output: If the strike continues for more than a day, you could offer your expressed milk in a cup. Try to avoid giving bottles if you can so his sucking urge will encourage him toward breastfeeding.

instruction: What gentle ways can I try to get my baby to start nursing again after a refusal?
output: Try nursing the baby in his sleep. Sing or rock with your shirt open and see if he’ll relax enough to latch. Nurse somewhere different — while walking, sitting in the car, at a friend’s house, outside, or in a warm bath. Offer a whole new position, for example have him sit facing you or hold him against your shoulder in a vertical position. Do a small dance or baby bounce while holding him in a nursing position and stop if he doesn’t like it. You can also try not offering for a day to let tensions cool, then keep your shirt up and breast available without pushing. Sometimes repeating whatever works for a few days helps.

instruction: Is it okay to try nursing while my baby is sleeping?
output: Yes. Nursing the baby in his sleep is probably the most commonly helpful suggestion for a nursing strike.

instruction: Should I pump while my baby refuses the breast?
output: Yes. Do pump or hand‑express your milk during this time both to maintain your milk supply and to prevent plugged ducts or mastitis.

instruction: My baby spits up a lot, has green frothy stools and a rash. Could my oversupply cause this?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Those are common signs of an overabundant milk supply (oversupply or overactive letdown). Oversupply can lead to frequent spit‑up, frothy green stools that may cause diaper rashes, fussiness at the breast, and changes in diapers from yellow to green or mucousy.

instruction: What causes gas and frothy green stools if I'm breastfeeding?
output: Lactose in the milk is broken down by the enzyme lactase, which is produced on tiny finger‑like projections in the baby’s small intestine. If there’s so much milk that the lactose overwhelms the lactase, or if those tiny projections are temporarily damaged, the lactose can ferment and cause gas and frothy green stools.

instruction: I just saw a small streak of blood in my baby’s stool. Is that dangerous?
output: It makes sense to feel worried. Small amounts of bleeding can happen with oversupply and intestinal irritation; intestines are sensitive but tough, and small injuries to an intestine are described as no more serious than a scratch on another part of the body.

instruction: Why does the milk sometimes seem low in fat at the start of a feeding and fattier later on?
output: As milk is made, much of the fat stays near the milk‑making cells while the rest flows toward the nipple with the baby’s sucking. If the baby hasn’t nursed for a couple of hours, lower‑fat milk collects near the nipple and is drunk first; as the baby continues sucking, more fat is brought down and the milk becomes fattier later in the feeding.

instruction: My baby nurses eagerly until my milk releases, then arches away like she’s upset. Could that be oversupply?
output: Yes. A common sign of an overabundant milk supply is that the baby nurses eagerly until milk releases, then arches away as if angry. Oversupply is fixable and may explain other feeding difficulties you’re seeing.

instruction: Are there any unusual tricks that might help break a nursing strike?
output: Some approaches people try include nursing in the baby’s sleep; nursing while walking or in a warm bath; offering new positions (for example holding the baby vertically against your shoulder); doing a gentle dance or baby bounce; not offering for a day to let tensions cool and then keeping the breast available without pushing; and one reported method is sitting in an office chair, being spun gently until slightly dizzy, then offering the breast. Whatever works may need repeating for a few days.

instruction: If I have too much milk, how will reducing my supply help my baby?
output: If you can drop your milk supply somewhat, your baby won’t get full before she gets the fattier milk she needs. That will slow her digestion and give her lactase more time to work, which can reduce fermentation, gas, and frothy stools.

instruction: My baby seems to bob on and off the breast and rarely nurses to sleep. Could that mean my milk supply is too high?
output: Yes. Babies who bob on and off the breast, seldom nurse to sleep, or pull off spluttering are common signs of an overabundant milk supply or overactive letdown.

instruction: My baby's stools are frothy and green and he seems gassy — what could be causing this?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If your milk supply is very large, your baby may get so much milk that the lactose overwhelms the lactase enzyme in her small intestine, or the tiny finger-like projections that produce lactase may be temporarily damaged. When lactose isn't broken down it can ferment, causing gas and frothy green stools and sometimes diaper rashes. Small amounts of bleeding can also occur if those intestinal projections are irritated.

instruction: How does the fat content in breastmilk change during a feeding?
output: As milk is made, much of the fat stays near the milk-making cells while lower-fat milk flows down the ducts first. As the baby continues to suck, more of the fat is pulled down and the fat level rises. After nursing, the fat gradually drifts back up away from the nipple until the next feeding.

instruction: What is block nursing and how can it help with oversupply?
output: Block nursing means nursing on the same side repeatedly until that breast is softened before switching to the other side. This lets the baby tap the higher-fat milk that lingers after a feeding and can slow production on the side you aren't using, which can help quiet an oversupply.

instruction: Can I just nurse on one side to reduce an overly generous milk supply?
output: Yes. You can nurse on just one side at a time and only nurse briefly on the other side if you are uncomfortable and need to relieve pressure. Staying on the first breast when the baby wants comfort nursing also lets the baby get the higher-fat milk that remains and helps slow production on the unused side.

instruction: If I switch to formula will that stop the blood in my baby's stool?
output: Switching to formula is not a solution for stool blood. Formula-fed babies often have blood in their stool, whether visible or not, so changing to formula does not necessarily remove the problem.

instruction: My baby seems to choke on the fast flow of milk — are there positions that can help?
output: Try shifting position. Leaning back (a laid-back position) can turn a fast "fire hose" flow into more of a fountain. Sitting up while hugging your baby's back and shoulders tips her head back and can give milk a straighter shot that some babies tolerate. Nursing lying down is another position some babies prefer when there is oversupply.

instruction: If my baby has a rash or blood in her stool, what foods should I try removing first?
output: If there is a rash or blood indicating an irritated intestine, consider removing potential irritants one step at a time from both your and your baby's diet, moving to the next step only if the first isn't enough: 1) dairy and soy products, 2) artificial colors and preservatives, 3) anything you might have binged on during pregnancy.

instruction: My baby is growing well but still has green frothy diapers and occasional blood — should I be very worried?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby's growth and behavior are fine but diapers remain green or frothy or there is occasional blood, most doctors say you can ignore minor bleeding; it can take a long time to disappear.

instruction: Why does milk sometimes come out fast at the start of a feeding and slow down later?
output: When a breast has been full, the milk that collects in the ducts flows first and is lower in fat. As the baby continues to suck, more fat is pulled down and the milk becomes higher in fat, so the character of the milk changes during the feeding.

instruction: How can I reduce my milk supply so my baby gets more of the fatty milk?
output: You can try nursing on one side each time and using block nursing so the baby softens one breast thoroughly before using the other. Nursing frequently so there isn't a backlog of milk can help. If oversupply is severe, you might begin block nursing after expressing both sides thoroughly so you start with softer breasts.

instruction: What might cause small amounts of bleeding in my baby's diaper?
output: Small amounts of bleeding can occur if the tiny finger-like projections in the baby's small intestine are damaged while irritated. The text notes intestines are sensitive but tough and small injuries can cause visible blood in the stool.

instruction: I've heard sage can help with oversupply — does it?
output: Some mothers have had success by adding the herb sage to their diets. The text also notes certain medications can help and suggests talking to your tech support person about other possibilities.

instruction: Why do some babies get bothered by a strong milk flow even if the supply seems modest?
output: Some women have very small storage capacity, which can create a lot of force behind even a modest milk supply. Those babies are more likely to be bothered by the fast flow than by irritated intestines.

instruction: Will breastfeeding less often prevent oversupply with future babies?
output: We tend to have more milk with each baby, so oversupply is more likely with each baby. Nursing for any old reason at any old time and not using a second side unless one of you wants to can help prevent or solve most oversupply issues.

instruction: My baby wants to nurse for comfort between feedings — should I let her switch to the other breast?
output: If your baby wants to nurse for comfort before the next full feeding, staying on the first breast lets her tap into the higher-fat milk that lingers after a nursing and also helps slow milk production on the side you aren't using.

instruction: My baby seems to be getting too much milk and keeps coughing while feeding. Could nursing lying down help with oversupply?
output: Nursing lying down can help some babies when there’s oversupply. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: My baby has green, frothy diapers and a little blood sometimes. Should I stop breastfeeding and switch to formula?
output: If your baby’s growth and behavior are fine but diapers are green or frothy or there’s occasional blood, most doctors say you can ignore minor bleeding because it can take a long time to disappear. Formula-fed babies often have blood in their stool too, so switching to formula isn’t a solution.

instruction: What foods should I try removing to see if my baby’s intestine is irritated from my milk?
output: Try removing potential irritants one step at a time from both your and your baby’s diet, not moving to the next step unless the first step isn’t enough. The suggested steps are: 1) dairy and soy products; 2) artificial colors and preservatives; 3) anything you might have binged on during pregnancy.

instruction: Someone told me sage helps with oversupply. Is that mentioned as an option?
output: Some mothers have had success by adding the herb sage to their diets.

instruction: I have severe oversupply. How should I start block nursing to try to reduce my supply?
output: If oversupply is severe, you might start block nursing by expressing both sides thoroughly so you begin with two breasts as softened as you can make them. You may need to repeat this several times before your supply begins to settle down.

instruction: My nipple is pierced and I’m worried it’s affecting feeding. What problems can nipple or areola piercings cause?
output: Nipple and areola piercings are the only types that could be problematic for nursing. Nipple piercings don’t usually affect milk production but can increase leaking by adding outlets. Areolar piercings can damage the nerves that affect milk ejection. Both can cause milk-ejection problems if scarring blocks milk flow.

instruction: Do I need to leave my nipple jewelry in during feedings to keep the hole open?
output: No. Jewelry left in place during nursing is a choking hazard and can affect latching or harm the soft tissues in the baby’s mouth. Remove nipple and areola rings or bars during feedings.

instruction: I want to keep my pierced holes open between feedings. Is there a safe option mentioned?
output: Some mothers use insertion tapers or PTFE (Teflon) barbells between feedings to keep the holes open.

instruction: If there’s scarring from an areola piercing and milk doesn’t let down well, what can help release milk?
output: If scarring has decreased your nerve response, you can use breast massage and compressions in addition to other milk release techniques.

instruction: I’m thinking of getting my nipples re-pierced after weaning. When is it best to do that?
output: If the piercing’s closed and you want it re-pierced, it’s best to wait about three months after weaning to let the old holes heal completely.

instruction: I found a firm, tender spot on my breast. Could this be a plugged duct and what might cause it?
output: A firm, tender spot can be a plugged duct. Plugs can result from milk not being taken out—for example if your baby suddenly sleeps through the night or you miss a feeding—or from pressure on the ducts from a bra that’s too snug or a baby carrier worn for a long period. Sometimes the cause is never learned.

instruction: How big can a plugged duct be?
output: A plugged duct may be the size of a marble or the size of your hand, depending on whether the plug is fairly far upstream (small blockage) or downstream (backing up milk from a broader area).

instruction: Could a plugged duct lead to something worse?
output: A plugged duct can result in mastitis, go along with mastitis, or be nothing more than an isolated plug.

instruction: I’ve had more milk with this baby than the last. Is that normal and does it affect oversupply risk?
output: We tend to have more milk with each baby, so oversupply tends to be more likely with each baby.

instruction: I feel bad because I keep trying to limit my milk supply and it’s stressing me out. Is it okay to stop worrying and just enjoy nursing?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Sometimes we get too caught up in trying to limit our milk supplies. Sharing baths, co-sleeping, and nursing just for the pleasure of it can all be part of getting the happy times back.

instruction: I have a firm, tender spot on my breast. What might that be and how worried should I be?
output: It could be a plugged duct: a firm, tender spot that can be as small as a marble or as large as your hand. Plugs can come from milk not being removed (for example if a feed is missed or your baby sleeps through the night), or from pressure on the ducts (a too-snug bra or a baby carrier). A plugged duct can lead to mastitis, occur with mastitis, or be an isolated plug. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What can I do at home to help a plugged duct get better?
output: The goal is to get the milk moving again and keep the breast soft. Nurse or express as often as you can. Try nursing with your baby’s chin pointed toward the plug and use a position where your breast can dangle so gravity helps (for example, baby on his back with the back of his head near an edge and you leaning over on your elbows). Avoid wearing a bra, especially underwires, or try a different style. Apply heat with a warm washcloth or heating pad right before nursing or expressing. You can also try massaging the area with an electric toothbrush or massager, or use techniques described for mastitis such as the bag of marbles technique.

instruction: Someone suggested lecithin for recurring plugged ducts. What dose is mentioned and how does it work?
output: The text mentions taking 2 tablespoons of lecithin daily or one 1,200 mg capsule three to four times a day. Lecithin helps break up fat particles and keep them from clumping; after a few weeks of daily lecithin some mothers with chronic plugged ducts find the plugs stop and it may be necessary to continue it to prevent return.

instruction: Can a tight bra cause a plugged duct?
output: Yes. Pressure on the ducts from a bra that’s too snug (including underwires) can cause a plugged duct, as can prolonged pressure from a baby carrier.

instruction: My baby usually feeds fine but suddenly slept through the night and now I have a lump. Could that be related?
output: Yes. Plugs can result from milk not being taken out, for example if your baby suddenly sleeps through the night or you miss a feeding. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Are there medical or professional treatments mentioned for breaking up a plugged duct?
output: Yes. The excerpt mentions using a physiotherapist for a painless ultrasound treatment to break up the plug and also using an electric toothbrush or massager against the plug.

instruction: How big can a plugged duct be?
output: A plugged duct can be as small as a marble or as large as your hand, depending on how far upstream or downstream the blockage is.

instruction: If I wean and want my nipple re-pierced, when should I wait to re-pierce?
output: If the piercings are close and you want them re-pierced, it’s best to wait about three months after weaning to let the old holes heal completely.

instruction: What are the common symptoms of postpartum depression I should look for?
output: Common symptoms include a persistent sad, anxious, or empty mood; sleeping too much or too little; reduced appetite and weight loss or increased appetite and weight gain; loss of interest or pleasure in activities including sex; restlessness, irritability, or excessive crying; persistent physical symptoms such as headaches, digestive problems, or chronic pain; difficulty concentrating, remembering, or making decisions; feeling guilty, hopeless, worthless, helpless, or pessimistic; and fatigue or loss of energy.

instruction: When does postpartum depression usually start and how long does it last?
output: Postpartum depression can hit anytime within the first year but tends to start by about ten weeks postpartum. It usually lasts at least a couple of weeks.

instruction: How common is postpartum depression?
output: Postpartum depression may strike as many as 25 percent of women in Western cultures.

instruction: Are there factors that make postpartum depression more likely?
output: Yes. It’s more common in women who have suffered depression in the past, and may be more likely following a traumatic birth, with mother-baby separation or breastfeeding problems, and in societies where new mothers have little family and cultural support.

instruction: I’m having thoughts about hurting myself or my baby. What should I do?
output: If you are thinking about death, suicide, or harming your baby, don’t wait. Get medical help right away. It makes sense to feel alarmed by these thoughts.

instruction: Can a plugged duct lead to other breast problems?
output: Yes. A plugged duct can result in mastitis, can go along with mastitis, or it may be an isolated plug.

instruction: Are some women more prone to plugged ducts for biological reasons?
output: Some women may be more prone to plugged ducts because of the particular blend of fats in their milk.

instruction: What are the common symptoms of postpartum depression?
output: Persistent sad, anxious, or empty mood; sleeping too much or too little; reduced appetite and weight loss or increased appetite and weight gain; loss of interest or pleasure in activities (including sex); restlessness, irritability, or excessive crying; persistent physical symptoms such as headaches, digestive problems, or chronic pain; difficulty concentrating, remembering, or making decisions; feeling guilty, hopeless, worthless, helpless, or pessimistic; and fatigue or loss of energy.

instruction: When does postpartum depression usually start and how long can it last?
output: Postpartum depression can start anytime within the first year but tends to begin by about ten weeks postpartum. It usually lasts at least a couple of weeks.

instruction: I've been feeling guilty and hopeless since my baby was born. Could this be postpartum depression and what increases the risk?
output: Feeling guilty and hopeless are listed symptoms of postpartum depression. It is more common in women who have suffered depression in the past, and may be more likely after a traumatic birth, with mother-baby separation, with breastfeeding problems, or in societies where new mothers have little family and cultural support. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Can continuing to breastfeed help if I'm depressed after having a baby?
output: Continued breastfeeding is described as one of the best things you can do to reduce or avoid depression and is also the best thing for your baby if you do become depressed. In addition, babies of depressed mothers who bottle-feed are said to be more likely to have developmental challenges than babies of depressed mothers who breastfeed. In mild cases, support, friendship, and help with breastfeeding may resolve the problem.

instruction: Are antidepressant medications compatible with breastfeeding?
output: Most antidepressants are compatible with breastfeeding. The excerpt recommends having your caregiver check Hale's Medications and Mothers' Milk for more information, and notes that your physician may suggest medication if you still feel sad.

instruction: What is D-MER and how does it differ from postpartum depression?
output: D-MER (Dysphoric Milk Ejection Reflex) is described as a little-recognized glitch where affected women feel brief unpleasant emotions only when their milk releases. The feelings (worthless, hopeless, useless, nausea, inability to concentrate) happen at let-down and then resolve within about a minute; the mother is otherwise fine and in love with her baby. Many women experience it and it is distinct from ongoing postpartum depression.

instruction: When I nurse I suddenly feel horrible but it goes away quickly—could that be D-MER?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Those brief, intense negative feelings that happen only when your milk releases and then pass within a minute match the description of D-MER.

instruction: If my unhappiness only happens during nursing and disappears otherwise, should I be treated for depression?
output: D-MER causes feelings only when milk releases and they resolve quickly; many affected women do not want to be treated for depression because their unhappiness is limited to nursing. There are treatments some women have found and described on resources such as the D-MER website, and some women have been able to continue nursing long term.

instruction: What are signs that my baby might have gastroesophageal reflux?
output: Signs of reflux in a baby can include frequent swallowing between feedings; arching when nursing; pulling on and off the breast as if they want to nurse but it causes pain; fussing in a car seat, when bent in the middle, or when lying flat; and salivating or having near-constant congestion or rumbly breathing.

instruction: How does reflux happen in babies?
output: Reflux occurs when the valve between the stomach and esophagus opens or does not close completely and stomach contents and acids rise up into the esophagus. All humans have reflux many times a day and babies have it more often. Not all reflux is noticeable or uncomfortable, but when it is severe the acids rising from the stomach into the esophagus can be painful.

instruction: My baby arches and pulls off the breast frequently. Could that be reflux and what should I watch for?
output: Those behaviors are listed among reflux signs: arching when nursing and pulling on and off the breast as if wanting to nurse but experiencing pain. Other reflux signs include frequent swallowing between feedings, fussing when bent or in a car seat, salivating, or near-constant congestion or rumbly breathing. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Is there anything that can help keep a baby's stomach contents down?
output: Because the opening from the stomach to the esophagus is usually on the right side, anything that puts the right side higher than the left side may keep stomach contents down better.

instruction: How common is postpartum depression?
output: Postpartum depression may strike as many as 25 percent of women in Western cultures.

instruction: I'm having thoughts about harming myself and I'm scared—what should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you are thinking about death, suicide, or harming your baby, don't wait. Get medical help right away. These thoughts are not typical of postpartum depression.

instruction: I sometimes get sudden negative feelings while breastfeeding, but otherwise I love my baby. Could this be D-MER and am I alone?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If you suspect you have D‑MER, you are not alone. One mother who experienced it set up the website d‑mer.org and several hundred women responded with similar experiences. We don’t know much yet about D‑MER, but that site also describes several treatments that helped her, and she was able to nurse her child into toddlerhood.

instruction: My baby arches his back and pulls on and off the breast. Could these be signs of reflux?
output: These can be signs of reflux. Other symptoms of reflux in a baby listed include frequent swallowing between feedings, pulling on and off the breast as if nursing causes pain, fussing in a car seat or when bent in the middle or lying flat, and salivating or having near‑constant congestion or rumbly breathing.

instruction: How can I hold my baby to help reduce reflux during and after feeds?
output: Anything that puts the baby’s right side higher than the left can help keep stomach contents down. Nursing so that the baby’s right side is higher (for example, holding the baby in your left arm) can be soothing. The Magic Baby Hold is noted as helpful because it raises the baby’s right side. Sleeping with the baby on his left side and considering a wedge under the mattress to keep his head higher than his feet are also mentioned. Minimize time in a car seat because that position puts pressure on the baby’s stomach.

instruction: Is it helpful to thicken my baby’s feeds or use simethicone drops for reflux?
output: Research does not support thickened feeds or simethicone drops for reflux. The text notes that there are medications that can help, but thickening feeds and simethicone are not supported by the research cited.

instruction: When should I ask a doctor about my baby’s reflux symptoms?
output: If your baby’s symptoms persist or he seems particularly bothered, you can ask your doctor to evaluate him for reflux and to refer you to a gastroenterologist.

instruction: My nipples are very sore and it hurts to nurse. Does breastfeeding always have to hurt?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Breastfeeding does not always have to hurt. The most common cause of sore nipples in the early days is the baby taking the breast poorly, which can be due to position, a tense baby, or temporary effects from birth medications. Engorgement can also lead to sore nipples by preventing a good mouthful and causing a shallow latch. Other causes include blebs, mastitis, tongue‑tie, thrush, early pregnancy, and skin conditions such as eczema.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby’s latch is causing my nipple pain?
output: If the baby is attaching poorly, you will probably see a distinct change in the shape of your nipple when it leaves his mouth: it may look creased, have a white stripe, or have one side flattened. That distortion suggests the latch is the problem.

instruction: What can I try at home to fix sore nipples caused by a poor latch or engorgement?
output: You can try a laid‑back nursing position and one of the more mother‑controlled latches. Letting the baby work on getting a good latch in a relaxed setting, such as spending a day or half a day in bed, may help. If the nipple remains distorted, check for tongue‑tie. Also, hand‑express a little milk after feeding and let it dry on your nipples; your milk has anti‑inflammatory and anti‑bacterial properties that can help heal nipple wounds.

instruction: My baby fusses a lot in his car seat after feeding. Could that be reflux and what should I do about the car seat?
output: Fussing in a car seat is listed as a possible symptom of reflux, because that position can put pressure on the baby’s stomach. The text advises minimizing time spent in a car seat to reduce that pressure.

instruction: Does every baby who has reflux show obvious symptoms or pain?
output: No. All humans have reflux many times a day and babies have it more often than adults. Not all reflux is noticeable and not all reflux makes the baby uncomfortable. But when reflux is severe, the acids rising from the stomach into the esophagus can be painful.

instruction: Why does holding the baby with the right side higher help reduce reflux?
output: The opening from the stomach to the esophagus is usually on the right side, so putting the baby’s right side higher than the left may help keep stomach contents down.

instruction: My nipples hurt but they don’t change shape after nursing. What else could be causing the pain?
output: If the nipple isn’t distorted when it leaves the baby’s mouth, the pain may be from other causes such as blebs, mastitis, tongue‑tie, thrush, early pregnancy, or skin conditions like eczema. These skin issues can cause nipple pain even without visible distortion.

instruction: Can my breast milk help treat nipple wounds or infections?
output: Yes. Hand‑express a little milk after the feeding and let it dry on your nipples. The milk’s anti‑inflammatory and anti‑bacterial properties can help heal nipple wounds and other skin and even eye infections, which is a reason to learn hand expression.

instruction: My baby pulls on and off the breast and seems to salivate a lot. Are these signs I should consider reflux evaluation?
output: Yes. Pulling on and off the breast as if nursing causes pain and salivating or near‑constant congestion or rumbly breathing are listed symptoms of reflux. If symptoms persist or the baby seems particularly bothered, you can ask a doctor to evaluate him and consider a gastroenterology referral.

instruction: Someone told me nipple pain is normal and I just have to wait for nipples to toughen. Is that accurate?
output: That’s not accurate. The text states that breastfeeding does not always have to hurt and nipple or breast pain is a message that something needs fixing rather than a constant companion. Addressing the cause—such as latch problems, engorgement, or other conditions—is important.

instruction: My nipples are sore and look creased after feeds. What might be causing this and what can I try?
output: Many parents wonder about this. A creased nipple after feeding usually means the baby’s attachment is distorting the nipple. You may see creasing, a white stripe, or one side flattened when the nipple leaves the baby’s mouth. If the nipple is distorted, try the laid-back nursing position and one of the more mother-controlled latches. You might also spend a day or half a day in bed letting your baby work on getting a good latch in a relaxed setting. If the nipple is still distorted, check whether tongue-tie could be involved.

instruction: Can engorgement cause sore nipples even if my baby can latch?
output: Yes. Engorgement can lead to sore nipples when the breast is too full for the baby to get a good mouthful; if he can latch at all the latch may be very shallow.

instruction: My nipples hurt but they don’t look distorted when my baby unlatches. What else could cause the pain?
output: If the nipple isn’t distorted, skin problems can be the cause. Less common causes include eczema. Blebs, mastitis, tongue-tie, and thrush can also cause sore nipples.

instruction: When should I use purified lanolin on my nipples and how does it help?
output: Purified lanolin helps keep the cells’ own moisture intact. A healthy nipple doesn’t need ointment, but if your nipple is chapped or slightly damaged, a light coating of purified lanolin can keep newly formed cells from drying out and allow them to slide across the damaged area to bridge the gap more quickly. This moist wound healing is effective only if you also address what caused the damage.

instruction: Is it okay to wash an injured nipple with soap and water?
output: Yes. A nipple wound is like any other skin wound and can be an opening for bacteria, so washing an injured nipple with a mild soap and water several times a day makes sense. There’s no need to wash healthy nipples.

instruction: Can I put over-the-counter antibiotic ointment on my sore nipples and will the baby get it?
output: Over-the-counter antibiotic ointments stay in place better than creams and may be less likely to cause irritation while protecting against infection. A vanishingly small amount applied two or three times a day after nursing will be gone by the time your baby nurses again, so there’s no need to wash it off. If you want a moist wound effect and to hold the ointment in place, you can put plastic wrap over the nipple after applying ointment, but be sure to remove the plastic before nursing.

instruction: Are hydrogel pads worth trying for sore nipples?
output: Hydrogels designed for nipples feel cool and comfortable, don’t stick to wounds, and help protect the nipple’s moisture balance and from bumps. They’re fairly expensive and can be used only a few days before they become waterlogged with milk and must be replaced. Some mothers find them extremely helpful; others don’t.

instruction: Can breast shells help protect very sore nipples?
output: Breast shells can act as armor for very sore nipples and were originally designed to help flat or inverted nipples stand out. However, they tend to hold external moisture in, which can slow healing. Don’t wear them at night because they can slide away and cause further damage. Wash and dry them frequently so they don’t collect bacteria. You can use one when nursing to keep the baby from kicking a sore nipple, but leave them off as much as possible to speed healing.

instruction: Could sore nipples be an early sign of pregnancy?
output: Yes. Sore nipples can be an early sign of pregnancy.

instruction: I’m worried my baby isn’t getting enough milk. Will giving a supplement reduce my milk supply?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Giving unnecessary supplements (extra milk besides nursing) is a sure recipe for decreasing your milk production because your baby will fill up on the supplement and take less of your milk. If the baby truly isn’t getting enough milk and the reason can’t be eliminated quickly, he will need extra milk so he can grow as he should. While you’re working on fixing any breastfeeding problem, it’s important to make sure the baby gets enough milk, even if that means a supplement for a while. Before you begin supplementing, it can be helpful to check with an LLL Leader or another tech support person.

instruction: What short-term steps can I try at home to help my baby get a better latch?
output: Try the laid-back nursing position and one of the more mother-controlled latches. You might also spend a day or half a day in bed letting your baby work on getting a good latch in a relaxed setting. If the nipple remains distorted after these steps, consider whether tongue-tie could be involved.

instruction: If my nipple has a white stripe after feeding, what does that mean?
output: A white stripe when the nipple leaves the baby’s mouth is a sign that the baby’s attachment is distorting the nipple. That points to a latch problem you can try to improve with different positions or mother-controlled latches, and if it persists you may want to look at tongue-tie as a possible factor.

instruction: Can my milk help heal nipple wounds?
output: Yes. Hand-expressing a little milk after the feeding and letting it dry on your nipples can help. Your milk has anti-inflammatory and anti-bacterial properties and can help heal nipple wounds and other skin and even eye infections.

instruction: If my nipple pain comes from skin problems rather than latch, what tools might help?
output: If the pain relates to skin problems, tools that may help include hand-expressed milk left to dry on the nipple, purified lanolin for chapped or slightly damaged nipples, washing an injured nipple with mild soap and water several times a day, hydrogels designed for nipples, and small amounts of over-the-counter antibiotic ointment applied two or three times a day. These measures help only if you also address what caused the pain in the first place.

instruction: Should I wear breast shells all day to protect sore nipples?
output: You can use breast shells to protect a sore nipple, but they tend to hold external moisture in which can slow healing. Don’t wear them at night because they can slide and cause further damage. Wash and dry them frequently to prevent bacteria. Leave them off as much as possible to speed healing, and you can use one while nursing to keep the baby from kicking a sore nipple.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby truly needs supplementation or if I should avoid it?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Be sure your baby really, truly needs it before giving any supplement. Unnecessary supplements are a sure recipe for decreasing your milk production because your baby will fill up on the supplement and take less of your milk. If your baby truly isn’t getting enough milk and the reason can’t be eliminated quickly, he’ll need extra milk so he can grow. Also remember that babies breastfeed better when they’re calm and not frantically hungry, so while you’re fixing a breastfeeding problem it’s important to make sure he gets enough milk even if that means a temporary supplement.

instruction: What is the best thing to use if I have to supplement my baby?
output: The best option for a supplement is your own milk that you’ve pumped or hand-expressed. If you don’t have expressed milk, donated pasteurized human milk is the next best option.

instruction: If donor human milk isn’t available, who can help me decide what to give my baby?
output: If you don’t have enough of your own milk and donor human milk isn’t available, your baby’s doctor can advise you about what to use for a supplement.

instruction: Can giving my baby supplements reduce my milk supply?
output: Yes. Giving unnecessary supplements — extra milk besides nursing — is a sure recipe for decreasing your milk production because your baby will fill up on the supplement and take less of your milk.

instruction: How much supplement should I give — what are some simple ways to think about it?
output: Only your baby knows the answer. For a small dip (for example during a busy time), he may just need extra nursing. If his growth has slowed a bit, the supplement he needs may be the extra you pump while you sort out a minor nursing problem; he may soon refuse some of what you’ve pumped and you can phase it out. If his weight gain is faltering and your supply isn’t yet supporting catch-up, he’ll need more supplementation while you pump to build your supply and also give supplements in addition to what you pump.

instruction: My baby was getting small amounts and now I’m offering larger supplements — he sometimes seems to overeat. Is that normal?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. If a baby has been on short rations for a while and is then offered unlimited supplements, he may not take much at first but then start to pig out. His stomach had been accustomed to smaller amounts and takes a few days to be able to hold more. Paced bottle-feeding lets him stop when he’s full.

instruction: What is an at-breast supplementer and how does it work?
output: An at-breast supplementer is a tubing device at the breast. Soft tubing runs from a container of milk to your nipple like a long, flexible straw. When the baby nurses, he takes both the tubing and the nipple into his mouth and gets the extra milk as he sucks. There are several commercial brands.

instruction: Can I make a homemade at-breast supplementer? What would I need?
output: Yes. One homemade version uses a regular baby bottle with a regular bottle nipple and a 5 french feeding tube (ask your health care provider for this). Cut a slightly larger hole in the bottle nipple tip, put your supplement in the bottle, and install the bottle nipple either right side up or projecting into the bottle — either way works.

instruction: Should I offer a supplement before or after nursing to help breastfeeding?
output: After you’ve had a week or so to learn your baby’s patterns, you can offer the supplement before nursing. That allows him to finish at the breast and probably also encourages better nursing.

instruction: My baby is behind on weight — can he catch up, and how does supplementation fit in?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If a baby falls behind, he needs to gain extra fast to catch up. If he falls behind and then grows at a normal rate he’ll remain as far behind as he was; he needs to grow faster than normal for a while to catch up, and that requires more food than normal. Once he catches up, his intake will settle to normal, but until then expect a steeper-than-average line on his growth chart.

instruction: Who can help me avoid unnecessary supplementation or use supplements in a way that supports breastfeeding?
output: Before you begin supplementing, it may help to check with your LLL Leader or another tech support person. That person may be able to help you avoid supplementation or help you use it in a way that supports rather than undermines breastfeeding.

instruction: Why is donor milk sometimes hard to get and who usually receives it?
output: Donated human milk supplies are limited, so not all requests can be filled. Most donated milk goes to premature babies and those who cannot tolerate formula. Donors are screened for illnesses that can pass into the milk, which is then pasteurized and frozen. Milk from a human milk bank may be available through your hospital or directly from the milk bank with a prescription, and the cost is fairly high though some medical insurance policies and government programs cover it.

instruction: Does paced bottle-feeding help when giving supplements?
output: Yes. Paced bottle-feeding allows the baby to stop when he’s full.

instruction: If I’m pumping to build my supply because my baby isn’t gaining, should I still give him supplements?
output: If your baby’s weight gain is faltering and your supply isn’t yet sufficient, you’ll probably be pumping to build your supply while also giving your baby supplements in addition to what you’ve pumped.

instruction: My baby was given extra formula but only took a little at first and now seems to want a lot at once — is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. It can be normal: a baby who’s first used to smaller amounts may not take much at first but then start to pig out. The baby’s stomach is accustomed to smaller amounts and usually takes a few days to be able to hold more.

instruction: How can I feed extra milk so my baby still nurses at the breast?
output: It helps to offer the supplement before nursing once you have a sense of his needs (often after a week or so). Paced bottle-feeding also allows him to stop when he’s full. Using a device that you feel comfortable with — for example an at-breast supplementer or a paced bottle — can let him finish at the breast and probably encourages better nursing.

instruction: What is an at-breast supplementer and how does it work?
output: An at-breast supplementer uses soft tubing that runs from a container of milk to your nipple like a long flexible straw. When the baby nurses he takes both the tubing and the nipple into his mouth and gets the extra milk as he sucks. There are several commercial brands, and homemade versions using a bottle and feeding tube are also described.

instruction: Can I make a homemade at-breast supplementer? What do I need?
output: Yes. A described homemade version uses a regular baby bottle with a bottle nipple and a 5 french feeding tube (or tubing from a commercial device). Cut a slightly larger hole in the nipple tip, put the supplement in the bottle, install the nipple (either right side up or projecting into the bottle — either works), keep one end of the tubing below the milk, and use a finger-length of tape along the tubing to hold the other end at your nipple.

instruction: Will using a bottle make my baby prefer it over breastfeeding?
output: Many parents worry about this. A baby who is fed well with a bottle and not at the breast can quickly come to prefer the bottle. This is a real and common problem.

instruction: How should I position the tubing so my baby actually gets the milk while nursing at the breast?
output: Many women find it easiest to run the tubing along the breast where the baby’s tongue will be, but any position that gets the tubing inside the sucking tunnel of the baby’s upper lip and cupped tongue will work. Run the tape along the tubing instead of across it to keep the tubing secure on your breast.

instruction: My baby fell behind on weight — can extra feeding help him catch up?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A baby who falls behind needs to gain weight faster than normal for a while to catch up, because babies are supposed to be gaining weight from birth on. If he falls behind and then grows only at the normal rate, he will remain as far behind as he was. Catching up requires more food than normal until he reaches the expected weight; once he catches up his intake will settle to normal and you may see a steeper-than-average line on his growth chart until then.

instruction: Should I be upset if my baby fills up with a bottle but not at my breast?
output: Many parents wonder about this. It can be discouraging to see a baby relax and fill up happily with a bottle when he didn’t at the breast. That difference can reflect nipple preference, flow preference, or a baby simply going where food is easiest — it’s a real and common issue.

instruction: Are there bottle options or techniques that support breastfeeding better?
output: New understandings about how babies feed from the breast and how bottles work have shown ways to use bottles that work better with breastfeeding.

instruction: How long will it take my baby’s stomach to hold larger amounts after starting supplements?
output: The baby’s stomach usually takes a few days to be able to hold larger amounts after being used to smaller feeds.

instruction: If I offer a supplement before nursing, will that let my baby finish at the breast?
output: Offering the supplement before nursing can allow him to finish at the breast and probably encourages better nursing. After about a week you’ll often have a sense of what he needs at different times and can time the supplement accordingly.

instruction: I tried a homemade supplementer but the milk won’t ‘zip’ down when he starts sucking — what should I do?
output: Experiment with the tubing placement until you find an arrangement that lets milk zip quickly from the bottle to the baby when he starts sucking. If you’re having trouble, it helps to talk with someone who has experience with supplementers.

instruction: Why might my baby prefer the bottle over breastfeeding?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Bottles are an easy way to supplement and don’t have much of a learning curve, so a baby who is fed well with a bottle and not at the breast can quickly come to prefer the bottle. It can be due to nipple confusion, nipple preference, flow preference, or simply the baby going where food is easiest to get.

instruction: Could giving a supplement before breastfeeding help my baby nurse better?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Giving the supplement before breastfeeding can make breastfeeding more appealing: you may see the baby relax and fall asleep milk-drunk at your breast, feed longer and better at the breast, and breastfeed more patiently when the flow is slow. This increased interest may boost your supply faster. There is a risk the baby can fill up on the supplement and not breastfeed well, so give a bit less supplement than usual and watch how he finishes at the breast.

instruction: How much supplement should I give if I feed before breastfeeding?
output: Give a bit less supplement than you normally would and then see if the baby is satisfied with the at-breast last course. If he doesn’t drain your breast after having the supplement, give him less supplement next time.

instruction: My baby is still hungry after breastfeeding from one side — what should I do?
output: If he’s still hungry after breastfeeding from one side, switch to the other side. If he’s still hungry after that, you can give him more supplement, then bring him back to the breast again to finish the feeding.

instruction: What is paced bottle-feeding and how do I do it?
output: Paced bottle-feeding helps the baby gain control and avoid overfeeding by pausing the feeding periodically. Pause when you see feeding signs (such as forehead and eyes crinkling, fingers splaying and tensing, eyes widening, or swallows turning to gulps). Lean the baby forward slightly until the milk in the nipple runs back into the bottle; you don’t need to unlatch him and you can tip the bottle back when he’s ready. If you take the bottle out, rest it in a shhh position against his lips so he knows it hasn’t gone away; offer it again and if he’s not interested, let the feeding end.

instruction: What signs tell me to pause during a bottle feeding?
output: Pause the feeding if you see the baby’s forehead and eyes crinkle, his fingers splay and tense, his eyes widen, or his swallows change to gulps.

instruction: Do I need to unlatch my baby when I pause a bottle feed?
output: No — there’s no need to unlatch him. You can lean him forward slightly until the milk runs back into the bottle and tip the bottle back again when he likes. If you do take the bottle out, rest it in a shhh position against his lips so he knows it hasn’t gone away.

instruction: How can I choose a bottle nipple that supports breastfeeding?
output: Choose a bottle nipple that is similar to your own nipple. For example, if you have short nipples, look for short bottle nipples; this helps minimize competition between the bottle and your nipple. Avoid orthodontic nipples — research shows their shape isn’t what your nipple looks like in the baby’s mouth, and they don’t help teeth.

instruction: What should I do if my baby starts refusing supplements once my milk supply increases?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your milk supply has increased and the baby’s weight has caught up, the baby may start refusing supplements — that’s a cue that supplements won’t be needed much longer. If he’s still taking some, try decreasing the amount slowly over several days and see if his weight stays up; if it doesn’t, slow down and give it more time.

instruction: Can bottle-feeding methods encourage a baby to breastfeed better?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Using bottles in ways that put the baby in control and using certain supplementing methods can often encourage a baby to breastfeed better, though it doesn’t work for every baby or in every situation.

instruction: If my baby doesn’t drain my breast after a supplement, what should I change next time?
output: If he doesn’t drain your breast after having the supplement, give him a bit less supplement next time.

instruction: Where can I find more ideas for feeding bottles in a way that supports breastfeeding?
output: There is a tear sheet with more ideas for feeding bottles in a way that supports breastfeeding.

instruction: How do I pause a bottle feeding so my baby doesn't get too much milk?
output: Pause the feeding periodically when you see signs the baby is feeding too fast. Look for forehead and eyes crinkling, fingers splaying and tensing, eyes widening, or swallows turning to gulps. Lean the baby forward slightly until the milk in the nipple runs back into the bottle; you don't need to unlatch. Tip the bottle back again when the baby wants to resume. If you do take the bottle out, rest it in a shhh position against his lips so he knows it hasn't gone away, then offer it again and let the feeding end if he's not interested.

instruction: My baby is gulping and seems to take in air — should I worry?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Rapid gulping is what leads to swallowed air; swallowed air usually just comes out as burps rather than causing distress. To reduce gulping and swallowed air, hold the baby sitting mostly upright so the bottle is mostly horizontal, which also slows the flow so he can stop and start sucking when he wants.

instruction: What signs show my baby is feeding too quickly or getting too much milk?
output: Watch for forehead and eyes crinkling, fingers splaying and tensing, eyes widening, and swallows turning to gulps. Those are cues to pause the feeding so the baby can regain control.

instruction: Do I need to unlatch the bottle when I pause a feeding?
output: No. You don't need to unlatch the bottle when you pause. If you do take it out briefly, rest it in a shhh position against his lips so he knows it hasn't gone away, then offer it again; if he's not interested, let the feeding end.

instruction: How should I choose a bottle nipple so it doesn't interfere with breastfeeding?
output: Choose a bottle nipple that is similar to your own nipple (for example, short nipples if you have short nipples) to help minimize competition. Avoid orthodontic nipples. Use a slow-flow or newborn nipple and continue it as long as your baby needs bottles. Keep in mind flow rates aren't well standardized between companies or even packages, so you may need to discard some nipples that are slower or faster than the ones your baby likes.

instruction: Are orthodontic bottle nipples good for breastfeeding or teeth?
output: Avoid orthodontic nipples. Research shows their shape isn't what your nipple looks like in the baby's mouth, they don't help teeth grow in straight as claimed, and babies tend to pull their tongues back when using them in a way that's counterproductive for breastfeeding.

instruction: How should I position my baby during bottle feeding to slow the flow and protect ears and teeth?
output: Hold the baby sitting mostly upright so the bottle is mostly horizontal to reduce the flow. A more upright position helps limit bottle-feeding–related dental caries and ear infections because milk isn't pooling in the mouth and throat, and it allows the baby to stop and start sucking when he wants.

instruction: Should I switch which side I hold my baby on during a bottle feeding?
output: Yes. Switch sides at least once during a feeding to help prevent a side preference when breastfeeding and to stimulate equal eye and facial muscle development.

instruction: Will ink from an existing tattoo get into my breast milk?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Tattoo ink molecules are too large to pass into breast milk, so existing tattoos are completely safe for your baby.

instruction: Can I get a tattoo while I'm still breastfeeding?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Tattooing during breastfeeding carries a risk of infection, which is less likely if the wound is kept clean and treated appropriately. It's suggested to wait at least until your child's first birthday to give your body a chance to recover from childbirth, and many tattooists prefer to wait until after you've weaned and may refuse to tattoo pregnant or breastfeeding clients.

instruction: What infection risks related to tattoos should I know about while breastfeeding?
output: Infection is a risk of tattooing during breastfeeding; keeping the wound clean and treated appropriately makes infection less likely. Human milk banks will not accept donations from mothers who have had a tattoo in the past twelve months because of the risk of hepatitis C, tetanus, and HIV from contaminated needles. Tattoo removal is usually done with a laser and can also carry a risk of infection if the wound care is inadequate.

instruction: What should I look for when choosing a tattooist if I plan to get tattooed after breastfeeding?
output: Choose a tattooist who follows universal precautions: sterilization of the tattoo machine using an autoclave; single-use inks, ink cups, gloves, and needles; bagging of equipment to avoid cross contamination; and thorough hand washing with disinfectant soap. Don't be surprised if the tattooist wants to wait until after you've weaned.

instruction: If I briefly remove the bottle, how should I hold it so my baby knows it's still there?
output: Rest the bottle in a shhh position against his lips so he knows it hasn't gone away. Then offer it again; if he's not interested, let the feeding end.

instruction: Should I feed my baby on a strict schedule or when he shows hunger cues?
output: Feed your baby when he shows hunger cues rather than on a schedule.

instruction: As my baby grows, should I move to a faster flow nipple?
output: Choose a slow-flow or newborn nipple and continue to use it as long as your baby needs bottles. Breast flow doesn't tend to increase over time, so the bottle flow rate should stay the same. Note that flow isn't well standardized between companies or even packages, so you may need to discard nipples that are slower or faster than the ones your baby prefers.

instruction: I already have several tattoos. Are they safe now that I'm breastfeeding?
output: Yes. Tattoo ink molecules are too large to pass into your milk, so existing tattoos are completely safe for your baby.

instruction: Can I get a new tattoo while I'm still breastfeeding? I'm nervous about it.
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Tattooing during breastfeeding carries a risk of infection, so it makes sense to wait. The text advises waiting at least until your child’s first birthday, and many tattooists won’t knowingly tattoo a pregnant or breastfeeding mother.

instruction: Will a human milk bank accept my milk if I got a tattoo recently?
output: Human milk banks will not accept donations from mothers who have had a tattoo done in the past twelve months because of the risk of hepatitis C, tetanus, and HIV from contaminated needles.

instruction: How should I choose a tattooist when I'm ready to get a tattoo after breastfeeding?
output: Choose a tattooist who follows universal precautions: sterilize the tattoo machine using an autoclave; use single-use inks, ink cups, gloves, and needles; bag equipment to avoid cross-contamination; and practice thorough hand washing with disinfectant soap. Don’t be surprised if the tattooist prefers to wait until after you’ve weaned.

instruction: Is laser tattoo removal okay while I'm breastfeeding? I'm worried about risks.
output: Many parents wonder about this. Tattoo removal is usually done with a laser; the only risk mentioned is an infection from inadequate wound care.

instruction: Does smoking while breastfeeding affect my baby or my milk?
output: Yes. A nursing baby is exposed to the nicotine and cotinine in the mother’s system. Nicotine can lower your prolactin level, which can reduce your interest in breastfeeding, your supply, and the fat content of your milk.

instruction: What practical steps can I take to limit my baby's exposure to smoke while I’m breastfeeding?
output: Smoke as little as possible; always go outside to smoke; wear a smoking jacket and take it off before picking up your baby; never smoke in the car with your child; and nurse before you have a cigarette rather than after.

instruction: Can I use a nicotine patch while I'm breastfeeding?
output: If you use a nicotine patch, be sure to take it off at night.

instruction: My baby isn’t latching deeply and my nipple comes out flattened. Could this be a tongue-tie?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A tongue-tie can prevent a baby from moving the tongue freely, which can stop a baby from maintaining a deep latch and can flatten or damage the nipple.

instruction: What signs should make me suspect my baby has a tongue-tie?
output: Signs include a frenulum attached at the tip pulling the tongue into a notch or heart shape; the frenulum can also attach just behind the tip, in the middle, or at the base (base attachments are hardest to see and sometimes cause the worst problems); a high, narrow palate; a tongue that rolls under or has a flat front edge when extended; a tongue that doesn’t lift when the baby cries; a crease down the middle; a dip in the center of the tongue when it lifts or cries; clicking when nursing from breaks in suction; a tongue that tires easily and quivers; and feedings that take a long time or cause the baby to tire quickly.

instruction: Can a tongue-tie actually harm my nipple during breastfeeding?
output: Many parents wonder about this. A too-tight tongue can abrade the tip of the nipple or compress it tightly, causing blisters or wounds or flattening one side of the nipple and shaping it like a new lipstick.

instruction: Are tongue-ties easy to spot in newborns?
output: Not always. While many clinicians recognize a frenulum attached to the tip of the tongue, frenula can also attach just behind the tip, in the middle, or at the base, and frenula attached to the base of the tongue are the hardest to see and sometimes cause the worst problems.

instruction: Why do I sometimes hear a clicking noise when my baby nurses?
output: Clicking when a baby nurses can be caused by breaks in suction, which may occur with a tongue-tie.

instruction: My baby tires quickly and feedings take forever—could that be a tongue-tie?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. A too-tight tongue may tire easily and quiver, and feedings might take a long time or the baby might tire out right away.

instruction: What is tongue-tie and how can it affect breastfeeding?
output: Tongue-tie (a tight frenulum) can limit the tongue’s ability to lift or extend, so the baby may not remove milk well. It can abrade or compress the nipple, causing blisters, wounds, or flattening one side of the nipple. It can also cause clicking from breaks in suction, long or tiring feedings, and a tongue that quivers or tires easily.

instruction: What signs should I look for that might mean my baby is tongue-tied?
output: Signs include a frenulum attached at the tip, just behind the tip, in the middle, or at the base; the tongue pulled into a notch or heart shape; a high, narrow palate; a tongue that rolls under or has a flat front edge when extended; a tongue that doesn’t lift when the baby cries; a crease or dip down the middle of the tongue; clicking when nursing; and a tongue that tires or quivers.

instruction: I can’t see a frenulum but my baby seems to have feeding problems — could a frenulum attached at the base cause trouble even if it’s hard to see?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A frenulum attached at the base of the tongue can be hardest to see and sometimes causes the worst problems, so it can still cause feeding difficulty even if you don’t see it.

instruction: My baby clicks when nursing and gets tired quickly. Could that be due to tongue-tie?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Clicking can be caused by breaks in suction, and a too-tight tongue may tire easily, quiver, and make feedings take a long time or cause the baby to tire out quickly — all consistent with tongue-tie.

instruction: How is tongue-tie treated, and what happens in the procedure?
output: You’ll need to see a doctor to diagnose and treat it. Treatment is usually a quick frenotomy: a snip or two with a small pair of blunt-end scissors or a laser in the doctor’s or dentist’s office. Only local anesthesia is needed, or none at all. There may be a cry from being held still, a drop or two of blood, and usually the baby latches immediately and nurses more easily afterward.

instruction: Will a baby’s frenulum often break on its own during the first year if I wait?
output: Some surgeons or dentists recommend waiting, but the frenulum rarely breaks on its own during the first year.

instruction: If my baby still has trouble latching after a frenotomy, does that mean the procedure failed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Sometimes the frenulum isn’t snipped deeply enough and a second procedure is needed. If your baby doesn’t latch painlessly in the week or so after the procedure, don’t worry — if the first nursing afterward was better, it should be again after the tongue has fully healed. The baby may hold the tongue back while it heals, and older babies can take time to learn new tongue movements; a lactation consultant can suggest exercises.

instruction: Does a frenotomy usually require general anesthesia?
output: Only local anesthesia is needed, or none at all for a frenotomy.

instruction: Could leaving a tongue-tie untreated cause problems later on?
output: Untreated tongue-tie can cause later difficulty with speech, cleaning the teeth with the tongue, swallowing pills, licking ice cream cones, and kissing.

instruction: If I decide not to have a frenotomy and my baby can’t remove milk well, what can I do to make sure he gets milk?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If the frenulum limits the baby’s ability to remove milk and you don’t have a frenotomy, you may need to pump for him as well.

instruction: How soon after a frenotomy might I notice an improvement in nursing?
output: Often the baby latches immediately and nurses more easily and effectively right after the frenotomy; if the first nursing afterward was better, it should be again once the tongue has fully healed.

instruction: Can a tight tongue affect the shape of my baby’s palate?
output: Yes. A too-tight tongue that can’t lift to spread the palate may be associated with a high and narrow palate.

instruction: What is nipple vasospasm and what does it feel like after a feeding?
output: A nipple vasospasm happens when muscles inside the tip of the nipple spasm and force blood out, turning the tip white, then bright red when the spasm relaxes. You may feel stinging or burning after a feeding; some women feel a pain that shoots deep into the breast. It can occur in one nipple, last several minutes, and can happen between feedings. It’s often misdiagnosed as a yeast infection.

instruction: Could nipple vasospasm be mistaken for a yeast infection?
output: Yes. Nipple vasospasm is often misdiagnosed as a yeast infection.

instruction: Who can help me find a doctor who does frenotomy?
output: Your Leader or a lactation consultant is likely to have names of local doctors who perform frenotomy; the text also lists a resource URL for frenotomy information.

instruction: My baby had a frenotomy and the first nursing was better but it's still painful now. Should I be worried?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If the first nursing afterward was better, it will be again after the tongue has fully healed. In the meantime your baby may be holding the tongue back while it heals, and it can take a baby a while to learn to move the tongue better, especially if the baby was older when snipped. A lactation consultant can suggest exercises that may help. Sometimes the frenulum isn’t snipped deeply enough and a second trip is needed.

instruction: If my baby’s frenulum is limiting milk removal but we don't want a frenotomy, what else might we need to do?
