output: Some have noted that the uterus clamps down more quickly and satisfactorily if the baby’s sucking drives the process.

instruction: I’m worried I’ll be immobile or affected by medications after my C-section and won’t be able to keep my baby with me. What can I do?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. You might opt to have family or friends with you 24/7, or as close to that as you can manage, so the baby can stay with you even though you’re immobile or affected by medications. A helper can hold and position the baby on your chest while the surgery is finished or in recovery.

instruction: How common are C-sections nowadays?
output: In many parts of the world, about one mother in three leaves the hospital with an abdominal incision; in some places the rates are even higher. This is well beyond the 10 to 15 percent that the World Health Organization finds reasonable.

instruction: My newborn was very sleepy and hardly nursed the first two days but started nursing on day three. Is that something that happens?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Newborns can be very sleepy and show little interest in nursing after birth medications or other birth effects. In one account a baby who was sleepy began waking up on the third day and then nursed eagerly; the mother had pumped and used finger-feeding of pumped milk until he was more alert.

instruction: Can my baby be placed skin-to-skin with me in the operating room after a cesarean?
output: Yes. If possible the baby can be laid on your chest above the drape while they finish the surgery. A helper can hold him and help position him near your breast. It can be a memorable moment for both of you, and if he nurses that is a wonderful extra. Your baby’s smell and touch can help emotional healing from the surgery.

instruction: What breastfeeding positions work right after a C-section so I don’t put pressure on my incision?
output: Your baby can nurse in a number of positions that avoid your incision: kneeling at your side; lying across your chest toward your opposite shoulder or below your opposite breast; cuddled in your armpit; or lying alongside your face with his feet toward the headboard. Any position that gives your baby access to your breast without being near your incision can work.

instruction: I’m having trouble bonding after an unexpected C-section. What can I do to connect with my baby?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Keep your baby with you as much as possible, even 24/7 if you can, so familiarity can grow. Spend as much time as you can with his bare skin against your bare skin: smell him, feel him, caress him. You can also admire and nuzzle him, take warm baths together, stroke and massage him, kiss his toes, and let him nurse while you soak if he can and wants to. Some mothers find small gestures, even unusual ones, help them feel closer over time.

instruction: Who can help me get breastfeeding started in the recovery room after a C-section?
output: Ask for help in the recovery room. You can make use of breastfeeding helpers on staff, a friend, a La Leche League Leader, or a private-practice International Board Certified Lactation Consultant (IBCLC).

instruction: My baby is all bundled up when they bring him to me after surgery. Can I undress him for skin-to-skin?
output: Yes. Feel free to undress the baby if he comes to you bundled up. A blanket will keep both of you warm while you have skin-to-skin contact.

instruction: My baby won’t latch after a traumatic birth. Is that common and why might it happen?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Many babies won’t latch after a difficult birth, and some mothers aren’t sure they even want them to. This can happen because the birth didn’t follow the usual sequence of motions and hormones that help bonding and the start of breastfeeding.

instruction: If my pregnancy is labeled high-risk, can I still aim for a more normal birth and help breastfeeding get started?
output: Talk to your caregiver about what you can do to make your birth as normal and low-risk as possible and about how you can help get breastfeeding off to a good start. While some interventions your caregiver lists may be needed, you may be able to avoid others. Most mothers and babies can breastfeed even after severe birth complications, though the more complicated the birth the harder it may be to get started, so line up your birth resources and help.

instruction: Can my partner share skin-to-skin time with the baby while I recover and am affected by medications?
output: Yes. Your partner can take shifts, leaning back in a chair to share skin time with your baby. You might also have family or friends with you so the baby can stay with you even if you are immobile or affected by medications.

instruction: Will keeping my baby with me help breastfeeding go more smoothly?
output: Yes. More and more hospitals are keeping mother and baby together because they recognize how much more smoothly breastfeeding goes when mother and baby are kept together. Skin-to-skin contact also supports that process.

instruction: Could my baby’s sucking actually help my uterus after delivery?
output: Some hospitals have noted that the uterus clamps down more quickly and satisfactorily if the baby’s sucking drives the process.

instruction: When is a good time to try finding a breastfeeding position after my cesarean?
output: After surgery, while the incision is still numb and temporarily comfortable, may be the simplest time for you and your helper to find a breastfeeding position that will work well for the next couple of days.

instruction: I’m unsure about being with my baby right away after a traumatic birth. Will staying close help even if I don’t feel like it?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Keeping your baby with you 24/7, even if you don’t feel like you want to be with him yet, can help familiarity develop so your bodies recognize each other on a primal level. Bit by bit you’ll find more about him to adore.

instruction: I had an unexpected C-section and I feel like something is missing — is that a normal reaction?
output: Many mothers feel disconnected after a birth that was traumatic or not what they hoped. It’s common to have questions like this. Some mothers describe feeling as if the baby isn’t really theirs at first. Nursing, spending lots of time together, skin-to-skin contact, baths together, massage, and other small rituals often help the sense of connection grow over time.

instruction: My baby won’t latch after a difficult delivery. Will that last?
output: Many babies won’t latch after a difficult birth. Understand that this is often temporary; try to be patient rather than panicked or frustrated, and over time many babies do learn to latch.

instruction: What can I do to speed up bonding with my baby after a birth that didn’t go as planned?
output: It can help to keep your baby with you as much as possible, including lots of skin‑to‑skin time so their bare skin is against yours. Smell him, feel him, caress him. Try warm baths together, stroke and massage him, admire and nuzzle him, and let him nurse while you soak if he can and wants to. Holding your baby while someone gives you a relaxing massage also releases oxytocin, the bonding hormone. Making decisions about him — what he’ll wear, how to hold him, how to comfort him — can help you feel more nurturing.

instruction: Should I keep my baby with me 24/7 even if I don’t feel like being with him yet?
output: Yes — keeping your baby with you 24/7 can help. The familiarity that develops from being together helps your bodies recognize each other on a primal level, and over time the baby will grow on you. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Can taking a bath with my baby help us bond?
output: Yes. Taking a warm bath together, stroking and massaging him, admiring his skin, nuzzling him, and even letting him nurse while you soak if he can and wants to are suggested ways to foster connection.

instruction: Will nursing help me feel like the baby is mine?
output: Many mothers find that nursing strengthens their sense that the baby is theirs. One mother said the more they nursed, the more she believed he was her child. Also, most mothers and babies can go on to breastfeed no matter how the birth went.

instruction: Are there simple ways to release oxytocin to help bonding?
output: Yes. Letting yourself enjoy a relaxing sensation while holding your baby — for example, having a friend or family member give you a relaxing foot, scalp, shoulder, or back massage while you watch and hold him — releases oxytocin, the bonding hormone.

instruction: I feel like I missed something important in the delivery room. Can I still bond with my baby months later?
output: It’s possible to form that connection later. One mother described a moment at nine months when, while alone and touching her wet, unclothed baby, she felt a sudden, joyful recognition and a gap was filled. She felt completely different afterward and held him differently.

instruction: Does making choices about my baby help me feel more connected?
output: Yes. Making decisions about what he’ll wear, how to hold him, and how to comfort him helps you feel more nurturing toward him and can support bonding.

instruction: My baby was taken away after birth. Will I ever feel like he’s mine?
output: Some mothers who have an unusually traumatic birth or whose babies are taken away do feel disconnected at first. Keeping your baby with you when possible, lots of skin‑to‑skin contact, nursing when you can, baths together, massage, and spending quiet time alone with him are suggested ways that have helped other parents build that connection.

instruction: I heard of a mother licking her baby to bond — is that something people do?
output: Some mothers have reported unusual actions that helped them bond. One mother decided at three days to lick her baby and said, “I felt something melt as I did it,” and that it helped her feel better about her Cesarean. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: I don’t feel like breastfeeding after a difficult birth. Is that common, and what happens next?
output: It’s not unusual for some mothers to be unsure about breastfeeding after a difficult birth. It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The excerpt notes that most mothers and babies can go on to breastfeed despite how the birth went, and suggests patience and time for bonding and nursing to develop.

instruction: How can I connect physically with my baby if he won’t latch yet?
output: Spend time with your baby’s bare skin against your bare skin, smell and caress him, stroke and massage him, take warm baths together if possible, and hold and watch his face while you allow yourself to relax. These physical contacts are suggested ways to build recognition and bonding even when latching hasn’t started.

instruction: Will writing down my birth story help me process how I feel about it?
output: Yes. Writing your birth story — the good parts and the bad parts, what you saw and did, and how you felt — can help later. Your story may become precious to you as the beginning of your life with your child, and you may someday want to tell it in detail to a caring friend or your child.

instruction: I still feel disconnected months later. Are there small moments that can change that feeling?
output: Yes. Small, private moments can shift feelings. One parent described sitting beside a wading pool at nine months, touching her wet, unclothed baby while they were alone, and suddenly feeling joyful recognition and a gap fill in. After that moment she felt completely different and held him differently.

instruction: Should I write down the story of my birth?
output: Yes. Writing the story can help even years later. You can tell it in detail to a caring friend or someday to your child.

instruction: What should I include when I write my birth story?
output: Include the good parts and the bad parts, what you saw and did, and how you felt.

instruction: Will my birth story become important to me and my child?
output: Yes. Your story can become precious because it is the beginning of your life with your child.

instruction: Will there be life after birth? I'm scared I'll lose myself.
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. There really is life after birth, and it really will be wonderful most days.

instruction: If my labor didn't go as planned, can I still breastfeed?
output: Many parents wonder about this. No matter how the birth goes, most mothers and babies can go on to breastfeed.

instruction: What will help me keep breastfeeding while I recover from the birth?
output: You can use basics focused on keeping your milk supply high, keeping your baby well fed, and keeping your breast a happy place while you and your baby recover and learn to breastfeed.

instruction: Are there good days ahead after birth?
output: Yes, there are good days ahead.

instruction: Is it okay to tell only the good parts of my birth story?
output: It’s suggested to include both the good parts and the bad parts, along with what you saw, did, and how you felt.

instruction: Will I still bond with my baby even if we're both drowsy after birth?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Even when drowsy, parents and babies can feel that they belong to each other, and some parents feel they have known their baby all along.

instruction: How can I preserve the memory of the birth for years to come?
output: Tell the story in detail to a caring friend or write it down; writing it down can help even years later.

instruction: Should I include how I felt emotionally in my birth story?
output: Yes. Include how you felt along with what you saw and did.

instruction: Will breastfeeding be possible if I have birth issues and need recovery?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. No matter how the birth goes, most mothers and babies can go on to breastfeed, and there is guidance about keeping milk supply high and learning to breastfeed while you recover.

instruction: What does keeping my breast a 'happy place' mean while I recover?
output: The guidance mentions keeping your breast a happy place while you and your baby recover and learn to breastfeed.

instruction: Will my child want to hear the whole birth story someday?
output: You will probably want to tell it in detail someday, and you may even tell it to your child.

instruction: My newborn wouldn't latch in the hospital. What can I try at home to help him nurse?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Try skin-to-skin: recline at an easy angle with pillows for support, put the baby on your chest tummy-to-tummy, dry her with a towel and cover you both with a blanket for warmth. Let gravity keep her front against yours and rub, stroke, or soothe her as she settles. Many babies start searching for the breast within the first hour and may find it and latch on their own as they recover and begin sucking.

instruction: What is "laid-back breastfeeding" and how do I do it?
output: Laid-back breastfeeding means reclining comfortably so your baby lies on top of you, front to front. Arrange pillows or adjust the bed so your head, neck, shoulders, and body are supported and the baby is supported by your body or the bedding. A towel can be used to dry her and a blanket for warmth. Gravity will keep you together while your hands are free to rub or stroke her as she finds the breast.

instruction: How will I know when my baby is ready to nurse?
output: Look for early signs like drooling, sucking movements with the lips, bringing a fist to the mouth, licking, or lifting the head and bobbing the face on and off your skin. Babies often begin these behaviors and start sucking sometime in that first hour after birth.

instruction: My baby didn’t latch until a few hours after birth when we did skin-to-skin. Is it normal that he found the breast on his own?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Yes — lying skin-to-skin and allowing the baby to settle can let her find the breast on her own; some babies begin nursing after a couple of hours once they’ve dozed and then started searching and sucking.

instruction: Nursing feels pinchy and painful. What should I do to make it more comfortable?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If the latch is pinchy or painful, try moving the baby’s body or your position by just a little to see if that helps — it doesn’t have to be a big adjustment to make things more comfortable.

instruction: How long can my baby nurse at a time?
output: Your baby can nurse as long as you both want. After a while she may fall asleep with the nipple in her mouth or let go and fall asleep. You may also stop nursing because you need to shift position or get up.

instruction: If I stop a feeding, should I offer the other breast?
output: Take your cue from the baby. If she’s putting her fist in her mouth or fussing after you stop, she probably wants to nurse some more, so offering the other breast is reasonable based on her behavior.

instruction: Why does my newborn nurse, let go, doze, and then wake to nurse again?
output: Until birth your baby was fed continuously through the umbilical cord, so she’s designed to expect frequent feedings now. It’s normal for her to nurse, let go and doze for a bit, then wake and start looking for the nipple again; this pattern helps establish milk supply and a well-fed baby.

instruction: How should we support the baby’s head and body during laid-back breastfeeding?
output: Recline comfortably with pillows or bedding supporting your head, neck, and shoulders so you’re leaning back. The baby lies on your front and is supported by your body or the bedding around you so her head, neck, shoulders, and body are all supported while you can use your hands to rub or stroke her.

instruction: My newborn cried a lot right after birth and didn’t try to nurse. Is that okay?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Yes — some babies cry first as they recover from the journey from womb to world. Soothe her, stroke her, talk to her, and snuggle; as she settles she will usually begin sucking sometime in the first hour.

instruction: Do newborns find the breast by sight or by other senses?
output: Newborns find the breast by feel and by smell, not by sight. They move toward the breast using those senses as they search and latch.

instruction: I’m fumbling while trying to help my baby latch. Is that a problem?
output: Fumbling is normal and can even be helpful. You don’t need to be skilled; sharing the baby’s efforts by guiding and soothing is part of the process.

instruction: How soon after birth should I expect my baby to start trying to nurse?
output: Many babies begin to show sucking behaviors and start trying to nurse sometime in that first hour after birth, though some may need a little time to recover and settle before they begin.

instruction: My newborn is making sucking motions and bringing her fist to her mouth — is she ready to breastfeed yet?
output: Yes. Those signs — drooling, sucking movements with the lips, bringing her fist to her mouth, or licking — usually mean she’s beginning to think about sucking, often sometime in the first hour. She may lift her head and bob her face toward your skin; you can help her move closer to the breast or support her as she finds the nipple. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: She’s near my nipple but I’m scared I’ll do it wrong. Do I need to be skilled to help her latch?
output: You don’t need to be skilled. Fumbling is normal and can even be helpful. When her face is near your nipple she may lift her head, open her mouth wide, latch, and begin to suck. You can support her and let her find the nipple by feel and smell.

instruction: When my baby latches it feels like a strong tug and my nipple is compressed. Is that normal and what can I do?
output: It’s normal that a newborn has a strong little mouth and may tug and compress your nipple more than you expected. If it’s downright pinchy or painful, try moving her body or yours just a bit to see if it helps — it doesn’t have to be a big adjustment to make things more comfortable. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: How long can my baby nurse at a feeding?
output: Your baby can nurse as long as you both want. After a while she may either fall asleep with your nipple in her mouth or let go and fall asleep. If you end the feeding, take your cue from her about whether to offer the other breast.

instruction: She keeps nursing, letting go, dozing, then waking and looking for the nipple again — is that normal?
output: Yes. Until now she was fed continuously through the umbilical cord, so she’s designed to expect frequent feedings. It’s normal for her to nurse, let go and doze, then wake and look for the nipple again. This pattern helps establish a good milk supply and a well-fed baby.

instruction: What is laid-back breastfeeding and why would I try it?
output: Laid-back breastfeeding is leaning back and letting gravity hold the baby; it’s also called Biological Nurturing. Advantages include not needing a precise hold because gravity helps stick the baby against you; your torso opens up so the baby can lie in many positions; gravity gives consistent support without pressure points; the baby’s whole body touches you, triggering instinctive responses; the baby is less likely to flail; you can relax and have at least one hand free.

instruction: Do I have to follow a long list of steps to do laid-back breastfeeding correctly?
output: No. One of the advantages of laid-back breastfeeding is there is no long list of instructions. Gravity brings the baby toward your breast, so you don’t have to worry about a precise hold. You get to decide your and your baby’s position and how much either of you wears.

instruction: Can I use laid-back breastfeeding all the time or should I switch later?
output: You can choose. You may use laid-back breastfeeding as much as you like, modify it, enjoy it for naps, or switch completely once you and your baby are more skilled. Consider it training wheels for other positions.

instruction: Will my baby flail her arms and get in the way when nursing in the laid-back position?
output: Your baby isn’t likely to flail her arms and get in her own way because she feels secure against you when laid back. Her whole body touching yours helps trigger instinctive responses and comfort.

instruction: If I need to end a feeding to get up, should I offer the other breast afterward?
output: If you are the one who ends the feeding, take your cue from the baby about whether to offer the other breast. If she’s putting her fist in her mouth or fussing, she probably wants to nurse some more.

instruction: Is it true babies find the nipple by sight or by smell and touch?
output: Babies find the breast by feel and by smell, not sight. She uses these senses to move toward the nipple and latch.

instruction: I have sore nipples and feel anxious — could laid-back breastfeeding help?
output: Some breastfeeding helpers and mothers reported that using Biological Nurturing led to a fabulous latch and less pain, with one mother surprised there was no pain. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Do I have to support the baby the whole time in laid-back breastfeeding, or can I relax my shoulders?
output: When you’re laid back, gravity helps hold the baby, so your torso opens and you can be totally relaxed — no tight shoulders or aching wrists. You’ll have at least one hand free to stroke your baby or pick up a drink.

instruction: What does the baby do right before she latches?
output: Before latching she may drool, make sucking movements with her lips, bring her fist to her mouth, or lick. After a while she may lift her head and bob her face on and off your skin; when her face is near your nipple she will lift her head, open her mouth wide, latch, and begin to suck.

instruction: If the latch is painful even after small adjustments, what should I expect next?
output: If small movements of her body or yours don’t make nursing more comfortable, try different positions; you can modify or switch positions as you and your baby become more skilled. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What are the benefits of laid-back breastfeeding?
output: Laid-back breastfeeding gives your baby full, consistent support with no gaps or pressure points. Gravity brings your baby toward your breast as he bobs, instead of pulling his head away. Your baby’s whole body touches yours, which triggers instinctive responses. He’s less likely to flail his arms because he feels secure, and you can be more relaxed with at least one hand free.

instruction: Does gravity actually help my baby latch when I use the laid-back position?
output: Yes. In the laid-back position gravity brings your baby toward your breast as he bobs, helping him move to the nipple rather than pulling his head away.

instruction: Do I have to be completely topless to try the sitting-up, baby-led approach?
output: No. You don’t have to be topless. You can wear a button-down shirt, jacket, or robe and just open it. The text also suggests undressing your baby down to his diaper.

instruction: How should I hold my baby if I want to breastfeed while sitting up?
output: Lean back a little (not as much as with laid-back breastfeeding). Hold your baby vertically against your chest with his head under your chin and follow his lead. Keep his body against yours, and help him as feels right. When his cheek or nose is near your nipple he will probably move his head, open wide, and gently take your breast.

instruction: Is it okay to push on my baby’s head to get a better latch?
output: Pushing on his head almost never helps and can make him dislike being at the breast. It also pushes his chin toward his chest, which makes it hard for him to get a good mouthful. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What can I do if my baby fusses and won’t latch in the baby-led position?
output: If he fusses, try calming him by holding him vertically for a minute or two and maybe walking and talking to him, then try again. You can also bring his lower face into contact with your breast to help him focus. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Can I use laid-back breastfeeding only for naps or should I switch later?
output: That’s up to you. You can modify it, enjoy it for naps, or switch completely once you and your baby are more skilled. The text describes it as training wheels for other positions.

instruction: How do I hold my baby in the sitting-up 'shortcut' position?
output: Choose a breast and hold your baby so his body angles across yours with his hip resting on or near your thigh and his whole front against your front. His face, cheek, or chin needs to touch your breast, or your nipple should rest above his top lip so he understands what to do.

instruction: How should I support my baby’s head when I hold him behind his back and shoulders?
output: Hold him behind his back and shoulders so there are no gaps between you. His head will rest on your wrist or forearm, and he can control it himself. If he fusses, calm him and try again.

instruction: My nipples hurt from latching—will laid-back breastfeeding help with pain?
output: Some parents report that using the laid-back approach produced a painless latch and relief, even when nipple wounds hadn’t healed. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: If I sit up to breastfeed, will my baby pull his head away more often than in laid-back?
output: The text says that when you sit up the baby’s head can be pulled away, whereas gravity in the laid-back position brings the baby toward the breast instead of pulling his head away.

instruction: Why would I put the nipple between my baby’s upper lip and nose?
output: If your nipple falls in the space between his upper lip and nose, he can tip his head back slightly to take it into his mouth.

instruction: I’m anxious and have sore nipples—will laid-back help even if I’m tense?
output: Some helpers and mothers describe that even when a mother was anxious and had sore nipples, the baby latched well and there was no pain. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Is it normal for my baby to lunge quickly toward my breast when he’s feeding?
output: Yes. Be prepared that he might move really quickly, like a lunge. Keep his body against yours and help him in whatever way feels right.

instruction: How do I position my baby using the sitting-up shortcut so he can latch?
output: Choose a breast and hold your baby so his body angles across yours, his hip resting on or near your thigh, with his whole front against your front. Let his face, cheek, or chin touch your breast, or place your nipple above his top lip so he can find it by feel. Hold him behind his back and shoulders so there are no gaps; his head can rest on your wrist or forearm and he can control it.

instruction: My baby keeps pushing his head away and crying. Can I press his head toward the breast to make him latch?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Don’t push on his head. Pushing almost never helps, can make him dislike being at the breast, and pushes his chin toward his chest, which makes it hard for him to get a good mouthful. Instead, try calming him, hold him vertically for a minute or two, maybe walk and talk to him, and then try again.

instruction: What should I do to calm my baby before trying to nurse?
output: Begin when your baby shows early cues like sucking on his hand, smacking his lips, or turning his head toward you. If he is upset, bring him higher on your chest, stroke him, and talk to him in the voice he knows. If he seems agitated from hunger, a little colostrum expressed and fed with a dropper or spoon can calm him and help him focus.

instruction: How should I support my baby when I'm sitting up to nurse?
output: Hold him behind his back and shoulders with his front to your front. Tuck his shoulders in closely to you so he feels stable. If he feels unstable he may wave his arms, which can make latching much more difficult.

instruction: Why does my baby need room for his lower jaw when nursing?
output: Your baby’s lower jaw is the moving jaw and needs plenty of room to move and be deeply placed on your breast. If fingers or anything else are in the way of his lower jaw, it can’t move properly. Shape or lift your breast from a respectful distance so his chin can sink into the breast and his head can tip back a bit to bring the lower jaw forward.

instruction: My fingers are near my baby's mouth while trying to help him latch. Is that a problem?
output: Yes. Keep your fingers totally away from where his chin and lower jaw need to be. If your fingers are in the way of his lower jaw, it can’t move properly. You can shape or lift your breast, but do it from a respectful distance so his chin can sink in.

instruction: How can I help my baby tip his head back slightly to take the nipple?
output: Place your nipple so it falls in the space between his upper lip and nose; that lets him tip his head back slightly to take it into his mouth. You can also let his cheek rest just above your nipple and help him move to take it in. Babies latch by feel and will often turn and open when their cheek touches the breast.

instruction: Why does it hurt when my baby only sucks on the nipple?
output: When a baby just chews on a nipple he won’t get much milk and it will hurt. Most milk is in the ducts in the breast, so the baby needs a big mouthful of breast beyond the nipple to be comfortable and get the milk.

instruction: What does it mean that the baby needs a big mouthful?
output: A good latch puts a lot of breast tissue in the baby’s mouth beyond the nipple. That’s where the milk ducts are, so when the baby has a big mouthful it is comfortable and he gets milk. Nursing is breastfeeding, not nipple-feeding.

instruction: My baby is fussy while trying to latch. What can I try right away?
output: If he fusses, try bringing his lower face into contact with your breast to help him focus. If that doesn’t help, calm him by holding him vertically for a minute or two, maybe walk and talk to him, then try again. Holding him behind his back and shoulders so there are no gaps can also help his stability while he searches.

instruction: Is it okay to flatten my breast while nursing? I’ve heard of a sandwich technique.
output: Some women like or need to hold their breast when they nurse. They flatten their breast as they would a big sandwich.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is getting a deep enough latch with his lower jaw?
output: You can rest your index finger under your nose like a mustache and make exaggerated chewing motions; your finger doesn’t move. The baby’s lower jaw is the moving jaw and needs to be deeply placed on the breast. Make sure his chin can sink into the breast and his head is tipped back a bit so the lower jaw comes forward.

instruction: Should I worry if my baby waves his arms while trying to latch?
My baby keeps chewing the nipple and feeding hurts me. What does the text suggest?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby just chews on the nipple he won’t get much milk and it will hurt. The milk is mostly in the ducts in the breast, so he needs a big mouthful of breast beyond the nipple for comfort and milk transfer.

instruction: How should I hold my baby when I'm sitting so he can latch better?
output: Hold him with his front against your front, supporting him behind his back and shoulders. Tuck his shoulders in closely to you so he feels stable while he moves his head to search; instability can make him wave his arms and make latching harder.

instruction: My nipples hurt because my baby seems to be just chewing on them — why is that and what should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The milk is mostly in the ducts inside your breast, not on the nipple. A baby who just chews on the nipple won’t get much milk and it will hurt. The baby needs a big mouthful of breast beyond the nipple; when that happens breastfeeding is comfortable and that's where the milk is.

instruction: How much of my breast should my baby take into his mouth when he latches?
output: The baby should take a big mouthful of breast beyond the nipple. That large mouthful is where the milk is and is when nursing is comfortable.

instruction: Can I hold my breast while nursing to help my baby latch?
output: Some women like or need to hold their breast and flatten it like a big sandwich to help the baby grasp more tissue. If you hold it, keep your thumbs way back so there's room for the baby's lower jaw to take a big bite, and keep fingers well away from where his lower jaw and chin need to be.

instruction: Is it okay to put my finger under my baby's nose to help his jaw while nursing?
output: You can rest your index finger under your own nose to feel how the lower jaw moves, but when nursing do not put fingers where the baby's lower jaw needs room. Keep your fingers totally away from the area where his chin and lower jaw must be.

instruction: Do I have to lift or hold my breast the whole time during a feeding?
output: Not necessarily. First try starting where nature and gravity have put your breast. If you do lift the breast for the baby, you will probably have to hold it throughout the feeding. Some babies do need the breast stabilized or lifted slightly to make breathing easier, but unless you are very long-breasted or wide-breasted it will probably be easier to let the breast be where it naturally sits.

instruction: My baby's nose looks buried in my breast and I'm worried he can't breathe — what can I try?
output: It makes sense to feel worried. In most cases babies get plenty of air if their heads are tilted back so the nose is free or nearly free. If his nose is buried, try shifting him to tip his head back more, maybe by pulling his back and shoulders in closer. You can also lift the breast slightly by pressing the flat of your hand on your chest between breast and collarbone and lifting.

instruction: Does the baby's upper jaw have to be over the nipple for good nursing?
output: No. The baby's lower jaw is the moving jaw and needs plenty of room; the upper jaw isn't all that important for nursing.

instruction: Should I try to latch my baby when his mouth is wide open on purpose?
output: No. Mothers used to be told to latch when the mouth was wide open, but mammal mothers don’t take that much control. Unless there's a specific need, the baby will probably do best if he picks the moment. Your job is to make that moment possible by having a well-supported baby in easy reach of the breast.

instruction: Does tipping my baby's head back help with swallowing while nursing?
output: Yes. Tipping his head back a bit helps swallowing; it's easier to swallow when the chin is lifted. The baby does better if his chin is in firm contact with the breast and his nose is lifted free or nearly free of the breast.

instruction: If I lift my breast to make breathing easier, will I need to hold it the whole feeding?
output: If you lift the breast for your baby, you'll probably have to hold it throughout the feeding. Some babies do need the breast stabilized or lifted slightly to make breathing easier, but it's not necessary for every feeding.

instruction: My baby keeps waving his arms and can't seem to latch — what might help?
output: Babies who don't feel stable may wave their arms while they move their head to search, which can make latching difficult. Holding him with his shoulders tucked in closely to you and supporting him behind his back and shoulders helps keep him stable while he moves his head to search.

instruction: Where should my thumbs be if I hold my breast like a sandwich for nursing?
output: When holding the breast like a sandwich, keep your thumbs way back on the breast so there is room for a big lower-jaw bite, and keep fingers away from the lower-jaw area.

instruction: Do specific holds or positions always matter for breastfeeding success?
output: When all is going well, specific holds and positions don't really matter because you and your baby figure out your own system. However, holds and positions have been used to help mothers and babies get a good latch when trouble occurs, for example with babies affected by labor medications.

instruction: Is it okay if my baby's nose is just lightly touching my breast while he nurses?
output: Yes. Even if his nose is lightly touching the breast, most babies can still breathe just fine. If he truly couldn't breathe, he lets go.

instruction: Do I have to lift or hold my breast throughout the feeding?
output: Not necessarily. First try starting where Nature and Gravity have already placed your breast. If you lift the breast for your baby, you will probably have to hold it throughout the feeding. Some babies do need the breast stabilized or lifted slightly to make breathing easier. Unless you are very long-breasted or wide-breasted, it will probably be easier to let your breast be where Nature puts it.

instruction: How can I lift my breast slightly if I need to?
output: You can lift your breast slightly by pressing your hand on your chest between the breast and your collarbone and lifting.

instruction: My baby seems to need the breast stabilized to breathe while nursing — what holds or positions can help?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Some babies do need the breast stabilized or lifted slightly to make breathing easier. Positions that can help include the cradle hold, cross-cradle (across-the-chest) hold, the clutch hold, and side-lying; each positions the baby so the chest is snuggled into the breast and lets you support or shape the breast as needed.

instruction: What is the cradle hold and how should I position my baby's head and body?
output: In the cradle hold, hold your baby's front against yours by supporting him behind his back and shoulders. His head should rest on your forearm or wrist (not the crook of your elbow) and his hip on or near your thigh. His chest is snuggled into your breast with your nipple near his nose; as he opens to take the nipple, gently snuggle him closer. If he seems confused, shift him so his cheek rests near your nipple or use the nipple tilt.

instruction: How do I do the across-the-chest or cross-cradle hold?
output: Hold the baby with the arm opposite the breast he will latch onto, with his front under your breasts and his hip near or resting on your thigh. Use your hand to support his neck (not the back of his head) while your arm hugs him; his chest should be snuggled against the mound of your breast. Your other hand can shape or stabilize your breast. As he opens to take the nipple, gently snuggle his chest closer; touching his cheek or using the nipple tilt may help.

instruction: What is the clutch hold and when is it suggested?
output: The clutch hold positions the baby next to you with his front against your side, your hand under his shoulders and neck, and his body supported between your arm and the side of your body. It is often suggested for mothers who have had C-sections because the baby’s weight is kept away from the incision. The baby will be a bit below your breast, which may work well if your breasts are fairly large; you may need to move him into a semisitting position if your breasts are smaller.

instruction: How do I breastfeed in the side-lying position at night?
output: Lay your baby on her side facing you and pull her down so your nipple is level with her nose or eye. You can align your breast to match her mouth, keeping her fingers out of the way and rolling the nipple in last. As she latches, press on the middle of her back to bring her closer; she may be somewhat arched, and bringing her middle back closer helps keep her head tipped back. Tuck a small rolled towel or receiving blanket behind her back to hold her there if you like.

instruction: My baby opens but then seems confused about latching — what can I do to help him take the nipple?
output: If he seems confused as he opens, gently snuggle him closer so his cheek rests near your nipple or use the nipple tilt. You can also touch his cheek to encourage him as he opens to take the nipple.

instruction: Do specific holds and positions matter once breastfeeding is going well?
output: When all is going well, specific holds and positions don’t really matter because you and your baby will figure out your own system. Positions are most useful when you are having trouble getting started, to provide a recipe to follow until you know what works for you.

instruction: Why are certain holds taught or recommended in hospitals?
output: Over the years, helpers developed holds and positions to help mothers and babies get a good latch, especially for babies who may be dealing with the challenges of labor medications. The cross-cradle hold, for example, works especially well with small babies and is often taught in hospitals.

instruction: How should I support my baby's neck and head in the cross-cradle hold?
output: In the cross-cradle hold your hand should support the baby’s neck (not the back of his head) while your arm hugs him against you; his chest should be snuggled against your breast.

instruction: If my breasts are fairly large, which position might be easier for nursing?
output: The clutch hold may work well if your breasts are fairly large because the baby will be a bit below the breast in that position.

instruction: What is the clutch hold and why might it be suggested after a C-section?
output: The clutch hold positions your baby next to you, front against your side, with your hand under his shoulders and neck and his body supported between your arm and your side. The baby will be a bit below your breast. It is often suggested after a C-section because the baby’s weight is kept away from the incision.

instruction: My breasts are small. Do I need to change the clutch hold position?
output: You can use the clutch hold, but your baby may be a bit below your breast. If your breasts are smaller, you may need to move him into a semisitting position so he reaches the nipple more easily.

instruction: How do I position my baby for side-lying nursing?
output: Lay your baby on her side facing you and pull her down so your nipple is level with her nose or eye. You can make a sandwich of your breast aligned to her mouth with your fingers well out of the way, rolling the nipple in last. As she latches, press on the middle of her back to bring her closer and help keep her head tipped back. Tucking a small rolled towel or receiving blanket behind her back can hold her there and free a hand.

instruction: Is side-lying useful for night feedings?
output: Yes. Side-lying can be especially helpful at night and can also help a baby who is having difficulty in other positions.

instruction: My baby arches when latching. What can I do to tip his head back?
output: Press on the middle of his back to bring him closer; bringing the middle of his back closer helps keep his head tipped back.

instruction: I had a C-section and can’t get into a semi-reclining position. How can I latch my baby?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. You can lie flat on your back and let the baby lie on your chest while avoiding your incision, or kneel at your side with someone’s help as he finds the nipple. You can also use a clutch hold or sit upright with a pillow or two behind your back and a pillow on your lap to protect your incision.

instruction: If I used my hand to help my baby latch, can I let go right away?
output: Don’t relax immediately or he could lose your nipple. Give your baby time to get everything arranged in his mouth and start sucking. Then slowly relax the hand on your breast, and if relaxing makes your breast sag away, bring your baby in closer. Rearrange yourself comfortably and settle in.

instruction: Can I nurse while standing up? What should I do?
output: Sometimes babies do better if you stand up. You can sway or bounce gently to help calm your baby. The lack of a lap can be helpful. Keep your baby stable, let him lead the way, and try angling his legs down instead of holding him horizontal.

instruction: My baby seems to be getting milk but nursing hurts me a lot. Is that considered nursing well?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Nursing is going well if the baby is able to get the milk he needs without getting too tired AND you both feel comfortable. If you are feeling pain, then that part of the description isn’t working.

instruction: Can I use a small towel to help keep my baby in the side-lying position?
output: Yes. If you keep a small towel or receiving blanket rolled up nearby, you can tuck it behind her back to hold her there and have your hand free to stroke and enjoy her.

instruction: Why might a baby do better without being held in a lap when nursing?
output: Sometimes the lack of a lap helps; for example, standing and nursing removes the lap and can help some babies settle. Swaying or gentle bouncing while standing can also help calm the baby.

instruction: What does "make a sandwich of your breast" mean when helping a baby latch on side-lying?
output: It means aligning your breast to match her mouth with your fingers well out of the way, and rolling the nipple in last so the breast is positioned for her mouth as she latches.

instruction: Will the clutch hold work if my baby is smaller or my breasts are larger?
output: In the clutch hold the baby will be a bit below your breast, which may work well if your breasts are fairly large. If your breasts are smaller you may need to move him into a semisitting position.

instruction: My baby had trouble starting each nursing at first but later could nurse in different positions. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Some mothers find it hard at first but later are able to nurse in different positions and while doing other things as they and the baby become more comfortable.

instruction: How should I support my baby’s body in the clutch hold?
output: Support his body between your arm and the side of your body, and place your hand under his shoulders and neck to stabilize him.

instruction: I'm recovering from a C-section and can't recline—how can I position myself to breastfeed without upsetting my incision?
output: You can lie flat on your back and let the baby lie on your chest while avoiding your incision. With someone's help you can kneel at your side as the baby finds the nipple. You can also use a clutch hold or sit upright with a pillow or two behind your back and a pillow on your lap to protect the incision.

instruction: After I use my hand to get my baby latched, can I let go right away?
output: Don’t relax immediately. Give your baby time to arrange everything in his mouth and start sucking. Once he’s sucking, slowly relax the hand on your breast and bring your baby closer if relaxing makes your breast sag away. Then rearrange yourself comfortably and settle in.

instruction: My latch hurts even though the baby seems attached—what should I do?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Pain or a baby not getting milk means something needs adjusting. You can put a finger in the corner of his mouth, between his gums, to break the suction and slip your nipple out, but try not to do that too many times because it can be frustrating for him. Instead, get some help finding a more comfortable approach.

instruction: How do I break suction to take my nipple out of my baby's mouth?
output: Put a finger in the corner of the baby’s mouth, right between the gums, to break the suction. Then slip your nipple out. Try not to do this too many times because it can be frustrating for the baby.

instruction: Can you explain the nipple-tilt technique for getting a deeper latch?
output: Put your thumb or a finger near the base of your nipple on the side where the baby’s upper lip will be. Press or tug a little so the nipple tilts away from the baby. Present that full bit of breast to tease the baby’s lower face. If she opens wide reaching for the nipple, snuggle her straight in without shifting her. Use your thumb or finger to help push or tuck the bit of breast into her mouth; the nipple is the last part in, tilting away and unrolling inside her mouth. Keep the baby snug against your body, her chest against the mound of your breast, and let her control her head.

instruction: Is it safe to put my finger near the baby's jaw while positioning for latching?
output: Keep fingers well away from the baby’s lower jaw. The upper-jaw finger, though, can be right next to the nipple.

instruction: Should I use pillows to support my baby while nursing?
output: If your body supports your baby, there’s no need for pillows. If you need support for your arm, a squishy pillow or two may help. Supporting the baby on a pillow can put extra space between his body and yours and has caused more sore nipples than it has cured. In some situations a pillow can be a temporary help, and a flat-surfaced pillow may work better than a rounded one, especially with a small baby.

instruction: My nipples feel sore when I use a pillow under the baby—could the pillow be the problem?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Supporting your baby on a pillow can put extra space between his body and yours and has caused more sore nipples than it has cured. If using a pillow means you have to lift your breast to your baby, you probably don’t need the pillow. A flat-surfaced pillow may work better than a rounded one, especially with a small baby.

instruction: I sit to nurse but my legs are short and my lower back hurts—any simple adjustments?
output: Try a low footstool (or a thick book, diaper bag, or a coffee-table crossbar) to raise your lap. That can help support your arm and the baby and relax your lower back.

instruction: When I relax my hand after a latch, the baby often loses the nipple—what can I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Don’t relax immediately; give your baby time to arrange his mouth and start sucking. Once he’s sucking, slowly relax your hand, and bring the baby in closer if relaxing makes your breast sag away. If needed, break the suction and try again, but avoid doing that repeatedly, and seek help finding a more comfortable approach.

instruction: How can I help my baby take a deeper mouthful of breast?
output: Narrow the breast "sandwich" by compressing it so it’s easier for the baby to get a deep mouthful. You can combine this with the nipple-tilt technique. Keep your fingers well away from the baby’s lower jaw; the upper-jaw finger can be right next to the nipple.

instruction: Is it okay if my thumb briefly goes under my baby's upper lip when I tuck in the breast?
output: Yes. It’s fine if your thumb or finger ends up under the baby’s upper lip for a moment while tucking in the last bit of breast. Remember to keep the baby snug against your body and let her control her head.

instruction: My newborn is very small and tends to roll into pillow cracks—what kind of pillow should I try?
output: You may do better with a flat-surfaced pillow than with a rounded one, especially with a small baby because they tend to roll into the crack. Keep in mind supporting the baby on a pillow can create extra space and has caused more sore nipples than it cured.

instruction: If using a pillow means I have to lift my breast to my baby, should I keep using it?
output: If using a pillow means you have to lift your breast to your baby, you probably don’t need the pillow.

instruction: How do I know if breastfeeding is going well beyond how it looks?
output: Good breastfeeding is about how it feels and how well it works, not how it looks. Your baby is nursing well if he is able to get the milk he needs without getting too tired and if you both feel comfortable. If you’re feeling pain or the baby isn’t getting milk well, something needs adjusting.

instruction: How can I tilt my nipple to help my baby get more breast when latching?
output: Tilting the nipple can give your baby more breast. You can combine a nipple tilt with narrowing the breast sandwich to help your baby get a deeper mouthful. When you do this, keep the lower-jaw fingers well away from the nipple; the upper-jaw finger can be right next to the nipple.

instruction: What does 'narrowing the sandwich' mean and how does it help with latching?
output: 'Narrowing the sandwich' means compressing a very full breast so it is easier for your baby to take a deep mouthful, similar to compressing a fat sandwich to fit in your mouth. You can combine this with a nipple tilt. Remember to keep the lower-jaw fingers well away from the nipple and the upper-jaw finger next to the nipple.

instruction: Can I use pillows to make nursing more comfortable?
output: If your body already supports your baby, you don’t need pillows. A squishy pillow or two may help if you need support for your arm, but supporting your baby on a pillow can put extra space between you and has caused more sore nipples than it has cured. A flat-surfaced pillow may work better than a rounded one for a small baby. If using a pillow means you have to lift your breast to your baby, you probably don’t need the pillow.

instruction: My nipples have been sore since I started using a nursing pillow — could the pillow be the cause?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Supporting a baby on a pillow can put extra space between your body and the baby and has caused more sore nipples than it has cured. A flat-surfaced pillow is less likely to create a crack for a small baby; and if the pillow means you must lift your breast to the baby, you probably don’t need it.

instruction: Should I use a footstool while nursing if my legs are short?
output: If you’re nursing sitting up and have short legs, a low footstool (or a thick book, diaper bag, or coffee-table crossbar) may raise your lap to support your arm and baby and help relax your lower back. You can also try crossing your legs or raising just one foot so your baby’s feet are lower than his head.

instruction: My baby won't latch after an epidural — is that something that happens?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Medications such as an epidural or interventions like induction or forceps can make some babies unable to respond in the usual way; the effects can last for days or weeks. Other babies may have physical or neurological difficulties. This can be a temporary setback; many mothers have worked through it and the baby may get the hang of nursing with time and help. A full-term, healthy baby does not need any food or liquids for at least twenty-four to thirty-six hours, so there is time to work on nursing.

instruction: If my baby isn't nursing yet, what should I do to keep my milk supply ready?
output: Keep your milk flowing. Your milk supply will start to increase automatically after the first couple of days whether you remove milk or not, but the amount you remove tells your breasts how much you’ll need. If your baby isn’t nursing, start hand-expressing or pumping so your breasts are ready when the baby can nurse. In the first few days colostrum is thick and usually easier to remove by hand expressing; massage and breast compressions help. You can ask for help expressing if needed.

instruction: How long and how often should I express if my baby still isn't nursing by Day Three?
output: If your baby isn’t nursing by Day Three, you can continue to hand-express or use a rental-grade pump. It’s helpful to express about fifteen minutes on each side every two to three hours, using breast massage to encourage more milk.

instruction: Is colostrum easier to hand-express or pump in the first few days?
output: In the first few days colostrum is thick and easier to remove by hand expressing than by pumping. Massage and breast compressions also help, and most mothers can express more colostrum in the first hours after birth than they can later.

instruction: My baby seems to act frustrated and pulls away from the breast — could that explain the poor latch?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. A baby who won’t breastfeed can’t breastfeed at the moment; if he acts frustrated or angry at the breast, it may be a place where he can let frustrations out, or he may be reacting like a child in a candy shop who throws a tantrum because of strong feelings. The key is to be patient and have good help; your baby can learn to nurse but it may take time.

instruction: Are rental-quality breast pumps available in hospitals if I need one by Day Three?
output: Most hospitals have rental-grade pumps readily available. You can continue to hand-express or use a rental pump if your baby isn’t nursing by Day Three, and some hospitals can provide access to these pumps or point you to local sources.

instruction: When can I take a break from expressing if my baby isn’t nursing yet?
output: After initial expressing you can take about a six-hour break to rest and be with your baby, and then begin expressing every two to three hours around the clock, or eight to ten times every twenty-four hours.

instruction: Why does the book say a flat pillow is better than a rounded one for small babies?
output: A rounded pillow can create a crack where a small baby tends to roll into the crease, so a flat-surfaced pillow may work better and be less likely to cause positioning problems.

instruction: What is the first of the 'Three Keeps' when breastfeeding isn't working?
output: The first 'Keep' is to keep your milk flowing. Early on your breasts are planning for future milk production and the amount of milk you remove tells them how much you’ll need. If the baby isn’t nursing, start hand-expressing or pumping, use massage and breast compressions, and express frequently so your breasts are ready when the baby can nurse.

instruction: Can the effects of labor medications on a baby's ability to nurse last more than a day?
output: Yes. For some babies the effects of labor medications or interventions can last for days or weeks, and other babies may have physical or neurological difficulties that affect nursing.

instruction: My baby isn't nursing yet. How can I keep my milk supply ready and growing so I'll be able to breastfeed when they are?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Your milk supply will start to increase automatically after the first couple of days whether you remove milk or not, but the early days are when your breasts plan for future production and the amount you remove now tells them how much you'll need. If your baby isn't nursing, start hand-expressing or pumping now. Colostrum is thick and is usually easier to remove by hand expressing than by pumping. Use massage and breast compressions to help. You can ask for help expressing. After an initial break of about six hours you can begin expressing every two to three hours around the clock (about eight to ten times in 24 hours). If your baby still isn't nursing by Day Three, continue hand-expressing or get a rental-grade pump; express about fifteen minutes on each side every two to three hours and use massage to encourage more milk.

instruction: My baby only gets tiny drops of colostrum. How can I feed those drops to my baby?
output: A little colostrum goes a long way. Hand-express into a plastic spoon and tip the drop(s) into your baby's mouth. If possible, have an assembly line so one spoon is being filled while another is being fed to the baby. If you have more than drops, collect from the spoon with an eyedropper or medicine dropper. If you are expressing into a pump funnel, you can wipe drops off the funnel with your finger and let the baby suck them off, and collect more from the pump's bottle with an eyedropper.

instruction: How often and how long should I express milk in the first days if my baby can't nurse yet?
output: After about a six-hour break you can begin expressing every two to three hours around the clock, or eight to ten times every twenty-four hours. It is helpful to express for about fifteen minutes on each side every two to three hours, using breast massage to encourage more milk.

instruction: Does skin-to-skin contact help a baby who isn't yet organized enough to breastfeed?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Bare skin against your own bare skin really helps encourage breastfeeding in a baby who is still getting himself organized. Keep a covering over both of you so you stay warm; with that covering your baby will be just as warm and won't burn calories trying to stay warm. Your heart and breathing will pace and steady him, and having your breast nearby gives him lunch when he is ready. Skin-to-skin encourages breastfeeding and also stabilizes and comforts your baby.

instruction: My baby mouths my nipple but won't suckle. What can I try to get him to start sucking?
output: Compress your breast between your thumb and fingers so that a few drops gather on your nipple; that may encourage him to start sucking.

instruction: Can I let my baby doze on my chest or at my breast, or should I avoid that until he can really nurse?
output: You can let him doze on your chest or at your breast as much as you like. Having your breast nearby and offering relaxed, happy, low-pressure feeding times helps remind him that your chest is a satisfying place and encourages breastfeeding while stabilizing and comforting your baby.

instruction: I've heard about mothers licking their babies. Why do mammals lick newborns, and does it ever help in humans?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. All mammals lick their babies after birth not only to clean them but also to stimulate breathing, digestion, and neural responses; licking also shows affection and enhances the sense of smell. Human mothers don't typically think to do this, but some mothers have felt drawn to it and their babies have responded surprisingly well.

instruction: If my baby hasn't nursed by several days of age even with skilled help, what else could I try to help breastfeeding start?
output: If your baby hasn't nursed by several days of age even with skilled help, you could try a nipple shield. Keep feeding times relaxed, happy, and low-pressure; play at breastfeeding when you both feel like it. Time, trust in the process, and a skilled helper are your best allies; almost all babies begin nursing, though it may take several weeks or more.

instruction: What should I do with the first large amount of colostrum some mothers can express in the hours after birth?
output: Most mothers can express more colostrum in the first hours post-birth than they get later; this is the large first meal your baby would normally get. If your baby isn't nursing, you can ask for help expressing it and feed those drops by spoon, dropper, or other supportive feeding methods until your baby is ready to breastfeed.

instruction: When should I consider renting a pump if my baby still isn't nursing?
output: If your baby isn't nursing by Day Three, when your volume starts to increase, you can continue to hand-express or get a rental-grade pump. Most hospitals have rental-grade pumps readily available or you can contact a local leader for sources.

instruction: Could giving lots of food at the very beginning affect my baby's future feeding needs?
output: There is some evidence that giving lots of food at first may rev up his metabolism and cause him to require more. A little colostrum goes a long way.

instruction: How can I use a pump funnel and bottle to feed my baby small amounts of expressed milk?
output: If you're hand-expressing into a pump funnel or pumping, you can wipe precious drops off the funnel with your finger and let your baby suck them off. Collect colostrum from the pump's bottle with an eyedropper or medicine dropper to feed the baby small amounts.

instruction: What support should I keep in place while I'm working on breastfeeding?
output: Keep in touch with someone who really understands breastfeeding and whose approach you respect and enjoy. A book can give general information, but for real help you need someone who understands breastfeeding personally and can provide skilled help and encouragement.

instruction: My newborn just mouths my nipple but won't suckle. What can I try to encourage him to start sucking?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. You can compress your breast between your thumb and fingers so a few drops gather on the nipple to encourage sucking. Also keep your baby close: let him doze on your chest or at your breast and have your breast nearby during relaxed, happy feeding times to encourage breastfeeding and comfort him.

instruction: Is it okay to let my baby doze on my chest or at my breast?
output: Yes. Letting your baby doze on your chest or at your breast as much as you like can encourage breastfeeding and helps stabilize and comfort your baby.

instruction: I’ve heard some mothers lick their babies after birth. What does that do and do babies respond?
output: Many parents wonder about this. All mammals lick their babies after birth to clean them and to stimulate breathing, digestion, and neural responses; licking is also used to show affection and enhance the baby’s sense of smell. Some human mothers who have licked their babies have found that their babies responded surprisingly well.

instruction: My baby still hasn't nursed after several days even with help. What options does the book suggest?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. One option mentioned is to try a nipple shield. Also keep the Three Keeps in mind—keep your milk flowing, keep your baby fed, and keep your baby close—and stay in touch with a knowledgeable breastfeeding helper such as a La Leche League Leader or an IBCLC (you can look for IBCLCs through ilca.org).

instruction: How should I approach feeding times if breastfeeding is not going smoothly?
output: Feeding times should be relaxed, happy, and low-pressure. Play at breastfeeding when you both feel like it instead of working at it because it’s mealtime. Time, trust in the process, and a skilled helper are your best allies.

instruction: What qualities should I look for in a breastfeeding helper?
output: Expect a helper to ask questions before giving ideas; be kind and respectful to you and your baby; be endlessly patient; be fully supportive of your choices and goals; know other people and places to turn to; have books on how to help; keep up to date on research; adjust ideas to your situation; be ready to change suggestions that aren’t working; leave you feeling better and stronger; and stick with you as long as you want.

instruction: Where can I find a local breastfeeding helper or consultant?
output: A good place to start is La Leche League if there is a Group or Leader in your community. You can also check ilca.org for a list of International Board Certified Lactation Consultants (IBCLCs) near you. Larger cities often have more resources.

instruction: How long might it take for my baby to start nursing?
output: Almost all babies begin nursing, though it may take several weeks or more. Babies have started at a variety of ages, including two weeks, four weeks, six weeks, three months, six months, and so on.

instruction: I'm really worried breastfeeding will never happen for us. Should I give up?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. It will work for many families—La Leche League Leaders focus on keeping the mother’s faith until the baby starts nursing—and in the meantime there are ways to keep your milk supply strong and supports to seek out.

instruction: Can premature or small or sick babies breastfeed?
output: Yes. The book says early babies, small babies, and sick babies are all designed to feed themselves at breast.

instruction: How can I get better at breastfeeding — are there tips for practice?
output: Practice makes perfect: it takes time for you and your baby to get the hang of this skill. You’ll breastfeed very frequently and get lots of opportunities to practice. Over time you’ll find positions and styles that fit your body and your baby’s preferences.

instruction: If a suggestion from a breastfeeding helper isn't working, what should they do?
output: A good helper should be ready to change any suggestion that’s not working for you and adjust information and ideas to your situation.

instruction: Are there recommended books for latching approaches I can read?
output: Some recommended books listed include Breastfeeding Made Simple by Nancy Mohrbacher and Kathleen Kendall-Tackett; The Latch by Jack Newman and Teresa Pitman; Mother-Baby Experiences of Nurturing by Suzanne Colson; and Supporting Sucking Skills in Breastfeeding Infants edited by Catherine Watson Genna.

instruction: What are the "Three Keeps" I should remember while breastfeeding?
output: The Three Keeps are: Keep your milk flowing, Keep your baby fed, and Keep your baby close. The excerpt also adds a Keep to stay in touch with someone who really understands breastfeeding.

instruction: My baby isn't latching reliably yet. Will they ever learn to latch, or should I be worried?
output: It will work. La Leche League Leaders don’t worry that the baby will never latch; they worry that the mother won’t be able to keep the faith until it happens. It takes time and frequent breastfeeding gives you lots of practice.

instruction: How long does it usually take to get the hang of breastfeeding?
output: It takes time for you and your baby to really get the hang of this new skill. Fortunately you’ll be breastfeeding very frequently and getting lots of opportunities to practice.

instruction: My newborn was born early — can an early baby breastfeed? I'm scared they won't be able to.
output: Babies are built to breastfeed. Early babies, small babies, sick babies — they’re all designed to feed themselves at breast. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: Where will I be able to nurse once we figure it out? I worry about being stuck sitting in one awkward chair.
output: Eventually you’ll be able to nurse in many places: on a chair and a sofa, in a parked car and in the park, lying on your side in bed or sitting cross-legged on the floor, even getting up to answer the door. You may also develop a style tailored to your body and your baby’s preferences.

instruction: While we're working on the latch, what can I do about my milk supply?
output: There are ways to keep your milk supply strong while you’re working on latch. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: I haven't found a comfortable nursing position yet. Will I ever find one that fits me?
output: You might find a style wildly different from anything mentioned — something tailored to your breast length, waist length, favorite postures, and your baby’s preferences. Eventually you and your baby will be pros.

instruction: Are there recommended resources for different latching approaches I can read or watch?
output: Yes. Recommended resources include: "Breastfeeding Made Simple" by Nancy Mohrbacher and Kathleen Kendall-Tackett; "The Latch" by Jack Newman and Teresa Pitman; "Mother-Baby Experiences of Nurturing" by Suzanne Colson; "Supporting Sucking Skills in Breastfeeding Infants" edited by Catherine Watson Genna; "Baby-Led Breastfeeding" by Christina Smillie (DVD); "Biological Nurturing: Laid-back Breastfeeding" by Suzanne Colson (DVD); and "Follow Me Mum" by Rebecca Glover (DVD).

instruction: Do La Leche League Leaders ever think a mother should stop trying to get a latch?
output: When La Leche League Leaders work with mothers, they don’t worry that the baby will never latch. Their concern is that the mother won’t be able to keep the faith until it happens.

instruction: Will I eventually be able to breastfeed without needing books or constant help?
output: Yes. At that point you’ll be able to put all the breastfeeding books back on the shelf. Your baby will be a pro. And you will be, too.

instruction: Can I breastfeed lying on my side in bed, or do I have to sit up?
output: Yes, you can breastfeed lying on your side in bed. The excerpt also mentions nursing while sitting cross-legged on the floor and in many other positions.

instruction: Are there technical books for helpers on supporting sucking skills?
output: Yes. "Supporting Sucking Skills in Breastfeeding Infants," edited by Catherine Watson Genna, is noted as an excellent, more technical book for a breastfeeding helper.

instruction: Do any of the recommended resources come as DVDs if I prefer watching to reading?
output: Yes. The excerpt lists DVDs: "Baby-Led Breastfeeding" by Christina Smillie, "Biological Nurturing: Laid-back Breastfeeding" by Suzanne Colson, and "Follow Me Mum" by Rebecca Glover.

instruction: How long should I do skin-to-skin with my newborn right after birth?
output: For at least the first two hours of your baby's life, keep your baby bare or diaper-only on your bare chest, skin to skin.

instruction: Do I need to have my baby weighed and get ointments right away, or can we wait?
output: Measurements, ointments, and similar procedures can wait. The first couple of hours are meant for skin-to-skin time together.

instruction: What's the right way to position my baby for skin-to-skin?
output: Place the baby on your bare chest, chest to chest. If you are wearing a shirt or gown, open it. Lean back comfortably so gravity holds the baby against you; you do not need to lie on your back. A regular blanket over both of you is enough.

instruction: What are the benefits of doing skin-to-skin with my baby right after birth?
output: Skin-to-skin helps stabilize the baby's heart rate, breathing, and temperature; it stabilizes your temperature; it can prevent baby blues later on; it reduces the baby's stress and pain from medical procedures; it reduces your stress; it increases interactions between you and your baby; and it increases the likelihood and length of breastfeeding.

instruction: How much will my baby eat in the first days? It seems very little — is that normal?
output: Yes. The colostrum your baby gets at first is small in quantity, which matches the baby's small stomach size (about the size of a large marble at birth).

instruction: My baby had sticky black poop — is that normal and will it stain cloth diapers?
output: The baby's first poop is sticky and black; that is the meconium. It can stain cloth, so many parents use disposables until all the meconium has passed.

instruction: How long will my postpartum bleeding last and what should I plan for?
output: You will have bleeding like a heavy menstrual period that can last for a couple of weeks. At times the flow can be really heavy, so you may want to use overnight pads and keep a pad or dark-colored towel on the bed at first.

instruction: I stood up and had a big gush of blood — is that expected?
output: Standing up the first time can cause a noticeable gush. This can happen during the early postpartum days.

instruction: When I breastfeed, I sometimes feel strong cramps and a gush of blood — is that normal?
output: When you breastfeed, your uterus contracts in response to oxytocin released by the baby's suckling, and you may get a gush of blood each time. The cramps can be strong, sometimes almost like labor, but they will soon fade.

instruction: What can I try when my newborn fusses and breastfeeding doesn't calm him?
output: Try breastfeeding first. If that doesn't work, try walking with him, talking or singing, a gentle bouncy step he might like, or going outside. Different things can soothe him.

instruction: How will I learn what my baby needs — are there signs I can look for?
output: Babies make little noises and have body signals you can learn: they may hold their body still for a minute before peeing; they may squirm and rub their fists against their face when tired and want to sleep; and they may bob their head against your chest when they want to nurse again. Over time you learn to identify these signals.

instruction: Is it okay to put my baby in a sling and go for a short walk soon after birth?
output: Yes. When the weather is nice, putting the baby in a sling and taking short walks can feel good and the fresh air and movement can help.

instruction: I'm worried because my recovery was harder — I had surgery and breastfeeding isn't easy. Will things get better?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Your early days may not go as smoothly if you're recovering from surgery, feeling after-effects of labor medications, or struggling with breastfeeding, but both you and your baby have instincts and skills that can help you work through early challenges.

instruction: I want my baby to stay close — is there any reason not to keep him in my sight a lot?
output: Many parents wonder about this. There is no reason your baby should be out of your sight in those early days; being close lets you notice cues and interact more with your baby.

instruction: Why are there so few wet diapers at first after birth?
output: Newborns get colostrum in small amounts, and their stomach at birth is about the size of a large marble, so there aren’t many diapers to change at first.

instruction: My baby’s first poop is black and sticky — is that normal and will it stain cloth diapers?
output: Yes. The first poop (meconium) is sticky and black; it’s the wastes that filled your baby’s intestines before birth. If you plan to use cloth diapers, you might want to use disposables long enough to get all the meconium because it can stain cloth.

instruction: How long does postpartum bleeding (lochia) last and what should I have on hand at home?
