output: The bleeding called lochia can last for a couple of weeks and can at times be really heavy. Standing up the first time can cause quite a gush. Many people use overnight pads and keep a pad or a dark-colored towel on the bed at first.

instruction: When I breastfeed, I get cramping and sometimes a gush of blood — is that expected?
output: Yes. When you breastfeed, your uterus contracts in response to oxytocin released by your baby’s suckling, and you may get a gush of blood each time. The cramps soon fade but can sometimes be almost labor-strong.

instruction: I feel starving after giving birth — is that normal?
output: Yes. Unless you’re recovering from surgery, you’ll probably be hungry after the birth. You’ve just had a real workout, so it’s common to want real food right after delivery.

instruction: I can’t sleep well after the baby is born — what causes that and will keeping the baby with me help?
output: You may sleep lightly because pregnancy levels of progesterone and estrogen drop quickly after birth and being a new mother raises your alertness. Keep your baby with you, and you’ll probably sleep better.

instruction: I’m overwhelmed by visitors in the hospital — what can I do to limit visits?
output: If you’re in the hospital, ask the staff to stop visitors at reception and suggest they visit when you get home. If you’re at home, post short visiting hours on the door and use strategies like a refrigerator list to direct visitors’ energy.

instruction: I’m leaking urine when I cough and my shirts get soaked with milk — is this normal and how long will it last?
output: Leaking in several ways is common after birth. Some women have temporary bladder leaking when they cough or sneeze. As your milk comes in, you may find you can’t keep a shirt dry. These kinds of leaking are described as temporary.

instruction: I’ve been crying a lot and feel emotional for days — should I be worried?
output: Many parents have tears and emotional days, and it’s common to have questions like this. If you find you are feeling anxious and depressed for days at a time, be sure to talk to your doctor or midwife as soon as possible because it could be postpartum depression.

instruction: I had a lot of IV fluids during labor and still look puffy — is that expected and will it affect my milk?
output: Yes. If you’ve been given a lot of IV fluids, you may look more pregnant after the birth with swollen eyes, fingers, and breasts. If you were on an IV for several days, expect to spend several weeks getting your basic body back, and be prepared for a possible delay in having your milk increase in volume. Eating protein or a lot of watermelon may help you unload the fluids.

instruction: Any tips for getting through the first few nights at home with a newborn?
output: Take plenty of naps, even an evening nap, and prepare your bedtime nest. Some parents set an energy bar on the bedside table so they don’t have to manage a wrapper at 3 a.m., and keep water or juice beside the bed because new mothers can get very thirsty.

instruction: Will my baby suddenly start needing lots of diaper changes once milk comes in?
output: Yes. Once your milk comes in, your baby will start filling a lot of diapers, so you should expect more diaper changes then compared with the very first days.

instruction: Does increasing interaction with my baby help breastfeeding last longer?
output: Increasing interactions between you and your baby increases the likelihood and length of breastfeeding.

instruction: I don’t feel immediate love for my baby and I feel guilty — could that change?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Some people fall in love with their baby instantly, while for others love can creep up over the first month or two; either pattern is described as coming eventually.

instruction: What is meconium and why is the first stool different?
output: Meconium is the sticky black first poop and consists of the wastes that filled your baby’s intestines before birth, which is why the first stools look different from later ones.

instruction: I'm still very swollen after delivery. Could that be from IV fluids, and what might help reduce the swelling?
output: Yes. If you were given a lot of IV fluids you may look more pregnant after the birth with swollen eyes, fingers, breasts, and more. An epidural can lower blood pressure so fluids may have been given to keep it normal. Eating protein or a lot of watermelon may help you unload the fluids. If you were on an IV for several days, expect to spend several weeks getting your basic body back and there may be a possible delay in your milk increasing in volume.

instruction: I've been feeling anxious and down for several days. What should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you find you are feeling anxious and depressed for days at a time, be sure to talk to your doctor or midwife as soon as possible. This could be postpartum depression, and getting help quickly can help you get back to enjoying your baby.

instruction: How long should I let my newborn nurse in the first few days?
output: Your baby can nurse as long as he wants. An unmedicated baby may nurse for an hour or more at a time in the first few days. A medicated, sleepy baby may need encouragement to stay awake and nurse longer.

instruction: My baby is very sleepy from labor medications. How often should I wake him to feed?
output: If your baby is too zonked from birth medications or jaundice to wake on his own, you can wake him up every three hours or so. Start about six hours after birth and aim for at least eight feedings in twenty-four hours, counting from the beginning of one feeding to the beginning of the next.

instruction: Can I switch breasts whenever I get uncomfortable while nursing?
output: Yes. In the first few days, if your nipple, arm, or nose makes you want to switch sides before the baby has stopped sucking, that’s fine. Change sides or don’t for any reason you like. Use both sides during the day, but there’s no need to obsess about giving exactly equal time.

instruction: My nipples hurt and I'm not sure the baby should nurse so long. What can I do?
output: Your nipples are up to the task—even for twins or more—but if they are calling for relief you might want to find a good La Leche League leader or other breastfeeding helper and do some problem solving.

instruction: How do I break my baby's suction safely if I need to get up during a feed?
output: Slip a finger in the corner of your baby’s mouth to break the suction. Use a finger about the size of your nipple, slide it into the corner between his gums, and turn your finger as you’d turn a screwdriver in a paint can lid. Then you can slide your nipple out without the gums clamping on it.

instruction: I had an IV for several days — could that delay my milk coming in?
output: Yes. If you were on an IV for several days, expect to spend several weeks getting your basic body back, and be prepared for a possible delay in having your milk increase in volume.

instruction: I don't feel an immediate rush of love for my baby. Is that normal?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Love can come as a thunderbolt, or it can creep up over the first month or two. Some mothers fall in love instantly; others notice that love grows over weeks. The passage describes both experiences and reassures that love does come.

instruction: Any practical tips for handling the first few nights at home with a newborn?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Take plenty of naps, even an evening nap, and prepare your bedtime nest. Keep an energy bar unwrapped on your bedside table so you can manage it at 3 a.m., and have water or juice beside your bed because new mothers can get very thirsty. Your nights will settle down, but not instantly.

instruction: Can keeping my baby in the bed with me help if he has jaundice?
output: In the example given, hospitals removed plastic cots and put the babies in the beds with their mothers. The mothers fed all day and the jaundice stopped; the nurses then walked around fine-tuning care. Rooming-in and frequent feeding helped in that situation.

instruction: What should I do if my baby falls asleep after only a few minutes of nursing?
output: If he falls asleep or stops nursing actively within a few minutes, you can encourage him to continue. If he’s too fuzzy to manage on his own, hand expression can carry him along in these first few days.

instruction: My baby got pain meds during delivery and won't wake to nurse. When should I wake him to feed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby is too zonked from birth medications or jaundice to wake on his own, you can wake him up every three hours or so, counting from the beginning of one feeding to the beginning of the next. Start about six hours after birth and aim for at least eight feedings in twenty-four hours.

instruction: How can I safely break my baby's suction if I need to get up for a moment?
output: Slide a finger into the corner of your baby’s mouth to break the suction. Use a finger about the size of your nipple, slide it into the corner between his gums, and turn your finger as you’d turn a screwdriver in a paint can lid. Then you can slide your nipple out without risk of the gums clamping on it.

instruction: Can I keep nursing on the first breast until either of us wants to stop?
output: Yes. If your baby is latched on and positioned comfortably, you can nurse from the first breast until one of you wants to stop. Sometimes he’ll take the other breast and sometimes he won’t, which is fine.

instruction: My newborn falls asleep after a few minutes of nursing. Should I try to get him to keep going?
output: If he falls asleep or stops nursing actively within a few minutes, you can encourage him to continue. If he’s too fuzzy to manage on his own in the first few days, hand expression can help carry him along.

instruction: What can I do if my baby is too fuzzy to nurse well in the first days?
output: Hand expression can carry him along in these first few days when he’s too fuzzy to manage nursing on his own.

instruction: Is it harmful if the hospital keeps my baby in a central nursery away from me at night?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Centralized nurseries are declining, and having your baby away from you is described as unnatural for both of you. When the baby isn’t with you, you lose control over what happens to him and whether he is supplemented; his heart rate, breathing, and temperature are less stable, his feeding cues are likely to be missed, and your milk generally comes in more slowly and less fully.

instruction: What does rooming-in mean in the hospital?
output: Rooming-in means the baby is in a bassinet or Isolette in the same room as the mother. You can have the baby on your chest, in your arms, or at your side as much as you like during the day, though many hospitals don’t want a mother to have her baby in her bed at night.

instruction: What is a sidecar bed in the hospital?
output: A sidecar bed is a baby bed attached to the mother’s hospital bed, used instead of a plastic box bassinet so the baby is next to the mother.

instruction: What does bedding-in mean and what does it do?
output: Bedding-in means mother and baby share the same bed both day and night, nursing as they choose. This helps them begin to synchronize their sleep patterns and is said to bring in a faster, more abundant milk supply.

instruction: I'm taking painkillers after delivery. Is it safe to sleep with my baby in bed with me?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you’re using painkillers, sleeping pills, or other consciousness-altering medications, your baby will be safer in someone else’s arms or in a bedside bassinet when you feel drowsy or plan to sleep.

instruction: How can a double bed make sharing sleep safer in the hospital?
output: Some hospitals now have double beds, enabling mother, partner, and baby to sleep together safely by putting the baby between the two adults at breast height so that pillows aren’t an issue.

instruction: Can I ask nurses for different sleeping or feeding arrangements in the hospital?
output: You can discuss arrangements with the friendliest nurse on that shift, and you have the right to switch nurses if yours is a mismatch.

instruction: I heard about hospitals getting rid of bassinets because of jaundice problems. What happened?
output: In one account, hospitals got rid of the plastic cots and put babies in beds with their mothers; the jaundice stopped. Mothers fed all day and nurses helped with positioning; problems like hypoglycemia, engorgement, sore nipples, and babies unable to latch were not seen, and the babies were often quiet with frequent suckling.

instruction: How many times should a newborn feed in 24 hours and when to start timing feedings?
output: Start counting from the beginning of one feeding to the beginning of the next about six hours after birth, and aim for at least eight feedings in twenty-four hours.

instruction: If I need to step away briefly while my baby is latched, will he want to nurse again when I come back?
output: You can slip a finger into the corner of his mouth to break the suction and do what you need to do; your baby may want to nurse again once you come back, and you can offer the breast if he seems interested.

instruction: Can I keep my newborn skin-to-skin in the hospital during the day?
output: Yes. Rooming-in is common: you can have your baby on your chest, in your arms, or at your side as much as you like during the day. Many hospitals place the baby in a bassinet or Isolette in the same room, and some offer sidecar beds as alternatives.

instruction: What are the benefits of 'bedding-in' (sharing the same bed with my baby)?
output: Bedding-in means mother and baby share the same bed day and night. It lets you nurse whenever you choose, helps your sleep patterns begin to synchronize with the baby's, encourages bonding, and is associated with a faster, more abundant milk supply.

instruction: Can my baby sleep in my hospital bed at night?
output: Many hospitals do not want a mother to have her baby in her bed at night. Some hospitals use a sidecar bed attached to the mother's bed instead, and rooming-in usually places the baby in a bassinet or Isolette in the same room.

instruction: I'm taking painkillers after delivery. Is it safe to share sleep with my baby in the hospital?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you are using painkillers, sleeping pills, or other consciousness-altering medications, your baby will be safer in someone else's arms or in a bedside bassinet when you feel drowsy or plan to sleep.

instruction: Do any hospitals offer beds that make co-sleeping with a partner easier and safer?
output: Yes. Some hospitals now have double beds that let mother, partner, and baby sleep together safely by placing the baby between the two adults at breast height so pillows aren't an issue.

instruction: If I don't like my nurse's approach to rooming-in, can I ask for someone else?
output: Yes. You can discuss arrangements with the friendliest nurse on that shift, and you have the right to switch nurses if yours is a mismatch.

instruction: How worried should I be about giving my newborn a little formula in the first days?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Newborn intestines are sterile and open early on, and they need your milk to help set up protective intestinal bacteria and provide a temporary seal while the intestines mature. Giving formula in the first days is not something to be taken lightly because it can affect the baby's intestinal bacteria and their protection.

instruction: If my baby gets a little formula now, how long can it affect their intestinal bacteria?
output: A little formula after the early days can alter your baby's protective intestinal bacteria for a full month. Also, a little formula anytime in the first few months before the intestines are well sealed can increase the risk of allergy and diabetes in susceptible children; the risk may be highest in the first few days and in babies born even a little early.

instruction: What can hospitals offer if my milk isn't available right after birth?
output: If your milk isn't available, banked human colostrum or mature milk is the next best choice. More hospitals are keeping banked milk on hand, and it pays to find out in advance what your birthplace does; if they don't have banked milk you could ask them to consider it.

instruction: What should I expect for my full-term baby's pees and poos in the first week?
output: In the first 24 hours, a slightly wet diaper and a black, tarry stool are normal. By the next 24 hours the baby will move most meconium and produce a little more urine. By the third day expect real changes in stool color, and by the end of the week you will likely see yellow stools and noticeably heavy diapers.

instruction: I see orange-red specks in my newborn's diaper on day three — is that bleeding?
output: Those orange-red deposits are commonly called brick dust or urate crystals. They are highly concentrated urine from not getting enough fluid and are very normal for breastfed babies on the third day right before the milk comes in; they will be gone as milk volume increases. They are often mistaken for bleeding after a baby boy's circumcision or a baby girl's normal vaginal bleeding from pregnancy hormones.

instruction: When should I contact someone about orange-red deposits in the diaper?
output: If the orange-red deposits continue after the fifth day, especially if your baby is having fewer than three poops a day, contact your baby's doctor and your breastfeeding helper.

instruction: Does being born by Cesarean change my baby's need for my milk or presence?
output: Babies born by Cesarean don't pick up their normal bacteria during birth, are at higher risk of picking up hospital bacteria, and may be in even greater need of your presence and your milk.

instruction: Do I need to record every feeding and diaper change in the hospital?
output: You may be tempted to track many details, but the details aren't important. In the early days you might watch poopy diapers and other signs, but once your baby has a solid pattern of growth people stop looking at the details.

instruction: How should my full-term baby's diapers look in the first 24 hours?
output: A full-term baby having a slightly wet diaper and a black, tarry stool in the first 24 hours is doing absolutely fine.

instruction: What changes in poop and urine should I expect by day 2 and day 3?
output: By the second 24 hours your baby will get enough colostrum to move most of the meconium and make a bit more urine. By the third day you should see real changes in the color of the poops.

instruction: When should I expect yellow poop and heavier diapers?
output: By the end of the first week you will probably be well into yellow poops and noticeably heavy diapers.

instruction: Why doesn't the newborn chart record pees?
output: The chart doesn't record pees because they aren't as important; in the first few days you may not see many wet diapers, and any bowel movements are usually accompanied by some urine.

instruction: I saw orange-red deposits in the diaper on day 3 — what is that and should I be worried?
output: Those orange-red deposits are commonly called brick dust or urate crystals; they are highly concentrated urine from not getting enough fluid. Many parents wonder about this. It is very normal for breastfed babies on the third day right before the milk comes in and will go away as milk volume increases.

instruction: What should I do if the orange-red deposits continue after day five and the baby has fewer than three poops a day?
output: If the deposits continue after the fifth day, especially with fewer than three poops a day, contact your baby's doctor and your breastfeeding helper.

instruction: How much weight loss after birth is considered acceptable?
output: Conventional wisdom says babies shouldn't lose more than 7 percent of their body weight before they start gaining, absolutely shouldn't lose more than 10 percent, and should be back at birth weight by two weeks. Note that IV fluids given to the mother can affect the apparent birth weight.

instruction: My baby lost a good amount of weight but is eager to feed, swallowing well, and producing more diapers—should I worry?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby has lost considerable weight but is eager and organized when he eats, is swallowing well, and is starting to produce more diapers, then he is probably doing fine; watch to make sure good progress continues.

instruction: My baby hasn't lost much weight but is sleepy, not swallowing, and not pooping — what should I do?
output: If your baby is sleepy, not swallowing, and not pooping, he probably needs help. You may need to feed him more often or express extra milk to get more food into him and to stimulate your breasts. Check with an LLL Leader, other breastfeeding helper, or your doctor if you don't see more pooping within a day or two.

instruction: Are issues like hypothermia, hypoglycemia, jaundice, engorgement, and excessive weight loss linked to anything specific in the early days?
output: Those problems are related to sleepiness and separation in the early days.

instruction: How can I best avoid early sleepiness and separation for my newborn?
output: The best way to avoid sleepiness and separation is to have an unmedicated birth and to keep your baby on or against your body most of the time in the early days.

instruction: I'm nervous about going home alone with a newborn — will the baby break if I hold him wrong or skip baths for a while?
output: Your baby won't break if you hold him wrong, and it's okay to delay bathing; babies are resilient, and your instincts and common sense will guide you. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Can maternal IV fluids during labor affect my baby's apparent weight loss after birth?
output: Yes. A mother who had a lot of IV fluids may deliver a baby who appears to weigh more at birth and thus seems to lose more weight afterward; this can make weight-loss measurements misleading.

instruction: My newborn sleeps for 4–5 hours at a stretch. Should I wake him to feed?
output: Many parents worry about this. If you had no birth medications, your baby stays in physical contact with you, he eats well when he eats, and he is gaining well, then sleeping longer than three hours is probably just who he is. Keep an eye on his weight.

instruction: Every time I put my baby down after nursing he screams and looks for the breast. Why is he doing this?
output: Your baby has discovered that the breast is the most comforting and comfortable place for him — the closest thing to his womb. He may protest when you take him off and root for the breast as a way of settling and comforting. This can happen even when your milk has come in early.

instruction: When my baby dozes at the breast, should I move him or try to burp him?
output: When he drifts off at the breast, don’t move him except to pillow his head more comfortably. Don’t try to burp him; snuggle with him until he falls into a deeper sleep with even breathing and no eyelid movement, or keep holding him.

instruction: Is it okay to keep my newborn against my body most of the time in the early days?
output: Yes. Keeping your baby on or against your body most of the time in the early days is described as the best way to avoid sleepiness and separation, especially if the birth was unmedicated.

instruction: Should I take my newborn's mittens off or leave them on so he won't scratch himself?
output: Take the mittens off. Babies may scratch themselves, but they are fast healers.

instruction: My baby makes weird throat noises and coughs sometimes. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Newborns can make strange sounds because they can’t clear their throat yet, so you may hear some freaky noises at times.

instruction: Can I skip bathing my newborn for a while if I'm tired or worried about water temperature?
output: Your baby won’t break if you don’t give him a bath for a month, and a bath with water that’s a bit too cool is not going to harm him.

instruction: What causes the 'second night' wakefulness and lots of crying?
output: On the second night your baby may suddenly realize he’s no longer in the womb and find it scary. He has been handled by many people and is adjusting to new noises, lights, sounds, and smells. He may discover his voice and protest being taken off the breast, rooting and crying for comfort.

instruction: Does my baby touching my breast while nursing do anything for my milk?
output: Yes. Babies feel comforted by touching with their hands, and his touch on your breast will increase your oxytocin levels and help boost your milk supply.

instruction: Are questions about newborn worries silly to ask leaders or other parents?
output: No question is silly. Many mothers call Leaders about common issues in the first few days, and getting answers will help you become fluent in this new language of parenting.

instruction: If my baby sleeps more than three hours and we were separated after birth, should I be concerned?
output: A baby away from his mother may shut down to conserve energy and sleep longer than he normally would; in such cases sleeping more than three hours can indicate the need for intervention. Keep an eye on his weight.

instruction: Which early problems are linked to sleepiness and separation?
output: Hypothermia, hypoglycemia, jaundice, engorgement, and excessive infant weight loss are all problems related to sleepiness and separation.

instruction: If I had an epidural, could that affect how easily my baby wakes to feed?
output: Yes. A baby whose mother had birth medications may be unable to rouse himself normally for a while and may sleep longer than he would otherwise.

instruction: What should I do if my baby roots and seems to want the breast after I've put him down?
output: That’s fine — it’s his way of settling and comforting. If he starts rooting and acting as though he wants to go back to the breast, let him do so; extra snuggling at the breast is often what he needs.

instruction: Is it normal for a newborn to startle, cough, or sneeze a lot in the first days?
output: Yes. Newborns can cough, sneeze, or startle, and these things are described as normal in the early days.

instruction: I’m worried my baby sleeps too much and isn’t getting enough milk. What signs in the first days should I watch?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Watch that he eats well when he does feed and that he’s gaining weight; if you had no medications and you keep him in contact with you, long stretches of sleep over three hours are often just his pattern, but monitor his weight.

instruction: My newborn sleeps for 4 hours at a stretch — should I wake him to feed?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If you had no birth medications, your baby stays in physical contact with you, eats well when he eats, and is gaining well, sleeping longer than three hours at a stretch is probably just who he is; keep an eye on his weight. If you had an epidural or other birth medications and your baby isn’t waking at least every two or three hours, it’s better to assume the medications are keeping him asleep and get some food into him.

instruction: I had an epidural — how often should my newborn be roused to feed?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If you had an epidural or other birth medications and your baby isn’t waking at least every two or three hours, it’s better to assume the side effects of the medications are keeping him asleep and to get some food into him.

instruction: What signs show my baby is in a wake-able time?
output: Wake-able times include when you feel some tension in an arm as you lift and drop it, when you see eye movement under the lids, when the mouth makes little sucking movements, or when any part of the baby is making movements; waking will be easier at those times.

instruction: How can I gently wake my baby for a feeding?
output: Dim bright light in her eyes or pull a drape if she faces a window; undress her or put her skin-to-skin against your chest; stroke her and call her name; rub her feet or wipe her face with a damp cloth; handle her gently. If those don’t work, lay her on the bed on her back with no wrapping and gently roll her from side to side, or hold her along your forearms and lift her up and lower her down while talking gently.

instruction: Is it okay to put a little colostrum or milk in my baby's mouth to wake her?
output: As a last resort you can put a little colostrum or milk in her mouth, waiting for her to swallow before adding more; some babies will sleep through a whole meal or two.

instruction: My baby latches and seems to nurse while still asleep — is that okay?
output: It’s fine if your baby latches and nurses in her sleep, especially if she’s in a laid-back breastfeeding position; your real goal is to help her eat.

instruction: The hospital brought my baby back swaddled — what should I do when I get her?
output: If your baby comes back swaddled, unwrap him and snuggle his bare or lightly clothed skin against yours with your blankets over both of you; he will quickly regain the heat he was losing and you can keep him with you from that point on.

instruction: Is swaddling safe for a newborn?
output: Research raises alarms: a swaddled newborn has poorer circulation, a lower temperature (and so does his mother), and gains weight more slowly, probably because he is less able to signal hunger and may be less aware that he is hungry. Mothers whose newborns are swaddled much of the time have more engorgement, lower initial supplies, and tend to handle their babies more roughly. Some babies with special concerns are calmed by swaddling, but it’s not for ordinary babies and certainly not in the first two hours. A swaddled baby is defenseless and can’t move, and sleeping with a swaddled baby is risky.

instruction: Should I swaddle my baby in the first two hours after birth?
output: It’s best not to swaddle an ordinary baby in the first two hours after birth, because the separation swaddling creates can influence how gently you treat your baby for his first year of life.

instruction: Is it safe for a swaddled baby to sleep with me in bed?
output: Babies normally sleep safely with their mothers, but sleeping with a swaddled baby is risky.

instruction: Why might swaddling cause slower weight gain in newborns?
output: Swaddled babies gain weight more slowly probably because they are less able to signal hunger and may be less aware that they are hungry.

instruction: My baby slept through a whole feeding — should I be worried?
output: Many parents wonder about this. An occasional baby will sleep through a whole meal or two; she might also latch and nurse in her sleep, which is fine. The real goal is to help her eat.

instruction: Are nipples supposed to hurt when breastfeeding?
output: Breastfeeding is not supposed to hurt. Ever. Your nipples are not supposed to crack or bleed, and they are not supposed to come out of your baby’s mouth creased, frayed, or warped.

instruction: If my baby was separated from me after birth, why might he sleep more than usual?
output: A baby away from his mother may shut down to conserve energy and sleep longer than he normally would; also a baby whose mother had birth medications may be unable to rouse himself normally for a while.

instruction: Will undressing my baby help wake him for a feeding?
output: Undressing him may do the trick by itself; if it doesn’t, putting him skin-to-skin against your chest or using other gentle methods can help wake him.

instruction: My newborn sometimes falls asleep while nursing or even latches and feeds in her sleep. Is that okay, or should I try to wake her to finish a meal?
output: That can be okay. The occasional baby will sleep through a whole meal or two, and some babies will latch and nurse in their sleep, especially in a laid-back breastfeeding position. The main goal is to help her eat, not to wake her up for its own sake.

instruction: The nurse brought my baby back all swaddled. Should I keep him wrapped or take the swaddle off?
output: Unwrap him and snuggle his bare or lightly clothed skin against yours, with your blankets over both of you. He will quickly regain the heat he was losing, and you can keep him with you from that point on.

instruction: I keep hearing that swaddling is helpful, but is it safe for an ordinary newborn?
output: Swaddling has uses and calms some babies with special concerns, but for ordinary babies it is not recommended. Research raises alarms: the swaddled newborn has poorer circulation, a lower temperature (and the mother’s temperature is lower, too), and swaddled babies tend to gain weight more slowly. Also, swaddled babies are defenseless and can’t move, so sleeping with a swaddled baby is risky.

instruction: Why might a swaddled baby gain weight more slowly?
output: Research suggests a swaddled baby gains weight more slowly probably because he is less able to signal hunger and is maybe less aware that he is hungry.

instruction: Is it okay to swaddle my baby during the first two hours after birth?
output: No. The text advises certainly not to swaddle in the first two hours, because the separation it creates can influence how gently you treat your baby for his first year of life.

instruction: My nipples hurt and sometimes sting when I nurse. Is that normal, and what should I do?
output: Breastfeeding is not supposed to hurt. Nipples may be supersensitive at first and you might feel some stinging for a day or two, and clothing may rub them. Those lesser sensations can be a normal part of getting used to breastfeeding. Actual pain or damage is not normal. Often pain is resolved by letting your baby lead more or making small adjustments to your or his position. If that doesn’t help, find a good helper quickly — ask friends for recommendations or call La Leche League. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: What should I watch or listen for to tell if my baby is actually swallowing milk?
output: Diapers tell you a lot, and by three days or so you’ll probably start to notice swallowing. Good swallowing of ample milk usually sounds like a whispered "keh keh keh" at about one keh per second. You may also see the lower jaw drop a little lower with a hesitation or pause when it drops; that drop-pause-close motion is also a swallow. Not every noise is a swallow, and some babies who nurse well are nearly silent, but watching and listening for these swallows can show that colostrum and milk are going from breast to baby.

instruction: My baby makes quick little sucks at first and then changes — what does that mean?
output: He may make quick, uttery little sucks when he first latches; those mean he’s organizing everything in his mouth and settling into a place where he can massage your breast and trigger a milk release — call it preparing to nurse. After that he’ll probably start some slower sucks, especially if he’s getting nice mouthfuls of colostrum or milk.

instruction: People keep telling me everything looks fine but I’m terrified I'm not doing this right. Any simple advice to get through the first weeks?
output: Put the baby first — he needs to nurse and be held, and those needs will be intense and continuous for a couple of weeks. Keep your baby supplies centralized where you’re likely to be eating, sleeping, and nursing; don’t worry about organizing, just put things in piles. Make several possible places to nurse, sleep, or eat and use whatever works, however silly it looks. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: If rearranging positioning doesn't stop nipple damage, who should I contact for help?
output: Find a good helper as quickly as you can: ask friends for recommendations and call La Leche League. Be cautious about anyone who uses absolute phrases like “the right way” or says the baby is just being lazy. Getting good help earlier will most likely help you feel better faster, body and mind.

instruction: Is it dangerous to sleep with my baby if I swaddle him so he can't move?
output: Yes. A swaddled baby is defenseless and can’t move. While babies normally sleep safely with their mothers, sleeping with a swaddled baby is risky.

instruction: How soon will I hear swallowing sounds at the breast?
output: By about three days or so you’ll probably start to notice swallowing. During the first few days you may not hear a steady, rhythmic swallowing sound because the amount of colostrum a baby takes can vary a lot, but you may hear the whispered "keh" sounds now and then or see the jaw drop-pause-close motion that indicates a swallow.

instruction: Are mothers affected when their newborn is swaddled a lot?
output: Yes. Mothers whose newborns are swaddled much of the time have more engorgement and lower initial milk supplies, and they tend to handle their babies more roughly, according to the research mentioned.

instruction: My baby was nursed well in the hospital bed but we can’t make it work anywhere else. Any practical tips?
output: Make several possible places to nurse and try different setups. One parent put a pad of blankets on the living room floor and used that while they slowly learned other nursing positions. Whatever works, however silly it looks, go for it. There are no Breastfeeding Police who are going to come and check on you.

instruction: My nipples have cracked and bled. Should breastfeeding hurt like this?
output: Breastfeeding is not supposed to hurt. Nipples are not supposed to crack or bleed. That is not normal. Pain or damage is not expected; often small adjustments or letting the baby lead can help, but if problems persist find a good helper quickly — ask friends for recommendations or call La Leche League. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: My newborn doesn't seem to swallow when he first latches. Is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. At first he may make quick, fluttery little sucks as he organizes his mouth and settles into a place to massage your breast and trigger a milk release. After that he will probably start some slower sucks, especially if he is getting nice mouthfuls of colostrum or milk.

instruction: How can I tell if breastfeeding is actually working?
output: Colostrum and milk really going from breast to baby is a sign it's working. Babies often move from quick, fluttery organizing sucks to slower sucks when they are getting mouthfuls of colostrum or milk.

instruction: I'm terrified and overwhelmed — what should I focus on in the first days with the baby?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Put the baby first: he needs to nurse and be held, and those needs will be intense and fairly continuous for a couple of weeks. After the baby, put yourself next so you can rest, spend time with the baby, and eat and drink; your partner or a helper can tend to you. Other tasks can wait.

instruction: Where should I nurse the baby at home?
output: Make several possible places to nurse, sleep, or eat. Keep baby supplies centralized where you are likely to be eating, sleeping, and nursing. Use whatever works for you — for example, some people use a pad of blankets on the living room floor while learning other positions.

instruction: What practical things can my partner do to help with breastfeeding?
output: Feed the mother and keep food on hand, especially easy-to-eat, one-handed foods and foods high in fiber. Set up a sleeping arrangement that works, trying different options and staying flexible. Monitor visitors and read the mother's signals, run errands like groceries and mail, and call for breastfeeding help if the mother wants that assistance.

instruction: I'm worried about people visiting — how should visitors be handled after the baby arrives?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Monitor visitors: read the mother's signals and shoo people out if they've overstayed, or refer them to a job list on the refrigerator so they can help before they go.

instruction: I feel clumsy learning to breastfeed. Will I get better?
output: It's common to have questions like this. Breastfeeding is compared to learning to ride a bicycle: you may wobble at first and fall, but with practice you learn. Becoming a mother is like learning to swim — you take a deeper breath, start to paddle, and realize you're doing it; instinct, common sense, and trial and error help you learn and it works.

instruction: What does 'put yourself solidly next' mean for a new mother?
output: It means you need rest, time with the baby, and something to eat and drink as you heal. Let your partner, family, friend, or postpartum doula tend you while you tend the baby.

instruction: Should I try to organize everything perfectly for the baby right away?
output: Don’t worry about organizing everything at first. Keep supplies centralized and in piles if needed; you’ll have lots of time to streamline and organize as you go. Go with the flow of the moment rather than trying to establish a perfect pattern from the start.

instruction: Can my partner call a breastfeeding helper for us, or does the mother need to call?
output: Ordinarily breastfeeding helpers want the mother to call so they know she really wants involvement. But if it’s a few days post-birth and a helper hears the mother in the background via the partner giving information, helpers understand what’s going on and can respond.

instruction: The baby only makes quick little sucks and then stops — does that mean he's not getting colostrum?
output: At first quick, fluttery little sucks can mean he is organizing his mouth and settling into place to massage your breast and trigger a milk release. After that he will probably start slower sucks, especially when he is getting mouthfuls of colostrum or milk.

instruction: How long will the very intense nursing and holding last after birth?
output: The baby needs to nurse and be held intensely and fairly continuously for just a couple of weeks.

instruction: Is it okay to use funny or makeshift nursing positions while learning?
output: Yes. Use whatever works, however silly it looks. There are no Breastfeeding Police — the priority is finding positions that help you and the baby learn to nurse.

instruction: What simple errands should my partner expect to take over after the baby comes?
output: Running errands can include dropping off mail, picking up groceries, and doing errands the mother usually ran. These tasks help keep life going while things are new and unsettled.

instruction: How can we set up sleeping so everyone can manage the new baby?
output: Set up a sleeping arrangement that works for all of you. You may need to try a few different arrangements and it may involve separate sleeping spaces for a while. Be flexible.

instruction: Should I call a breastfeeding helper for my partner, or should she call herself?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure. LLL Leaders and other breastfeeding helpers ordinarily want the mother herself to call so they know she really wants their involvement. But if it is only a few days after birth and the helper hears the mother in the background giving information to repeat, they know exactly what’s going on and can help.

instruction: What do breastfeeding helpers usually prefer about who contacts them?
output: They ordinarily want the mother herself to call so they know she really wants their involvement.

instruction: If the mother is in the background and I give the helper information for her, will they still understand the situation?
output: Yes. When it’s a few days post-birth and a helper hears the mother in the background giving information to repeat, they know exactly what’s going on and have been there themselves.

instruction: I’m worried I’m not doing mothering right. What should I trust?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Basic mothering is a combination of instinct and common sense. Your baby’s cry prods you to respond, you instinctively speak in a soft, high voice that soothes, and you learn ways of moving, holding, and nursing by trial and error — and this approach works.

instruction: Why does holding my baby against my shoulder calm him?
output: Putting your baby against your shoulder calms him because it works.

instruction: Is it normal to learn holding and nursing by trial and error?
output: Yes. You learn ways of moving, holding, and nursing that no one tells you; motherhood is instinct, common sense, and trial and error, and it works.

instruction: How should I respond when my baby cries?
output: Your baby’s cry prods you to respond because your baby needs you. Many parents naturally speak in a soft, high voice that babies find soothing, and putting the baby against your shoulder can help calm him.

instruction: I’m overwhelmed the first few days — what should I focus on?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure. These first few days are a time to enjoy each other; you and your baby are both starting to let go of the edge of the pool.

instruction: What comparison is used to describe becoming a mother?
output: Becoming a mother is compared to learning to swim: you take a little deeper breath, let go of the edge, start to paddle, and realize you’re doing it.

instruction: Will I get better at mothering over time?
output: Some of mothering is what you’ve learned, some is the effort you make, and some is having faith in yourself. Someday you’ll realize you enjoy it and that you’re good at it.

instruction: If the mother asks me to call a breastfeeding helper for her, is that okay?
output: If she wants you to call, do so. Ordinarily helpers prefer the mother to call herself, but when it’s a few days post-birth and the helper hears the mother in the background, they understand the situation.

instruction: Do breastfeeding helpers understand when a partner is relaying information from the mother?
output: Yes. When helpers hear the mother in the background giving information to repeat, they know exactly what’s going on and have been there themselves.

instruction: What are the main ingredients of basic mothering?
output: Basic mothering is a combination of instinct and common sense, along with trial and error.

instruction: Is faith in myself part of becoming a mother?
output: Yes. Becoming a mother involves some learning, some effort, and some having faith in yourself.

instruction: When are the best days to try to enjoy bonding with my baby?
output: These first few days after birth are a time to enjoy each other as you both start to let go of the edge of the pool.

instruction: Why does my newborn make fast little "utter" sucks at the very start of a feeding?
output: She makes fast, uttering sucks to get everything arranged in her mouth so she can hit the right spot to trigger your oxytocin and get your milk flowing. If she can't get enough breast in her mouth, she'll do little uttering sucks, wait, then do more until she gets milk.

instruction: What happens after my milk starts flowing during a nursing session?
output: Once your milk is flowing, the rapid sucks give way to longer, slower sucks with a whispered "keh" sound, a hitch or pause in the jaw motion, or an almost digging motion as her lower jaw circles forward. You may notice a swallow with every one, two, or maybe three sucks.

instruction: My nipple hurts a little when baby latches. Is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. You may feel a moment of discomfort when your baby first latches, but it should fade quickly and should be getting better every day.

instruction: Should I switch breasts during a feeding or let my baby finish the first breast?
output: There is no need to interrupt the feeding to switch breasts. You can let your baby nurse from the first breast until he comes off on his own by letting go or falling asleep. Sometimes he will take the other breast if you offer, and sometimes he won't.

instruction: Why does my baby stop sucking partway through and just rest at the breast?
output: She may stop both sucking and swallowing to rest, which gives her a chance to collect herself, catch her breath, and wait for the next milk release. This resting between active sucking is normal.

instruction: My baby falls asleep nursing after about twenty minutes. Why does that happen?
output: After about twenty minutes of sucking, the baby’s level of cholecystokinin (CCK) has risen enough to make her sleepy and tell her she is full, which can put her to sleep and give her a rest from sucking.

instruction: Sometimes my baby wakes up about twenty minutes after stopping and seems starving. What causes that?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. About twenty minutes after she has stopped sucking, her CCK level has fallen again, and she may wake up convinced she is hungry even though she recently fed.

instruction: How can I tell that my baby is actually getting milk during a feed?
output: When milk is released, the rapid uttering sucks give way to longer, slower sucks often with a whispered keh sound or a hitch in the jaw, and you will probably notice a swallow with every one, two, or three sucks.

instruction: If nursing sessions usually start with a long series of little uttering sucks and pauses, what should I do?
output: If nursings usually begin with a long series of uttering sucks and pauses, check with your LLL Leader or another breastfeeding helper to troubleshoot.

instruction: Will I eventually get the hang of breastfeeding, or is it something I should expect to struggle with forever?
output: There is no hurrying it; day by day, like learning to ride a bicycle, you and your baby will come to the starting-to-roll feeling on your own. The process happens gradually as you practice together.

instruction: What does it mean when my baby relaxes, starts to lose the nipple, then gives a few sucks to draw it back in?
output: As her meal progresses she may relax and start to lose the nipple, then give a few sucks to draw it in again. Eventually sleep may overcome the pleasure and she will slide gently from your mouth and nap with her body against yours.

instruction: Is it okay if my baby doesn't take the second breast when I offer it after the first?
output: Yes. Sometimes he will take your other breast if you offer, and sometimes he won't. That is fine.

instruction: What does the book mean by saying a baby may "rest, neither sucking nor letting go" during a feed?
output: It means the baby may pause between active sucking and swallowing without letting go. This gives her a chance to collect herself and wait for the next milk release before resuming.

instruction: What is laidback breastfeeding and should I try it if I'm having discomfort?
output: Laidback breastfeeding is described elsewhere in the material as an option to consider; the text suggests you might want to take another look at laidback breastfeeding if you haven't tried it, implying it could be helpful if you are having issues.

instruction: My baby keeps pausing, then sucking quickly; sometimes she falls asleep with my nipple partially out. Is this normal?
output: Yes. Babies often pause to catch their breath, reorganize their mouths, or wait for the next milk release. As the feeding goes on there are more sucks before each swallow, periods of slow steady sucking and pauses, and their eyes usually close. They may lose the nipple and give a few quick sucks to draw it back in, then fall asleep and nap against you, or not quite let go until you gently slide a finger into the corner of her mouth to release her grip.

instruction: Should I switch breasts during a feeding or let her finish the first one?
output: There’s no need to interrupt a feeding to switch breasts. You can let your baby nurse from the first breast until she comes off on her own by letting go or falling asleep. Sometimes she’ll take the other breast if offered, and sometimes she won’t — that’s fine.

instruction: She seems to fall asleep after about twenty minutes of nursing. Why does that happen?
output: After maybe twenty minutes of sucking, the baby’s level of cholecystokinin (CCK) has risen enough to make her sleepy and tell her she’s full. About twenty minutes after she’s stopped sucking, the CCK level falls again and she may wake and want to nurse more.

instruction: I let my baby suck on a pacifier but then she sleeps — could that be a problem?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Sucking on a pacifier releases CCK, so a baby can fall asleep thinking she’s been fed. But because the breasts didn’t have that feeding’s milk removed, the result can be slower weight gain and lower milk supply.

instruction: What is cluster feeding? My baby seems to nurse a lot at certain times, especially evenings.
output: Cluster feeding means nursings often cluster together, especially at certain times of day. The clock is pretty useless for normal breastfeeding beyond tracking a sleepy baby to make sure she eats often enough. Evening is often a time of nursing marathons when nothing but more time at the breast seems to work. So long as your baby is gaining well, cluster feedings are absolutely normal.

instruction: My baby falls asleep at the breast, wakes soon after, nurses again, and isn’t gaining well. What should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby routinely falls asleep at the breast, wakes up soon after and nurses again and isn’t gaining well, see Chapter 18 and contact your local Leader or other breastfeeding helper to help figure out possible reasons and solutions.

instruction: How many poops should my newborn have in the first week?
output: By the middle of the first week, most babies need at least three poops a day that are nearly the size of the ‘okay’ circle you make with your thumb and forefinger in order to gain well. More is fine; if you see fewer, check with your midwife or doctor.

instruction: Can a bracelet or phone app reliably tell me which side I nursed on last?
output: Your breasts are probably a better guide. Many nursing mothers privately heft one breast and then the other before choosing a side, so a bracelet or app is usually unnecessary.

instruction: If my baby finishes one breast and falls asleep, should I wake her to offer the other breast?
output: There’s no need to wake her to switch. You can let her nurse from the first breast until she comes off on her own by letting go or falling asleep. Sometimes she’ll take the other if offered, sometimes not — either is fine.

instruction: Why does my baby want to nurse so much now — is this normal growth or a problem?
output: Babies often nurse a lot now because they are growing rapidly, have small mouths and immature intestines, and will never be more efficient feeders than they are at this time. Frequent nursing also helps the breasts learn how much milk to make. So long as your baby is gaining well, this heavy nursing makes sense.

instruction: How long after stopping sucking will my baby wake up again because of CCK falling?
output: About twenty minutes after she’s stopped sucking, her CCK level has fallen again and she may wake up ready to nurse again.

instruction: My baby sometimes loses my nipple, gives a few sucks to draw it back in, then sleeps. Should I be worried or try to re-latch her?
output: Many parents wonder about this. It’s normal for a baby to relax and lose the nipple, give a few sucks to draw it back in, and then fall asleep. You can gently slide a finger into the corner of her mouth to release her grip if needed; often she won’t rouse enough to complain.

instruction: If my baby uses a pacifier and falls asleep, could that affect my milk supply?
output: Sucking on a pacifier releases CCK and can cause a baby to fall asleep thinking she’s been fed. Because that sucking replaces a feeding at the breast, it means the breasts have lost a feeding’s worth of milk removal, which can lead to slower weight gain and lower supply.

instruction: Is it normal for my newborn to cluster-feed a lot during the day and evening?
output: Yes. Frequent cluster feeding is absolutely normal so long as your baby is gaining well. Your breasts are still figuring out how much milk to make, and a big early demand helps make feeding smoother later on.

instruction: My baby falls asleep at the breast, wakes up soon after, and keeps nursing but isn't gaining weight. What should I do?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If your baby routinely falls asleep at the breast, then wakes and nurses again and isn’t gaining well, contact your local Leader or other breastfeeding helper to help figure out possible reasons and solutions.

instruction: How many poopy diapers should my baby have by the middle of the first week?
output: By the middle of the first week, most babies need at least three poops a day that are nearly the size of the circle you make with your thumb and forefinger in order to gain well. More is fine; if you see fewer, check with your midwife or doctor.

instruction: My six-day-old is only having one stool a day. Is that normal for a newborn?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. A pattern of stooling only once every few days is common for an eight-week-old, but it is almost never true of a six-day-old. If your baby is having that few stools now, check with your midwife or doctor.

instruction: What color should my exclusively breastfed baby's poops be after the first few days?
output: After the first few days, most poopy diapers in exclusively breastfed babies are mustard-colored—bright yellow or spicy brown. Some babies have stringy or watery mucus or small-curd cottage-cheese–like stools. Lots of green can come from too much milk, letting a yellow stool sit and turn green, a diet high in green foods, green food coloring, iron supplements (including prenatal vitamins with iron), or bilirubin excretion during the first week. Scant green poops can be a sign of too little milk.

instruction: My breastfed baby's stools smell really bad. Should I be worried?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Occasionally an exclusively breastfed baby has poopy diapers that really don’t smell good; this may signal a minor food sensitivity and usually matters only if the baby seems bothered by it.

instruction: Besides diapers, how can I tell my baby is getting enough milk?
output: Look for a return to birth weight by two weeks and average gain after that of roughly 1 ounce (30 g) a day, though your baby may gain somewhat more or less. Also watch for comfortable nipples, a milk release within about a minute (usually sooner), and noticeable softening of the breast by the end of most nursings. During feeds expect open eyes at the start, slow sucks about one per second with periodic pauses, and finishing within half an hour at most feeds. Between nursings the baby should often fall gently asleep toward the end of nursing, have limp hands, and an unworried expression most of the time.

instruction: Do I need to time how many minutes my baby spends on each breast?
output: No. How many minutes a baby spends on each breast doesn’t matter, nor does whether he falls asleep at the breast or whether he nurses long enough to get to the hindmilk.

instruction: If my baby has plenty of poopy diapers, can I assume wet diapers are fine too?
output: Yes. Having ample stools means your baby is taking ample milk, and if a baby has plenty of poopy diapers you automatically know he has plenty of wet diapers. Be aware it’s possible to be fooled by diapers that seem sort of wet without many stools or much weight gain, but ample poops are a reliable sign.

instruction: What is the "Weight Gain Smile Syndrome" and should I panic if my doctor says my baby is still losing weight after things seemed to improve?
output: Many parents worry about this. The term describes the situation where a baby loses weight, breastfeeding starts to improve and the baby seems happier with more swallowing and diapers, but the doctor says he’s still losing weight and recommends supplementing. The text advises not to panic in that situation.

instruction: Do breastfed babies naturally smell unpleasant, and do we need scented baby lotions because of that?
output: No. Breastfed babies tend to smell like buttermilk, bread, even cheddar cheese or popcorn—yeasty or sharp but not unpleasant. Most scented baby lotions came on the market when formula feeding and its different aromas became more common.

instruction: When will diaper checks become less important for knowing if my baby is doing well?
output: A month or more from now your baby may switch to less frequent but larger stools, and by then you’ll have other ways of knowing he’s doing fine so the state of his diapers won’t matter as much.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is gaining weight properly?
output: Look for a return to birth weight by two weeks. After that, an average gain is roughly 1 ounce (30 g) a day, but your baby may gain somewhat more or less.

instruction: My baby was losing weight after birth but now seems happier and is producing more diapers—could this be the Smile Syndrome?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure. The Smile Syndrome is when a baby loses weight and then starts to gain, and two weight checks taken at different parts of that curve can look misleading. From only a couple of points on a chart you can’t tell whether the baby is truly losing or already gaining.

instruction: How many diapers should my newborn have each day in the first month?
output: Look for at least three okay (thumb-and-forefinger circle size) diapers each day during the first month or so.

instruction: What should my breasts feel like during and after most nursings?
output: Look for comfortable nipples, a milk release within a minute (usually much sooner), and noticeable softening by the end of most nursings.

instruction: What does normal nursing behavior look like at the breast?
output: Look for open eyes when the feed starts, periods of slow sucks at about one per second with periodic pauses, and finishing within a half hour at most feeds.

instruction: How should my baby act between feedings if things are going well?
output: Look for the baby falling gently asleep toward the end of the nursing (or contentment for at least a while before he nurses again), limp hands, and an unworried expression most of the time.

instruction: Does it matter how many minutes my baby spends on each breast or whether he falls asleep at the breast?
output: Those things don’t matter: how many minutes on each breast, whether he falls asleep at the breast at the end of the feeding, or whether he nurses long enough to get to the hindmilk are not important.

instruction: What affects long-term milk supply in the early weeks?
output: Within the first few weeks postpartum your breasts develop hormone receptors and respond to how much milk is removed. The more milk that is removed during this time, by nursing, pumping, or hand expressing, the higher your long-term milk supply will be.

instruction: I’m worried I don’t have enough milk—what is typical in the first weeks after birth?
output: Many parents wonder about this. In fact, the opposite is usually true at first: most women have more than enough milk. Nature is often overly generous early on, and it can take a month or more for supply to settle down to what the baby actually needs.

instruction: How quickly should I expect milk to let down after my baby latches?
output: A milk release should occur within a minute, usually much sooner.

instruction: Can breast compressions help with breastfeeding?
output: Breast compressions can move more milk.

instruction: When my baby is weighed, how should it be done so the readings are comparable?
output: Make sure the baby is weighed on the same scale, naked or in very similar clothing, with his stomach about as full as it was last time.

instruction: Is it important how frequently milk is removed in the early postpartum weeks?
output: Yes. The process expects milk to be taken out frequently and efficiently; it responds to milk removal by any means (nursing, pumping, hand expressing), and frequent removal in the first weeks helps set a higher long-term supply.

instruction: How is my long-term milk supply determined in the first few weeks after birth?
output: In the first few weeks your breasts develop hormone receptors that respond to how much milk is removed. The process expects milk to be taken out frequently and efficiently, and it responds to removal by nursing, pumping, or hand expressing. The more milk that is removed during this time, the higher your long-term milk supply will be. The process starts fresh with each baby.

instruction: Can pumping or hand expressing help build my supply if my baby isn't removing much milk yet?
output: Yes. The breasts respond to milk removal by any means — nursing, pumping, or hand expressing — so removing milk by pumping or hand expression in those early weeks will help your supply develop.

instruction: What are the most important things about how I nurse my baby?
output: Only two things matter: that you and your baby are comfortable, and that he is getting plenty of milk. Beyond those two points, there are no rules about nursing technique.

instruction: I feel very full at first but by six weeks my breasts seem less full. Did my milk disappear?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Nature is usually overly generous at first and it can take a month or more for your supply to settle down to what your baby actually needs. By around six weeks the unnecessary early fluids have gone away and breastfeeding becomes more comfortable, smooth, and easy — the milk is still there.

instruction: How do I do a breast compression while my baby is nursing?
output: During a sucking pause, place your fingers and thumb well away from the areola, then squeeze your breast gently (it shouldn’t hurt) and hold the pressure. You can press the breast against your chest or cup it between thumb and fingers. Your baby should start swallowing again. When the renewed swallowing stops, release to allow more milk to flow into the area, then compress again and shift your hand to compress different areas.

instruction: Can I use breast compressions while pumping?
output: Yes. You can do compressions while pumping; they may increase spurting and give you more milk.

instruction: I'm worried I have too much milk — is there something I can try to reduce oversupply?
output: Many parents wonder about this. If you’ve been using both breasts at each nursing and feel overabundant, try letting the baby nurse on one breast as long as you and he like. Many babies are happy with just one breast at a nursing, and that can help diminish overabundant milk. Using compressions can also help bring down more fat for him; staying on one side for one or more nursings is another suggestion in that situation.

instruction: If my baby stops sucking actively, will compressions help him start again?
output: Yes. If your baby stops actively sucking, a gentle compression can stimulate him to start sucking again by mimicking a milk release and creating pressure within the breast.

instruction: What kinds of foods should I ask visitors to bring so I can eat easily with one hand?
output: Ask for one-handed foods such as casseroles, sandwiches, finger foods, hummus-type dips, whole-grain crackers, pre-sliced nibblies, and smoothies. Also ask for healthy choices (whole grains, unprocessed meats, fresh or frozen fruits and vegetables, smaller amounts of dairy), high-calorie items (nut butters, olive and canola oils, whole-milk cheeses and yogurts, even leftover cake), and sturdy foods that are freezable or reheatable and have a long refrigerator life.

instruction: Does breast compressing help my baby get more milk if I'm trying to increase my supply?
output: Yes. If you’re increasing your milk supply, compressions can help ensure that your baby gets more of your available milk by mimicking a milk release and creating pressure within the breast.

instruction: Are there strict rules about how long or how often to nurse?
output: No. Beyond being comfortable for both of you and making sure your baby is getting plenty of milk, there are no rules about nursing technique.

instruction: Will compressions help if I'm in a hurry and need to shorten a nursing?
output: Sometimes compressions can shorten a nursing by increasing the flow and spurting of milk, so they may help when you’re in a hurry.

instruction: Does the milk-production process start over if I have another baby later?
output: Yes. The process starts fresh with each baby.

instruction: Where should I place my fingers so I don't block the areola when compressing?
output: Place your fingers and thumb well away from the areola, either pressing the breast against your chest or cupping it between thumb and fingers, and squeeze gently so it doesn’t hurt.

instruction: If I have an oversupply, can compressions change the milk composition my baby gets during a feeding?
output: Compressions can help bring down more fat for your baby when you have an oversupply, which can affect what he gets during the feeding. Staying on one side for one or more nursings is another approach mentioned for oversupply.

instruction: I keep hearing 'sleep when the baby sleeps' but there’s so much to do—what does the baby think about sleeping arrangements?
output: Your baby would answer the question of where to sleep with one word: together. The common suggestion is to sleep when the baby sleeps, though many parents feel they can’t because of tasks like household chores or personal care.

instruction: How can I shorten a nursing session if I'm in a hurry?
output: You can sometimes shorten a nursing session when you're rushed — it's worth a try.

instruction: What kinds of foods should I ask friends and relatives to bring?
output: Ask for a mix: healthy foods (whole grains, unprocessed meats, fresh or frozen fruits and vegetables, smaller amounts of dairy); high-calorie items (nut butters, olive and canola oils, whole-milk cheeses and yogurts, cake); one-handed options (casseroles, sandwiches, finger foods, hummus-type dips, whole-grain crackers, pre-sliced nibblies, smoothies); and sturdy foods that are freezable or reheatable and have a long refrigerator life.

instruction: What are good high-calorie snacks to have on hand while breastfeeding?
output: High-calorie choices from the list include nut butters, olive and canola oils, whole-milk cheeses and yogurts, and even leftover birthday cake.

instruction: I'm overwhelmed and can't sleep when the baby naps — how can I rest during the day with the house a mess?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Rest even if you can't sleep: find spots to practice laid-back mothering by leaning back at a relaxed angle, fully supported by pillows, with your baby snoozing on your chest (shirt open when you can). Relax in that position as much as possible whether the baby is eating or sleeping; rest speeds healing and helps keep you from feeling overwhelmed.

instruction: Is it better for my baby and me to sleep together at night?
output: Mothers and babies have slept together throughout history; separating mothers and babies at night is a recent idea. Many people found they finally got some sleep when they let their babies sleep on their chest or beside them. Sharing sleep doesn’t guarantee a good night’s sleep at first, though.

instruction: Why does my newborn want to nurse so much at night?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Newborns may need a lot of nursing at night at the beginning because their internal clock is backward; over time they gradually learn the difference between day and night.

instruction: Can I learn to nurse while lying down, and when should I practice it?
output: Yes. One nighttime skill to work on during the day is learning to nurse lying down. You might use laid-back breastfeeding all night or prefer nursing on your side with the baby on his side next to you, so practice side-lying during the day.

instruction: What does 'laid-back mothering' look like?
output: Laid-back mothering means leaning back at a relaxed angle, fully supported by pillows as needed, with your baby snoozing on your chest and your shirt open when you can so he can nurse or sleep comfortably.

instruction: My baby used to kick me a lot at night during pregnancy — why is he still active now and how can I calm him?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Babies who were active at night before birth may be lively at night after birth too. Keeping the lights low and soothing him with as little commotion as possible may help; over time he will learn the difference between day and night.

instruction: What's an easy daily goal I can set so I still get something done?
output: Try accomplishing one small thing a day, like cleaning one counter or writing one thank-you note. Don’t make the task too difficult.

instruction: Why is resting on the couch important while caring for my newborn?
output: Resting helps your body heal — you’re healing a uterus while your baby’s brain, liver, heart, and lungs develop, his immune system is boosted, and his intestines maintain integrity. Rest speeds healing and helps keep you from feeling overwhelmed.

instruction: What are good one-handed foods to eat when I'm breastfeeding and holding the baby?
output: One-handed options include casseroles, sandwiches, finger foods, hummus-type dips, whole-grain crackers, pre-sliced nibblies, and smoothies.

instruction: What should friends bring that will keep well in the fridge or freezer?
output: Ask for sturdy foods that are freezable or reheatable and have a long refrigerator life so they last when you need them.

instruction: Will sleeping with my baby make me get a full night's sleep right away?
output: Sharing sleep can help some people get rest, but it doesn’t guarantee a good night’s sleep at first. Newborns may still be active at night and need soothing or nursing.

instruction: Is nursing on my side at night a reasonable option?
output: Yes. You may prefer to nurse on your side with the baby on his side next to you; practicing side-lying during the day can help you feel comfortable doing it at night.

instruction: Is it okay to do just one small thing a day while I'm recovering?
output: Yes. Accomplish one small thing a day and don’t make the task too difficult — maybe cleaning one counter or writing one thank-you note.

instruction: I feel guilty for resting and not doing more — what is my body doing while I lie on the couch?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. While you rest you are healing a uterus; your baby is getting millions of new brain cells; his liver, heart, and lungs are developing; his immune system is being boosted; and the integrity of his intestines is being maintained.

instruction: Could what I'm feeling in the first week after birth be the baby blues?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Sometime in the first week or two after birth you may get the baby blues — a day or a few days of feeling like "What have I gotten myself into?" or "Everything is just wrong." Symptoms can include tearfulness, uncertainty, forgetfulness, restlessness, irritability, nightmares, or even negative feelings toward the baby. These feelings are gone within a few days or a week.

instruction: How can my partner or a friend help me if I'm having the baby blues?
output: Having a partner or close friend be there can help: offer a shoulder to cry on, an understanding ear, or gentle hands you can trust with the baby for a bit.

instruction: What is the "grandma hour" and why does it happen late in the day?
output: The "grandma hour" is a late-in-the-day time when many new mothers wish someone else could take over for a while. By then many babies are frazzled from a day of sharp, bright sounds and new activities and get overwhelmed; nursing is their biggest comfort, but they may be low on milk in the breasts and the milk flow can slow.

instruction: Why does my milk flow seem slower in the evening even though I make more milk then?
output: Although milk production is often at its highest point of the day, the baby may have worked the breasts down to a low volume, and having less milk in the breasts slows milk flow.

instruction: What can I try when my baby gets fussy at the end of the day?
output: Try breast compressions to help your baby get more milk faster and put a bit more oomph behind milk release. A change of scenery or a different person who doesn’t smell of milk can also settle some babies. You can try taking a nap together in the afternoon or offering to nurse about an hour before he usually gets upset; soothing him before he gets wired up may make for a more peaceful evening, and at worst will give you both a little rest.

instruction: How do I do the magic baby hold (colic hold) to calm a fussy baby?
output: Hold your baby’s back against your chest so you are both facing forward. Bring your left arm over your baby’s left shoulder and hold his right thigh so he has one arm on either side of your arm and you have a solid grip on his leg. You can hold him facing the floor, facing out, or rest your left hand (still holding his thigh) on your hip. Keep your shoulders relaxed; you’ll have a hand free if you like. Using your left arm puts your baby on his left side, which some babies find especially soothing.

instruction: Will the magic baby hold leave me with a free hand?
output: Yes. With the magic baby hold your shoulders are relaxed and you have a hand free if you like while you still have a solid grip on your baby’s leg.

instruction: My baby refuses the breast in the evening—does that always mean he isn't hungry?
output: Not always. Many babies in the late-day period don’t want milk; they may want to go back to the womb, be trying to put themselves to sleep, or be seeking something different to break the end-of-day spell. Sometimes a different person or a change of scenery settles them; other times they keep nursing.

instruction: Is it normal to feel both tender and tough after delivering a baby?
output: Yes. A new mother is often gentle, loving, and selfless, but she’s also tough: she delivers a baby and then cares for that baby around the clock, showing toughminded resolve as well as love and tenderness.

instruction: What do breast compressions do during nursing?
output: Breast compressions help your baby get more milk faster and put a bit more oomph behind your milk release.

instruction: When do baby blues typically start and how long do they last?
output: Sometime in the first week or two after birth you may get the baby blues. They usually last a day or a few days and are gone within a few days or a week.

instruction: Could taking a nap together in the afternoon help the evening fussiness?
output: Yes. Taking a nap together in the afternoon is one suggested way to head off the late-day upset; it can give you both a little rest and may help avoid the baby becoming wired later.

instruction: My baby gets fussy every evening — what can I try to head off that "grandma hour" so evenings are calmer?
output: It can help to take a nap together in the afternoon or to offer to nurse about an hour before he usually gets upset. A change of scenery or being held by a different person who doesn’t smell of milk can also settle him. Soothing him before he gets wired up may make for a more peaceful evening, and at worst it will give you both a little rest.

instruction: How do I do the Magic Baby Hold (colic hold)?
