output: Hold your baby’s back against your chest so you two face forward. Bring your left arm over his left shoulder and hold his right thigh; he’ll have one arm on either side of your arm and you’ll have a solid grip on his leg. You can hold him so he faces the floor, faces out, or rest your left hand, still holding his thigh, on your hip. Keep your shoulders relaxed and you’ll have a hand free. Using your left arm also puts your baby on his left side, which some babies find especially soothing.

instruction: My baby screams during diaper changes — should I keep going?
output: If your baby is shrieking there’s no need to doggedly pursue the diaper change. Think Baby First: stop to nurse and console, then try again. Interrupting what you’re doing to offer comfort will usually mean he settles more readily.

instruction: Why can’t I see my baby’s face in the Magic Baby Hold?
output: With that hold you usually won’t be able to see your baby’s face, but you will feel his body’s tension and be able to tell when he’s calming or getting tense.

instruction: When should I offer to nurse to prevent fussiness?
output: One idea is to offer to nurse about an hour before he usually gets upset. Also, doing Carry, Contact, Walk, and Talk before crying starts can reduce crying overall.

instruction: What is the baby bounce and how do I do it?
output: Start with swaying, and if that isn’t enough add a sway with a little hitch. With the Magic Baby Hold you’ll feel his body’s tension; if you notice the back of his head start to wrinkle or his body tense, turn just a bit so he has new scenery in front of him. The bounce plus the new scenery often helps.

instruction: Is there anything that helps a baby who clearly needs to nurse besides more nursing?
output: Nothing really helps a baby who needs to nurse except more nursing.

instruction: What is Ron Barr’s advice for reducing crying?
output: He recommends Carry, Contact, Walk, and Talk and has found that doing these before the crying starts will mean less crying overall. A quick response to your baby’s unhappiness will help him settle again more readily.

instruction: I’m worried my baby is hard to settle and I don’t know why — is there anything that can help me track improvement?
output: It may help to put a smiley face on the calendar on days when your baby was happier and look back to see a positive trend. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: If the Magic Baby Hold hides his face but I want a free hand, are there holding options that give me a hand free?
output: Yes. In the Magic Baby Hold you can rest your left hand, still holding his thigh, on your hip so your shoulders are relaxed and you have a hand free while keeping a solid grip on his leg.

instruction: My baby gets really fussy at night — is there anything simple that might help calm me while I soothe him?
output: When the baby is being really fussy at night, going out to look at the moon and thinking about other mothers and babies under the same moon can be calming. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Are early fussy periods normal in the first weeks?
output: Yes. In the first couple of weeks most of the fussy times are just the baby adjusting to Life on Earth. Offering to nurse again and soothing his distresses can help, and fussiness is almost always temporary.

instruction: If the Magic Baby Hold doesn’t calm him because he needs to nurse, what should I do next?
output: If the baby needs to nurse, nothing else really helps — more nursing is what he needs.

instruction: Should I interrupt what I’m doing to soothe a mildly unhappy baby?
output: Yes. Don’t hesitate to interrupt tasks to offer comfort — a quick response to your baby’s unhappiness will mean he’s settled again more readily.

instruction: What does Carry, Contact, Walk, and Talk mean for soothing my baby?
output: Carry, Contact, Walk, and Talk are steps recommended for soothing an unhappy baby. Doing these before crying starts has been found to mean less crying overall.

instruction: Should I stop what I’m doing to comfort my baby if he seems unhappy?
output: Yes. A quick response to your baby’s unhappiness will help him settle more readily, so don’t hesitate to interrupt tasks to offer comfort.

instruction: My baby is shrieking during a diaper change — should I keep going until it’s done?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. There’s no need to doggedly pursue a diaper change on a shrieking baby. Think baby first: stop to nurse and console, then try the diaper change again.

instruction: Are fussy times normal in the first couple of weeks?
output: Yes. In the first couple of weeks most of the fussy times are just the baby adjusting to life on earth, and fussiness is almost always temporary.

instruction: My newborn is hard to settle and I don’t know why — any simple things I can try?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Offer to nurse again, enjoy moments of contentment when they occur, and you can mark happier days with a smiley face on the calendar to see a positive trend over time.

instruction: Where can I find more information about colic, reflux, or extra soothing techniques?
output: There’s information about colic, reflux, and other common causes of baby distress in Chapter 18. For more baby-soothing techniques, see the Tear-Sheet Toolkit; you can also look on the llli.org forums or call your local LLL Leader.

instruction: How often should I expect my baby to eat in these early days?
output: It varies from baby to baby and day to day. Rather than clocking frequency, watch how your baby behaves after nursing: staying awake and calmly looking around, drifting off while nursing, or having an arm drop like a dead weight when lifted are all signs he’s setting his own pace.

instruction: We left the hospital with a medicated, sleepy baby — how often should I offer the breast?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Assume he’ll need to eat at least every three hours, and more likely every two hours through much of the day, and at least a couple of times through the night.

instruction: How long should a typical feeding take?
output: It varies. A complete meal generally takes around twenty minutes and sometimes as long as forty. A baby may want one, two, three, or even four sides in a feeding.

instruction: My baby nurses in five minutes and then sleeps for hours — is that mentioned?
output: Many parents wonder about this. An occasional baby can routinely zip through a whole feeding in five minutes and be satisfied for several hours; if you feel your baby always gets too much too fast, check the oversupply information in Chapter 18.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is getting plenty of milk during a feed?
output: A baby who’s getting plenty often has open eyes at the start, may doze later in the feed and fall asleep at the end, sometimes with unswallowed drops of milk on his mouth or with his body limp like a rag doll.

instruction: My baby dozes through long, slow feeds and then his eyes fly open when I take him off the breast — what does that indicate?
output: Many babies who sleep through most of their meals use numerous sucks to get every swallow and doze as much as they can; when taken off the breast their eyes may fly open, they may watch you with a worried expression, and try to get back on the breast. Some of these babies sleep really well at night.

instruction: Should I expect baths and diaper changes to be stressful right now?
output: There’s no need to make baths and diaper changes an added stress. It won’t be long before they become playtime as you and your baby settle into routines.

instruction: My baby keeps turning down the breast during early feedings — what should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Offer your breast, and if he keeps turning you down, see Chapter 18 and the Tear-Sheet Toolkit for suggestions on keeping a baby well fed during a wobbly start.

instruction: How can I track whether my baby’s days are getting better over time?
output: Put a smiley face on the calendar on days when your baby was happier and look back over the calendar to see the positive trend.

instruction: How long does a typical breastfeeding take?
output: A complete feeding is generally around twenty minutes and sometimes as long as forty minutes. An occasional baby can routinely finish in about five minutes and then be satisfied for several hours.

instruction: My baby only nurses for five minutes and then seems full for hours. Is that normal?
output: Yes. An occasional baby can zip through a whole feeding in five minutes and be satisfied for several hours. The text notes that these babies can get their milk so fast they may not linger for cuddling or snacking the way other newborns do.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is getting plenty of milk during a feeding?
output: A baby who is getting plenty of milk usually has open eyes at the start, may doze later in the feed, and often falls asleep at the end, sometimes with unswallowed drops of milk on his mouth or with his body limp.

instruction: My baby sleeps through most of his meals and then wakes up frantic when I take him off the breast. Should I be worried?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Babies who sleep through most of their meals tend to have long, slow feedings and may be using many sucks to get each swallow. They often doze to conserve energy and, when taken off the breast, may open their eyes and try to get back on. These babies usually aren’t gaining enough.

instruction: What does it mean when my baby's fists are clenched?
output: When a baby is hungry he tends to clench his fists tightly and bring them toward his face. If he falls asleep hungry his fists usually stay clenched. As he gets milk he relaxes, starting with his face, then his shoulders, and finally his fists will unclench and become limp.

instruction: Should I offer both breasts at one feeding or just one?
output: Think of each breast as a serving. Some babies want two servings in a row, some fall asleep after the first and save the second for later, and some take three or four before dozing. It doesn’t really matter how many servings he takes in a row as long as he’s satisfied when finished.

instruction: One of my breasts starts to feel uncomfortable if I don’t nurse from it for a while. What does that mean?
output: You can trust that your breasts will let you know if you’re not feeding enough from one side. If you go too long without emptying one breast, it will start feeling uncomfortable, signaling that it’s time to have the baby nurse from that side.

instruction: Is there really a difference between foremilk and hindmilk? Do I need to worry about getting to the hindmilk?
output: Your breasts make only one kind of milk. The milk can seem different because cream rises. If you nurse often it stays mixed; if you wait longer some fat can rise and later gets squeezed down and mixes back in. All your milk is nutritious, and the fat variations give some variety. It isn’t an issue for most babies.

instruction: My baby swallows a lot but seems unhappy and has green, frothy diapers. Could that be a problem?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If your baby does plenty of swallowing but seems unhappy or has green or frothy diapers, that can be a sign of oversupply.

instruction: Will my baby grow better if I wait until he seems really hungry before feeding him?
output: He probably won’t grow nearly as well. Babies need to eat more often than adults because their food digests very quickly — about sixty to ninety minutes — and they thrive with frequent snacks rather than waiting until meals are fully digested.

instruction: What can happen if a baby pig outs and overloads their stomach?
output: If a baby really pig outs and overloads the stomach, they risk indigestion, gas, and cramps. Staying in the middle with frequent snacks, never fully empty or excruciatingly full, keeps their stomachs happier and supports growth.

instruction: Why does milk sometimes taste thinner at the start of a feeding?
output: The milk can seem thinner at the start because cream rises. When you wait longer between feedings some fat can creep up the ducts, leaving lower-fat milk behind until the next nursing mixes the fat back in. All of it is nutritious.

instruction: My baby takes several short feedings in a row and then falls asleep. Is that normal?
output: Yes. Some babies want two servings right in a row, some fall asleep after the first and save the second for later, and some take three or four servings before dozing. Since you’ll nurse again whenever the baby tells you he needs it, the number of servings in a row isn’t important as long as he’s satisfied at the end.

instruction: If I wait until my breasts feel really full to feed, will that affect milk supply?
output: Our milk supplies are designed for frequent feedings. If we wait until our breasts feel really full, they’ve already started to slow.

instruction: My baby nods off while nursing with milk on his mouth. Is that okay?
output: Yes. A baby who’s getting plenty of milk may doze later in the feed and fall asleep at the end, sometimes with unswallowed drops of milk on his mouth or with his body limp as a rag doll.

instruction: Why does my breastmilk sometimes look watery and other times creamy?
output: Your breasts make one kind of milk. It can seem different because cream (the fat) rises. If you nurse very often the fat stays mixed in; if you wait longer between feedings some fat can rise up the ducts and leave a lower-fat milk behind. At the next nursing the fat is squeezed down again and mixes back in. All of it is nutritious.

instruction: My baby swallows a lot but seems unhappy and has green, frothy diapers. What could that mean?
output: If your baby is swallowing plenty but seems unhappy or has green, frothy diapers, that pattern is associated with oversupply. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Should I wait until my baby is really hungry before I offer the breast so he'll grow better?
output: Waiting until your baby is really hungry probably won't help growth. Babies need to eat often because breast milk digests very quickly—about sixty to ninety minutes—and milk production is highest when you nurse before you feel you need to. If you wait until your breasts feel very full, production has often already started to slow down.

instruction: How quickly does breast milk digest and what does that mean for how often babies need to eat?
output: Breast milk digests very quickly—about sixty to ninety minutes. Because of that, babies need to eat often rather than waiting long periods between meals.

instruction: My baby cries before I offer the breast. Does crying mean he is hungry?
output: Crying is a late indicator of hunger. Babies give subtle cues first—eyelids opening, hands coming to the face, mouth movements—then more obvious cues like rooting and whimpering. If you offer when you see the earlier cues, your baby will probably take the breast gently; by the time he is crying he may be too stressed to eat and nursing can take longer and be harder.

instruction: I'm worried I don't have enough milk. What are common reasons a baby might seem hungry?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Often nothing is wrong—newborns need to eat very often and parents may not expect that. Other causes include someone making you worry, a baby not taking the breast well because of positioning (held too far away, too high, or too low), sore nipples or lots of sucking without much swallowing (signs of poor milk removal), following a schedule that doesn't fit your baby, or a minor issue in the baby such as tongue-tie. If positioning changes don't help, check with a breastfeeding helper.

instruction: My nipples are sore. Does that mean my baby isn't getting enough milk?
output: Sore nipples are often a tip-off that milk removal could be improved and that your baby may not be getting milk as efficiently. It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Try adjusting positioning—many babies do better with a laidback position—and if comfort and the way the baby nurses don't improve, check with a breastfeeding helper.

instruction: My baby sucks a lot but I don't hear many swallows. What might that indicate?
output: Lots of sucking without much swallowing is a tip-off that your baby may not be taking the breast as well as he could. That can come from being held too far away, too high, or too low. Try different positioning—many babies do a better job when allowed to position themselves—and if you don't see improvement, check with a breastfeeding helper; a minor issue like tongue-tie can sometimes make sucking less effective.

instruction: Someone keeps telling me my baby shouldn't be eating so often. I'm getting anxious—what should I do?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. New mothers often get worried when others question frequent nursing. Surrounding yourself with women who have breastfed and know good nursing can be helpful; groups such as La Leche League are mentioned as supportive places. Remember that newborns need frequent feeding and that frequent nursing is normal.

instruction: Could following a parenting schedule be why my baby seems constantly hungry?
output: Yes. Following a book, parenting program, or schedule that doesn't fit your baby can be an issue. Babies differ in stomach size, activity, sleep needs, and efficiency, and some breasts have less storage space than others—storage capacity isn’t visible from the outside.

instruction: What should I try if my baby isn't latching well?
output: Try adjusting how you hold the baby—avoid holding too far away, too high, or too low. Many babies do better with a laidback position and often position themselves well when given the chance. Sore nipples or lots of sucking without swallowing are signs to improve positioning. If you don't see improvement, check with a breastfeeding helper who can assess for issues such as tongue-tie.

instruction: If I let my breasts get very full before nursing, will that affect my milk supply?
output: Yes. If you wait until your breasts feel very full, milk production has often already started to slow down. Milk production tends to stay highest when you nurse before you feel you need to.

instruction: If my baby gets very hungry and cries, will nursing be easier or harder?
output: Nursing will often be harder. As hunger builds a baby's body and mouth tense and crying is a late indicator of hunger; by then he can be too stressed to eat and the nursing may take longer and be uncomfortable.

instruction: My baby seems to be nursing a lot and someone keeps asking me, 'Are you sure he's getting enough?' Should I worry he's not getting enough milk?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. In the early weeks babies are often inefficient at nursing while your milk supply and the baby’s growth are getting established. Follow your baby’s lead and look for signs such as poopy diapers and weight gain to reassure yourself. Try to surround yourself with women who have breastfed successfully for practical support.

instruction: My nipples are sore after nursing. Does that mean my baby isn’t getting milk well?
output: Sore nipples are a common sign that milk removal may be less than adequate. It can mean positioning or latch problems (for example, holding the baby too far away, too high, or too low). Try repositioning — many mothers find laidback positioning helps — and if comfort and the baby’s nursing don’t improve, check with an experienced breastfeeding helper.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is sucking but not getting much milk?
output: Lots of sucking without much swallowing is a tip-off that the baby may not be getting much milk. Also watch for sore nipples, which often signal less-than-adequate milk removal. Try following the baby’s lead and adjusting positioning; if you don’t see improvement, seek help from a breastfeeding helper.

instruction: Someone suggested I follow a strict feeding schedule. Should I stick to a book or program?
output: Cookie-cutter instructions don’t fit all babies. Babies differ in stomach size, activity, sleep needs, and feeding efficiency. If a schedule doesn’t suit your baby, try going totally with your baby’s cues — that often leads to a happier baby and more poopy diapers within a day or two.

instruction: My baby cries a lot and people say it might be my milk. Could my diet be causing his fussiness?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Fussiness may have nothing to do with feeding — it can be temperament or a temporary stage. Pay attention to poopy diapers and weight gain for reassurance. If you’re concerned about diet-related causes, get help from an experienced breastfeeding helper and consult resources on colic and feeding.

instruction: Are there special foods or drinks I must have to make enough breast milk?
output: No. Many cultures use foods or herbs that mothers believe support milk, but there’s nothing you must eat or drink to make enough nutritious milk. You don’t need special drinks to make milk, and you don’t need more fluids than you want to produce milk.

instruction: Do I need to drink a lot of water to keep my milk supply up?
output: You don’t need more fluids than you want to make milk, and a mild dehydration won’t decrease supply. If your urine is medium to pale yellow, you’re doing fine. Oxytocin release can make you suddenly thirsty, so carrying a water bottle can help.

instruction: Will eating more calories make me produce more milk?
output: Eating more won’t make more milk, and not eating enough won’t necessarily make less. You don’t need a perfect diet to keep your milk nutritious; milk is made from your blood. Healthy eating is mainly to keep you well enough to care for yourself and the baby.

instruction: I’m a vegetarian. Is there anything special I need to worry about for breastfeeding?
output: If you’re vegetarian or vegan, make sure you understand how to maintain your vitamin B12 levels. Otherwise, you don’t need a special diet to make nutritious milk.

instruction: My family has allergies. Should I change my diet while breastfeeding to prevent reactions in my baby?
output: If there’s a family history of allergies, you might avoid or go light on common allergens in your diet at first and reintroduce them slowly to see how your baby does. The most likely dietary culprits are cow milk and dairy. Some babies who react may be fine if their mothers don’t eat large quantities at once.

instruction: How much caffeine is safe while nursing in the first weeks?
output: Go easy on caffeine for the first month or so while your baby’s body is still maturing. Watch the baby for jitteriness once you add caffeine, and keep intake light enough that you can still nap when you get the chance.

instruction: I tried repositioning but my baby still nurses poorly. Could something be wrong with him?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If repositioning and following your baby’s lead don’t improve comfort or the way he nurses, check with an experienced breastfeeding helper. Some babies have minor issues such as tongue-tie, which is usually dealt with quickly and easily.

instruction: Someone keeps telling me to go to La Leche League. Is that helpful?
output: La Leche League meetings are mentioned as a great place to find women who have enjoyed breastfeeding and can recognize good nursing. Surrounding yourself with experienced, successful breastfeeders can provide useful practical support.

instruction: I’m tempted to start formula because my baby cries a lot. Should I switch right away?
output: Many parents feel unsure about this. Before you panic and start formula, seek quick, skilled help: consult resources on feeding and colic and talk with an experienced breastfeeding helper as soon as you can so you and your baby get prompt support.

instruction: How can I tell if I'm drinking enough water while breastfeeding?
output: If your urine is medium to pale yellow, you are doing fine. Oxytocin release can make you suddenly thirsty, so carrying a water bottle helps.

instruction: I'm worried my diet will make my milk bad. Do I need a perfect diet to breastfeed?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Your milk is made from your blood, and you don’t need a perfect diet to keep your milk nutritious. The healthy food guidelines are mainly to keep you well enough to take care of your baby and yourself.

instruction: Can eating more food make me produce more milk?
output: Eating more won’t make more milk, and not eating enough won’t make less milk. Calories are pretty simple in that way.

instruction: I'm vegetarian — anything special I should do while nursing?
output: If you are vegetarian or vegan, make sure you understand how to keep up your vitamin B12 levels.

instruction: My family has lots of allergies. Should I avoid certain foods while breastfeeding?
output: If your baby comes from an allergic family, you might want to avoid or go light on common allergens in your diet at first and ease into them slowly to see how your baby does. The most likely culprits are cow milk and dairy products.

instruction: Can my favorite spicy foods bother my breastfed baby?
output: Some cultures use a milder diet for nursing mothers for the first month, but after about the first month most babies are not bothered by foods in their mother’s diet. Your favorite foods will flavor your milk somewhat and your baby will learn the family menu through breastfeeding.

instruction: How much caffeine is safe while my newborn is very young?
output: Go easy on caffeine for the first month or so while your baby’s body is still maturing. Watch him for jitteriness once you add it, and go light enough that you can nap easily when you get the chance.

instruction: Is it normal for my breastfed baby to have a lot of gas?
output: All mammals have intestinal gas every day, so a gassy but not bothered baby is fine. If your baby is frequently gassy and uncomfortable, he might be getting too much milk.

instruction: My baby squirms and seems to need a burp during feeds — how should I burp him?
output: Try holding him against your shoulder and rub or pat his back gently; you might want to cover your shoulder with a cloth to catch spit-up. You can also sit him up in your lap, bending him slightly forward while you gently pat his back. There’s no need to pound.

instruction: Do I have to burp my baby if I carry him all the time?
output: Many cultures that carry their babies don’t burp them. A baby who is carried upright most of the time will naturally burp when he needs to.

instruction: My baby spits up a lot — what can I try to reduce it?
output: Many babies aren’t bothered by spitting up, and the amount often looks like more than it is. Spitting up can happen after larger meals at longer intervals, so offering the breast more often so the baby takes less each time can help. Carrying your baby upright, especially after nursings, can also help.

instruction: I'm having trouble getting my newborn settled in the sling. Any tips?
output: Start with a snug sling so your newborn is stabilized in a fairly upright position with his head about level with your breasts, not down by your waist. Arrange the baby with his face above the level of the sling, start moving as soon as he’s settled, and pat him through the fabric and talk to him. Non-adjustable slings can be shifted to find a good angle and may need a thin blanket in the bottom for a newborn.

instruction: Can a sling be dangerous for my newborn's breathing?
output: Be careful that the baby is not curled up with his chin against his chest, which can restrict his breathing. Babies usually hate being dropped into a ‘black hole’ that squeezes their head, keeps them from seeing out, and can interfere with breathing.

instruction: My baby gets fussy soon after feeds when the milk comes out fast. Could that be air?
output: Some babies squirm shortly into the feeding, especially if the milk comes out fast and they are noisily gulping; they may need a quick burp early in the feed so that air doesn’t get trapped below the milk.

instruction: If dairy makes my baby fussy, does that mean I must avoid it completely?
output: If dairy makes your baby fussy, your baby may still be fine as long as you don’t eat large quantities at one time. You might go light on dairy and watch how your baby does.

instruction: My newborn keeps spitting up after feeds. What can I try to reduce it?
output: Many babies aren’t bothered by spitting up and it often looks like more than it is. Try offering milk more often so your baby takes less at each feeding; this can help them keep more down. Carrying your baby upright, especially after nursings, can also help. Some cultures carry babies and offer small snacks frequently, which seems to help. If spitting up makes your baby uncomfortable, there are additional fussy-baby ideas you can try.

instruction: Is it normal that my baby spits up a lot but doesn’t seem upset?
output: Yes. Many babies don’t seem bothered by spitting up, and the amount of milk they lose usually looks like a lot more than it really is.

instruction: Would feeding larger meals less often cause more spitting up?
output: Yes. Spitting up can happen as a result of larger meals given at longer intervals. Offering milk more often so your baby takes less each time may help.

instruction: How should I position my newborn in a sling so he can breathe and see?
output: Your newborn will probably do best in a snug sling, stabilized fairly upright with his head at about the level of your breasts, not down by your waist. Arrange your baby with his face above the level of the sling so he can see out. Avoid dropping him into a deep pocket that squeezes his head, and be careful he isn’t curled with his chin against his chest, which can restrict breathing.

instruction: I’m nervous about using a sling for the first time. Any simple tips to get started?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Start with the baby snug and fairly upright, with his head near your breast level. Once he’s settled, begin moving, pat him through the fabric and talk to him. Non‑adjustable slings can be shifted to find a good angle; a thin blanket in the bottom may help a newborn. Keep experimenting and try again in a week or two if it simply doesn’t work right away.

instruction: Can I use a ring sling with a newborn? How should the legs be placed?
output: Yes. Ring slings can be worn with your newborn upright and facing you. The legs can be crisscrossed inside the sling or dangling outside of it. To make the sling snug, pull the loose fabric through the rings or use a rubber band to gather extra fabric.

instruction: If I carry my baby hammock style, does their head and feet need to be level?
output: If you wear the baby hammock style, he will probably want his head higher than his feet.

instruction: My non-adjustable sling feels awkward. Any quick fixes?
output: Non‑adjustable slings can be shifted to find a good angle; you may need to put a thin blanket in the bottom for a newborn. You can also use a rubber band to gather extra fabric quickly in some slings. Keep experimenting and check instructions or videos for more help, or ask for suggestions at an LLL meeting.

instruction: Will using a pacifier help soothe my newborn between feeds?
output: Pacifiers were designed to substitute for the breast, but in the early weeks if a baby wants to suck he usually wants to eat. Twenty minutes of sucking raises a baby’s CCK so he thinks he’s full, and sucking on a pacifier can trick that system and reduce intake at a time when a baby is meant to grow quickly. Pacifiers have their place, but they can reduce feeding intake in early weeks.

instruction: Can pacifier use cause problems with breastfeeding or weight?
output: Pacifiers are linked to early weaning, though the reason isn’t clear. Sucking on a pacifier can trick the CCK system so a baby feels full and may take in less milk, which can affect weight gain. Pacifiers may also be associated with sucking problems, especially if started in the first few days.

instruction: Are there any infection risks if my child uses a pacifier in daycare?
output: Yes. Children who use pacifiers in day care are reported to be more prone to thrush and ear infections.

instruction: Should I avoid latex pacifiers for my baby?
output: Latex pacifiers have been linked to latex allergies.

instruction: Could long-term pacifier use change my child’s mouth shape or teeth?
output: Prolonged pacifier use can affect mouth development, including raising a child’s palate and crowding the teeth.

instruction: I rely on a pacifier a lot to calm my baby. Is that a concern?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Mothers who rely on pacifiers tend to develop fewer baby‑calming skills of their own. Using a pacifier often can reduce opportunities to practice other calming techniques.

instruction: Where can I get more help learning to use a sling properly?
output: For more help with your sling, check for instructions or videos online, ask for suggestions at an LLL meeting, and keep experimenting. If it doesn’t seem to work now, try again in a week or two.

instruction: Should I use a pacifier in the first few weeks?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Pacifiers were designed to substitute for the breast, and sucking for about twenty minutes raises a baby’s CCK so the baby thinks he’s full. In the early weeks, if a baby wants to suck he wants to eat; a pacifier can reduce intake when the baby is meant to grow quickly. Pacifiers are linked to early weaning and may be associated with sucking problems, especially if started in the first few days. If you’re tempted to use a pacifier, it makes more sense to call a helper to see what’s going on.

instruction: Can a pacifier make my baby gain less weight?
output: Yes. Sucking for about twenty minutes raises a baby’s CCK so the baby thinks he’s full, and sucking on a pacifier can trick that CCK system so the baby’s weight gain will suffer.

instruction: Are pacifiers linked to early weaning?
output: Pacifiers are linked to early weaning, though the reason for the connection isn’t clear.

instruction: Could starting a pacifier in the first few days cause sucking problems?
output: Yes. Pacifiers may be associated with sucking problems, especially if they’re started in the first few days, though the reason isn’t clear.

instruction: If my newborn keeps wanting to suck, should I give a pacifier or call someone?
output: Many parents wonder about this. In the early weeks, if a baby wants to suck he wants to eat; rather than reach for a pacifier, it makes more sense to call a helper to see what’s going on.

instruction: Do babies in day care who use pacifiers get more infections?
output: Children who use pacifiers in day care are more prone to thrush and ear infections.

instruction: Are latex pacifiers safe for babies with allergies?
output: Latex pacifiers have been linked to latex allergies.

instruction: Can long-term pacifier use change my child’s mouth or breathing?
output: Prolonged pacifier use can affect mouth development: it can raise a child’s palate and crowd the teeth, narrow the nasal passages, and lead to an increased risk of sleep apnea and later speech problems.

instruction: Do orthodontic or exercise pacifiers help jaw development?
output: Orthodontic and exercise pacifiers really aren’t effective. They are sometimes recommended for nighttime use in a breastfed baby, but nursing meets sucking needs and also provides emotional needs, good jaw development, and a full tummy.

instruction: How soon can I put my baby on a strict feeding schedule?
output: It makes sense to wonder about this. Setting up a strict schedule risks an underfed baby and early weaning because many babies can’t wait the book-recommended intervals. Unscheduled nursing becomes flexible and easy to fit around activities and helps the baby grow.

instruction: Will nursing make it hard to do other tasks like shopping or working?
output: Nursing, once you and your baby have had some practice, becomes a minor, quick, and flexible part of the day. You can fit nursing into many activities—for example, a quick nursing before going to the grocery store can let you shop longer without stopping—and some people do work while nursing.

instruction: If I respond every time my newborn cries, will I spoil them?
output: Many parents worry about this. No — babies aren’t manipulative, and meeting their needs gives them a reliable base. At this young age a baby’s wants are the same as his needs, and meeting either one does not spoil him. When the child is older, around two, wants and needs may differ and parents help sort that out, but for now the baby needs love.

instruction: Can I spoil my nursing baby by picking them up whenever they cry?
output: Many parents wonder about this. No. Babies aren’t manipulative, perceptive, sneaky, or subversive. At this young age their wants are the same as their needs, and meeting either one won’t spoil them. Providing for babies gives them a totally reliable base from which to explore and make sense of the world.

instruction: Is it okay to feed or comfort my baby whenever they seem to want it?
output: Yes. At this young age a baby’s wants are the same as their needs, and meeting either one is fine. Meeting their needs gives them a reliable base for beginning to explore and make sense of the world.

instruction: If I always pick up my baby right away, will she learn to be dependent?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Babies aren’t manipulative or subversive; they simply are. Providing for them builds a reliable base, so meeting their needs now does not mean they are being spoiled.

instruction: When should I start helping my child learn patience and tolerance?
output: When the same baby is about two years old, sometimes wants and needs will be different. At that point it will be up to you to help him sort wants from needs and to help him learn patience, tolerance, and kindness.

instruction: Does meeting my baby's wants now prevent them learning patience later?
output: No. At this young age their wants are the same as their needs, and meeting those needs gives them a reliable base. Later, around two years old, you will help them sort wants and needs and learn patience, tolerance, and kindness.

instruction: Why do you say babies can’t be spoiled?
output: Because babies aren’t manipulative, perceptive, sneaky, or subversive — they simply are. At this young age their wants equal their needs, and meeting those needs provides a reliable base for exploration and learning.

instruction: What does a baby need most at this age?
output: Love. Meeting a baby’s needs provides a reliable base from which they can begin to explore and make sense of the world, and for now all a baby needs to learn is love.

instruction: How did people care for nursing babies when life was unpredictable long ago?
output: Nursing babies existed long before clocks, when finding food, tending animals, and avoiding sudden dangers were unpredictable. Raising a baby then had to be flexible, and that flexible approach worked well.

instruction: Should I worry that my baby is manipulative when they cry at night?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Babies aren’t manipulative, perceptive, sneaky, or subversive — they simply are — so crying is not manipulation but a way of signaling needs that should be met.

instruction: At what age might my child's wants differ from their needs?
output: When the same baby is two years old, sometimes his wants and needs will be different. That is when caregivers help sort the difference and teach patience, tolerance, and kindness.

instruction: If my toddler sometimes wants something that isn’t a need, what should I do?
output: When a child is around two years old, wants and needs can differ. It will be up to you to help him sort wants from needs and to teach patience, tolerance, and kindness.

instruction: Why compare providing for babies to reliable gravity?
output: The comparison means that just as reliable gravity gives a stable expectation, meeting a baby’s needs provides a totally reliable base from which the baby can begin to explore and make sense of the world. If basic support were unreliable we would feel insecure, and the same is true for babies.

instruction: Is it wrong to respond immediately to a nursing baby’s cries for comfort or feeding?
output: No. At this young age a baby’s wants are the same as their needs, and meeting either one is appropriate. Providing for babies builds a reliable base for future exploration and learning.

instruction: How should I think about my baby's needs versus wants right now?
output: Right now, a baby’s wants are the same as their needs, so meeting either is fine and won’t spoil the baby. For now the main thing the baby needs to learn is love, and providing for them gives a reliable base for later development.

instruction: Is it normal to still feel unsettled six weeks after giving birth?
output: Yes. New mothers often say it takes six weeks or more to feel settled as they recover, connect with the baby, get breastfeeding going, and adjust to motherhood. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Why do many cultures give new mothers a "babymoon" after birth?
output: Those cultures give a mother a month or so of help with housework and meals so she can connect with her baby, recover, get breastfeeding going, and adjust to motherhood.

instruction: My newborn sometimes gives quick eyes-closed smiles after nursing — is that just gas?
output: Those early eyes-closed smiles signal emotional comfort, not gas.

instruction: My nipples are still sore after two weeks. When should I get help?
output: If your nipples are still bothering you after two weeks, check with an LLL Leader or other skilled breastfeeding helper. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: I keep hearing about "laid-back breastfeeding." Should I try that?
output: If you haven’t tried the laid-back breastfeeding position, give it a whirl — you may find it makes breastfeeding much more comfortable and relaxing.

instruction: How do I know when to offer the breast — should I wait for hunger cues or watch the clock?
output: Offer whenever your baby seems unsettled rather than strictly watching the clock. The current approach is to feed when the baby gives hunger cues instead of feeding only on a rigid schedule.

instruction: Can I offer the breast just because I miss my baby or want to finish reading?
output: Yes. You can offer to nurse for your reasons — because you miss him, because it feels like a while, or simply because you feel like it. If it makes your baby happy, that’s good enough. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: My baby nurses much more often than my friend’s. Should I be worried?
output: There are several normal reasons: some babies are less efficient at getting milk or tire before they’re full; some mothers can’t store a lot of milk; sometimes it’s just a personality difference. As long as your baby is gaining well and you’re okay with the extra nursing, all’s well. If you’re not okay with it, your baby isn’t gaining well, or you suspect a bigger problem, talk to a Leader or your baby’s doctor.

instruction: Are babies supposed to be all business when nursing at two to six weeks?
output: A baby this age is generally all business when he’s eating, though you’ll also see quick and gentle smiles at times when he’s feeling good after nursing or between feedings.

instruction: Should I put my baby to the breast when he cries, or will that spoil him?
output: Putting your baby to breast when he cries will not spoil him. Your baby’s physiology is geared to constant holding and frequent nursing.

instruction: My baby has been crying a long time and can’t nurse — what should I do first?
output: If he’s been crying for a while, he may be too upset to nurse. Give him some cuddling and soothing, then offer the breast again. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: When might I first see my baby give a social smile with eyes open and looking at me?
output: By six weeks you may begin to see social smiles with the baby’s eyes open and looking right at you.

instruction: My newborn wants to nurse every hour. Is that normal?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Some babies can’t get milk as efficiently or tire before they’re full, so they do fine as long as they can eat frequently. It’s also normal for babies to cluster-feed, taking several short feedings in an hour or two, often before a longer sleep. Nursing frequently also helps keep your milk supply in better shape.

instruction: Should I feed my baby on a strict schedule every three or four hours?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. In the past mothers were told to feed on a schedule, but research showed that led to breastfeeding problems, so the recommendation is now to feed when the baby gives hunger cues rather than strictly every three or four hours. Deliberately holding a baby off has not been shown to provide benefits and can slow weight gain in the long run.

instruction: My baby cries—should I put him to the breast or will that spoil him?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Putting your baby to the breast when he cries will not spoil him. If he’s been crying for a while he may be too upset to nurse, so try cuddling and soothing first, then offer the breast again.

instruction: Someone told me my baby is just using me as a pacifier. Is that true?
output: Not at all. Nursing feeds, pacifies, puts a baby to sleep, comforts, reassures, and relaxes. If you offer the breast only as food and not for comfort, you cut out calories the baby gets with those other reasons and are more likely to have supply problems.

instruction: I’m worried my baby isn’t gaining enough weight. What should I do?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. If your baby isn’t gaining well, if you’re not okay with how often you’re nursing, or if you suspect a bigger problem, talk with a Leader or your baby’s doctor.

instruction: Can nursing very often cause low milk supply?
output: No. Nursing frequently fills babies better and helps keep milk supplies in better shape. By contrast, offering the breast only as food and not for comfort makes supply problems more likely.

instruction: What is conversational nursing?
output: Conversational nursing is part of nursing as your baby’s first human relationship. As nursing becomes harmonious, whim nursing often includes longer interactions—nursing represents your longest conversations and you can hold them whenever you and your baby like.

instruction: Is it normal for babies to have several short feedings in a short time?
output: Yes. That’s called cluster-feeding: the baby takes several short feedings during an hour or two, often tanking up in preparation for a longer sleep. You’re encouraged to offer the breast whenever your baby is unsettled, even if you nursed recently.

instruction: My baby only takes small amounts then seems hungry again. Should I limit nursing?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Some babies take small, frequent nursings and do fine as long as they eat frequently; these little whim nursings allow a baby to take in plenty of milk. If your baby is not gaining well or you’re not comfortable with the pattern, talk to a Leader or your baby’s doctor.

instruction: Will holding my baby off the breast prevent bad habits or spoilage?
output: Deliberately holding a baby off interferes with the baby’s and your physiology, has not been shown to provide benefits, and slows weight gain in the long run. Putting your baby to the breast when he cries will not spoil him.

instruction: Where can I get help for other breastfeeding concerns in the first weeks?
output: If you have other questions, look on the llli.org forums and then contact your local LLL Leader for real‑person support and help.

instruction: Is it okay if my baby is gaining faster than the growth chart lines?
output: Some babies are built to grow faster than the lines on the chart. If your baby is gaining well and you’re okay with the extra nursing, that can be fine—some babies simply grow faster than the approved rate of gain.

instruction: If I use the breast mostly for comfort rather than food, will that affect my milk supply?
output: Offering the breast for comfort as well as food supports milk supply. If you offer the breast only as food and not as a pacifier, you’re more likely to end up with supply problems and you lose the extra calories and comfort nursing provides.

instruction: Is it okay to offer my breast just to comfort my baby, or will he be "just using me as a pacifier"?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Nursing does feed your baby, but it also pacifies, puts him to sleep, comforts, reassures, and relaxes him. Offering your breast for comfort gives those calories and other benefits, supports your milk supply, and helps breastfeeding run smoothly.

instruction: What are the benefits of "just-because" or conversational nursings?
output: Just-because nursings help put healthy rolls on your baby’s arms, make both of you feel secure and happy, get your lifelong relationship off to a close and connected beginning, give your milk supply a solid base, and make your job much easier.

instruction: My breasts feel soft now — does that mean my milk supply is low?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. By about six weeks many women find their breasts are fairly soft most of the time and feel full only if the baby goes longer than usual between nursings. Supply and demand have often balanced, excess fluids and milk have gone away, and breastfeeding is running more smoothly. If your baby continues to gain well, you can relax about your milk supply.

instruction: I used to leak a lot but now I barely leak. Is that normal?
output: Yes. As supply and demand balance, you may not leak as much. Your breasts become more efficient at releasing milk only when your baby is actually at your breast, though sometimes a baby’s cry, thinking about your baby when you’re apart, or pumping can still release milk.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is thriving in the early weeks?
output: Signs of a thriving baby include: beginning to gain weight after your milk comes in; passing birth weight by at least 10 to 14 days; gaining appropriately (the average is roughly 1 ounce, about 30 g, per day in the early months but that is only an average and some babies gain more or less); having at least three yellow poops about the size of the circle your thumb and forefinger make and five colorless, odorless, heavy wet diapers each day; no trouble latching on or staying attached; eyes open and interested for the first part of nursing; slow, steady sucks for part of every nursing beginning soon after the feeding starts; content for at least a few minutes after nursing and generally consolable by nursing again; sometimes calm, alert times between feedings; visibly filling out with plumper thighs, fuller cheeks, and deepening wrist creases; and growing in length and head circumference.

instruction: How many wet diapers should my baby have each day?
output: Your baby should have about five colorless, odorless, heavy wet diapers each day.

instruction: When should my baby pass his birth weight?
output: A baby should pass birth weight by at least ten to fourteen days.

instruction: What's a typical daily weight gain in the early months?
output: The average rate is roughly 1 ounce (about 30 g) per day during the early months, but that is only an average and your baby may gain more or less. Your doctor or midwife will monitor weight gain during regular visits.

instruction: Some of the thriving signs aren’t happening for my baby — what should I do?
output: It makes sense to feel worried. If any of the listed signs aren’t happening for your baby, check with an LLL Leader or your baby’s doctor.

instruction: Could my breasts always being soft be normal?
output: Yes. Breasts that are always fairly soft can be due to having a lot of storage capacity or because your baby nurses often enough to keep them from overfilling. A happy, thriving baby who’s growing well and filling diapers indicates nursing is fine.

instruction: Can thinking about my baby or a baby’s cry cause milk to let down?
output: Yes. Sometimes a baby’s cry or even thinking about your baby when you’re apart can lead to a milk release; pumping can also release milk.

instruction: Should I wear breast pads even if I don’t leak?
output: If you don’t leak, don’t use breast pads.

instruction: What does it mean that milk begins to flow only at the baby’s request?
output: It means that as weeks go by your breasts become more efficient and milk is released mainly when your baby is actually at your breast. In the very early days milk might release at random times or when you were especially full, but later it usually flows at the baby’s request, though cries, thinking about the baby, or pumping can still trigger a release.

instruction: If I offer the breast only for food and not comfort, could that affect my milk supply?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Offering the breast only as food and not as a pacifier cuts out the calories and other calming reasons babies nurse, and you’re more likely to end up with supply problems and lose the pleasure and ease of going with the flow of breastfeeding.

instruction: What happened when some parents handled their babies as little as possible and showed little affection?
output: There were some very unhappy mothers and babies as a result.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is getting enough milk?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Signs of a thriving baby include: beginning to gain weight after your milk comes in; passing birth weight by at least 10–14 days; gaining weight appropriately (the average is roughly 1 ounce/30 g per day in the early months, but babies vary); having at least three yellow poops about the size of the thumb-and-forefinger circle and five colorless, odorless, heavy wet diapers each day; no trouble latching or staying attached; slow, steady sucks for part of every nursing; being content for at least a few minutes after nursing and usually consoled by nursing again; sometimes calm, alert times between feedings; visible filling out (plumper thighs, fuller cheeks, deeper wrist creases) and smooth skin; and growth in length and head circumference. If these signs aren’t happening, check with an LLL Leader or your baby’s doctor.

instruction: How many wet diapers and poops should my breastfed baby have each day?
output: Your baby should have at least three yellow poops (about the size of the circle your thumb and forefinger make) and five colorless, odorless, heavy wet diapers each day.

instruction: My baby hasn’t regained birth weight by two weeks — should I be worried?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Babies should pass their birth weight by at least ten to fourteen days. If your baby hasn’t reached that milestone, check with an LLL Leader or your baby’s doctor.

instruction: What is a normal rate of weight gain for a newborn?
output: The average rate in the early months is roughly 1 ounce (about 30 g) per day, but that’s only an average and your baby may gain more or less. Your doctor or midwife will monitor weight gain at regular visits.

instruction: How much milk do exclusively breastfed babies usually take each day?
output: By about four weeks of age, most exclusively breastfed babies take about 25 to 27 ounces (750–800 ml) of milk a day. By the end of the first week many mothers are already producing about three‑quarters of that amount, and by two weeks they’re nearing their peak.

instruction: My baby suddenly wants to nurse all the time — does that mean I’m losing my supply?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. If your baby’s diaper output stays high, there’s no need to panic; this pattern most likely means a growth spurt, which is a sign he’s getting lots of milk. Emptying a breast more often increases output, so the extra nursing boosts supply during the growth spurt itself. After two or three days he’ll usually return to his normal pattern and your milk production will adjust again within a day or so.

instruction: When do growth spurts typically happen?
output: Growth spurts can happen just about anytime. You may hear common timing like around ten days, three weeks, six weeks, three months, six months, and so on, but they’re not limited to those times. If you nurse whenever either of you likes, you may never notice them at all.

instruction: If I remove less milk will my supply drop?
output: Take less out and less is made; take milk out and more milk will be made. There is an upper limit, and some women are never able to provide all the milk their babies need. If you’re concerned about your supply, get help now.

instruction: What should I do if my baby has trouble latching or staying attached?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Difficulty latching or staying attached is one of the signs not consistent with a thriving baby. If this is happening, check with an LLL Leader or your baby’s doctor.

instruction: How can I tell if my baby is filling out and growing well physically?
output: A baby who is filling out will have plumper thighs, fuller cheeks, and deepening creases on the wrists. Her skin will look smooth, not loose or baggy, and she will be growing in length and head circumference.

instruction: If my baby nurses a lot during a growth spurt, will that permanently increase my milk supply?
output: Emptying a breast more thoroughly and more often increases its output, but that sudden feeding frenzy boosts your supply only during the growth spurt itself, not permanently.

instruction: Should I worry about measuring how many ounces my baby takes at each feeding?
output: Precise figures don’t matter. A baby’s intake varies from nursing to nursing and day to day; as long as he’s gaining well, you can forget the numbers and watch your baby to let him tell you what he needs and when.

instruction: After a few days of frequent nursing my breasts feel very full and there aren’t many feedings — what will happen?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. After a growth spurt the baby will usually return to his normal feeding pattern and your milk production will adjust again within a day or so.

instruction: Who will keep track of my baby’s weight gain during the early visits?
output: Your doctor or midwife will monitor the baby’s weight gain during regular visits. If signs of thriving aren’t happening, check with an LLL Leader or your baby’s doctor.

instruction: My baby suddenly wants to nurse constantly for two or three days — is that a growth spurt and will my milk change afterward?
output: It sounds like a growth spurt. For two or three days babies often nurse much more, then they usually return to their normal feeding pattern and may nap more. Your milk production will adjust again within a day or so. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: When should I expect growth spurts to happen?
output: You may hear about spurts around ten days, three weeks, six weeks, three months, six months, and so on, but they can happen just about anytime. If you nurse whenever either of you likes, you may never notice them at all.

instruction: I worry I don’t have enough milk. Is milk supply fixed or can it change?
output: Milk production isn’t a rigidly set supply. Take milk out and more milk will be made; take less out and less is made. There is an upper limit, and some women never provide all the milk their babies need. The early weeks are used to establish expected milk production capacity, so if you’re truly worried, get help as soon as you can.

instruction: I think I have too much milk. Will that settle down on its own?
output: Many mothers feel they have too much milk. Breasts make extra milk at the start as a backup, and by the end of the first six weeks most people settle down to what their babies need. Some breasts keep making more and may need persuasion to cut back; there are ideas for this elsewhere. If oversupply stops, you may feel like you’re losing supply, but your baby is almost certainly thriving.

instruction: My baby is unhappy but refuses to nurse right now. What can I try to calm him?
output: Try other things: some babies dislike a wet diaper, some need a change of position, view, or motion. Take your baby into the fresh air, sing to him, or dance with him. In between attempts, offer to nurse again. Nursing often solves many baby problems besides hunger and will often become the baby’s answer.

instruction: My baby is becoming less willing to nurse over time. What might cause that?
output: Possible reasons include being flooded every time your milk releases, any signs of illness or extreme stuffiness, white patches in his mouth, or having been given bottles. There is more on breast refusal elsewhere, and it can also help to touch base with your LLL Leader. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Why does my baby cry a lot even when I’ve fed him?
output: The single most common cause of crying is a baby missing his mother. Babies who aren’t held cry significantly more than babies who are, especially around six weeks. There are other possible causes discussed elsewhere, and if the baby is truly unhappy you should check with your baby’s doctor.

instruction: What are "sparklers" when people talk about baby personalities?
output: Sparklers are babies who are doing, fussing, and demanding all the time. They are often high-energy and high-maintenance, may have minor physical discomforts that need distraction (a food sensitivity or cranky intestines or just their personality), and tantrums come easily because they’re already slightly on edge. They seek brain stimulation to shine their brightest.

instruction: If I nurse my baby whenever he wants, will I notice growth spurts less?
output: Yes. If you’re nursing your baby whenever either of you likes, you may never notice growth spurts at all.

instruction: My baby used to choke on big letdowns and now that it’s calmer I worry I’m losing my supply. Is that normal?
output: That can happen with oversupply. Those of us with oversupplies get used to overly full, dripping breasts and choking babies and may think we’re losing supply when that stops. Check earlier notes about thriving babies; yours is almost certainly thriving.

instruction: Are there any books you recommend for dealing with a fussy baby?
output: Some recommended books are The Fussy Baby Book by William and Martha Sears and Colic Solved by Bryan Vartabedian.

instruction: I keep a list of calming ideas but I still feel guilty when nothing works. Are there usually partial solutions for chronic fussiness?
output: There are usually at least partial solutions to chronic fussiness, including time. Sometimes the baby simply outgrows a difficult beginning. It may help to post a tear sheet of Fussy Baby Ideas where you can find it when you need other solutions. Many parents wonder about this.

instruction: Could white patches in my baby’s mouth be related to refusing the breast?
output: Yes, white patches in his mouth are listed as one of the reasons a baby may become less comfortable with nursing over time.

instruction: My baby cries a lot but isn't hungry — could he be missing me?
output: Yes. The single most common cause of crying is a baby missing his mother. Babies who aren’t held cry significantly more, especially around six weeks.

instruction: People say I shouldn't pick him up every time he cries — will I spoil him if I respond?
output: Many parents wonder about this. You cannot spoil a baby at this age by responding to his needs. When a baby cries he is asking for help because he can’t help himself; responding quickly and reliably builds trust, confidence, lifetime resiliency, and helps him become secure and independent sooner.

instruction: What are sparklers and orchids when people talk about baby personalities?
output: Sparklers are high-energy, fussing, and demanding babies who often need distraction from minor physical discomforts (like a food sensitivity or cranky intestines) and who seek brain stimulation. Orchids are high-need babies who may find many environments overly stimulating, confusing, or scary; they need their mother’s soothing presence, may not leave her lap easily, and blossom with tender care.

instruction: My high-energy baby has frequent tantrums — is that normal for a sparkler?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. Yes: sparklers are often slightly on edge, high-energy, and high-maintenance, and tantrums can come easily for them. Time and maturation usually help lessen these needs.

instruction: I've tried ideas from books and nursing hasn't helped. What should I do if my baby is truly unhappy?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. If none of the usual ideas seems to be the problem and you have a truly unhappy baby, check with your baby’s doctor.

instruction: Are there recommended books about fussy babies I can read?
output: Yes. The Fussy Baby by William Sears and Martha Sears and Colic Solved by Bryan Vartabedian are mentioned as helpful. The Fussy Baby also includes an I Love U massage that may help.

instruction: Will responding quickly to my baby's cries make him dependent and less independent later?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Responding quickly and reliably builds trust and wires the brain to cope with stress positively, which makes him able to be secure and independent sooner than if he had to manage all on his own.

instruction: Someone told me to let my baby 'cry it out' to teach independence — is that supported here?
output: The excerpt notes that children raised on the old 'exercise their lungs, let them cry it out' methods can become adults experiencing high rates of anxiety, low self-esteem, and stress-related illness, so that approach is not presented as a positive strategy.

instruction: What is the I Love U massage I heard about?
output: The I Love U massage is an idea included in The Fussy Baby book and the excerpt says it may help with fussiness.

instruction: My baby won't leave my lap and seems scared of new places — could he be an orchid?
output: Many parents wonder about this. Yes: orchids are high-need babies who may find many environments overly stimulating, who don’t leave their mother’s lap easily, who don’t rush to other children, and who wilt if yelled at but blossom with tender care.

instruction: Is it true babies who aren't held cry more around six weeks?
output: Yes. The excerpt specifically says babies who aren’t held cry significantly more, especially around six weeks.

instruction: Should I post a Fussy Baby Ideas tear sheet on my fridge to use when my baby is fussy?
output: Yes. The excerpt suggests that posting the Fussy Baby Ideas tear sheet from the back of the book on your refrigerator can help you have somewhere to turn when your baby is fussy and nursing hasn’t helped yet.

instruction: Can chronic fussiness get better on its own?
output: Yes. There are usually at least partial solutions to chronic fussiness, and time is one of them; sometimes the baby simply outgrows a difficult beginning.

instruction: How does responding to a crying baby affect his brain pathways?
output: Responding quickly and reliably helps develop neural pathways that result in calm, confidence, and coping. Refusing to answer cries can lay down 'no one cares' pathways, decreasing trust and self-worth and wiring the brain to react to future stresses more intensely.

instruction: I'm getting conflicting advice from family and professionals and I feel unsure — what does the excerpt say about trusting my instincts?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The excerpt says mothers often get misinformation from trusted people and that encouraging mothers to trust themselves and validating their instincts is vital; La Leche League is noted for validating that a mother's bodily response to a crying baby is important.

instruction: Can I spoil my baby by picking him up every time he cries?
output: It’s not possible to spoil a baby at this age by responding to his needs. Responding quickly and reliably helps develop neural pathways that support a secure, resilient person.

instruction: What can happen if I ignore my newborn's cries?
output: Many parents worry about this. Ignoring a crying baby lays down 'no one cares' pathways, decreases his trust, can make him feel neglected, hopeless, and despairing, lower his feelings of self‑worth, and wire his brain to react to future stresses more intensely. Children raised on the 'let them cry it out' approach can become adults with high rates of anxiety, low self‑esteem, and stress‑related illness.

instruction: I’m getting different advice from relatives and my pediatrician; should I trust my instincts about breastfeeding and responding to my baby?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Mothers often ask people they trust (mothers, mothers‑in‑law, pediatricians) and can get misinformation. Encouraging mothers to trust themselves and validating that their body reacts when a baby cries is important.

instruction: I’ve been feeling anxious and unhappy since my baby was born. How long should I wait before talking to my doctor?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. If feelings of anxiety, unhappiness, or depression continue for more than a few days, talk to your doctor.

instruction: Is it safe to take my breastfed baby out soon after birth?
output: Your breastfed baby is sturdy right from the start. As soon as you want to be out and about, your healthy baby can certainly join you. It’s not going out that makes a baby sick; being held by sick people does, so encourage hand washing and follow your instincts about passing the baby around.

instruction: Is it okay to nurse in public? I’m nervous about people seeing me.
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Absolutely — nursing in public is allowed; no province, state, or country is known to prohibit it, and many specifically protect it. Most likely people will at most get a brief glimpse of skin, and the more breastfeeding is seen, the more it becomes accepted.

instruction: What are practical ways to nurse discreetly in public?
output: Practice at home or with friends in front of a mirror. Wear clothes you can nurse in comfortably (two‑piece outfits that pull up from the bottom work well). Your baby’s body will hide most bare skin; a cardigan covers remaining areas. Wearing a tank top with small cutouts under your shirt can help. Dark prints hide wet spots. Press your forearm against the other side to prevent leaking, or pull the unused side up a bit to hide any wet spot. When the baby latches, briefly turn your body or head away; meet others’ eyes with a friendly expression or casually avoid eye contact. Note that a blanket or special cover tends to attract attention.

instruction: People tell me to 'let the baby cry' so he becomes independent—does that approach have risks?
output: It makes sense to feel unsure about this. The older 'let them cry' approach can lead to children who become adults with high rates of anxiety, low self‑esteem, and stress‑related illness. Mothers are hardwired to respond; responding to needs supports secure development.

instruction: What should I ask people to do before they hold my newborn?
output: Encourage others to wash their hands before holding the baby, and follow your instincts about whether or not you want to pass him around.

instruction: When my baby cries my body hurts—does that mean something is wrong with me?
output: It makes sense to feel unsettled by that. Feeling physical distress when your baby cries is described as a biologically imperative response, and encouraging mothers to trust that reaction is important.

instruction: Will nursing in public make strangers see my breasts and upset my partner?
output: Many partners worry about that. Most likely strangers will only get a brief glimpse of skin, though they may notice you are nursing. Increased visibility of breastfeeding tends to increase acceptance over time.

instruction: Should I use a blanket or special cover when nursing in public to be more private?
output: A blanket or specially designed shawl or cover‑up tends to attract more attention than simply nursing. Limiting nursing to very private situations can also limit how much the baby nurses and therefore can limit the baby’s food.

instruction: Are breastfed babies less likely to get sick than formula‑fed babies if I take them out?
output: A formula‑fed baby’s immune system is described as more limited and he is much more susceptible to illness. The text says a breastfed baby is sturdy from the start and can go out when you want to be out and about.

instruction: I’m worried I’m doing something horribly wrong when I follow my instincts with my crying baby. Is that common?
output: It’s common to have questions like this. Many mothers worry that following their instincts is wrong because they get conflicting or incorrect information from people they trust; encouraging mothers to trust themselves is highlighted as important.

instruction: How can I nurse in public without showing too much skin?
output: Practice at home or with friends in front of a mirror to see what others see. Wear two-piece outfits with tops you can pull up from the bottom — you don’t need special nursing clothes. Your baby’s body will hide almost all the bare skin; a cardigan will cover anything baby’s body doesn’t. You can also wear a tank top underneath with circles cut out for nursing so your outer shirt covers the circles and the tank covers anything else. Dark-colored prints help hide wet spots.

instruction: I keep leaking milk on the side I’m not using. How can I prevent wet spots?
output: When you’re nursing on one side, press your forearm against the other side to help prevent leaking. If that isn’t enough, pull the side the baby isn’t nursing on up just a bit so any wet spot won’t be a bull’s-eye around your nipple. Dark-colored prints also help hide any wet spots.

instruction: Should I use a nursing cover or shawl in public?
output: A blanket or specially designed shawl or cover-up actually attracts more attention than just nursing. Many people find nursing without a cover draws less notice.

instruction: I feel awkward when my baby latches in public. What can I do so people don’t notice?
output: Turn your body away briefly or turn your head away for the moment your baby latches; people tend to follow your eyes and are unlikely to notice. You can meet others’ eyes with a friendly, open expression or casually avoid eye contact — most people will automatically match your behavior. It’s common to have questions like this.

instruction: Will only nursing at home affect my baby’s feeding?
output: Limiting nursing to very private situations can limit nursing, which limits your baby’s food. Nursing in public also models breastfeeding for others and helps people learn by watching other mothers nurse.

instruction: Do I need special nursing clothes or can I use regular clothes?
output: You don’t need special nursing clothes. Two-piece outfits with tops you can pull up from the bottom work well. A cardigan can cover exposed areas and a tank with nursing cutouts under your shirt can be helpful, especially with twins. Dark-colored prints help hide wet spots.

instruction: I’m going back to work and thinking about bottles. When should I introduce a bottle?
output: If you plan to use a bottle because you’re returning to work, consider waiting until both you and your baby love breastfeeding and feel like old pros at it. The text says there’s no magic window and waiting reduces the risk that bottles will take over.

instruction: Can I put some breast milk in the freezer for emergencies without giving bottles now?
output: Yes. You can express milk and store it in the freezer as emergency milk without getting your baby used to a bottle.

instruction: Someone wants to give my baby a bottle so my partner can help with feedings. Is that okay?
output: Having someone else give a bottle in order to participate in feeding misses a key point about breastfeeding: it’s a relationship that only a mother and baby have. Partners can participate in other ways and teach babies that there are relationships beyond breastfeeding. If you do use a bottle sometimes, it’s worth the extra effort to use your own milk because even occasional formula carries intestinal changes and illness and allergy risks and should be used only if no human milk is available.

instruction: Is giving a daily or weekly bottle 'just in case' okay for a fully breastfed baby?
output: Giving a daily or weekly bottle just in case to an otherwise fully breastfed baby is usually much more of a problem than a solution.

instruction: My baby isn’t gaining well. Can I use a bottle for supplementation?
output: If your baby isn’t gaining well at any age, a bottle can be used for supplementation and can be used very successfully or very unsuccessfully.

instruction: Will bringing my baby with me mean I can’t go out with my partner?
output: Babies are very portable. You can go out with your partner to dinner, the movies, bowling, or many activities and bring your baby along. Babies often sleep through movies, and if they don’t you can nurse in the dark so no one notices. You don’t have to leave your baby to get out of the house; bringing him can make the outing better for both of you.

instruction: Are dark prints helpful for nursing clothes?
output: Yes. Dark-colored prints help hide any wet spots from leaking while nursing.

instruction: I feel pressured to get away from my newborn, but I don’t want to. Is it normal to want to stay close while breastfeeding?
output: Many mothers hear that they should get away from the baby, but at this stage they often don’t feel like going because it’s like the falling-in-love stage and togetherness is important as you get to know each other. Babies are portable so you can still go out with your partner and bring the baby along. It makes sense to feel unsure about this.

instruction: Can I pump and freeze breast milk for emergencies without giving my baby bottles now?
output: Yes. You can express milk and store it in the freezer for an emergency without getting your baby used to a bottle.

instruction: My partner wants to give the baby a daily bottle so we can help with feeding. Is that a good idea?
output: Giving a daily or weekly bottle to an otherwise fully breastfed baby is usually more of a problem than a solution. Having someone else give a bottle to participate in feeding also misses the point that breastfeeding is a relationship only a mother and baby have.

instruction: If I do give a bottle sometimes for convenience, should it be formula or my milk?
output: If you use a bottle from time to time for casual reasons, it’s well worth the extra effort to use your own milk in it. Even occasional formula comes with intestinal changes and illness and allergy risks and should be used only if no human milk is available.

instruction: People keep telling me I need time away from my baby. Is it normal to feel like that?
output: Many mothers hear this, but in the early breastfeeding relationship they usually don’t feel like going away. This is like the falling-in-love stage with your baby, and togetherness is important as you get to know each other.
